Shared By Fanpage: Tài Liệu Khóa Học UniMap
 
VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN BỘ ĐỀ DỰ ĐOÁN ĐẶC BIỆT NĂM 2026 
Chúc các em lớp VIP 90: Ôn là TRÚNG TỦ - Thi là TRÚNG TUYỂN 
 
Câu hỏi 11.  Em thấy VIP 90 1 ngày cô phát hành 3 mục, vậy quy trình học VIP 90 như thế nào để hiệu 
quả nhất? 
Trả lời 
Để học hiệu quả VIP 90 em nên: 
Bước 1: Làm kĩ; nghiêm túc Bài thi Online trước buổi học để tự đánh giá mức độ hiểu – vận dụng theo 
từng đơn vị kiến thức 
 
Bước 2: Học Từ vựng trong Bài thi Online trước buổi học với tính năng cao cấp: EZ Vocab và Review 
nhanh từ vựng   
 
Bước 3: Theo dõi cô giảng bài; chữa bài 1 cách cẩn thận – kĩ lưỡng 
Bước 4: Ôn tập lại kiến thức đã học ở bước 3. Học từ vựng – cấu trúc mới mà cô mở rộng khi giảng và 
Tập dịch lại toàn bộ bài với Tính năng Tập dịch thông minh 
Bước 5: Làm bài thi Online sau buổi học. Trong đó: Bài thi Online Vận dụng cần làm đúng 100%; Bài 
thi Online Nâng cao có thể không làm nếu mục tiêu chỉ 8+ nhưng cô vẫn khuyến khích các em làm Bài 
thi Online này.  
 
 
 
 
 
 
Chúc các em may mắn, đạt điểm số cao nhất trong kì thi tốt nghiệp THPT 2026 và trúng tuyển NV1  
Đăng Ký Khóa Học Online Tại Fanpage: Tài Liệu Khóa Học UniMap
 


Shared By Fanpage: Tài Liệu Khóa Học UniMap
 
VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN BỘ ĐỀ DỰ ĐOÁN ĐẶC BIỆT NĂM 2026 
Chúc các em lớp VIP 90: Ôn là TRÚNG TỦ - Thi là TRÚNG TUYỂN 
 
Câu hỏi 12. Em chỉ mong muốn mức 7-8+, nên trung tâm có thể điều chỉnh mức điểm bắt buộc đạt bài 
thi xuống thấp hơn 10 được không? 
    Trả lời 
     Hiện khóa học có Quy định làm đúng 100% bài thi Online với bài thi Vận dụng và không có sự 
thay đổi giảm nào với các em mục tiêu 7+; 8+. Em có thể làm lại để làm đúng 100% bài thi. 
 
Câu hỏi 13.  Em đăng ký combo PRO3M với PRO3M PLUS thì nên học VIP90 của PRO3M hay PRO3M 
PLUS ạ? 
Trả lời 
Em học VIP 90 của PRO 3M hay PRO 3MPLUS đều được vì 2 khóa giống nhau. 
 
Câu hỏi 14. Khóa PRO3M thì có mục Bài thi nâng cao không? 
Trả lời 
     VIP 90 thuộc COMBO PRO 3M cũng có Bài thi Online nâng cao em nhé! 
 
Câu hỏi 15. Tài liệu của khóa học được cung cấp như thế nào? có file tổng hợp không ạ? 
      Trả lời 
       Tài liệu khóa học được cung cấp đầy đủ dưới mỗi buổi học/bài thi Online tương ứng và 01 File 
Tổng hợp đầy đủ cho cả tuần ở mục Tài liệu nổi bật (Để tải Tài liệu em truy cập Mục tiện ích; chọn 
Tài liệu; Cọn Tài liệu nổi bật) 
       Thời gian đăng tải: Dự kiến 12h trưa thứ 7 hàng tuần 
 
Câu hỏi 16.  Livestream khóa này có tương tác với cô Phương được không ạ? 
     Trả lời 
      Trong quá trình tham gia buổi học cô sẽ tương tác với các em bằng cách trả lời các câu hỏi quan 
trọng ở thời điểm thích hợp nhằm tránh gián đoạn buổi học quá nhiều lần. Tất cả câu hỏi đều được Team 
trợ giảng trả lời ngay nên em yên tâm là mọi câu hỏi được trả lời ngay lập tức.  
 
 
Câu hỏi 17.  Khi nào có lộ trình khóa VIP90/combo VIP90 PLUS và có thì xem ở đâu ạ? 
Trả lời 
Với khóa học VIP 90 em học tuần tự theo Lịch phát hành; Layout khóa từ Tuần 1 đến Tuần 15  
Với VIP 90PLUS thì em Kết hợp học VIP 90 và Luyện thêm 6-10 đề/1 tuần ở 2 khóa Luyện đề PRO 
VIP và Ngân hàng đề thi (Tùy thời gian em dành cho việc học và luyện môn Anh)  
 
Câu hỏi 18.  Bộ đề tinh hoa của khóa Luyện đề 8+, VIP90 và Plus 3 có giống nhau không? Nếu khác thì 
khác như thế nào? 
Trả lời 
      Bộ đề Tinh hoa 2026 với dự kiến 20+ đề thi ở các Khóa Luyện giải đề; Plus 3; VIP 90 là như nhau. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Chúc các em may mắn, đạt điểm số cao nhất trong kì thi tốt nghiệp THPT 2026 và trúng tuyển NV1  
Đăng Ký Khóa Học Online Tại Fanpage: Tài Liệu Khóa Học UniMap
 


Shared By Fanpage: Tài Liệu Khóa Học UniMap
 
VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN BỘ ĐỀ DỰ ĐOÁN ĐẶC BIỆT NĂM 2026 
Chúc các em lớp VIP 90: Ôn là TRÚNG TỦ - Thi là TRÚNG TUYỂN 
 
Câu hỏi 19.  Em không đủ thời gian để vào học hàng ngày thì phải như thế nào? 
Trả lời 
      Em cần cố gắng sắp xếp để học hết toàn bộ nội dung của khóa. Thời gian học linh động theo thời gian 
rảnh của em vì sau LIVE cô đều lưu bài lại dưới dạng Video để em học lại nhiều lần 
 
Câu hỏi 20.  Làm sao để liên hệ trợ giảng ở khóa khi học VIP90? 
Trả lời 
       Để liên hệ trợ giảng em Nhấn vào biểu tượng Chat với trợ giảng ở góc dưới cùng bên trái màn hình 
nhé. 
 
Câu hỏi 21. Có cần phải học hết bài bổ trợ thì mới học đến nội dung VIP90 không ạ. 
Trả lời 
     Bài Bổ trợ sẽ phát hành vào Chủ nhật mỗi tuần. Em học hết kiến thức của tuần đó rồi học Bổ trợ sau 
nhé. Ưu tiên nhất vẫn là nội dung các tuần tuần VIP 90. Phần Bổ trợ thì em làm sau cũng được nhưng vẫn 
nên làm – luyện kĩ.    
 
Chúc các em may mắn, đạt điểm số cao nhất trong kì thi tốt nghiệp THPT 2026 và trúng tuyển NV1  
Đăng Ký Khóa Học Online Tại Fanpage: Tài Liệu Khóa Học UniMap

sections.raw_text:
Shared By Fanpage: Tài Liệu Khóa Học UniMap
TUẦN SỐ 01 
 
VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
TÀI LIỆU LIVESTREAM ĐỘC QUYỀN 
VIP 90 (2026): TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM BỘ CÂU HỎI  
DỄ TRÚNG TỦ & LUYỆN BỘ ĐỀ TINH HOA  
[V90.01.01] DÀNH TRỌN ĐIỂM CÂU HỎI  
 
TỪ LOẠI VÀ TRẬT TỰ TỪ (BUỔI 1) 
Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương  
 
 
A. TỪ LOẠI 
DANH TỪ 
Đứng sau to be 
Tom is a student. 
Đứng sau mạo từ hoặc các từ hạn định như 
some, many, another … 
Some students are very hard-working. 
 
Vị trí 
Đứng sau tính từ và tính từ sở hữu 
He’s a good student. 
His cat is cute.  
Đứng sau động từ và làm tân ngữ cho động từ This app helps children to learn English.   
Đứng sau giới từ 
 
He’s keen on pop music.  
 
Một số hậu tố thường gặp của danh từ: -tion/-sion, -ment, -ness, -ity, -er/-or, -ist, -ance/-ence, -ship, -
hood, -dom 
 
Ví dụ:  
• -tion / -sion: information (thông tin), decision (quyết định), discussion (sự thảo luận) 
• -ment: development (sự phát triển), agreement (sự đồng ý), achievement (thành tựu) 
• -ness: happiness (hạnh phúc), kindness (lòng tốt), darkness (bóng tối) 
• -ity: activity (hoạt động), possibility (khả năng), equality (sự bình đẳng) 
• -er / -or: teacher (giáo viên), actor (diễn viên), inventor (nhà phát minh) 
• -ist: artist (nghệ sĩ), scientist (nhà khoa học), pianist (nghệ sĩ piano) 
• -ance / -ence: performance (màn trình diễn), existence (sự tồn tại), difference (sự khác biệt) 
• -ship: friendship (tình bạn), leadership (khả năng lãnh đạo), membership (tư cách thành viên) 
• -hood: childhood (thời thơ ấu), adulthood (tuổi trưởng thành), brotherhood (tình huynh đệ) 
• -dom: freedom (sự tự do), wisdom (trí tuệ), kingdom (vương quốc) 
 
TÍNH TỪ 
Đứng sau to be và các động từ nối như 
become, feel, seem, look … 
He looks tired. 
Vị trí 
Đứng trước danh từ 
He’s a friendly person. 
 
Một số hậu tố thường gặp của tính từ: -ful, -less, -ous, -able/-ible, -al, -ive, -ic, -y, -ish, -ed, -ing  
 
 
 
 
Trang 1 
 
Cô Phương chúc các em lớp VIP 90: ÔN LÀ TRÚNG TỦ - THI LÀ TRÚNG TUYỂN 
 
 
Vì quyền lợi chính đáng của chính các em. Không chia sẻ tài liệu ĐỘC QUYỀN này cho người khác! 
Đăng Ký Khóa Học Online Tại Fanpage: Tài Liệu Khóa Học UniMap
 


Shared By Fanpage: Tài Liệu Khóa Học UniMap
TUẦN SỐ 01 
 
VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
Ví dụ:  
• -ful: helpful (hữu ích), beautiful (đẹp), careful (cẩn thận) 
• -less: hopeless (vô vọng), careless (bất cẩn), useless (vô dụng) 
• -ous: dangerous (nguy hiểm), famous (nổi tiếng), nervous (lo lắng) 
• -able / -ible: eatable (có thể ăn được), possible (có thể), visible (có thể nhìn thấy) 
• -al: natural (tự nhiên), personal (cá nhân), cultural (thuộc văn hoá) 
• -ive: active (năng động), creative (sáng tạo), effective (hiệu quả) 
• -ic: economic (thuộc kinh tế), historic (mang tính lịch sử), poetic (thuộc thơ ca) 
• -y: rainy (có mưa), noisy (ồn ào), lucky (may mắn) 
• -ish: childish (trẻ con), selfish (ích kỷ), foolish (ngu ngốc) 
• -ed: interested (cảm thấy hứng thú), bored (chán), tired (mệt) 
• -ing: interesting (thú vị), boring (nhàm chán), tiring (gây mệt) 
 
* Phân biệt tính từ đuôi -ing và tính từ đuôi -ed 
Tính từ đuôi -ing 
Tính từ đuôi -ed 
Dùng để diễn tả bản chất, đặc điểm, tính cách của 
đối tượng, có tính chất như thế nào (gây ra điều gì) 
Ví dụ: 
This book is boring. 
He is such a boring man. 
Dùng để diễn tả trạng thái, cảm xúc của đối 
tượng, bị làm cho như thế nào (bị động) 
Ví dụ: 
I felt bored. 
 
 
ĐỘNG TỪ 
Đứng sau danh từ hoặc đại từ làm chủ ngữ  
The manager went out two hours ago. 
Sau V khuyết thiếu (modal verbs): may, 
might, must, should,... 
You should drink more water. 
Vị trí 
Dùng to + V (nguyên mẫu) để chỉ mục đích He came early to have a good seat.  
Theo sau các động từ trong các cấu trúc cố 
định: Động từ + V nguyên mẫu có to 
He decided to accept the offer. 
Theo sau giới từ trong các cấu trúc cố định: 
so as to/in order to  
He worked overtime in order to earn some 
more money.  
 
Một số hậu tố thường gặp của động từ: -ize/-ise, -ify, -ate, -en 
 
Ví dụ:  
• -ize / -ise: organize (tổ chức), realize (nhận ra), modernize (hiện đại hoá) 
• -ify: simplify (đơn giản hoá), identify (xác định), classify (phân loại) 
• -ate: activate (kích hoạt), motivate (tạo động lực), communicate (giao tiếp) 
• -en: strengthen (làm mạnh hơn), widen (làm rộng ra), shorten (làm ngắn lại) 
 
 
 
 
Trang 2 
 
Cô Phương chúc các em lớp VIP 90: ÔN LÀ TRÚNG TỦ - THI LÀ TRÚNG TUYỂN

Shared By Fanpage: Tài Liệu Khóa Học UniMap
TUẦN SỐ 01 
 
VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
TRẠNG TỪ 
Đứng sau hoặc trước động từ thường 
He read the document very carefully. 
He carefully wrapped the present.  
Vị trí 
Đứng trước tính từ hoặc quá khứ phân từ  He was seriously injured. 
Đứng ở đầu câu, bổ nghĩa cho cả câu 
Unfortunately, he failed to meet the deadline.  
 
Hậu tố thường gặp nhất của trạng từ là -ly: deeply 
 
B. TRẬT TỰ TỪ 
CỤM 3 TỪ (3-word phrases) 
Đây là dạng hay ra trong đề thi  
 
1️. Adv + Adj + N 
Cụm từ 
Nghĩa 
environmentally friendly products 
sản phẩm thân thiện với môi trường 
highly skilled workers 
công nhân tay nghề cao 
extremely useful tools 
công cụ hữu ích cực kỳ cao 
 
2. Adj + N (bổ nghĩa) + N (chính) 
 
Cụm từ 
Nghĩa 
big car park 
bãi đỗ xe lớn 
modern office building 
toà nhà văn phòng hiện đại 
international trade agreement 
hiệp định thương mại quốc tế 
Lưu ý luôn luôn dịch từ dưới dịch lên  
 Nếu đáp án có chứa danh từ số nhiều thì danh từ số nhiều luôn đứng cuối cùng:  
➢ international trade agreements 
 → agreements (N số nhiều) đứng cuối 
 = các hiệp định thương mại quốc tế 
 
➢ effective communication strategies 
 → strategies (N số nhiều) đứng cuối 
 = các chiến lược giao tiếp hiệu quả 
 
 
 
 
 
Trang 3 
 
Cô Phương chúc các em lớp VIP 90: ÔN LÀ TRÚNG TỦ - THI LÀ TRÚNG TUYỂN 
 
 
Vì quyền lợi chính đáng của chính các em. Không chia sẻ tài liệu ĐỘC QUYỀN này cho người khác!

Shared By Fanpage: Tài Liệu Khóa Học UniMap
TUẦN SỐ 01 
 
VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
3. Adj + Adj + N 
Khi có 2 tính từ + 1️ danh từ, thứ tự tính từ theo 2 kiểu quy tắc: 
a. 2 Tính từ nằm trong các dạng khác nhau trong cấu trúc OSASCOMP 
Thứ tự 
Loại 
Opinion 
ý kiến (beautiful, nice) 
Size 
kích thước (big, small) 
Age 
tuổi (old, new) 
Shape 
hình dạng (round) 
Colour 
màu (red) 
Origin 
nguồn gốc (Vietnamese) 
Material 
chất liệu (wooden) 
Purpose 
mục đích (sleeping bag) 
Ví dụ: 
Cụm từ 
Phân tích 
big red car 
Size + Colour + N 
beautiful old house 
Opinion + Age + N 
small round table 
Size + Shape + N 
essential social skills 
Opinion + Purpose + N 
b. 
Nếu không nằm trong cấu trúc Opsascomp thì có thể theo cấu trúc: Descriptive Adj (mô tả 
quan điểm, tính chất, mức độ, kích thước) + Classifying Adj (tính từ phân loại để ghép với 
danh từ chính thành một cụm) + Noun 
- Classifying Adj luôn đứng gần danh từ hơn 
Ví dụ:  
 important social skills: các kỹ năng xã hội quan trọng  
 advanced medical equipment: thiết bị y tế cao cấp 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Trang 4 
 
Cô Phương chúc các em lớp VIP 90: ÔN LÀ TRÚNG TỦ - THI LÀ TRÚNG TUYỂN 
 
 
Vì quyền lợi chính đáng của chính các em. Không chia sẻ tài liệu ĐỘC QUYỀN này cho người khác! 
Đăng Ký Khóa Học Online Tại Fanpage: Tài Liệu Khóa Học UniMap
 


Shared By Fanpage: Tài Liệu Khóa Học UniMap
TUẦN SỐ 01 
 
VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
4. N + N + N (Ba danh từ đi liền nhau) 
Trong đó: 
Danh từ cuối cùng = danh từ chính (head noun) 
 Hai danh từ đứng trước = bổ nghĩa (như tính từ) 
Lưu ý: Khi dịch, ta cần dịch từ cuối lên trước. 
Ví dụ:  
• customer service department → department (chính) = phòng dịch vụ khách hàng 
• company training program → program (chính) = chương trình đào tạo của công ty 
• student loan application → application (chính) = đơn xin vay vốn sinh viên 
• health insurance policy → policy (chính) = chính sách bảo hiểm y tế 
• data analysis techniques → techniques (N số nhiều đứng cuối) = các kỹ thuật phân tích dữ liệu 
• sales performance report → report (chính) = báo cáo hiệu suất bán hàng 
• market news analysis → analysis (chính) = phân tích tin tức thị trường 
 
PRACTICE 
Read the following texts and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct option that best fits

[V90.01.04] TINH HOA ĐỌC HIỂU (BUỔI 1) 
 
 
Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương  
LEVEL 1 
 
 
Text 1 
Blended learning combines online study with face-to-face lessons in the classroom. This method 
allows students to learn in different ways and at a flexible pace. Online materials such as videos, quizzes, 
and digital tasks help learners review lessons at home. In class, teachers can focus more on discussion, 
practice, and answering questions. Blended learning also helps students become more independent and 
responsible for their own progress. However, it requires good planning and basic technology skills from 
both teachers and learners. Not all students have equal access to devices or the internet, which can trigger 
difficulties. Despite these challenges, blended learning offers a balanced approach that uses the strengths 
of both traditional teaching and online learning to support better understanding and engagement. 
 
Question 1. Which of the following is NOT mentioned as an online material that helps learners review 
lessons at home?  
A. Digital tasks  
B. Online textbooks  
C. Videos  
D. Quizzes 
Question 2. According to the text, blended learning _______.  
A. reduces the role of classroom teachers  
 
 
B. replaces face-to-face lessons completely  
C. limits students’ control over learning  
D. encourages learner autonomy 
Question 3. The word “trigger” in the text is closest in meaning to ______. 
A. cause 
B. shape 
C. solve 
D. change 
Question 4. As stated in the text, blended learning helps ________.  
A. strengthen long-term academic motivation  
B. improve comprehension and student involvement  
C. build stronger teacher–parent cooperation  
D. increase students’ exam preparation skills 
 
 
Trang 30

Text 2 
Vocational education plays a vital role in modern education systems by preparing learners for 
specific careers through practical training. Unlike purely academic pathways, vocational programmes 
focus on developing job-ready skills that can be applied directly in the workplace. Students often receive 
hands-on experience in fields such as engineering, healthcare, hospitality, and information technology, 
which helps bridge the gap between education and employment.  
Another key strength of vocational education is its close connection with industry needs. Courses 
are frequently updated to reflect technological change and labour market demands, ensuring that graduates 
remain relevant and competitive. In addition, vocational education can improve social mobility by 
offering clear career routes for students who prefer applied learning. However, it still faces challenges, 
including outdated stereotypes and unequal funding compared to academic education. When properly 
supported and valued, vocational education contributes to a skilled workforce, reduces unemployment, 
and strengthens economic development. 
Question 5. According to paragraph 1, vocational education is _______.  
A. theory-based and research-focused  
B. mainly academic in its approach  
C. broad and knowledge-centred  
D. practical and career-oriented 
Question 6. The word “bridge” in paragraph 1 can be best replaced by ______.  
A. compare 
B. narrow 
C. prevent 
D. support 
Question 7. The word “relevant” in paragraph 2 is OPPOSITE in meaning to ______. 
A. important 
B. outdated 
C. unpleasant 
D. permanent 
Question 8. According to paragraph 2, vocational education continues to face _______.  
A. limited student interest nationwide  
B. weak links with local industries  
C. social and financial disadvantages  
D. slow growth in course enrolment 
Question 9. If properly supported and valued, vocational education can do all of the following EXCEPT 
_______.  
A. strengthen economic development  
B. improve academic research output  
C. reduce unemployment levels  
D. contribute to a skilled workforce 
 
Text 3 
Higher education plays an essential role in modern societies by shaping how individuals think, 
reason, and respond to complex challenges. Universities do more than transmit specialised knowledge; 
they train students to analyse information critically and apply theory to real-world situations. Through 
sustained academic engagement, learners gradually develop independence and intellectual discipline. 
Exposure to different viewpoints also encourages broader thinking, helping students understand social 
change, ethical responsibility, and global interdependence. As a result, higher education often equips 
graduates with the adaptability needed in fast-changing professional environments.   
Beyond intellectual growth, higher education strongly influences personal development and social 
mobility. Students are required to manage competing demands, cope with pressure, and take responsibility 
for long-term decisions. These experiences foster maturity and resilience over time. At the same time, 
higher education contributes to economic and social progress by preparing a skilled workforce capable of 
innovation. However, rising costs and unequal access remain serious concerns. Addressing these 
challenges is necessary if higher education is to remain inclusive and continue supporting both 
individual advancement and wider societal development. 
 
Trang 31

A. challenges 
B. students 
C. situations 
D. universities

D. enhances long-term career stability

B. weakness 
C. attraction 
D. discovery

_______.  
A. strengthens national cultural identity  
 
B. expands access to lifelong learning

LEVEL 3 
D. supports economic and social advancement 
Question 14. Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence in paragraph 2? 
Addressing these challenges is necessary if higher education is to remain inclusive and continue 
supporting both individual advancement and wider societal development. 
A. Only by remaining inclusive and promoting personal and social progress can higher education 
effectively deal with its existing challenges.  
B. The ability of higher education to respond to current challenges depends on its role in personal growth 
and broader social progress.  
C. For higher education to sustain inclusiveness and contribute to individual and societal progress, 
existing challenges must be effectively tackled.  
D. Continued personal advancement and social development enable higher education to remain inclusive 
and confront its challenges. 
 
Text 4 
In an era marked by accelerated technological disruption and volatile labour markets, adult learners 
have emerged as a defining feature of contemporary education. No longer limited to early-life study, 
learning has become a recurring necessity as individuals confront career displacement, evolving 
professional standards, and shifting social expectations. Adult education, therefore, operates within a 
context of continuous adaptation, where knowledge acquisition is closely tied to survival, relevance, and 
long-term employability rather than formal certification alone.  
Adult learners typically engage with education in a purposeful and self-directed manner. Drawing 
on accumulated professional and personal experience, they are often able to evaluate ideas critically and 
apply abstract concepts to concrete situations. However, this engagement is shaped by structural 
constraints. Balancing academic demands alongside employment, family obligations, and financial 
pressure requires sustained resilience and advanced self-management. These pressures can intensify 
cognitive load, yet they also cultivate persistence, strategic thinking, and reflective learning habits that 
younger learners may lack.  
At a societal level, adult learners play a crucial role in economic resilience and social 
cohesion. By reskilling and upskilling, they enable industries to remain competitive in the face of 
innovation-driven change. Furthermore, adult education supports social mobility by offering pathways 
for reintegration and advancement. When institutions respond flexibly to adult learners’ needs, education 
becomes not merely corrective but transformative for both individuals and society.

Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
TÀI LIỆU LIVESTREAM ĐỘC QUYỀN 
VIP 90 (2026): TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM BỘ CÂU HỎI  
DỄ TRÚNG TỦ & LUYỆN BỘ ĐỀ TINH HOA  
[V90.01.04B] THI ONLINE: TINH HOA ĐỌC HIỂU  
 
(BUỔI 1) – ĐỀ VẬN DỤNG 
Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương  
 
 
LEVEL 1 
Text 1 
A gap year is a period of time, usually one year, that students take off before starting university. 
During this time, they may travel, work part-time, volunteer, or learn new skills. The purpose of a gap 
year is not to waste time, but to gain real-life experience and think carefully about future plans. For some 
students, it is a good choice because it helps them become more independent and confident. They can 
discover their interests and choose a suitable major later. However, taking a gap year also requires careful 
planning. Without clear goals, students may lose motivation or delay their studies. Therefore, a gap year 
can only be a valuable decision if it is used wisely and responsibly.  
Question 1. Which of the following is NOT mentioned in the paragraph as one of the things students may 
do during a gap year? 
A. Volunteer or learn new skills 
B. Travel to different places 
C. Work part-time 
 
D. Start their university studies  
Question 2. The word “gain” in the text is CLOSEST in meaning to ______. 
A. obtain 
B. reduce 
C. trade 
D. consider  
Question 3. The word “it” in the text refers to ______.  
A. careful planning 
B. motivation  
C. a gap year                D. a valuable decision 
 
Text 2 
Online learning allows students to study anytime and anywhere as long as they have an internet 
connection. It is flexible and convenient, especially for those who live far from school or have busy 
schedules. Students can also replay recorded lessons to understand difficult parts better. However, online 
classes sometimes make learners feel isolated because they do not have much face-to-face interaction with 
teachers and classmates. 
In contrast, traditional learning takes place in a classroom, where students can communicate directly 
with others. This helps them develop social skills and stay more focused during lessons. Teachers can 
also give immediate feedback and support. On the other hand, traditional learning requires students to 
follow a fixed timetable and travel to school every day. 
Question 4. The word “It” in the text refers to _______. 
A. school  
B. connection 
C. online learning  
D. internet  
Question 5. According to the passage, why do some students feel isolated in online classes? 
A. Because there is little direct interaction 
B. Because they must travel long distances 
C. Because teachers refuse to give feedback 
D. Because lessons are too difficult to follow 
Question 6. The word “immediate” in the text is OPPOSITE in meaning to _______. 
A. beautiful 
B. positive 
C. delayed 
D. instant 
 
Trang 34

VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
LEVEL 2 
Text 3 
In many Asian countries, such as Japan, South Korea, and China, exams play an important role in 
the education system. Students often take major national exams to enter high school or university. These 
exams are usually written and focus on testing knowledge in subjects like math, science, and languages. 
The results can strongly affect students’ academic and career opportunities. Because of this system, 
students often spend a lot of time preparing for tests and attending extra classes. Exams are seen as a fair 
and clear way to measure students’ performance. 
In contrast, several European countries, such as Finland, Germany, and the Netherlands, use more 
assignments and projects in schools. Students are asked to write essays, complete research tasks, and give 
presentations. Teachers assess their progress through continuous evaluation instead of relying only on 
final exams. This approach encourages critical thinking, creativity, and independent learning. 
Question 7. The word “affect” in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to ______.  
A. improve 
B. buy 
C. function 
D. impact 
Question 8. According to the text, exams in many Asian countries _______. 
A. depend mostly on projects and essays 
B. mainly focus on creativity and presentations 
C. are used as a fair way to assess students  
D. reduce pressure on academic performance 
Question 9. Which of the following is NOT mentioned in paragraph 2 as one of the tasks students are 
required to do? 
A. Take national exams 
 
B. Write essays 
C. Complete research assignments 
D. Give presentations 
Question 10. According to paragraph 2, assessment through assignments and projects encourages all of 
the following, EXCEPT _______. 
A. Critical thinking 
 
B. High scores  
C. Creativity 
 
D. Independent learning 
 
Text 4 
Vocational training focuses on practical skills that prepare students for specific jobs. It is 
common in countries like Germany and Australia, where technical schools cooperate closely with 
businesses. Students learn through hands-on practice in areas such as mechanics, cooking, nursing, or 
information technology. This type of education often takes less time than a university degree and allows 
learners to enter the workforce earlier. Many employers value vocational qualifications because graduates 
already have real-world experience and job-related skills. 
On the other hand, university education provides broader academic knowledge and theoretical 
foundations. Students study subjects in greater depth and develop skills such as research, analysis, and 
critical thinking. A university degree can open doors to professional careers in fields like law, medicine, 
or engineering. Although it usually requires more years of study and higher costs, it may offer long-term 
career flexibility and opportunities for advancement in a competitive job market. 
Question 11. Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence in paragraph 1? 
Vocational training focuses on practical skills that prepare students for specific jobs. 
A. Vocational education teaches practical skills without linking to jobs. 
B. Vocational education teaches practical skills for particular careers.  
C. Vocational education prepares students for jobs through theory only. 
D. Vocational education focuses on general skills for any career. 
 
Trang 35

A. compare 
B. approach 
C. leave 
D. involve 
Question 13. Why do many employers prefer vocational qualifications? 
A. Because graduates are trained mainly in academic research. 
B. Because graduates have deeper theoretical knowledge in many fields. 
C. Because graduates spend more years studying at university. 
D. Because graduates already have job-related skills and experience.

A. provides mainly practical skills for specific jobs 
B. provides broader knowledge and theoretical foundations  
C. allows students to enter the workforce earlier 
D. requires less time and lower costs than vocational study

A. Mechanic 
B. Lawyer 
C. Doctor 
D. Engineer 
LEVEL 3 
Text 5 
Critical thinking refers to the ability to analyze information objectively, question assumptions, and 
evaluate different perspectives before forming a conclusion. It goes beyond memorizing facts or repeating 
textbook knowledge. Instead, students are encouraged to interpret evidence, identify bias, and construct 
logical arguments. In an era of information overload and digital media, critical thinking has become an 
essential competency that enables young people to make informed decisions and navigate complex social 
realities. 
A notable example can be found in Finland’s education model. Finnish schools integrate project-
based learning and open discussions into daily lessons. Students are encouraged to debate social issues, 
analyze real-world problems, and reflect on their learning process. Such an approach illustrates how 
critical thinking can be systematically nurtured rather than treated as an abstract ideal. 
However, in many education systems, critical thinking remains limited in practice. A large number 
of schools still prioritize standardized testing and rigid curricula. As a result, students are often trained to 
reproduce predefined answers rather than challenge ideas or explore alternative viewpoints. Classroom 
discussions may be constrained, and creativity can be overshadowed by the pressure to achieve high exam 
scores. This environment unintentionally discourages intellectual curiosity and independent reasoning. 
Question 16. According to the passage, critical thinking ________. 
A. depends completely on standardized testing systems 
B. focuses mainly on memorizing textbook knowledge 
C. requires students to correctly repeat predefined answers 
D. involves analyzing information and questioning assumptions  
Question 17. The word “complex” in paragraph 1 is OPPOSITE in meaning to ______. 
A. successful 
B. difficult 
C. complicated 
D. simple 
Question 18. Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence in paragraph 2? 
Finnish schools integrate project-based learning and open discussions into daily lessons. 
A. Finnish schools integrate daily lessons into projects and open discussions. 
B. Project work and open dialogue are built into everyday teaching in Finnish schools.  
C. Project-based learning in Finnish schools replaces regular daily lessons. 
D. Daily lessons in Finnish schools are separated from projects and discussions.

TÀI LIỆU LIVESTREAM ĐỘC QUYỀN 
VIP 90 (2026): TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM BỘ CÂU HỎI  
DỄ TRÚNG TỦ & LUYỆN BỘ ĐỀ TINH HOA  
[V90.01.04A] THI ONLINE: TINH HOA ĐỌC HIỂU  
 
(BUỔI 1) – ĐỀ NÂNG CAO 
Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương  
 
 
Read the following passage and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct answer to each of 
the questions from 1 to 8. 
In recent years, education has undergone a profound transformation as digital technologies have 
become woven into everyday life. Classrooms are no longer confined to four walls, nor is learning 
restricted to fixed schedules. Within this shifting landscape, blended learning has emerged as a pragmatic 
response to the demands of modern learners. By combining face-to-face instruction with structured online 
components, it seeks to integrate the strengths of traditional pedagogy and digital innovation rather than 
position them in opposition. 
Blended learning operates on the premise that different modes of delivery can complement one 
another. In-person sessions provide immediacy, social interaction, and opportunities for spontaneous 
clarification, while online platforms offer flexibility and personalised pacing. Students can revisit 
recorded lectures, engage with interactive tasks, and access supplementary materials tailored to their level 
of understanding. This dual structure not only accommodates diverse learning preferences but also fosters 
greater autonomy, as learners assume increased responsibility for managing their progress. 
At an institutional level, blended learning encourages more strategic use of classroom time. 
Instead of dedicating entire lessons to information transmission, educators can adopt a flipped approach 
in which foundational content is explored independently before class. Face-to-face meetings then become 
spaces for discussion, problem-solving, and collaborative inquiry. Such reconfiguration can deepen 
conceptual understanding and cultivate higher-order thinking skills, as students apply rather than merely 
absorb knowledge. 
Nevertheless, the effectiveness of blended learning depends on thoughtful design and equitable 
access to technology. Without reliable internet connectivity or digital literacy, the model risks widening 
existing educational disparities. When implemented with clear objectives and adequate support, however, 
blended learning represents not simply a temporary adjustment but a sustainable framework for twenty-
first-century education, capable of balancing structure with flexibility and innovation with human 
connection. 
Question 1. The word “pragmatic” in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to ______. 
A. one-sided 
B. realistic 
C. prosperous 
D. straightforward 
Question 2. Face-to-face classes provide all of the following EXCEPT _______.  
A. social interaction  
 
B. personalised pacing  
C. immediacy 
 
D. opportunities for spontaneous clarification 
Question 3. The word “their” in paragraph 2 refers to ______.  
A. materials 
B. platforms 
C. students 
D. tasks  
Question 4. The word “dual” in paragraph 2 is OPPOSITE in meaning to ______. 
A. rigid 
B. flexible 
C. single 
D. blended 
 
Trang 38

Question 5. Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence in paragraph 3? 
At an institutional level, blended learning encourages more strategic use of classroom time. 
A. From an organisational perspective, classroom time is utilised more deliberately through the adoption 
of hybrid instruction.  
B. Within educational institutions, the deliberate allocation of lesson time promotes the implementation 
of hybrid teaching models.  
C. At the level of institutions, the careful management of instructional time facilitates the expansion of 
blended education.  
D. Across institutions, the strategic structuring of class periods results in the wider integration of mixed-
mode learning. 
Question 6. Which of the following is TRUE according to paragraph 4? 
A. Blended models automatically reduce existing academic inequalities.  
B. Digital instruction replaces the need for human interaction.  
C. Hybrid education can endure when supported by clear planning.  
D. Internet access alone guarantees effective hybrid implementation. 
Question 7. Which paragraph mentions personalised learning? 
A. Paragraph 1 
B. Paragraph 2 
C. Paragraph 3 
D. Paragraph 4 
Question 8. Which paragraph mentions that direct instruction happens outside class?  
A. Paragraph 1 
B. Paragraph 2 
C. Paragraph 3 
D. Paragraph 4 
 
Read the following passage and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct answer to each of 
the questions from 9 to 18. 
In an era defined by perpetual connectivity, the presence of smartphones in schools has become 
both inevitable and contentious. What was once a discreet personal device has evolved into a 
multifunctional tool capable of communication, research, entertainment, and surveillance. As adolescents 
increasingly inhabit digital spaces alongside physical classrooms, educational institutions face a delicate 
dilemma: whether to restrict, regulate, or integrate mobile phones into the learning environment. School 
phone policies have therefore emerged not merely as disciplinary guidelines but as reflections of broader 
debates about attention, autonomy, and the purpose of schooling itself. 
Proponents of stricter regulations argue that smartphones fragment attention and erode academic 
focus. Notifications, social media feeds, and messaging applications compete relentlessly for cognitive 
bandwidth, undermining sustained concentration and deep work. Empirical studies have associated 
excessive phone use with diminished retention and heightened anxiety, suggesting that unrestricted access 
may compromise both intellectual and emotional well-being. [I] In response, some schools have instituted 
comprehensive bans during instructional hours, while others require devices to be stored in lockers or 
signal-blocking pouches. These measures aim to cultivate an environment conducive to reflection 
and disciplined inquiry rather than perpetual distraction. 
Yet an outright prohibition can appear incongruous in a society where digital literacy is 
indispensable. Smartphones can function as pedagogical assets when harnessed judiciously: they facilitate 
instant research, collaborative platforms, language-learning applications, and multimedia production. 
Moreover, for many students, mobile devices provide a sense of security and connection to family, 
particularly during emergencies. Critics of rigid bans contend that education should model responsible 
use rather than enforce abstinence, equipping learners with the discernment necessary to navigate digital 
ecosystems ethically and productively. [II]

VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
Consequently, a growing number of institutions are adopting nuanced frameworks that balance 
restriction with instruction. These policies delineate clear boundaries during lessons while permitting 
supervised academic use, thereby integrating technology without allowing it to dominate. [III] Ultimately, 
school phone policies encapsulate a broader pedagogical question: whether schools should insulate 
students from the digital world or prepare them to engage with it thoughtfully. The answer lies less in 
absolutism than in deliberate, context-sensitive governance. [IV] 
Question 9. Which of the following best summarises paragraph 1? 
A. School phone policies reflect debates over regulating the inevitable smartphone presence in education.  
B. An increasing number of schools are divided over banning smartphones that disrupt attention in 
classrooms.  
C. Smartphone growth forces institutions to discipline distracted adolescents more strictly.  
D. Educational institutions confront integration challenges as smartphones increasingly become 
unnecessary tools. 
Question 10. As stated in paragraph 1, smartphones _______ 
A. have become prohibited within modern educational institutions.  
B. have replaced traditional classroom tools in contemporary schools.  
C. have shifted attention away from core academic objectives.  
D. have undergone a significant transformation in function and scope. 
Question 11. According to paragraph 2, smartphones, when used excessively or without limits, can 
_______.  
A. foster long-term dependency and weaken students’ interpersonal relationships.   
B. increase exposure to misinformation and reduce academic credibility.   
C. undermine both learning quality and students’ mental well-being.   
D. contribute to widespread disciplinary breakdown within school environments. 
Question 12. Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence in paragraph 2? 
These measures aim to cultivate an environment conducive to reflection and disciplined inquiry 
rather than perpetual distraction. 
A. A culture of continuous interruption is expected to foster attentive and methodical academic 
engagement.  
B. A setting defined by constant diversion is intended to produce sustained and systematic intellectual 
effort.  
C. A climate shaped by ongoing distraction is designed to generate reflective and disciplined study 
practices. 
D. An atmosphere supportive of careful thought and structured investigation is what these policies seek 
to establish. 
Question 13. The word “incongruous” in paragraph 3 mostly means ______. 
A. inappropriate 
B. illicit 
C. disposable 
D. distrustful 
Question 14. The word “they” in paragraph 3 refers to ______. 
A. emergencies 
B. assets 
C. students 
D. smartphones

A. Balanced regulation may better prepare students for responsible digital engagement.  
B. Strict prohibitions tend to produce stronger academic performance in schools. 
C. Integrating smartphones inevitably strengthens students’ independent learning habits.  
D. Digital literacy develops naturally when devices remain constantly accessible. 
Question 17. Where in the passage does the following sentence best fit? 
The objective is not to demonise devices but to recalibrate their role within the educational sphere. 
A. [IV] 
B. [III] 
C. [I] 
 D. [II] 
Question 18. Which of the following best summarises the passage? 
A. Schools increasingly impose strict smartphone prohibitions because research shows declining 
concentration and anxiety inevitably undermine academic performance.  
B. Educational institutions redesign learning environments primarily to integrate smartphones, since their 
multifunctional role now defines contemporary schooling.  
C. Policymakers conclude that technological integration outweighs traditional classroom discipline as 
smartphones reshape students’ academic and social development. 
D. Schools debate restricting or guiding smartphone use, balancing distraction concerns with digital 
literacy through context-sensitive regulation.

DANH ĐỘNG TỪ VÀ ĐỘNG TỪ NGUYÊN MẪU 
Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương  
 
 
1. VERB + V-ING 
 
Verb 
Nghĩa 
Ví dụ 
acknowledge 
công nhận 
He acknowledged breaking the rules.  
(Anh ấy thừa nhận đã vi phạm.) 
admit 
thừa nhận 
She admitted cheating/ to cheating. (Cô ấy thừa nhận gian lận.) 
advise 
khuyên 
I advise taking a taxi. (Tôi khuyên nên đi taxi.) 
anticipate 
kỳ vọng 
We anticipate receiving feedback soon.  
(Chúng tôi kỳ vọng sẽ nhận phản hồi.) 
avoid 
tránh 
Avoid making mistakes. (Tránh mắc lỗi.) 
allow 
cho phép 
We do not allow eating in the classrooms.  
(Chúng tôi không cho phép ăn trong phòng học.) 
consider 
cân nhắc 
I considered moving abroad. (Tôi cân nhắc ra nước ngoài.) 
delay 
trì hoãn 
They delayed signing the contract. (Họ trì hoãn ký hợp đồng.) 
deny 
phủ nhận 
She denied stealing money. (Cô ấy phủ nhận ăn cắp.) 
detest 
ghét 
I detest waiting. (Tôi ghét chờ đợi.) 
dislike 
không thích 
I dislike working late. (Tôi không thích làm khuya.) 
encourage 
khuyến khích 
We encourage speaking your mind in these sessions. (Chúng tôi 
khuyến khích bạn bày tỏ ý kiến của mình trong các buổi họp này.) 
enjoy 
thích 
I enjoy swimming. (Tôi thích bơi.) 
fancy 
thích 
I fancy going out. (Tôi muốn ra ngoài.) 
finish 
hoàn thành 
She finished writing the report. (Cô ấy viết xong báo cáo.) 
imagine 
tưởng tượng 
Imagine living there. (Hãy tưởng tượng sống ở đó.)

Nghĩa 
Ví dụ

tendency to 
xu hướng

(Một số người có xu hướng phản ứng quá mức với vấn đề nhỏ.)

tiềm năng

6. MỘT SỐ ĐỘNG TỪ CÓ CẢ 2 DẠNG V-ING VÀ TO V 
 
Verb 
Cấu trúc  
Ví dụ  
remember + to V (nhớ phải làm gì)

(Hãy nhớ nộp báo cáo.) 
remember 
remember + V-ing (nhớ đã làm gì)

(Tôi nhớ đã nộp báo cáo rồi.) 
forget + to V (quên phải làm gì)

forget 
forget + V-ing (quên đã làm gì)

(Tôi sẽ không bao giờ quên đã gặp cô ấy.) 
try + to V (cố gắng làm gì)

(Tôi đã cố giải quyết vấn đề.) 
try 
try + V-ing (thử làm gì) 
Try restarting the system.  
(Hãy thử khởi động lại hệ thống.) 
regret + to V (tiếc phải làm gì) 
I regret to inform you…  
(Tôi rất tiếc phải thông báo…) 
regret 
regret + V-ing (tiếc đã làm gì) 
I regret telling him the truth.  
(Tôi hối hận vì đã nói sự thật.) 
stop + to V (dừng lại để làm gì) 
He stopped to drink water.  
(Anh ấy dừng lại để uống nước.) 
stop 
stop + V-ing (dừng làm việc gì) 
He stopped smoking.  
(Anh ấy đã ngừng hút thuốc.) 
mean + to V (có ý định làm gì) 
I didn’t mean to hurt you.  
(Tôi không có ý làm bạn tổn thương.) 
mean 
mean + V-ing (đồng nghĩa với việc gì) This means working harder.  
(Điều này có nghĩa là làm việc chăm chỉ hơn.) 
need + to V (cần làm gì) 
I need to finish this task.  
(Tôi cần hoàn thành nhiệm vụ này.) 
need 
need + V-ing (cần được làm gì) 
The room needs cleaning.  
(Căn phòng cần được dọn.) 
 
 
 
Trang 47

Shared By Fanpage: Tài Liệu Khóa Học UniMap
TUẦN SỐ 01 
 
VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
TÀI LIỆU LIVESTREAM ĐỘC QUYỀN 
VIP 90 (2026): TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM BỘ CÂU HỎI  
DỄ TRÚNG TỦ & LUYỆN BỘ ĐỀ TINH HOA  
[V90.01.06] TINH HOA ĐỌC HIỂU (BUỔI 2) 
 
 
Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương  
LEVEL 1 
 
 
Text 1 
Lifelong learning means continuing to learn at every stage of life, not only when we are young. In 
today’s fast-changing world, knowledge and skills can quickly become obsolete. New technology, new 
jobs, and new ways of working require people to keep improving themselves. By learning throughout life, 
people can stay confident and ready for new challenges. Lifelong learning does not always happen in a 
classroom. It can take place through online courses, reading books, watching educational videos, or 
learning from daily experiences. Many adults choose to study new languages, develop computer skills, or 
improve communication abilities. These efforts can help them find better jobs or perform more effectively 
at work. In addition, lifelong learning supports personal growth. It allows people to discover new interests, 
meet different kinds of people, and better understand the world around them. By staying curious and open 
to new ideas, individuals can build a more meaningful and successful life. 
 
Question 1. The word “obsolete” in the text is closest in meaning to ______. 
A. impactful 
 
B. outdated 
 
C. simple 
 
D. constant  
Question 2. Lifelong learning can take place through all of the following EXCEPT _______. 
A. memorising fashion trends  
B. watching educational videos  
C. attending online courses   
D. reading books  
Question 3. The word “them” in the text refers to ______. 
A. efforts 
 
B. jobs 
 
C. skills 
 
D. adults  
 
 
 
Trang 55

TUẦN SỐ 01 
 
VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
LEVEL 2 
 
Text 2 
Night school has become an important educational option for people who cannot attend classes 
during the day. Many adults choose night courses because they work full-time or have family 
responsibilities that prevent them from studying in traditional settings. By offering flexible schedules, 
night schools make education more accessible to a wider range of learners. Students who attend night 
school are often highly motivated. After a long day at work, they still make the effort to improve their 
qualifications and expand their knowledge. Courses usually focus on practical skills, such as language 
learning, computer training, or professional certification. This practical approach allows learners to apply 
what they study directly to their jobs.   
However, attending night school can be challenging. Balancing work, study, and personal life 
requires strong time-management skills and determination. Fatigue may also affect concentration and 
performance in class. Despite these difficulties, night school provides valuable opportunities for personal 
and professional development, proving that education does not have to be limited to a specific age or stage 
in life. 
 
Question 4. Why do night schools hold appeal for many adults? 
A. Because they provide free meals and transport services.  
B. Because they guarantee immediate promotion at work.  
C. Because they offer flexible learning opportunities.  
D. Because they reduce the overall length of study programs. 
Question 5. The word “they” in paragraph 1 refers to ______. 
A. night schools 
 
B. traditional settings 
 
C. flexible schedules 
 
D. students  
Question 6. According to paragraph 1, night school courses _______ 
A. are mainly intended for daytime university students.  
B. are limited to theoretical knowledge only.  
C. are organised for short-term social activities.  
D. are designed to be useful in real life. 
Question 7. The word “Fatigue” in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to ______. 
A. tiredness 
 
B. effort 
 
C. support 
 
D. pleasure  
Question 8. Night school proves that _______.  
A. education depends on early childhood training  
B. education is possible at any age  
C. education requires daily attendance  
D. education only benefits recent school graduates 
 
 
 
Trang 56 
 
Cô Phương chúc các em lớp VIP 90: ÔN LÀ TRÚNG TỦ - THI LÀ TRÚNG TUYỂN

TUẦN SỐ 01 
 
VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
Text 3 
Private education has long been regarded as an alternative to public schooling, often associated 
with smaller class sizes, specialised programmes, and enhanced academic support. Many parents choose 
private institutions because they believe these schools can offer a more structured environment and closer 
supervision. With greater financial resources, private schools may invest in modern facilities, advanced 
technology, and diverse extracurricular activities that enrich students’ overall development.   
Another perceived advantage of private education lies in its flexibility. Independent schools are 
sometimes able to design their own curricula, allowing them to introduce innovative teaching methods or 
focus strongly on particular subjects such as languages, arts, or science. This autonomy can create a 
distinctive academic culture that appeals to families seeking specific educational values or standards.  
However, private education also raises important questions about equality and access. Tuition fees 
can be prohibitively expensive, limiting opportunities to those from more affluent backgrounds. Critics 
argue that this may widen social gaps and reinforce privilege. While private schools may provide certain 
benefits, ensuring quality education for all students remains a broader societal responsibility. 
 
Question 9. Which of the following is NOT mentioned as a feature of private education?  
A. Free tuition for all students  
B. Enhanced academic support  
C. Smaller class sizes  
 
D. Specialised programmes 
Question 10. The word “enrich” in paragraph 1 is OPPOSITE in meaning to _______. 
A. simplify 
B. worsen 
C. increase 
D. value  
Question 11. According to paragraph 2, independent schools ______.  
A. follow a strictly standardised national curriculum  
B. depend entirely on government-designed programmes  
C. have greater flexibility in how they organise teaching  
D. are required to avoid focusing on specific subjects 
Question 12. The word “autonomy” in paragraph 2 can be best replaced by ______. 
A. freedom 
B. attraction 
C. expense 
D. standard 
Question 13. As stated in paragraph 3, private education ______.  
A. may not be equally accessible to everyone  
B. is freely available to students from all backgrounds  
C. ensures identical opportunities for every family  
D. guarantees equal admission regardless of income 
 
 
 
Trang 57

TUẦN SỐ 01 
 
VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
LEVEL 3 
 
Text 4 
In contemporary education, where curricula are increasingly standardised and assessment-driven, 
field trips remain a rare opportunity to extend learning beyond the confines of the classroom. They provide 
experiential encounters that cannot be fully replicated through textbooks or digital simulations. By 
situating knowledge within authentic environments, field trips transform abstract concepts into 
tangible realities.   
Whether visiting a historical monument, a scientific research centre, or a natural reserve, students 
are immersed in contexts that stimulate curiosity and critical inquiry. Observing artefacts firsthand or 
witnessing ecological systems in operation fosters deeper cognitive engagement. Such excursions often 
cultivate interdisciplinary thinking, as learners are encouraged to connect theoretical frameworks with 
lived experience. Moreover, field trips can enhance social cohesion, requiring collaboration, adaptability, 
and responsible conduct in unfamiliar settings.  
However, organising these activities entails logistical complexities and financial considerations, 
which may deter some institutions. Concerns regarding safety, accessibility, and curriculum alignment must 
be carefully addressed. Despite these challenges, well-structured field trips can be profoundly transformative. 
They not only consolidate academic understanding but also nurture independence, resilience, and cultural 
awareness—qualities that are indispensable in an increasingly interconnected world. 
Question 14. Field trips offer unique learning experiences that _______.  
A. replace traditional lessons in most academic subjects  
B. depend mainly on textbooks and online materials  
C. go beyond what students can gain from books or digital tools  
D. focus only on recreational activities outside school 
Question 15. Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence in paragraph 1? 
By situating knowledge within authentic environments, field trips transform abstract concepts into 
tangible realities.   
A. By placing ideas in real-world settings, field trips turn theoretical notions into concrete understanding.  
B. By turning theoretical notions into concrete understanding, field trips place ideas in real-world settings.  
C. By presenting concrete understanding first, field trips create real-world settings from theoretical 
notions.  
D. By creating real-world settings out of concrete understanding, field trips develop theoretical notions 
from it. 
Question 16. Which of the following best summarises paragraph 2? 
A. Field trips link theory with experience and strengthen collaborative skills.  
B. Field trips increase curiosity and provide exposure to public institutions.   
C. Field trips develop observation skills and encourage social responsibility.   
D. Field trips promote teamwork and improve behaviour in unfamiliar places. 
Question 17. The word “entails” in paragraph 3 mostly means ______. 
A. employs 
B. prevents 
C. involves 
D. accesses  
Question 18. Which of the following can be inferred from the last paragraph? 
A. Field trips automatically guarantee students greater academic examination results.  
B. Institutions prioritise safety concerns over students’ personal development.  
C. Financial barriers permanently prevent schools from organising field experiences. 
D. Schools may avoid trips if the costs and organisation seem too demanding. 
 
Trang 58

TUẦN SỐ 01 
 
VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
TÀI LIỆU LIVESTREAM ĐỘC QUYỀN 
VIP 90 (2026): TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM BỘ CÂU HỎI  
DỄ TRÚNG TỦ & LUYỆN BỘ ĐỀ TINH HOA  
[V90.01.06B] THI ONLINE: TINH HOA ĐỌC HIỂU  
 
(BUỔI 2) – ĐỀ VẬN DỤNG 
Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương  
 
 
LEVEL 1 
Text 1 
Preparing students for the job market is one of the main goals of modern education. Schools should 
not only provide academic knowledge but also help students develop practical skills. For example, 
students need communication skills, teamwork, and problem-solving abilities to succeed in the workplace. 
In addition, basic digital skills are important because many jobs today require the use of technology. 
Schools can organize career guidance programs, internships, and workshops to give students real 
experience. Learning how to write a CV and perform well in a job interview is also useful. When students 
understand what employers expect, they can prepare themselves better. As a result, they will feel more 
confident and ready to enter the job market after graduation. 
 
Question 1. The word “themselves” in the text refers to ______. 
A. students  
B. employers  
C. workshops  
D. internships 
Question 2. Which of the following is NOT mentioned in the text as one of the skills that helps students 
succeed? 
A. Teamwork 
 
B. Communication 
 
C. Languages  
 
D. Problem-solving 
Question 3. The word “important” in the text is CLOSEST in meaning to ______. 
A. plentiful 
B. expensive 
C. valuable 
D. essential  
 
Text 2 
School uniforms remain a common requirement in many education systems. Supporters argue that 
uniforms promote equality by reducing visible differences in wealth and fashion. When students wear 
the same clothes, they may feel a stronger sense of belonging and school identity. Uniforms can also 
create a more disciplined learning environment and reduce distractions related to appearance. In addition, 
parents often find them practical and cost-effective in the long term. 
However, some people believe that school uniforms are outdated. They argue that clothing is a form 
of self-expression, especially for teenagers who are developing their identities. Strict dress codes may 
limit creativity and individuality. Moreover, uniforms do not automatically solve deeper issues such as 
bullying or academic pressure. All in all, whether uniforms are necessary or outdated depends on how 
schools balance discipline with personal freedom. 
 
Question 4. According to supporters, school uniforms ________ 
A. replace the need for school discipline 
B. prevent all forms of bullying in schools 
C. promote equality and a sense of belonging  
D. completely remove academic pressure 
 
Trang 59

TUẦN SỐ 01 
 
VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
Question 5. The word “reducing” in the text is OPPOSITE in meaning to _______. 
A. declining 
B. working 
C. attending 
D. increasing 
Question 6. The word “their” in the text refers to _______. 
A. teenagers  
B. identities 
C. school uniforms  
D. some people  
 
LEVEL 2 
Text 3 
Education is widely considered a fundamental human right. Supporters of free education argue that 
every individual, regardless of economic background, deserves equal access to learning opportunities. 
When governments provide free schooling, they reduce social inequality and give disadvantaged students 
a fair chance to succeed. An educated population also contributes to economic growth, social stability, 
and democratic participation. From this perspective, free education is not simply a benefit but a long-term 
investment in national development. 
On the other hand, some believe that education can be seen as a privilege due to limited public 
resources. Governments must allocate funding carefully, and maintaining high-quality free education 
requires significant financial commitment. In certain contexts, families who can afford tuition may be 
expected to contribute. Nevertheless, many argue that while advanced or specialized programs may 
involve costs, basic education should remain accessible to all as a universal right. 
 
Question 7. The word “fundamental” in the text is closest in meaning to ______. 
A. simple 
B. cheap 
C. advanced 
D. basic 
Question 8. According to the passage, when governments provide free education, they ________. 
A. give disadvantaged students a fair chance 
B. eliminate all economic and political problems 
C. no longer need to manage public resources 
D. require families to pay higher tuition fees 
Question 9. Why do some people believe education should not always be free? 
A. Because free education weakens participation 
B. Because governments have limited resources  
C. Because schooling supports national development 
D. Because disadvantaged students do not value learning 
Question 10. The word “significant” in the text is opposite in meaning to ______.   
A. small 
 B. considerable  
C. enormous  
D. national 
 
 
Trang 60

TUẦN SỐ 01 
 
VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
Text 4 
In reality, most students spend years in school without ever being taught how to learn. They 
memorize formulas, underline key words, and revise the night before exams. They chase grades, not 
understanding. But have you ever asked yourself why you forget most of what you studied just a few 
months later? Many learners are trained to absorb information quickly and reproduce it on paper. 
Once the test is over, the knowledge quietly disappears. Is that really education, or just short-term 
survival? 
Learning how to learn means understanding your own thinking process. It involves questioning 
ideas, connecting concepts, and reflecting on mistakes. Real learning happens when you can explain a 
topic in your own words and apply it in a new situation. It is less about collecting facts and more about 
building insight. So, next time you open a textbook, ask yourself: are you studying for a grade, or are you 
actually learning something meaningful? 
 
Question 11. Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence in paragraph 1? 
Many learners are trained to absorb information quickly and reproduce it on paper. 
A. Pupils are trained to remember ideas without expressing them on paper. 
B. Learners are guided to understand information slowly and analyze it deeply. 
C. Students are expected to question information instead of writing it down. 
D. Many students are taught to take in information fast and write it in exams.  
Question 12. Which of the following is NOT mentioned as a sign of students who do not truly know how 
to learn? 
A. Question ideas 
 
B. Revise the night before exams 
 
C. Underline key words 
 
D. Memorize formulas  
Question 13. According to the passage, what is a consequence of learning in the wrong way? 
A. Learners apply ideas in new situations easily. 
B. Students develop deeper insight into concepts. 
C. Knowledge disappears soon after the test is over.  
D. Understanding becomes stronger over time. 
Question 14. According to the passage, learning how to learn means ________. 
A. understanding your own thinking process 
B. memorizing repeating facts in exams 
C. collecting as much information as possible 
D. studying only to achieve higher grades 
Question 15. The word “collecting” in paragraph 2 can be best replaced by ______. 
A. knowing 
B. catching 
C. gathering 
D. preventing 
 
 
Trang 61

TUẦN SỐ 01 
 
VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
LEVEL 3 
Text 5 
Before the widespread use of the Internet, academic dishonesty was limited by physical 
constraints. Students who wished to cheat often relied on handwritten notes hidden in exam rooms, copied 
homework from classmates, or secretly exchanged answers during tests. Plagiarism required access to 
printed books or libraries, which made it more time-consuming and easier to detect. Although cheating 
certainly existed, it demanded effort and carried a higher risk of being caught. Information was not 
instantly accessible, and academic misconduct was relatively restricted in scale. 
With the arrival of the Internet, the landscape changed dramatically. Students gained immediate 
access to countless online sources, essays, and answer keys. Copying and pasting became effortless, and 
contract cheating services began offering customized assignments for a fee. The digital environment 
blurred the boundaries between research and plagiarism, making misconduct both more 
convenient and more difficult to monitor. Educational institutions have since had to adopt plagiarism-
detection software and stricter policies. 
Now, the rise of artificial intelligence introduces a new ethical dilemma. AI tools can generate 
essays, solve complex problems, and imitate academic writing styles within seconds. While they can 
support learning, they also make undetectable dishonesty increasingly plausible. The challenge today is 
not merely preventing cheating, but redefining academic integrity in an age where machines can think 
alongside students. 
 
Question 16. Academic dishonesty before the Internet took place in the following forms, EXCEPT 
_______. 
A. hiding handwritten notes during exams 
B. using online AI tools to generate essays  
C. copying homework from classmates 
D. exchanging answers secretly in tests 
Question 17. The word “detect” in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to ______.  
A. succeed 
B. weaken 
C. attract 
D. discover 
Question 18. Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence in paragraph 2? 
The digital environment blurred the boundaries between research and plagiarism, making 
misconduct both more convenient and more difficult to monitor.  
A. The online setting has clearly separated research from plagiarism and reduced unnoticed misconduct. 
B. The online setting made it harder to distinguish research from plagiarism and easier to commit 
unnoticed misconduct.  
C. The digital context strengthened the line between research and plagiarism and simplified monitoring 
misconduct. 
D. The Internet environment distinguished research from plagiarism and made misconduct less convenient 
and more visible. 
Question 19. What can be inferred from the emergence of AI in education? 
A. It completely removes ethical concerns and academic misconduct issues. 
B. It offers both learning support and new risks of academic dishonesty.  
C. It replaces traditional education systems without negative consequences. 
D. It eliminates the need to rethink academic integrity standards. 
Question 20. The word “they” in paragraph 3 refers to _______.  
A. essays 
B. AI tools  
C. complex problems 
D. writing styles  
 
 
Trang 62

VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
TÀI LIỆU LIVESTREAM ĐỘC QUYỀN 
VIP 90 (2026): TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM BỘ CÂU HỎI  
DỄ TRÚNG TỦ & LUYỆN BỘ ĐỀ TINH HOA  
[V90.01.06A] THI ONLINE: TINH HOA ĐỌC HIỂU  
 
(BUỔI 2) – ĐỀ NÂNG CAO 
Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương  
 
 
Read the following passage and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct answer to each of 
the questions from 1 to 8. 
In contemporary societies marked by economic volatility and technological acceleration, learning 
is no longer confined to youth. The traditional model in which education precedes a stable, lifelong career 
has gradually eroded. Instead, adults increasingly find themselves returning to classrooms—both physical 
and virtual—in response to shifting labour markets and evolving professional standards. Within this 
context, adult learners have become a defining feature of modern education systems rather than an 
exception to them.  
Adult learners differ significantly from their younger counterparts in both motivation and 
experience. Many pursue further study to reskill, secure promotion, or transition into new industries. 
Others seek intellectual enrichment or personal fulfilment after years devoted to employment or family 
responsibilities. Unlike school-aged students, adults often bring substantial practical knowledge to their 
studies, allowing them to connect theoretical frameworks with lived realities. This experiential foundation 
can deepen classroom discussions and enhance critical reflection. 
However, adult education is not without its challenges. Time constraints, financial pressures, and 
competing obligations frequently complicate sustained engagement. Balancing coursework with 
employment and caregiving demands requires resilience and disciplined self-management. Moreover, 
returning to formal study after a prolonged hiatus can provoke anxiety, particularly in environments 
saturated with digital tools. Institutions that recognise these constraints often adopt flexible delivery 
modes, blended formats, and modular structures to accommodate diverse circumstances. 
Despite these obstacles, adult learners contribute significantly to social and economic resilience. 
By updating competencies and cultivating adaptability, they strengthen workforce sustainability and civic 
participation. Their presence also reshapes educational culture, encouraging pedagogical approaches that 
value dialogue, relevance, and applied learning. In an era where obsolescence can occur rapidly, adult 
education embodies the principle that learning is not a preliminary stage of life but a continuous, strategic 
process essential for navigating uncertainty and sustaining personal and collective growth. 
 
Question 1. The word “them” in paragraph 1 refers to ______. 
A. labour markets 
 
B. adult learners 
C. modern education systems 
D. professional standards 
 
Trang 63

Question 2. Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence in paragraph 2? 
Adult learners differ significantly from their younger counterparts in both motivation and 
experience. 
A. Variations in ambition and prior exposure lead younger students to diverge from mature learners.  
B. Distinct levels of enthusiasm and accumulated knowledge cause adolescents to contrast with older 
participants.  
C. Marked differences in drive and background distinguish mature students from those of a younger age 
group.  
D. Differences in commitment and experiential depth prompt younger cohorts to stand apart from mature 
students. 
Question 3. Adults pursue further study to do all of the following EXCEPT _______.  
A. transition into new industries  
B. expand their social networks  
C. reskill for updated professional demands  
D. secure career advancement 
Question 4. The word “hiatus” in paragraph 3 can be best replaced by ______. 
A. demand 
B. break 
C. crisis 
D. field  
Question 5. The word “obsolescence” in paragraph 4 is OPPOSITE in meaning to ______. 
A. relevance 
B. authority 
C. influence 
D. complexity  
Question 6. Which of the following is TRUE according to paragraph 4? 
A. Adult education supports workforce sustainability and civic engagement.  
B. Adult learners struggle to adapt within modern labour markets.  
C. Lifelong study primarily replaces traditional early-life schooling.  
D. Adults’ participation in further education does not bring economic benefits.  
Question 7. Which paragraph mentions challenges to adult learners? 
A. Paragraph 1 
B. Paragraph 2 
C. Paragraph 3 
D. Paragraph 4 
Question 8. Which paragraph compares adult learners and young cohorts?  
A. Paragraph 1 
B. Paragraph 2 
C. Paragraph 3 
D. Paragraph 4 
 
Read the following passage and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct answer to each of 
the questions from 9 to 18. 
In little more than a decade, artificial intelligence has shifted from a specialised research domain 
to an embedded feature of everyday life. Algorithms now curate news feeds, recommend purchases, 
screen job applications, and even assist in medical diagnostics. As automation permeates diverse sectors, 
the competencies required for meaningful participation in society are undergoing profound recalibration. 
[I] Against this backdrop, educators and policymakers increasingly warn of an emerging “Generation AI” 
whose prospects may hinge on early exposure to computational thinking and digital fluency. 
Central to these concerns is the spectre of a widening social divide. Children who acquire coding 
skills, data literacy, and algorithmic awareness are likely to navigate future labour markets with greater 
confidence and adaptability. By contrast, those excluded from such instruction risk relegation to passive 
consumption of technologies they neither understand nor influence. The divide, therefore, is not merely 
technical but epistemic. [II] Without equitable access to computing education, existing socioeconomic 
disparities could be entrenched, as technological capital becomes a decisive determinant of opportunity. 
 
 
 
Trang 64 
 
Cô Phương chúc các em lớp VIP 90: ÔN LÀ TRÚNG TỦ - THI LÀ TRÚNG TUYỂN

TUẦN SỐ 01 
 
VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
Yet the issue extends beyond employability. Computational literacy cultivates analytical 
reasoning, problem decomposition, and collaborative problem-solving—capacities transferable across 
disciplines. When children learn how algorithms function, they are better equipped to interrogate 
automated decisions, recognise bias, and safeguard privacy. In this sense, teaching computing is 
inseparable from fostering informed citizenship in an algorithmically mediated public sphere. To omit 
such instruction would be to leave future generations ill-prepared for critical engagement with systems 
that increasingly shape civic and personal life. 
However, implementing universal computing education presents formidable challenges. [III] 
Schools must address disparities in infrastructure, teacher training, and curricular coherence. A narrow 
emphasis on technical proficiency alone may also prove insufficient; ethical reflection and 
interdisciplinary integration are equally indispensable. If approached thoughtfully, the ambition to 
equip all children with computational competence can mitigate inequality. [IV] The question is not 
whether artificial intelligence will influence the next generation, but whether education systems will 
respond with foresight and inclusivity, ensuring that Generation AI becomes a bridge rather than a barrier. 
Question 9. Algorithms can do all of the following EXCEPT ______.  
A. evaluate candidates for employment  
B. conduct surgical operations independently  
C. organise personalised media content  
D. suggest consumer products 
Question 10. The word “relegation” in paragraph 2 is OPPOSITE in meaning to ______. 
A. interference 
B. negligence 
C. promotion 
D. surrender  
Question 11. According to paragraph 2, unequal access to computing education _______.  
A. may limit children’s interest in technological innovation.   
B. may deepen existing social inequalities.   
C. may reduce overall demand for digital professionals.   
D. may slow the pace of artificial intelligence development. 
Question 12. Which of the following best summarises paragraph 3? 
A. Computing education develops civic-critical capacities essential for navigating algorithmic societies.  
B. Computing instruction enhances transferable reasoning skills that mainly support future career 
mobility.  
C. Algorithm education strengthens privacy awareness while improving interdisciplinary collaboration 
across subjects.  
D. Digital literacy prepares students for technological environments and supports analytical classroom 
performance. 
Question 13. The word “they” in paragraph 3 refers to ______. 
A. disciplines 
B. algorithms 
C. capacities 
D. children  
Question 14. As stated in paragraph 3, what would happen if children were not taught computing skills?  
A. They would become dependent on digital systems in daily life.  
B. They would face disadvantages in the present employment markets.  
C. They would struggle to adapt to rapid socioeconomic changes.  
D. They would be ill-prepared to engage critically with AI systems.

 D. [II]

B. As AI transforms society, schools must expand coding instruction to secure future economic mobility

C. As automation spreads across industries, educational reform should prioritise technical proficiency to 
maintain national innovation capacity.  
D. As algorithms influence daily life, infrastructure expansion and teacher training become central to

TUẦN SỐ 01 
 
VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
TÀI LIỆU LIVESTREAM ĐỘC QUYỀN 
VIP 90 (2026): TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM BỘ CÂU HỎI  
DỄ TRÚNG TỦ & LUYỆN BỘ ĐỀ TINH HOA  
[V90.ĐH.01️] ĐỌC HIỂU C1 – C2 BỘ ĐỀ DỄ TRÚNG TỦ 
 
(BUỔI 1) 
Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương  
 
 
Read the following passage and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct answer to each of 
the questions from 1 to 8. 
Although many people think it is a modern phenomenon, distance learning has been around for at 
least 200 years in one form or another. Historical examples of long-distance learning include students 
being sent a series of weekly lessons by mail. The technological advances of the past 20 or so years, 
however, have meant that this form of education is now a credible alternative to face-to-face learning.  
Indeed, 1996 saw the establishment of the world’s first ‘virtual university’ in the United States, 
showing how far distance learning has come in a relatively short space of time. While it is now possible 
to obtain a large variety of online degrees, which is the best type of education to pursue? 
When comparing the two systems, the most obvious difference lies in the way that instruction is 
delivered. Distance learning is heavily dependent on technology, particularly the internet. In a face-to-
face course, students may only require a computer for the purpose of writing an essay. In comparison, 
when learning remotely, technology is the principal means of communication. Face-to-face instruction 
must take place in real time and in one location. Conversely, distance learning can happen at any time and 
in any location, since the learning is not restricted by geography.  
The flexibility this provides means that students may be better able to learn at their own pace, but 
it may also mean that learners have to be well organized and self-disciplined. In other words, they must 
be more highly motivated in order to do well in distance-learning courses. Finally, with face-to-face 
learning, the teacher and student have the opportunity to develop a personal relationship. In a virtual 
classroom, by contrast, the teacher may seldom or never actually meet the student. This may make it hard 
for teachers to understand their students’ specific learning needs. 
Question 1. Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence in paragraph 1? 
Historical examples of long-distance learning include students being sent a series of weekly lessons 
by mail. 
A. Long-distance learning once required students to travel every week. 
B. In earlier times, students attended weekly classes in distant locations. 
C. In the past, distance learners received weekly lessons through the mail.  
D. Historical distance education depended mainly on live online lessons. 
Question 2. The word “obtain” in paragraph 2 can be best replaced by _______. 
A. earn 
B. sell 
C. think 
D. start 
 
Trang 67
