Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
Question 3. Which of the following is NOT mentioned about distance learning in paragraph 3? 
A. It can take place at any time and in any location. 
B. It depends heavily on technology, especially the internet. 
C. It happens in real time and in one location.  
D. It uses technology as the main means of communication. 
Question 4. The word “their” in paragraph 4 refers to ________. 
A. courses 
B. students  
C. learners  
D. words 
Question 5. The word “specific” in paragraph 4 is opposite in meaning to _______. 
A. affordable 
B. particular 
C. basic 
D. general  
Question 6. Which of the following is true according to the passage? 
A. Distance learning always helps teachers understand students’ specific needs better. 
B. Face-to-face learning depends mainly on the internet for communication. 
C. Virtual classrooms guarantee stronger personal relationships than traditional classes. 
D. Distance learning allows students to study at any time and in any location.   
Question 7. In which paragraph does the author mention the establishment of the first virtual university? 
A. Paragraph 1 
B. Paragraph 2 
C. Paragraph 3 
D. Paragraph 4 
Question 8. In which paragraph does the writer discuss the need for self-discipline? 
A. Paragraph 1 
B. Paragraph 2 
C. Paragraph 3 
D. Paragraph 4 
Read the following passage and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct answer to each of 
the questions from 9 to 18. 
When we hear that somebody changed the world, we typically imagine he or she must be an adult. 
The assumption is that changing the world requires experience, knowledge, and wisdom, and that these 
things only come with age. This is not always true, though. Greta Thunberg from Sweden was named 
Time magazine’s Person of the Year at the age of 16 for her continuing work to protect the environment. 
And Thunberg is not the only one. There are many other teenagers who may be little known but who 
are having a big impact on the world.  
Dafne Almazán, from Mexico, was a gifted child. She learned to read and write—without help—
by the age of 3, finished high school by 10, became the youngest psychologist in the world at 15, and 
completed an advanced degree from Harvard University by 18. These accomplishments are so impressive 
that Forbes magazine named her one of the 50 most powerful women in Mexico.  
According to the magazine, her strength lies in her ability to inspire others, such as the students at 
the CEDAT institute for gifted children in Mexico City. [I] At CEDAT, Almazán teaches Mandarin and 
psychology. [II] Her passion for teaching is important because gifted children sometimes struggle to fit 
in. [III] She hopes to inspire these children to make Mexico a better place. [IV] 
Yash Gupta was born in India but moved to California with his family as a child. Gupta admits 
that he was a wild child who always wanted his own way. When he was a high school freshman, though, 
Gupta found a positive focus for his energy. His eyeglasses broke when he was practicing taekwondo. 
While he was waiting for his new glasses to arrive, Gupta found it difficult to focus on studying because 
he could not see clearly.  
He wondered if others might have the same problem, so he did some research and discovered that 
at least 12 million children need glasses but cannot get them. This inspired Gupta to start a not-for-profit 
organization called Sight Learning. He founded it with his own savings and money he earned as a tutor. 
Since 2010, Sight Learning has donated over 60,000 pairs of old glasses to children in many parts of the 
world. 
 
Trang 68

women in Mexico? 
A. Because of her impressive accomplishments. 
B. Because of her long career in public service. 
C. Because of her business leadership experience. 
D. Because of her influence in global activism.

A. Changing the world is something teenagers can easily achieve. 
B. Changing the world requires things that only come with age.  
C. Changing the world means protecting the environment. 
D. Changing the world is possible without knowledge or experience. 
Question 11. Which of the following best summarises paragraph 4?

B. Moving from India to California shaped Yash Gupta’s early personality and habits.

Question 12. Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence in paragraph 1? 
There are many other teenagers who may be little known but who are having a big impact on the 
world.  
A. Several adolescents are widely recognized as they are obviously transforming the world. 
B. Numerous young people, though not famous, are influencing the world in meaningful ways.  
C. Many youths remain largely unknown and therefore are not believed to shape the world. 
D. Some teenagers only begin to influence society after they become well known to the public. 
Question 13. According to the passage, what is the mission of Sight Learning? 
A. To fund advanced university scholarships in California. 
B. To offer free tutoring services for high school students. 
C. To promote taekwondo training for underprivileged youth. 
D. To provide glasses to children who cannot afford them.  
Question 14. The word “them” in paragraph 5 refers to ________. 
A. glasses  
B. children  
C. others  
D. savings  
Question 15. The word “accomplishments” in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to _______. 
A. responsibilities 
B. traditions 
C. efforts 
D. achievements 
Question 16. Where in paragraph 3 does the following sentence best fit? 
If this happens, they may not reach their full potential. 
A. [I] 
B. [II] 
C. [III] 
D. [IV] 
Question 17. Which of the following is TRUE according to the passage? 
A. Dafne Almazán became the youngest psychologist at the age of eighteen.  
B. Sight Learning was funded mainly by large international corporations. 
C. Yash Gupta started his organization after graduating from university. 
D. At least 12 million children need glasses but cannot obtain them.

TUẦN SỐ 02 
 
VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
TÀI LIỆU LIVESTREAM ĐỘC QUYỀN 
VIP 90 (2026): TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM BỘ CÂU HỎI  
DỄ TRÚNG TỦ & LUYỆN BỘ ĐỀ TINH HOA  
[V90.02.04] TINH HOA ĐỌC HIỂU (BUỔI 3) 
 
 
Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương  
LEVEL 1 
 
 
Text 1 
Electronic waste, or e-waste, is becoming a serious problem in many countries. It includes old 
phones, broken laptops, used batteries, and other electronic devices that people throw away. As 
technology develops quickly, many people replace their devices more often, even when the old ones still 
work. As a result, the amount of e-waste is increasing every year. 
E-waste can harm the environment if it is not handled properly. Many electronic products contain 
harmful materials such as lead and mercury. When these materials are left in landfills, they can pollute 
the soil and water. In some places, workers try to take valuable parts from old devices without proper 
protection. This can cause health problems for them and damage the surrounding area. 
To solve this issue, governments and companies need to take action. They can create recycling 
programmes and encourage people to repair devices instead of throwing them away. At the same time, 
individuals should think carefully before buying new electronics. By reducing, reusing, and recycling, we 
can protect the environment and build a more sustainable future. 
 
Question 1. Which of the following is NOT mentioned as a type of e-waste? 
A. used batteries 
B. broken laptops 
C. cheap devices 
D. old phones 
Question 2. The word “properly” in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to ______. 
A. widely 
B. expensively 
C. willingly 
D. correctly 
Question 3. The word “they” in paragraph 2 refers to _____. 
A. workers 
 
B. materials 
 
C. valuable parts 
 
D. electronic products  
Question 4. Which of the following is TRUE according to paragraph 3? 
A. Recycling programmes encourage people to repair devices. 
B. Dealing with the issue of e-waste requires collective action. 
C. 3Rs are no longer suitable to build a sustainable future.  
D. People should stop buying new devices to protect the environment.

TUẦN SỐ 02 
 
VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
LEVEL 2 
 
Text 2 
The world is facing a serious problem called water bankruptcy, according to a recent United 
Nations report. This means that many natural sources of fresh water, like rivers and rainfall, can no longer 
meet the needs of people, plants, and animals. In many regions, people are using more water than nature 
can replace, especially from underground stores called aquifers. These took centuries to form, and once 
they are gone, they may never return.  
About three out of four people live in countries where water is scarce, dirty, or at risk. Around four 
billion people experience water shortage for at least one month each year. If this continues, many 
communities could struggle to get clean water for drinking, growing food, and other basic needs.  
The report warns that climate change, pollution, and growing populations make the problem worse. 
Water bankruptcy is different from a usual water crisis because the damage may be long-term and 
hard to reverse. Experts say that governments, farmers, businesses, and individuals must work together 
to save water, improve how it is used, and protect the remaining water systems.  
Question 5. The word “meet” in paragraph 1 can be best replaced by ______. 
A. challenge 
B. satisfy 
C. produce 
D. increase  
Question 6. The word “they” in paragraph 1 refers to ______. 
A. regions 
B. people 
C. needs 
D. aquifers 
Question 7. As stated in paragraph 2, water scarcity ______.  
A. affects regions with dry climates 
B. remains a future concern  
C. is a current and widespread crisis 
D. results from weak management 
Question 8. The word “struggle” in paragraph 2 is OPPOSITE in meaning to ______. 
A. manage 
B. attempt 
C. fail 
D. explore  
Question 9. Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence in paragraph 3? 
Water bankruptcy is different from a usual water crisis because the damage may be long-term and 
hard to reverse. 
A. Since its effects can persist over time and prove difficult to undo, water bankruptcy stands apart from 
an ordinary water crisis.  
B. Because an ordinary water crisis can create lasting harm that is difficult to repair, it differs from water 
bankruptcy.  
C. As a typical water crisis may bring about damage that remains for years and is hard to fix, it is unlike 
water bankruptcy.  
D. Given that a common water crisis can lead to enduring consequences that are challenging to reverse, 
it contrasts with water bankruptcy. 
 
Text 3 
Fast fashion has become one of the most controversial issues in the modern clothing industry, and 
brands like Shein show why this model raises serious concerns. The company produces a huge number of 
low-cost garments at high speed, releasing new designs almost every day. This “ultra-fast fashion” system 
allows trends to spread quickly through social media, encouraging consumers to buy more than they 
actually need. Because the clothes are so cheap, many people treat them as disposable items rather than 
long-term investments.

TUẦN SỐ 02 
 
VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
However, this rapid cycle of production and consumption comes at a high cost. Manufacturing 
large quantities of clothing requires significant amounts of water, energy, and raw materials. As a result, 
carbon emissions increase and textile waste continues to grow. Many unwanted or unsold garments 
eventually end up in landfills, adding to environmental pollution. There are also concerns about labour 
conditions in factories, where workers may face long hours and low wages in order to keep prices 
competitive. 
Critics argue that the industry must move towards more sustainable practices, such as improving 
transparency, reducing waste, and promoting responsible consumption. Consumers also play an important 
role by choosing quality over quantity and supporting brands that value environmental protection and fair 
working standards.  
Question 10. According to paragraph 1, Shein operates on ______ 
A. a limited, carefully planned release strategy.               B. an ultra-fast, high-volume production model.  
C. a quality-focused, small-scale design process.             D. a demand-based, slow production approach. 
Question 11. The word “they” in paragraph 1 refers to ______. 
A. garments 
B. concerns 
C. brands 
D. consumers 
Question 12. It implies that clothing manufacturing ______.  
A. depends heavily on advanced modern technology  B. provides economic benefits for many countries 
C. responds quickly to changing fashion trends           D. has serious environmental consequences 
Question 13. Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence in paragraph 2? 
Many unwanted or unsold garments eventually end up in landfills, adding to environmental 
pollution. 
A. Environmental contamination leads to large numbers of unwanted or unsold clothes being placed in 
rubbish sites over time.  
B. The growth of environmental pollution results in many unsold or unwanted garments being thrown 
into disposal areas eventually.  
C. By eventually being discarded in rubbish sites, numerous unsold or unwanted clothes contribute to 
environmental contamination. 
D. Increasing environmental contamination causes numerous unwanted or unsold clothing items to be 
sent to dumping grounds. 
Question 14. To tackle the problem of fast fashion, consumers should ______ 
A. prioritise quality and back responsible brands.          B. purchase new trends as frequently as possible.  
C. follow social media influences on clothing.               D. replace outfits whenever styles change. 
Question 15. Which of the following best summarises the text? 
A. Fast fashion spreads trends quickly through social media, encouraging frequent purchases and 
competitive factory pricing worldwide.   
B. Environmental damage from clothing production increases as factories expand output to meet global 
demand.   
C. Sustainable fashion depends on consumers choosing better brands while companies maintain rapid 
production. 
D. Fast fashion’s rapid, cheap production fuels waste, pollution, labour concerns, requiring industry and 
consumer change.

TUẦN SỐ 02 
 
VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
LEVEL 3 
 
Text 4 
Air pollution in New Delhi reached critical levels following the 2025 Diwali celebrations, exposing 
the frailty of the city’s atmospheric environment and underscoring longstanding systemic failings. In the 
days after the festival, where fireworks traditionally saturate the air with fine particulate matter, New 
Delhi registered some of the highest air quality index (AQI) readings in the world, with concentrations of 
PM2.5 far exceeding both national and World Health Organisation guidelines.   
This exacerbation compounds a chronic smog problem that occurs each winter, as vehicle 
emissions, industrial pollutants and crop-burning in adjacent states coalesce under cooler, stagnant air. 
Residents in the metropolis, home to tens of millions, woke up to a shroud of toxic haze that not only 
impaired visibility but also posed grave health risks — particularly respiratory and cardiovascular 
illnesses — to vulnerable communities such as children and the elderly.   
Public disdain has grown alongside the pollution, with demonstrations demanding substantive 
action to curb hazardous emissions. Critics have cast doubt on the effectiveness of short-lived mitigation 
efforts, such as temporary bans or attempts to wash out pollutants, arguing that deep-rooted regulatory 
reform and coordinated regional strategies are essential to protect public health and improve air quality 
sustainably.   
Overall, the 2025 air pollution crisis in New Delhi reveals how cultural practices, climatic 
conditions and inadequate environmental governance can converge to create a pervasive and persistent 
threat to urban liveability and population well-being. 
Question 16. The word “saturate” in paragraph 1 mostly means ______. 
A. shape 
B. spread                         C. flood                          D. seek 
Question 17. All of the following factors contribute to a chronic smog problem in New Delhi EXCEPT 
______.  
A. crop-burning activities in neighbouring states           B. industrial pollutants from manufacturing sectors 
C. vehicle emissions from urban transport                   D. scorching temperatures throughout the year 
Question 18. Vulnerable groups in New Delhi _______ 
A. experience reduced visibility during the winter months.  
B. reside mainly in densely populated urban districts.  
C. contribute significantly to rising pollution levels.  
D. are particularly prone to hazardous air pollution. 
Question 19. The word “disdain” in paragraph 3 is OPPOSITE in meaning to _____. 
A. violation 
B. instability 
C. regard 
D. awareness 
Question 20. Which of the following can be inferred from the passage? 
A. Temporary measures are unlikely to resolve Delhi’s pollution crisis.  
B. Diwali celebrations are the sole cause of toxic winter smog.  
C. Air pollution declines significantly after festival restrictions end.  
D. Public protests have already produced comprehensive policy reforms. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Trang 30

TUẦN SỐ 02 
 
VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
TÀI LIỆU LIVESTREAM ĐỘC QUYỀN 
VIP 90 (2026): TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM BỘ CÂU HỎI  
DỄ TRÚNG TỦ & LUYỆN BỘ ĐỀ TINH HOA  
[V90.02.04B] THI ONLINE: TINH HOA ĐỌC HIỂU  
 
(BUỔI 3) – ĐỀ VẬN DỤNG 
Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương  
 
 
LEVEL 1 
Text 1 
Microplastics are tiny plastic particles that are almost impossible to see. They are increasingly found in 
our drinking water. These particles come from broken-down plastic waste, synthetic clothing, and even 
cosmetic products. Although they are small, their impact may be significant. Scientists are still studying 
how microplastics affect human health, but early research suggests they could carry harmful chemicals 
into the body. What makes the problem more alarming is that most people consume them without knowing 
it. Unlike visible pollution, this threat hides in something we trust every day: clean water. To address the 
issue, governments need stricter regulations on plastic production, and individuals should reduce single-
use plastics. 
Question 1. Which of the following is NOT mentioned as a source of microplastics? 
A. Drinking water 
 
B. Cosmetic products  
 
C. Synthetic clothing 
 
D. Broken-down plastic  
Question 2. The word “they” in the text refers to ______. 
A. scientists  
B. microplastics 
C. humans 
D. chemicals  
Question 3. The word “single-use” in the text can be best replaced by ______. 
A. obtainable 
B. expensive 
C. reusable 
D. disposable  
 
Text 2 
If you have ever felt mysteriously cheerful after a stroll in the park, congratulations — you have just 
experienced biology doing its job. Scientists have discovered that spending time around trees lowers stress 
hormones, reduces blood pressure, and even improves concentration. In other words, trees are quietly 
working harder on your well-being than your expensive coffee ever could. Green spaces give our 
overstimulated brains a chance to rest from screens, traffic, and the constant buzzing of modern life. Even 
a short walk among leaves and birdsong can reset our mood and sharpen our thinking. So next time 
someone suggests “getting some fresh air,” do not roll your eyes. It turns out that hugging a tree may not 
solve all your problems, but it is surprisingly good for your mind. 
Question 4. The word “its” in the text refers to _______. 
A. congratulations 
B. biology  
C. a stroll  
D. the park 
Question 5. Which of the following is NOT mentioned as a benefit of spending time around trees in the 
passage? 
A. Lowering stress hormones 
B. Improving concentration 
C. Reducing blood pressure  
D. Increasing physical strength 
Question 6. The phrase “roll your eyes” in the text is OPPOSITE in meaning to _______. 
A. show enthusiasm 
 
B. express annoyance 
 
C. feel bored 
 
D. display irritation 
 
Trang 31

TUẦN SỐ 02 
 
VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
LEVEL 2 
Text 3 
Cities are often much hotter than the countryside around them. This effect is called the “urban heat island.” 
It happens because buildings, roads, and concrete surfaces absorb heat from the sun during the day and 
release it slowly at night. Unlike trees and grass, these materials do not cool the air. In addition, cars, 
factories, and air conditioners produce extra heat, making the temperature rise even more. As a result, 
cities can be several degrees hotter than nearby rural areas. 
Urban heat islands can cause serious problems. Higher temperatures increase energy use because 
people rely more on air conditioning. They can also lead to health issues, especially for elderly people 
and young children. To reduce this effect, cities should plant more trees, create green roofs, and use lighter 
building materials that reflect sunlight. These solutions can help make cities cooler and more comfortable 
places to live. 
Question 7. The word “release” in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to ______. 
A. emit 
B. collect 
C. block 
D. absorb 
Question 8. What is the “urban heat island” according to the passage? 
A. A natural island located near large cities where temperatures are higher 
B. A situation in which cities are hotter than the surrounding rural areas 
C. A weather event that occurs when cold air is trapped between tall buildings 
D. A phenomenon that causes all regions of a country to become equally warmer 
Question 9. Which of the following is NOT mentioned as an additional source of heat in cities according 
to the passage? 
A. Cars 
B. Factories 
C. Air conditioners 
D. Trees and grass 
Question 10. Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence in paragraph 2? 
Higher temperatures increase energy use because people rely more on air conditioning. 
A. As temperatures rise, people use air conditioners more, leading to higher energy consumption. 
B. Energy production increases in cities even when residents do not change their daily habits. 
C. When the weather becomes cooler, people reduce their use of air conditioning to save electricity.  
D. Air conditioning systems are designed to lower temperatures without affecting energy use. 
 
Text 4 
Invasive species often spread at an alarming rate because they enter ecosystems that are unprepared 
for their arrival. In their native habitats, their populations are naturally controlled by predators, diseases, 
and competition. However, when introduced to a new environment, these biological constraints may be 
absent. As a result, invasive species can reproduce rapidly and outcompete native organisms for food, 
space, and other essential resources. Global trade and travel have accelerated this process, unintentionally 
transporting plants, insects, and animals across continents. 
The consequences can be severe and long-lasting. Invasive species may disrupt food chains, 
degrade habitats, and even drive native species to extinction. Their dominance can also create economic 
burdens, particularly in agriculture and fisheries. Preventing their spread requires stricter biosecurity 
measures and greater public awareness. Once firmly established, invasive species are extremely 
difficult — and costly — to eradicate. 
 
 
 
 
Trang 32

VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
Question 11. The word “arrival” in paragraph 1 is opposite in meaning to ______. 
A. development 
B. emergence 
C. disappearance 
D. involvement 
Question 12. Why do invasive species spread rapidly in new environments, according to the passage? 
A. Because native species actively help them find food and shelter in the ecosystem 
B. Because there are often no biological constraints to control their population  
C. Because the climate in new environments is warmer and more suitable for reproduction 
D. Because they adapt their biology to become stronger than all other species 
Question 13. Which of the following is NOT mentioned in paragraph 2 as a consequence of invasive 
species? 
A. Disrupt food chains 
 
B. Degrade natural habitats 
C. Create economic burdens 
D. Support native species 
Question 14. The word “severe” in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to _______. 
A. mild 
B. serious 
C. ordinary 
D. easy 
Question 15. Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence in paragraph 2? 
Once firmly established, invasive species are extremely difficult — and costly — to eradicate. 
A. After invasive species become well established, removing them is difficult and expensive. 
B. Invasive species can be eliminated quickly and cheaply once they appear in a new habitat. 
C. The longer invasive species survive, the easier and less costly it is to control them. 
D. Governments usually find simple and affordable methods to deal with invasive species. 
 
LEVEL 3 
Text 5 
Air pollution is typically discussed in relation to respiratory illnesses, yet this narrow focus 
obscures a more unsettling reality: polluted air does not stop at the lungs. Fine particulate matter (PM2.5) 
is small enough to enter the bloodstream and travel to the brain. Once there, it can trigger inflammation, 
oxidative stress, and even structural damage to neural tissue. In other words, what we inhale may quietly 
interfere with how we think, remember, and regulate emotions. 
An increasing body of research links long-term exposure to polluted air with cognitive decline, 
reduced attention span, and heightened risks of neurodegenerative disorders such as Alzheimer’s disease. 
Children exposed to high pollution levels have been found to perform worse academically. This 
suggests that environmental toxicity may shape intellectual development from an early age. The 
implications are not merely medical but profoundly social. 
What is particularly disturbing is the invisibility of the threat. Unlike a sudden infection, 
neurological harm accumulates gradually and often goes unnoticed. By the time symptoms emerge, the 
damage may already be entrenched, raising uncomfortable questions about the true cost of modern urban 
life. It forces us to reconsider whether economic growth has been pursued at the expense of cognitive 
well-being. 
 
Question 16. According to paragraph 1, what is the more unsettling reality about air pollution? 
A. It affects not only the lungs. 
B. It causes breathing problems. 
C. Its levels are extremely high. 
D. It spreads infectious diseases. 
Question 17. The word “regulate” in paragraph 1 is CLOSEST in meaning to ______. 
A. succeed 
B. respect 
C. control 
D. hide 
 
 
Trang 33

Question 18. Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence in paragraph 2? 
Children exposed to high pollution levels have been found to perform worse academically. 
A. Academic failure among children is shown to be mainly caused by exposure to highly polluted 
environments. 
B. It is shown that children living in extremely polluted areas usually develop stronger learning abilities 
over time. 
C. Exposure to air pollution has no measurable effect on children’s school performance, according to 
research. 
D. Kids growing up in highly polluted environments have been shown to achieve lower academic results.  
Question 19. The word “unnoticed” in paragraph 3 is opposite in meaning to ______. 
A. ignorant 
B. disregarded 
C. noticeable 
D. ignored 
Question 20. Which of the following is TRUE according to the passage? 
A. Economic growth has clearly improved cognitive well-being in modern cities. 
B. Air pollution only causes immediate and easily noticeable health problems. 
C. Neurological damage from pollution is usually sudden and quickly detected. 
D. Inflammation and oxidative stress can occur in the brain due to polluted air.

TÀI LIỆU LIVESTREAM ĐỘC QUYỀN 
VIP 90 (2026): TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM BỘ CÂU HỎI  
DỄ TRÚNG TỦ & LUYỆN BỘ ĐỀ TINH HOA  
[V90.02.04A] THI ONLINE: TINH HOA ĐỌC HIỂU  
 
(BUỔI 3) – ĐỀ NÂNG CAO 
Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương  
 
 
Read the following passage and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct answer to each of 
the questions from 1 to 8. 
Europe is increasingly witnessing the intensification of extreme weather events, especially severe 
flooding driven by a changing climate. In early 2026 alone, multiple powerful storms and relentless 
rainfall battered much of the continent, from the Iberian Peninsula to the British Isles, resulting in 
widespread inundation of towns and rural areas. These events reflect shifts in atmospheric patterns, 
including a southward movement of the jet stream, which concentrates moisture and heavy rain over 
regions unprepared for such volumes of water. As a result, waterlogged soils and saturated river systems 
have heightened flood risks beyond what many local communities have historically experienced.  
Despite mounting evidence that these phenomena are consistent with climate change 
projections, scientists have criticised European governments for failing to acknowledge the scale of the 
threat. Experts argue that the unprecedented nature of these floods is not anomalous but lies well within 
expected outcomes from climate simulation models, underscoring a disconnect between scientific 
understanding and political response. Authorities in some regions have been taken by surprise by 
events that climate researchers have long warned could occur.  
The human stories behind these statistics are sobering. In parts of Spain and Wales, residents have 
watched whole streets submerged and vehicles swept away, while predictions of danger went unheeded 
or underestimated in official briefings. In one case, a local woman’s concerns about extraordinary rainfall 
went against comforting assurances from officials, only for catastrophic flooding to materialise hours 
later.  
This juxtaposition of escalating environmental risks and institutional inertia reveals a broader 
struggle within Europe to reconcile scientific warnings with policy action. As weather extremes become 
more frequent and severe, pressure is growing on policymakers to align climate strategies with the realities 
of a warming world rather than downplay or delay necessary adaptation and mitigation efforts.  
  
Question 1. The word “battered” in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to ______. 
A. generated 
B. struck 
C. preserved 
D. constrained 
Question 2. The word “which” in paragraph 1 refers to _____. 
A. the southward movement of the jet stream 
 
B. widespread inundation of towns and rural areas 
C. the intensification of extreme weather events 
D. moisture and heavy rain over regions 
Question 3. The word “anomalous” in paragraph 2 is OPPOSITE in meaning to ______. 
A. short-lived 
B. abnormal 
C. emerging 
D. expected 
 
 
 
Trang 35

B. European governments have fully acknowledged the scale of climate threats.  
C. Extreme rainfall in Europe has remained within normal historical patterns. 
D. European officials accurately anticipated the timing of catastrophic flooding.

A. Paragraph 1 
B. Paragraph 2 
C. Paragraph 3 
D. Paragraph 4 
Question 8. Which paragraph mentions a personal story? 
A. Paragraph 1 
B. Paragraph 2 
C. Paragraph 3 
D. Paragraph 4 
 
Read the following passage and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct answer to each of 
the questions from 9 to 18. 
Avalanches have long been one of the most dramatic natural hazards in mountainous regions, but 
recent weather patterns suggest that climate change is reshaping the way these events unfold. In many 
parts of Europe’s Alps and other ski-oriented landscapes, experts have observed unusual combinations of 
snowfall and temperature fluctuation that create snowpacks with unstable layers. These weak layers form 
when early-season snow falls on a warmer surface, then becomes buried by subsequent snowstorms, 
making slopes increasingly susceptible to sudden, powerful avalanches that can catch even experienced 
backcountry skiers by surprise. 
Interestingly, the total number of avalanche fatalities in recent years has not shown a clear upward 
trend, partly because improvements in forecasting, rescue operations, and safety equipment have helped 
mitigate some of the risks. [I] However, the nature of avalanche risk is changing, with temperature swings 
and alternating periods of rain and snow producing more complex and unpredictable snow layers. In 
practical terms, this means that even if avalanches are not becoming more frequent overall, they can occur 
under conditions that are harder to anticipate using traditional methods.

inter sports enthusiasts and local authorities are starting to grapple with the consequences of this 
shift. Many ski resorts face uncertain viability as warming temperatures shorten the snow season 
and reduce reliable snow cover at lower elevations. [II] In some areas, lack of consistent snowfall has 
forced temporary closures, while higher elevations see heavy, moisture-laden snowfalls that increase 
avalanche danger. Climbers and skiers who venture off-piste — beyond groomed, controlled terrain — 
are particularly exposed, often underestimating the instability beneath seemingly solid surfaces. 
[III] The evolving dynamics of avalanches reflect broader environmental changes that affect 
winter recreation, local economies, and public safety. Mountain communities and policymakers now 
recognise that traditional assumptions about snow and avalanche behaviour may no longer hold, 
necessitating more sophisticated risk assessment, enhanced education for winter tourists, and more 
adaptive safety protocols. As climate models project continued warming, understanding the interplay 
between extreme weather and mountain hazards will be essential to protect both humans and the delicate 
alpine environment. [IV]

deal with = cope with xử lý / giải quyết

communicate with

(Cô ấy giao tiếp với khách hàng hằng ngày.)

(Nhiều người cảm thông với nạn nhân.)

hợp tác với

(Các nhà nghiên cứu hợp tác với chuyên gia nước ngoài.) 
tuân thủ

= abide by

UNDERSTANDING PARENT–TEEN CONFLICTS 
A practical guide for families navigating adolescence 
During adolescence, rapid emotional and cognitive development can create tension at home. Teenagers 
strive for autonomy while parents attempt to protect and guide, and misunderstandings often stem (1) 
______ different expectations about responsibility, freedom, and identity. Rather than viewing conflict 
as failure, families can treat it as an opportunity to grow and recalibrate communication. 
Many disagreements arise when parents and teens argue (2) ______ boundaries, academic performance, 
or screen time. Some young people hesitate to confide in adults because they fear criticism, while 
parents may blame their children (3) _____ being uncooperative without fully understanding the 
pressures they face. Over time, repeated tension can result in emotional distance if concerns are left 
unaddressed. 
What can families do? 
● Listen to each other actively before responding. 
● Reflect (4) ______ recurring patterns instead of reacting impulsively. 
● Avoid escalating situations when teens insist (5) ______ immediate independence. 
● Work together to establish rules that everyone can agree on. 
Healthy relationships do not depend on the absence of conflict; they depend on the willingness to 
communicate with empathy and consistency. With patience and mutual respect, families can transform 
confrontation (6) ______ constructive dialogue.

B. beyond 
C. of 
 D. from

B. from 
C. with

B. to 
C. on

B. into 
C. at 
 D. from

ERRATIC WEATHER PATTERNS ACROSS THE GLOBE 
A shifting climate reality 
Across continents, weather systems are becoming increasingly unpredictable, with prolonged 
droughts coinciding with unprecedented floods and unseasonal storms disrupting agricultural cycles. 
Scientists attribute these anomalies (7) ______ rising greenhouse gas concentrations, while coastal 
communities struggle to adapt (8) ______ intensifying cyclones and sea-level rise. In many regions, 
farmers can no longer rely (9) _____ historical rainfall patterns, forcing them to reconsider crop 
selection and irrigation strategies. 
Extreme events not only devastate infrastructure but also interfere (10) ______ food distribution 
networks and energy supply chains. Governments must now prepare (11) ______ compound disasters 
in which heatwaves, wildfires, and water shortages occur simultaneously. Without coordinated 
mitigation efforts, these volatile patterns may ultimately result (12) ______ systemic failures to 
transition toward sustainable economic models.

MENTAL HEALTH CHALLENGES AMONG ADOLESCENTS 
A complex and multifaceted concern 
In recent years, mental health concerns among teenagers have intensified, shaped by academic 
competition, digital immersion, and evolving social expectations. Many adolescents place their trust 
(13) ______ online validation, which can distort self-perception and heighten vulnerability to anxiety 
disorders. Emotional turbulence is often associated (14) _____ identity formation, yet persistent 
exposure to comparison culture may contribute to chronic feelings of inadequacy. 
When distress remains unacknowledged, it can gradually result in social withdrawal and 
diminished motivation. Some teenagers hesitate to seek professional guidance because they fear being 
stigmatised or even accused (15) ______ exaggerating their struggles. Meanwhile, institutions 
sometimes fail to comply (16) ______ comprehensive mental-health frameworks, leaving support 
systems fragmented and reactive rather than preventive.  
Addressing this issue requires more than symbolic campaigns; it calls for institutions to invest 
(17) ______ long-term counselling systems and evidence-based prevention models. Schools that 
genuinely believe in emotional education are more likely to integrate psychological literacy into their 
curriculum rather than treating distress as disciplinary misconduct. When families and educators 
commit (18) ______ sustained dialogue, teenagers are better equipped to regulate stress and navigate

TUẦN SỐ 02 
 
VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
TÀI LIỆU LIVESTREAM ĐỘC QUYỀN 
VIP 90 (2026): TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM BỘ CÂU HỎI  
DỄ TRÚNG TỦ & LUYỆN BỘ ĐỀ TINH HOA  
[V90.02.06] TINH HOA ĐỌC HIỂU (BUỔI 4) 
 
 
Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương  
LEVEL 1 
 
 
 
Text 1 
[I] Climate change is not only harming the planet, but it is also affecting human health in serious 
ways. [II] Rising temperatures, extreme weather, and changing rainfall patterns have made many diseases 
more common and harder to control. [III] In some regions, warmer weather allows mosquitoes to survive 
longer and spread illnesses such as dengue fever and malaria. [IV] 
Floods and heavy storms can also damage clean water systems. When water becomes polluted, 
diseases like cholera and other stomach infections can spread quickly. At the same time, heatwaves can 
cause heatstroke, dehydration, and heart problems, especially among older people and young children. 
Air pollution, which is linked to climate change, can worsen asthma and other breathing conditions.  
Climate change also affects food production. Droughts and crop failures may lead to poor nutrition, 
making people weaker and more likely to get sick. To protect public health, governments need to improve 
healthcare systems, reduce pollution, and prepare for extreme weather. Taking action now can help 
prevent many future health problems. 
 
Question 1. Where in paragraph 1 does the following sentence best fit? 
As a result, more people are at risk of infection than before. 
A. [III] 
B. [I] 
C. [IV] 
D. [II] 
Question 2. As stated in paragraph 2, heatwaves can cause all of the following health issues EXCEPT 
______. 
A. dehydration 
 
B. cholera 
 
C. heart problems 
 
D. heatstroke 
Question 3. The word “worsen” in paragraph 2 is OPPOSITE in meaning to _____. 
A. weaken 
 
B. damage 
 
C. prevent 
 
D. improve 
Question 4. Which of the following is TRUE according to paragraph 3? 
A. Droughts and crop failures can weaken people’s health.  
B. Improved nutrition often follows periods of severe drought.  
C. Extreme weather strengthens food production systems worldwide.  
D. Public health improves naturally after crop shortages. 
 
 
Trang 53

VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
LEVEL 2 
 
Text 2 
Elephants and rhinos are facing an increased risk of poaching in many parts of Africa and Asia. 
These large animals are hunted illegally for their ivory tusks and rhino horns, which are sold at high prices 
on the black market. Although international trade in ivory is banned in many countries, demand for these 
products still exists. As a result, poachers continue to kill wildlife for profit. 
Poaching has serious consequences for both animals and ecosystems. When elephants are killed, 
their herds lose important leaders who guide younger members and protect them from danger. Rhinos, 
which already have low population numbers, are pushed closer to extinction each time one is hunted. The 
loss of these species can disturb the balance of nature, affecting other plants and animals in the area. 
Governments and conservation groups are working hard to protect these endangered animals. They 
increase patrols in national parks, use modern technology to track movements, and educate local 
communities about wildlife protection. However, long-term success depends on reducing demand for 
ivory and rhino horn worldwide. Protecting elephants and rhinos is essential for preserving 
biodiversity and maintaining healthy ecosystems. 
Question 5. According to paragraph 1, poaching ______ 
A. increases illegal trade in rare products.  
B. reduces the demand for ivory worldwide.  
C. discourages black market activities.  
D. is driven by financial profit. 
Question 6. The word “them” in paragraph 2 refers to ______. 
A. leaders 
B. younger members 
C. herds 
D. ecosystems 
Question 7. The word “disturb” in paragraph 2 can be best replaced by ______. 
A. control 
B. include 
C. tackle 
D. upset 
Question 8. Which of the following is NOT mentioned as a measure taken to protect elephants and rhinos? 
A. Educating local communities about wildlife protection.  
B. Increasing patrols inside national parks.  
C. Imposing heavier prison sentences on poachers.  
D. Using modern technology to monitor animal movements. 
Question 9. Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence in paragraph 3? 
Protecting elephants and rhinos is essential for preserving biodiversity and maintaining healthy 
ecosystems. 
A. Safeguarding biodiversity and stable ecosystems ensures the survival of elephants and rhinos.   
B. The survival of elephants and rhinos strengthens biodiversity and ecological balance.   
C. Preserving biodiversity and stable ecosystems depends on safeguarding elephants and rhinos.  
D. Maintaining ecological balance requires biodiversity to protect elephants and rhinos. 
 
Text 3 
A recent study has revealed that pollution from wood burning is responsible for around 8,600 
premature deaths in the United States each year. Although many people consider wood a natural and 
traditional source of heat, especially in colder regions, the smoke it produces contains harmful fine 
particles known as PM2.5. These tiny particles can enter the lungs and bloodstream, increasing the risk 
of respiratory and cardiovascular diseases. As a result, wood smoke has become a significant but often 
overlooked public health concern.

TUẦN SỐ 02 
 
VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
In many urban and suburban areas, residential wood burning contributes heavily to winter air 
pollution. Fireplaces and wood stoves release large amounts of particulate matter, sometimes exceeding 
emissions from industrial sources or vehicles. Vulnerable groups, including children, older adults, and 
people with existing health conditions, are particularly at risk. Long-term exposure to polluted air can 
worsen asthma, trigger heart attacks, and shorten life expectancy. 
Experts argue that stronger regulations, improved stove technology, and public awareness 
campaigns are necessary to reduce these risks. While wood burning may seem like a harmless 
household practice, its broader environmental and health impacts suggest the need for cleaner and 
safer alternatives. 
Question 10. The word “it” in paragraph 1 refers to _____. 
A. heat 
B. source 
C. wood 
D. pollution 
Question 11. According to paragraph 1, PM2.5 is described as a harmful pollutant that ______. 
A. damages buildings and infrastructure in polluted cities.  
B. spreads quickly through soil and water systems.  
C. can directly affect vital organs and lead to serious illness.  
D. weakens forest ecosystems in surrounding rural areas. 
Question 12. Which of the following best summarises paragraph 2? 
A. Residential wood burning increases winter pollution and harms vulnerable groups.  
B. Industrial and vehicle emissions create greater winter pollution than residential wood burning. 
C. Wood stoves produce fewer harmful particles than factories and transport sources.  
D. Winter air pollution damages infrastructure more severely than human health. 
Question 13. Vulnerable groups are mentioned in paragraph 2 to _______.  
A. demonstrate how pollution strengthens community resilience 
B. explain why winter heating remains environmentally friendly 
C. highlight who is most affected by wood-burning pollution 
D. suggest that industrial emissions pose limited health risks 
Question 14. The word “trigger” in paragraph 2 mostly means ______.  
A. seek 
B. cause 
C. spread 
D. stop 
Question 15. Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence in paragraph 3? 
While wood burning may seem like a harmless household practice, its broader environmental and 
health impacts suggest the need for cleaner and safer alternatives.  
A. Seen as a harmless household habit, wood burning has broader health and environmental effects 
requiring cleaner, safer options.  
B. Cleaner, safer alternatives are viewed as harmless household habits that create broader health and 
environmental consequences through wood burning.  
C. Wood burning appears as a harmless household habit that produces wider environmental and health 
consequences despite cleaner, safer options.  
D. Unlike cleaner, safer options, environmental and health consequences make wood burning seem like a 
harmless household habit in daily life. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Trang 55

TUẦN SỐ 02 
 
VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
LEVEL 3 
 
Text 4 
In February 2020, the village of Pentre in South Wales was hit by devastating floods after an 
exceptionally wet winter, prompting residents to reconsider how the surrounding forests were managed. 
Decades of industrial logging had left the woodland overgrown and vulnerable, with clear-felling practices 
by authorities such as Natural Resources Wales (NRW) criticised for stripping hillsides bare and 
undermining local confidence in official stewardship. The floods highlighted the need for a different 
approach — one that involved the community itself. 
Inspired by a Scottish land project, local activist Chris Blake and the grassroots group Welcome to 
Our Woods launched the Skyline project, advocating a cooperative forest management plan with NRW 
rather than top-down directives. Over 18 months, villagers and officials worked together to design a 
sustainable forest strategy that favoured “continuous cover” forestry — thinning trees selectively to 
preserve ecosystem health — over destructive clear-felling.   
The collaboration yielded practical changes, including reformed logging practices, new local 
timber-use initiatives, and educational programmes to teach green skills. Although the forest remains 
public land, Treherbert secured a historic co-management agreement with the government, setting an 
important precedent for sustainable, community-led environmental stewardship.  
Pentre’s experience offers a hopeful model for other post-industrial regions seeking to reclaim 
agency over their landscape and future. By integrating local knowledge with long-term ecological 
planning, the village demonstrates how communities can play a central role in protecting natural resources 
while building resilience and economic opportunities at the same time. 
Question 16. According to paragraph 1, after years of industrial logging, ______.  
A. the forest became economically dependent on large-scale timber extraction.  
B. the woodland was left dense and more exposed to environmental harm.  
C. the surrounding hillsides experienced declining public trust and oversight failures.  
D. the local ecosystem faced increasing pressure from unmanaged recreational use. 
Question 17. The word “undermining” in paragraph 1 is OPPOSITE in meaning to _____. 
A. adjusting 
 
B. weakening 
 
C. evaluating 
 
D. strengthening 
Question 18. What is the aim of the Skyline project?  
A. To expand commercial logging through centralised government control.  
B. To replace public forests with privately managed timber estates.  
C. To strengthen top-down forestry policies across Wales.  
D. To create a cooperative, participatory model of forest management. 
Question 19. The word “yielded” in paragraph 3 mostly means _____. 
A. resulted from 
B. brought about 
C. stuck to 
 D. relied on 
Question 20. It is suggested in paragraph 4 that community involvement can ______. 
A. strengthen sustainable forest management in urban regions 
B. support ecological protection and local social resilience 
C. combine environmental protection with economic development 
D. promote cooperation between residents and policymakers 
 
 
Trang 56

TUẦN SỐ 02 
 
VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
TÀI LIỆU LIVESTREAM ĐỘC QUYỀN 
VIP 90 (2026): TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM BỘ CÂU HỎI  
DỄ TRÚNG TỦ & LUYỆN BỘ ĐỀ TINH HOA  
[V90.02.06B] THI ONLINE: TINH HOA ĐỌC HIỂU  
 
(BUỔI 4) – ĐỀ VẬN DỤNG 
Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương  
 
 
LEVEL 1 
Text 1 
Soil may look like simple dirt, but it is actually full of life. A single handful of healthy soil can contain 
millions of tiny organisms, including bacteria, fungi, and insects. These living things help break down 
dead plants and animals, turning them into nutrients that help new plants grow. Healthy soil also holds 
water, which protects crops during dry periods and reduces flooding after heavy rain. Without rich soil, 
farmers cannot grow enough food, and natural ecosystems become weak. However, soil can be damaged 
by pollution, overuse of chemicals, and cutting down trees. When we protect soil, we protect our food 
supply and the environment at the same time. 
Question 1. Which of the following is NOT mentioned in the passage as a tiny organism found in healthy 
soil? 
A. Bacteria 
B. Fungi 
C. Insects 
D. Earthworms 
Question 2. The word “them” in the text refers to ______. 
A. new plants  
 
B. nutrients  
 
C. living things 
 
D. dead plants and animals 
Question 3. The word “protect” in the text is OPPOSITE in meaning to ______.  
A. obtain 
B. preserve 
C. destroy 
D. support  
 
Text 2 
Bottled water is often marketed as pure, fresh, and safer than tap water. However, this image of “clean” 
water is not always accurate. In many cases, bottled water comes from the same sources as municipal tap 
water, yet it is sold at a much higher price. Moreover, plastic bottles can release tiny particles, including 
microplastics, into the water itself. There is also the environmental cost: producing and transporting 
billions of plastic bottles requires large amounts of energy and fossil fuels. Most empty bottles are not 
recycled and end up in landfills or oceans, contributing to long-term pollution. While bottled water may 
seem convenient, its hidden impacts on both human health and the environment make its “clean” image 
far more complicated than it appears. 
Question 4. According to the text, it is often marketed that bottled water _______. 
A. is more eco-friendly than tap water  
B. comes from natural mountain springs 
C. is free from microplastics and particles 
D. is pure, fresh, and safer than tap water 
Question 5. The word “it” in the text refers to _______. 
A. bottled water 
 
B. this image  
 
C. “clean” water  
 
D. municipal tap water 
Question 6. The word “impacts” in the text is CLOSEST in meaning to _______. 
A. advantages 
B. influences 
C. benefits 
D. drawbacks 
 
 
Trang 57

TUẦN SỐ 02 
 
VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
LEVEL 2 
Text 3 
Sunscreen is widely used to protect human skin from harmful ultraviolet rays. However, some chemical 
ingredients in sunscreen, such as oxybenzone and octinoxate, have been found to harm coral reefs. When 
swimmers enter the ocean, these substances wash off their skin and dissolve in the water. Scientific 
studies show that even small amounts of these chemicals can damage coral DNA, disrupt their growth, 
and cause coral bleaching. Coral reefs are extremely sensitive ecosystems, so they can be affected quickly. 
The damage to coral reefs is not a minor issue. Reefs provide habitat for many marine species and support 
fishing and tourism industries around the world. When coral ecosystems weaken, biodiversity declines, 
and coastal communities may suffer economic losses. Although sunscreen protects humans, its 
environmental impact raises important concerns about the balance between personal health and marine 
conservation. 
Question 7. Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence in paragraph 1? 
When swimmers enter the ocean, these substances wash off their skin and dissolve in the water. 
A. Sunscreen remains completely on the skin even after long periods of swimming. 
B. Swimmers carefully remove sunscreen from their skin before entering the ocean. 
C. The ocean water prevents sunscreen from spreading once swimmers get in. 
D. As people swim, these chemicals come off their bodies and mix into the seawater. 
Question 8. Which of the following is NOT mentioned in paragraph 1 as a harmful effect of sunscreen 
chemicals on coral reefs? 
A. Damaging coral DNA 
 
B. Increasing reproduction rates  
C. Causing coral bleaching  
D. Disrupting coral growth 
Question 9. The word “they” in paragraph 1 refers to ______. 
A. coral reefs  
B. ecosystems 
C. these chemicals 
D. scientific studies 
Question 10. The word “declines” in paragraph 2 is OPPOSITE in meaning to _______.  
A. increases 
B. decreases 
C. weakens 
D. reduces 
 
Text 4 
Electronic waste, or e-waste, refers to discarded devices such as smartphones, laptops, televisions, 
and batteries. As technology develops rapidly, people replace their devices more frequently. This 
leads to a dramatic increase in electronic waste worldwide. Many of these products contain toxic materials 
like lead, mercury, and cadmium. When e-waste is not properly recycled, these harmful substances can 
leak into the soil and water, causing serious environmental pollution. Informal recycling practices, 
especially in developing countries, often expose workers to dangerous chemicals without adequate 
protection. 
The environmental consequences of e-waste extend beyond pollution. The production of electronic 
devices requires large amounts of energy and rare natural resources. When devices are thrown away 
instead of being reused or repaired, valuable materials are wasted. This cycle increases pressure on mining 
activities and contributes to climate change. Therefore, the growing volume of e-waste has become a 
major global environmental challenge.

LEVEL 3 
Text 5 
Mangrove forests are often overlooked, dismissed as muddy swamps at the edge of the sea. Yet 
these ecosystems are among the most biologically productive on the planet. Situated along tropical and 
subtropical coastlines, mangroves serve as critical nurseries for countless marine species, including fish, 
crustaceans, and molluscs. Their complex root systems create sheltered habitats where young organisms 
can grow before moving into open waters. Without mangroves, the stability of many coastal food webs 
would be severely compromised. 
Beyond biodiversity, mangroves function as powerful natural barriers. Their dense roots reduce 
wave energy, limit coastal erosion, and protect communities from storm surges and rising sea levels. In 
an era when climate extremes are intensifying, this protective role is far from trivial. Moreover, mangrove 
forests are remarkably efficient carbon sinks, storing significant amounts of carbon in both their biomass 
and underlying sediments. 
Despite their ecological significance, mangroves continue to be cleared for aquaculture, urban 
expansion, and infrastructure development. This destruction not only undermines coastal resilience but 
also accelerates climate change. This reveals how shortsighted environmental policies can generate long-
term global consequences. The loss of mangroves also threatens food security and local livelihoods 
that depend heavily on coastal ecosystems.

TÀI LIỆU LIVESTREAM ĐỘC QUYỀN 
VIP 90 (2026): TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM BỘ CÂU HỎI  
DỄ TRÚNG TỦ & LUYỆN BỘ ĐỀ TINH HOA  
[V90.02.06A] THI ONLINE: TINH HOA ĐỌC HIỂU  
 
(BUỔI 4) – ĐỀ NÂNG CAO 
Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương  
 
 
Read the following passage and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct answer to each of 
the questions from 1 to 8. 
Recent legal developments in the United States have brought a surprising environmental threat 
into the spotlight: tyre pollution and its potential role in killing vulnerable salmon. A three-day trial in a 
federal court in San Francisco was convened after west coast fishers and conservation organisations sued 
major tyre manufacturers. They claim that a chemical additive found in vehicle tyres is washing into rivers 
and creeks and contributing to the mass deaths of coho salmon and other fish species. If the case succeeds, 
it could have far-reaching consequences for how industrial pollution is regulated globally.   
The scientific background to the lawsuit stems from years of ecological research. For decades, 
scientists observed that coho salmon returning to freshwater streams to spawn were dying soon after heavy 
rain. These fish displayed strange behaviours such as disorientation and distress before dying, leading 
researchers to suspect urban stormwater runoff was responsible. After extensive investigation, a team of 
researchers identified a toxic compound known as 6PPD-quinone, a by-product of a common tyre 
preservative, as a likely culprit. This substance forms when tyres wear down and the chemical reacts with 
ozone, eventually ending up in waterways.   
The fishers argue that the chemical’s presence in rivers is not only harming the salmon but also 
threatening the livelihoods of communities that depend on fisheries. Coho salmon populations are already 
considered endangered in parts of the Pacific Northwest, and their continued decline raises serious 
ecological and economic concerns. Critics of the tyre industry claim that manufacturers should be 
held accountable under environmental protection laws.   
Defendants in the trial contend that the science is still evolving and that the additive is essential 
for tyre safety, with no readily available alternative. Scientists, lawyers and environmental advocates are 
watching closely, as the ruling could set a precedent for environmental litigation and strengthen efforts to 
control pollutants linked to biodiversity loss. 
 
Question 1. The word “far-reaching” in paragraph 1 is OPPOSITE in meaning to _____. 
A. extensive 
B. intense 
C. complex 
D. limited 
Question 2. The word “culprit” in paragraph 2 can be best replaced by ______. 
A. reason 
B. solution 
C. influence 
D. demand 
Question 3. The word “their” in paragraph 3 refers to ______. 
A. Coho salmon populations 
B. fisheries 
  
C. communities 
 
D. rivers  
 
 
 
 
 
Trang 61

Read the following passage and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct answer to each of 
the questions from 9 to 18. 
The escalating problem of textile waste has become a defining environmental challenge of our 
time, particularly in nations with voracious consumer habits. In Australia alone, approximately 1.5 billion 
new garments were purchased over the past year, equating to roughly 55 items per person. Despite the 
surge in secondhand sales and reuse initiatives, more than half of unwanted clothes still end up in landfill, 
while only a small fraction—around nine per cent—are recycled. The inability to divert these textiles from 
waste streams contributes significantly to carbon emissions and exorbitant water consumption, 
highlighting the need for more systemic and sustainable approaches to fashion consumption.  
One promising paradigm shift emerges from the notion of reimagining what happens to garments 
at the end of their life cycle. [I] Rather than consigning clothes to landfill once they are no longer 
wearable, designers and advocates urge consumers to prioritise strategies like re-wearing, repairing, and 
donating. Such practices keep garments circulating within communities and diminish the demand for 
newly manufactured items, thereby reducing their overall environmental footprint. Crucially, clear 
donation guidelines help ensure that only items of suitable quality enter resale and charity 
networks, maximising their reuse potential.  
Innovative solutions are also being pioneered at the intersection of design and circular economy 
principles. A New Zealand ethical fashion label has taken a particularly radical step by eliminating plastic 
and synthetic trims from every aspect of its production. This allows end-of-life garments to be converted 
into biochar, an organic material that enriches soil and can be reused in agricultural contexts. [II] By 
deconstructing clothes and reincorporating their organic elements into new forms of utility, this initiative 
challenges the conventional lifecycle of apparel and proposes a restorative alternative to extractive fashion 
models.

B. The enhancement of reuse prospects results in resale and charitable channels, introducing precise

TUẦN SỐ 02 
 
VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026

TÀI LIỆU LIVESTREAM ĐỘC QUYỀN 
VIP 90 (2026): TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM BỘ CÂU HỎI  
DỄ TRÚNG TỦ & LUYỆN BỘ ĐỀ TINH HOA  
[V90.ĐH.02] ĐỌC HIỂU C1 – C2 BỘ ĐỀ DỄ TRÚNG TỦ 
 
(BUỔI 2) 
Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương  
 
 
Read the following passage and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct answer to each of 
the questions from 1 to 8. 
In 2015, a series of huge earthquakes struck Nepal, killing over 8,800 people. After the initial 
destruction to buildings, the lack of food security presented a longer-term threat to Nepali citizens. 
Even before the earthquakes, up to seventy percent of people in mountainous areas were classified 
as having poor or unreliable access to food. In these remote locations, stocks of grain are typically stored 
in people’s homes. This meant that thanks to the earthquakes, eighty percent of households in the worst-
affected areas lost their entire stocks of food. Even those with enough money to replace lost food found 
that they could not access markets in towns, as transport connections were broken by destroyed roads or 
landslides. 
Some 3.5 million people found themselves needing food assistance. UN agencies, including the 
WFP and UNICEF—the UN’s children’s agency—launched a $415 million appeal. Initially, UN 
helicopters dropped high-energy biscuits to help those in immediate need, but with time, they brought 
more than 2,000 tons of food into the country, much of it having been sent from neighboring India. The 
Red Cross also raised $26 million and used these funds to distribute much-needed food and water. 
Although food aid helped Nepal through one of its darkest hours, by 2021, 4.6 million people 
remained food-insecure, and the Red Cross was still on the ground. In fact, research by USAID showed 
that ten percent of homes were severely food-insecure. 
Improving Nepal’s infrastructure may be the best way to increase—and, most importantly, 
stabilize—food security. As things stand, farmers have little access to quality seeds or up-to-date 
agricultural technologies. Transport links also remain limited in this relatively small and incredibly 
mountainous country. Helping countries like Nepal requires global investment before, not after, disasters 
like the 2015 earthquakes. 
(Adapted from Reading for the Real World 3) 
Question 1. The word “presented” in paragraph 1 can be best replaced by _______. 
A. solved 
B. removed 
C. posed  
D. avoided 
Question 2. The word “their” in paragraph 2 refers to ________. 
A. the earthquakes 
 
B. households  
 
C. stocks of food 
 
D. worst-affected areas  
Question 3. Which of the following is NOT mentioned in paragraph 3 as an organization that assisted in 
Nepal? 
A. WFP 
B. UNICEF 
C. The Red Cross 
D. USAID  
Question 4. The phrase “on the ground” in paragraph 4 is opposite in meaning to _______. 
A. operating remotely 
B. working directly 
C. physically present 
D. actively helping 
 
 
 
Trang 65

Read the following passage and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct answer to each of 
the questions from 9 to 18. 
Biomass is any organic, decomposable matter that can be used either as a fuel in the production of 
heat or electricity, or converted into liquid fuel substitutes for gasoline and diesel. As it is derived from 
living, or recently living, plants and animals that can be replaced in a relatively short period of time, it is 
generally considered to be a renewable source of energy. 
Although the production process varies slightly depending on the type of biomass used, it is, in 
essence, not dissimilar to that of a traditional coal-fired power station. Biomass—typically wood chips or 
pellets—is burned in a combustion chamber in place of coal to boil water. This produces high-pressure 
steam that is passed through a turbine, causing it to rotate. This, in turn, powers a generator that creates 
electricity. A transformer then increases the voltage of the electricity before distributing it to homes and 
businesses. Methane gas captured from the anaerobic digestion of animal waste or collected from landfills 
can also be used as a fuel during the initial stages of the process. Since this is a relatively simplistic 
process that is modelled on existing technology, there has been a significant growth in the number 
of biomass power plants around the world. 
During the process of photosynthesis, plants harness radiant energy from the sun in order to 
convert water and carbon dioxide (CO₂) into oxygen and carbohydrates. As the amount of CO₂ released 
into the atmosphere when biomass is burned is equal to the amount captured during photosynthesis, 
proponents of bioenergy claim that it is a carbon-neutral source of energy. However, this is debatable for 
several reasons.  
Firstly, biomass can help to minimise carbon emissions if fast-growing crops such as willow are 
cultivated on unproductive land. [I] However, cutting down forests for energy releases CO₂ that otherwise 
would have been captured. [II] Secondly, proponents fail to take into account that harvesting and 
transporting biomass over great distances generate hazardous air pollutants, which are not offset during 
the process. [III] While the effects of burning biomass can be mitigated to a certain extent by choosing to 
burn less polluting biomass, it is highly doubtful that it can ever be classed as truly carbon neutral. [IV] 
(Adapted from Skillful 4: Reading & Writing) 
 
Trang 66

Question 9. According to the passage, what is biomass? 
A. A type of synthetic fuel created entirely from chemical reactions in laboratories 
B. Fossil fuels formed from ancient plants and animals over millions of years 
C. A non-renewable energy source extracted from underground coal reserves 
D. Organic, decomposable matter used to produce heat, electricity, or liquid fuels  
Question 10. The word “it” in paragraph 1 refers to ________. 
A. gasoline  
B. biomass  
C. a fuel  
D. electricity 
Question 11. According to paragraph 2, what role does biomass play in the process of generating 
electricity? 
A. It is burned to produce steam that turns a turbine connected to a generator. 
B. It directly converts sunlight into electricity through solar panels. 
C. It stores electricity in batteries before distributing it to homes. 
D. It replaces turbines by generating electricity without combustion. 
Question 12. Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence in paragraph 2? 
Since this is a relatively simplistic process that is modelled on existing technology, there has been a 
significant growth in the number of biomass power plants around the world. 
A. Because the process relies on outdated technology and requires complex adjustments, biomass power 
plants have expanded rapidly. 
B. As the process is quite simple and based on already available technology, the number of biomass power 
plants worldwide has increased greatly.  
C. As the production method demands highly specialised equipment, only a limited number of biomass 
power plants have been constructed globally. 
D. Since the process differs greatly from traditional power generation, the growth of biomass facilities 
has remained relatively slow worldwide. 
Question 13. Which of the following statements is TRUE according to the passage? 
A. Transporting biomass over long distances can generate hazardous air pollutants. 
B. Biomass is formed over millions of years in the same way as coal and oil. 
C. Biomass power plants generate electricity without using turbines or generators. 
D. Methane gas cannot be used as a fuel source in biomass energy production. 
Question 14. Which of the following best summarises paragraph 3? 
A. The combustion of biomass does not always result in lower emissions than fossil fuels without any 
controversy. 
B. Biomass power generation is identical to coal-fired systems and therefore widely accepted as 
environmentally friendly. 
C. Biomass is considered carbon neutral as it releases the same CO₂ absorbed during photosynthesis, but 
this claim is debated.  
D. Photosynthesis permanently removes carbon dioxide from the atmosphere, eliminating climate change 
concerns entirely. 
Question 15. The word “harness” in paragraph 3 is closest in meaning to _______. 
A. exploit 
B. ignore 
C. release 
D. abandon 
Question 16. Where in paragraph 4 does the following sentence best fit? 
And it can take centuries to re-establish these forests and pay off this carbon debt. 
A. [I] 
B. [II] 
C. [III] 
D. [IV]

A. All biomass sources are equally sustainable regardless of how they are obtained. 
B. Biomass energy produces absolutely no environmental impact at any stage of its production. 
C. Biomass releases less carbon dioxide than it absorbs under all circumstances. 
D. Whether biomass is carbon neutral or not relies on how it is produced and transported.  
Question 18. Which of the following best summarises the passage? 
A. Biomass energy completely eliminates carbon emissions and has no environmental drawbacks, making 
it the most sustainable alternative to fossil fuels worldwide. 
B. Biomass is a renewable energy source produced from organic matter, but its claimed carbon neutrality 
remains controversial due to environmental and logistical concerns.  
C. The passage mainly explains how coal-fired power stations operate and why traditional fossil fuels 
remain essential for global electricity production. 
D. Photosynthesis alone guarantees that all bioenergy systems are environmentally harmless, regardless 
of deforestation, transportation, or land-use practices.

TÀI LIỆU LIVESTREAM ĐỘC QUYỀN 
VIP 90 (2026): TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM BỘ CÂU HỎI  
DỄ TRÚNG TỦ & LUYỆN BỘ ĐỀ TINH HOA  
[V90.03.01] LÀM CHỦ KIẾN THỨC NGỮ PHÁP  
 
GIỚI TỪ & CỤM GIỚI TỪ (BUỔI 2) 
Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương  
 
 
Các cấu trúc tính từ đi với giới từ thường gặp 
1. Tính từ + for 
Cấu trúc 
Nghĩa 
Ví dụ 
famous for 
nổi tiếng vì 
The city is famous for its festivals.  
(Thành phố nổi tiếng vì các lễ hội.) 
known for 
được biết đến vì 
She is known for her creativity.  
(Cô ấy được biết đến vì sự sáng tạo.) 
responsible for 
chịu trách nhiệm về 
He is responsible for the project.  
(Anh ấy chịu trách nhiệm về dự án.) 
suitable for 
phù hợp với 
This book is suitable for children.  
(Cuốn sách này phù hợp với trẻ em.) 
good for 
tốt cho 
Walking is good for your health.  
(Đi bộ tốt cho sức khỏe.) 
bad for 
có hại cho 
Smoking is bad for your lungs.  
(Hút thuốc có hại cho phổi.) 
ready for 
sẵn sàng cho 
She is ready for the exam.  
(Cô ấy đã sẵn sàng cho kỳ thi.) 
prepared for 
chuẩn bị cho 
They were prepared for the storm.  
(Họ đã chuẩn bị cho cơn bão.) 
eligible for 
đủ điều kiện cho 
He is eligible for a scholarship.  
(Anh ấy đủ điều kiện nhận học bổng.) 
grateful for 
biết ơn vì 
I am grateful for your help.  
(Tôi biết ơn sự giúp đỡ của bạn.) 
thankful for 
biết ơn vì 
We are thankful for this opportunity.  
(Chúng tôi biết ơn cơ hội này.) 
sorry for 
xin lỗi vì / cảm thấy 
tiếc cho 
She felt sorry for the mistake.  
(Cô ấy cảm thấy tiếc vì lỗi đó.) 
appropriate for 
thích hợp cho 
This film is not appropriate for children.  
(Bộ phim này không phù hợp với trẻ em.) 
necessary for 
cần thiết cho 
Water is necessary for life.  
(Nước cần thiết cho sự sống.) 
essential for/to 
thiết yếu cho 
Discipline is essential for success.  
(Kỷ luật rất cần thiết cho thành công.) 
eager for 
háo hức về 
She is eager for new challenges.  
(Cô ấy háo hức với những thử thách mới.) 
qualified for 
đủ trình độ cho 
She is qualified for the job.  
(Cô ấy đủ trình độ cho công việc.) 
late for 
trễ 
He was late for school.  
(Anh ấy đến trường trễ.)

Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
2. Tính từ + to 
Cấu trúc 
Nghĩa 
Ví dụ 
similar to 
tương tự với 
This method is similar to the previous one.  
(Phương pháp này tương tự phương pháp trước.) 
equal to 
ngang bằng với 
No one is equal to her in experience.  
(Không ai ngang bằng cô ấy về kinh nghiệm.) 
married to 
kết hôn với 
She is married to a doctor.  
(Cô ấy kết hôn với một bác sĩ.) 
related to 
liên quan đến 
The problem is related to funding.  
(Vấn đề liên quan đến tài chính.) 
used/ accustomed to quen với 
She is accustomed to working late.  
(Cô ấy quen làm việc muộn.) 
opposed to 
phản đối 
They are opposed to the new policy.  
(Họ phản đối chính sách mới.) 
devoted to 
tận tâm với 
He is devoted to his family.  
(Anh ấy tận tâm với gia đình.) 
committed to 
cam kết với 
She is committed to her goals.  
(Cô ấy cam kết với mục tiêu của mình.) 
addicted to 
nghiện 
He is addicted to online games.  
(Anh ấy nghiện trò chơi trực tuyến.) 
allergic to 
dị ứng với 
She is allergic to seafood.  
(Cô ấy dị ứng với hải sản.) 
sensitive to 
nhạy cảm với 
Children are sensitive to criticism.  
(Trẻ em nhạy cảm với lời chỉ trích.) 
polite to 
lịch sự với 
He was polite to the guests.  
(Anh ấy lịch sự với khách.) 
rude to 
thô lỗ với 
She was rude to her teacher.  
(Cô ấy thô lỗ với giáo viên.) 
kind to 
tốt bụng với 
He is always kind to strangers.  
(Anh ấy luôn tốt bụng với người lạ.) 
loyal to 
trung thành với 
She is loyal to her friends.  
(Cô ấy trung thành với bạn bè.) 
grateful to sb for sth biết ơn ai vì điều gì 
I am extremely grateful to all the teachers for their help.  
(Tôi cực kỳ biết ơn tất cả các thầy cô đã giúp tôi.) 
important to 
quan trọng đối với 
Education is important to success.  
(Giáo dục quan trọng đối với thành công.) 
beneficial to 
có lợi cho 
Yoga is beneficial to mental health.  
(Yoga có lợi cho sức khỏe tinh thần.) 
immune to 
miễn nhiễm khỏi 
No one is immune to criticism.  
(Không ai miễn nhiễm khỏi sự chỉ trích.)

3. Tính từ + with 
Cấu trúc 
Nghĩa 
Ví dụ 
satisfied with 
hài lòng với 
She is satisfied with the results.  
(Cô ấy hài lòng với kết quả.) 
angry with 
tức giận với  
She was angry with her brother.  
(Cô ấy tức giận với em trai.) 
annoyed with 
khó chịu với 
I am annoyed with him.  
(Tôi khó chịu với anh ta.) 
upset with 
buồn / khó chịu với 
She was upset with her friend.  
(Cô ấy buồn vì bạn mình.) 
familiar with 
quen thuộc với 
I am familiar with this software.  
(Tôi quen với phần mềm này.) 
crowded/packed with 
đông đúc, chật kín 
với 
The stadium was crowded with fans.  
(Sân vận động đông kín người hâm mộ.) 
filled with 
ngập tràn với 
The room was filled with laughter.  
(Căn phòng tràn ngập tiếng cười.) 
covered with 
phủ đầy với 
The ground was covered with snow.  
(Mặt đất phủ đầy tuyết.) 
equipped with 
được trang bị với 
The lab is equipped with modern devices.  
(Phòng thí nghiệm được trang bị thiết bị hiện đại.) 
associated with 
gắn liền với 
Stress is often associated with long working hours. 
(Căng thẳng thường gắn với giờ làm dài.) 
impressed with/by 
ấn tượng với 
I was impressed with her performance.  
(Tôi ấn tượng với phần trình diễn của cô ấy.) 
content with 
bằng lòng với 
He is content with a simple life.  
(Anh ấy bằng lòng với cuộc sống giản dị.) 
patient with 
kiên nhẫn với 
Teachers must be patient with students.  
(Giáo viên phải kiên nhẫn với học sinh.) 
strict with 
nghiêm khắc với 
She is strict with her children.  
(Cô ấy nghiêm khắc với con mình.)

4. Tính từ + of 
Cấu trúc 
Nghĩa 
Ví dụ 
afraid of 
sợ

aware of

capable of

incapable of 
không có khả năng

(Anh ấy không thể nói dối.) 
fond of 
thích 
She is fond of classical music.  
(Cô ấy thích nhạc cổ điển.) 
proud of 
tự hào về 
They are proud of their achievements.  
(Họ tự hào về thành tích của mình.) 
ashamed of
