TUẦN SỐ 03 
 
VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
[III] A study analyzing U.S. census data from 1950 to 2000 found that as more women entered 
certain professions, wages in those occupations declined—even when accounting for education, 
experience, and skill level. [IV] This suggests that society devalues work as soon as it becomes associated 
with women. 
(Adapted from https://www.wcorporation.org/) 
Question 9. According to paragraph 1, how has gender shaped the workforce for decades? 
A. Gender has generally had no significant effect on job distribution or workplace hierarchy. 
B. Women have consistently held most executive and high-paying management positions. 
C. Both men and women have had equal access to leadership positions and salary opportunities. 
D. Men have occupied higher-paid leadership roles while women have worked in services.  
Question 10. The word “persistent” in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to _______. 
A. plentiful 
B. abundant 
C. tenacious 
 D. flexible 
Question 11. According to the passage, the “Glass Ceiling” and the “Glass Escalator” are ______. 
A. identical theories 
 
B. contrasting concepts  
C. unrelated ideas 
 
D. temporary trends 
Question 12. The word “them” in paragraph 2 refers to ________. 
A. pink-collar jobs  
 
B. men  
 
C. professions 
 
D. managerial positions  
Question 13. Which of the following best summarises paragraph 2? 
A. The paragraph suggests that leadership inequality disappears when professions become numerically 
dominated by women. 
B. The concept explains how workplace structures actively support men’s rapid promotion in traditionally 
female-dominated sectors. 
C. It argues that promotion patterns are mainly determined by individual ambition rather than structural 
gender advantages. 
D. The Glass Escalator describes how men in female-dominated professions are quickly promoted, 
reinforcing gender inequality.  
Question 14. Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence in paragraph 3? 
Despite requiring comparable education and responsibilities, jobs dominated by women 
consistently pay less than those dominated by men. 
A. Male-dominated careers tend to pay less as they require similar qualifications and responsibilities 
compared with female-dominated ones. 
B. Occupations filled by women involve less education and responsibility, which explains their 
significantly lower overall pay levels. 
C. Though they demand similar education and duties, female-dominated jobs regularly offer lower 
salaries than male-dominated ones.  
D. When two professions require equivalent training and duties, women generally receive higher wages 
than their male counterparts. 
Question 15. Where in the passage does the following sentence best fit? 
Research published in the journal Social Forces further confirms this pattern. 
A. [I] 
B. [II] 
C. [III] 
D. [IV]

Question 16. Which of the following statements is TRUE according to the passage? 
A. Pink-collar jobs usually pay more than male-dominated occupations. 
B. Male nurses are more likely to be promoted to supervisory positions.  
C. Women are often fast-tracked to leadership roles in nursing and education. 
D. Wages increase when more women enter a particular profession. 
Question 17. Which of the following can be inferred from the passage? 
A. Occupations tend to lose social and economic value when they become associated primarily with 
women. 
B. Salary levels in different professions rise steadily whenever more employees enter the same 
occupational field. 
C. Leadership inequality completely disappears once women numerically dominate a particular 
professional sector. 
D. Differences in wages across occupations are entirely explained by education, experience, and skill 
requirements.  
Question 18. Which of the following best summarises the passage? 
A. Modern labor markets have largely achieved equality, as promotion systems and salaries now operate 
fairly across most professions. 
B. Workforce differences mainly reflect individual preferences and market forces, with limited influence 
from systemic gender bias or structural inequality. 
C. Wage variations are primarily caused by economic specialization and productivity differences rather 
than gender-based discrimination in occupations. 
D. Gender inequalities persist in the workforce, with female-dominated jobs undervalued, lower paid, and 
leadership roles often favoring men.

Cụm động từ với Go 
Cụm động từ 
Nghĩa 
Ví dụ 
go off 
nổ tung; chuông reo; 
thức ăn bị ôi 
The alarm went off at 6 a.m.  
(Chuông báo thức reo lúc 6 giờ sáng.) 
go down with 
mắc bệnh, đổ bệnh 
She went down with the flu after the trip.  
(Cô ấy bị cúm sau chuyến đi.) 
go over 
xem lại, kiểm tra lại 
The teacher asked us to go over the answers carefully.  
(Giáo viên yêu cầu chúng tôi xem lại đáp án cẩn thận.) 
go up 
tăng lên 
Prices of fresh vegetables have gone up recently.  
(Giá rau tươi gần đây tăng lên.) 
go down 
giảm xuống 
The unemployment rate went down last year.  
(Tỷ lệ thất nghiệp đã giảm năm ngoái.) 
go in for 
yêu thích; tham gia 
She really goes in for classical music.  
(Cô ấy rất thích nhạc cổ điển.) 
go along with 
đồng ý, ủng hộ 
They decided to go along with the manager’s proposal.  
(Họ quyết định ủng hộ đề xuất của quản lý.) 
go through 
trải qua 
Many families went through difficult times during the 
crisis.  
(Nhiều gia đình đã trải qua thời kỳ khó khăn trong 
khủng hoảng.) 
go away 
rời đi 
Please go away and leave me alone for a moment.  
(Hãy rời đi và để tôi yên một lát.) 
go out 
ra ngoài; đèn tắt, mất 
điện 
The lights suddenly went out during the storm.  
(Đèn đột ngột tắt trong cơn bão.) 
go on 
tiếp tục; (thời gian) 
trôi qua 
The discussion went on for hours.  
(Cuộc thảo luận tiếp tục hàng giờ.) 
go back 
quay lại 
He plans to go back to his hometown next year.  
(Anh ấy dự định quay về quê năm sau.) 
go without 
không có thứ gì; xoay 
sở khi không có 
During the trip, we had to go without hot water.  
(Trong chuyến đi, chúng tôi phải xoay sở khi không có 
nước nóng.) 
go by 
trôi qua (thời gian); 
dựa vào 
As the years go by, people change their priorities.  
(Theo năm tháng trôi qua, con người thay đổi ưu tiên 
của mình.)

Nghĩa 
Ví dụ

keep away from

keep on 
tiếp tục làm gì

keep up 
duy trì; tiếp tục ở mức 
cao

keep back 
kìm lại; không nói cho 
ai điều gì

(Cô không thể kìm được nước mắt.) 
keep to 
tuân thủ 
Drivers must keep to the speed limit.  
(Tài xế phải tuân thủ giới hạn tốc độ.) 
keep at 
kiên trì làm gì 
If you keep at your studies, you will improve quickly.

Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương  
 
 
Cụm động từ với Give 
Cụm động từ 
Nghĩa 
Ví dụ 
give in 
nhượng bộ; nộp (bài, 
đơn…) 
After hours of debate, the committee finally gave in to 
public pressure.  
(Sau nhiều giờ tranh luận, ủy ban cuối cùng đã nhượng 
bộ trước áp lực dư luận.) 
give up 
từ bỏ 
Many students give up learning a language because 
they lose motivation.  
(Nhiều học sinh từ bỏ việc học ngoại ngữ vì mất động 
lực.) 
give off 
tỏa ra (mùi, ánh sáng, 
nhiệt…) 
The chemical substance gave off a strong smell.  
(Chất hóa học đó tỏa ra mùi rất mạnh.) 
give away 
cho tặng 
The charity organisation gave away hundreds of 
blankets to the homeless.  
(Tổ chức từ thiện đã tặng hàng trăm chiếc chăn cho 
người vô gia cư.) 
give something back trả lại cái gì 
He promised to give the book back after finishing it.  
(Anh ấy hứa sẽ trả lại cuốn sách sau khi đọc xong.) 
give out 
phân phát; phát ra; 
ngừng hoạt động 
Volunteers gave out food and water to the victims.  
(Các tình nguyện viên phân phát thức ăn và nước cho 
các nạn nhân.) 
give back 
trả lại 
The museum asked visitors to give back any borrowed 
audio guides.  
(Bảo tàng yêu cầu khách tham quan trả lại thiết bị 
hướng dẫn.)

Nghĩa 
Ví dụ 
fall behind 
tụt lại phía sau; chậm 
hơn

không thành công; đổ 
vỡ (kế hoạch, thỏa 
thuận) 
fall back on 
dựa vào; trông cậy 
vào khi gặp khó khăn 
When facing financial trouble, many people fall back 
on their savings.  
(Khi gặp khó khăn tài chính, nhiều người phải dựa vào tiền 
tiết kiệm.) 
fall down 
rơi xuống; sụp xuống 
The old building fell down after years of neglect.  
(Tòa nhà cũ đã sụp xuống sau nhiều năm bị bỏ mặc.) 
fall apart 
tan rã; hỏng hoàn toàn 
Without strong leadership, the organisation quickly fell 
apart.  
(Không có lãnh đạo mạnh mẽ, tổ chức nhanh chóng tan rã.) 
fall for 
bị lừa; phải lòng 
Many people fell for the online scam.  
(Nhiều người đã bị lừa bởi trò lừa đảo trực tuyến.) 
fall into 
rơi vào (tình trạng, 
thói quen) 
Some students fall into the habit of procrastinating during 
exams. 
(Một số sinh viên rơi vào thói quen trì hoãn trong mùa thi.) 
fall off 
giảm mạnh; rơi khỏi 
Sales fell off sharply after the price increase. 
(Doanh số giảm mạnh sau khi giá tăng.) 
fall short of 
không đạt được 
The results fell short of the company’s expectations.  
(Kết quả không đạt được kỳ vọng của công ty.) 
 
 
Trang 13

TUẦN SỐ 04 
 
VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
Cụm động từ với Call 
Cụm động từ 
Nghĩa 
Ví dụ 
call for 
yêu cầu; kêu gọi 
Environmental groups are calling for stricter regulations on 
plastic waste.  
(Các tổ chức môi trường đang kêu gọi các quy định nghiêm ngặt 
hơn về rác thải nhựa.) 
call off 
hủy bỏ 
The organisers decided to call off the outdoor concert because 
of the storm.  
(Ban tổ chức quyết định hủy buổi hòa nhạc ngoài trời vì cơn 
bão.) 
call in 
gọi điện tới; gọi ai 
tới giúp 
The company had to call in technical experts to fix the system 
failure.  
(Công ty phải gọi các chuyên gia kỹ thuật đến sửa lỗi hệ thống.) 
call back 
gọi lại 
I missed her phone call, so I will call her back later.  
(Tôi đã lỡ cuộc gọi của cô ấy nên sẽ gọi lại sau.) 
call on 
yêu cầu ai phát biểu; 
kêu gọi ai làm gì 
The professor called on several students to present their ideas.  
(Giáo sư yêu cầu một vài sinh viên trình bày ý tưởng.) 
call up 
gọi điện; gọi nhập 
ngũ 
He called up his friend to discuss the project. (Anh ấy gọi điện 
cho bạn để bàn về dự án.) 
call out 
gọi to; triệu tập; chỉ 
trích công khai 
The manager called out the employee for ignoring safety rules.  
(Quản lý đã chỉ trích nhân viên vì phớt lờ quy định an toàn.) 
 
 
Cụm động từ với Catch 
Cụm động từ 
Nghĩa 
Ví dụ 
catch on 
trở nên phổ biến 
The new eco-friendly fashion trend quickly caught on among 
young consumers. (Xu hướng thời trang thân thiện với môi 
trường nhanh chóng trở nên phổ biến với giới trẻ.) 
catch up with bắt kịp ai/cái gì 
Developing countries are trying to catch up with advanced 
economies. (Các nước đang phát triển đang cố bắt kịp các nền 
kinh tế tiên tiến.) 
catch up on 
cập nhật tin tức; làm 
bù việc bị chậm 
After returning from vacation, she spent the morning catching 
up on emails. (Sau khi trở lại từ kỳ nghỉ, cô ấy dành cả buổi 
sáng để xử lý email tồn đọng.) 
catch on to 
hiểu ra; nhận ra điều 
gì 
It took him a while to catch on to the hidden meaning of the 
joke. (Anh ấy mất một lúc mới hiểu ra ý nghĩa ẩn của câu đùa.) 
catch out 
phát hiện ai sai; bắt 
lỗi 
The teacher tried to catch out students who had not done the 
reading. (Giáo viên cố phát hiện những học sinh chưa đọc bài.) 
catch at 
cố nắm lấy; chộp lấy The child caught at his mother’s hand in fear. (Đứa trẻ vội nắm 
lấy tay mẹ vì sợ.) 
 
 
Trang 14

VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
Cụm động từ với Set 
Cụm động từ 
Nghĩa 
Ví dụ 
set up 
thành lập; thiết lập 
The entrepreneur set up a small technology company after 
graduating.  
(Doanh nhân đó đã thành lập một công ty công nghệ nhỏ sau 
khi tốt nghiệp.) 
set off 
khởi hành; làm kích 
hoạt 
The explorers set off early in the morning to avoid the heat. 
(Những nhà thám hiểm khởi hành từ sáng sớm để tránh nắng 
nóng.) 
set aside 
gạt sang một bên; 
dành dụm 
She sets aside part of her salary every month for emergencies. 
(Cô ấy dành dụm một phần lương mỗi tháng cho những trường 
hợp khẩn cấp.) 
set out 
bắt đầu với mục tiêu 
rõ ràng 
The researcher set out to investigate the causes of climate 
change.  
(Nhà nghiên cứu bắt đầu với mục tiêu tìm hiểu nguyên nhân 
của biến đổi khí hậu.) 
set about 
bắt đầu làm việc gì 
một cách nghiêm túc 
The team quickly set about solving the technical problem. 
(Nhóm nhanh chóng bắt tay vào giải quyết vấn đề kỹ thuật.) 
set back 
làm trì hoãn 
The project was set back by unexpected financial difficulties.  
(Dự án bị trì hoãn do những khó khăn tài chính bất ngờ.) 
set in 
bắt đầu và có xu 
hướng kéo dài (thời 
tiết, tình trạng) 
After several days of rain, a cold wind set in across the region.  
(Sau nhiều ngày mưa, một đợt gió lạnh bắt đầu kéo dài trong 
khu vực.) 
set apart 
làm cho khác biệt; 
tách ra 
Her dedication to social work sets her apart from many 
others.  
(Sự cống hiến cho công tác xã hội khiến cô ấy trở nên khác 
biệt với nhiều người khác.) 
 
PRACTICE 
Read the following texts and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct option that best fits 
each of the numbered blanks from 1 to 30.  
Text 1 
As environmental concerns grow, many communities are taking action to reduce plastic waste. Last year, 
a group of university students decided to (1) ______ a volunteer organisation dedicated to promoting 
sustainable living. During their weekly campaigns, they often (2) ______ reusable bags and leaflets to 
encourage eco-friendly habits. At the same time, the group continues to (3) ______ stricter regulations on 
single-use plastics.  
 
Question 1. A. give out  
B. fall through  
C. set up  
D. call for  
Question 2. A. set aside  
B. call in 
C. give out  
D. fall back on  
Question 3. A. look into  
B. call for  
C. give back  
D. set off

Last summer, a group of hikers planned a challenging expedition in the mountains. They were eager to 
(7) ______ before sunrise to avoid the intense midday heat. However, a sudden storm forced the team to 
(8) ______ the trip for safety reasons. Although their initial plan was ambitious, they realised the journey 
might (9) ______ their expectations because of the harsh conditions. Nevertheless, the hikers refused to 
(10) ______ their dream of reaching the summit someday.

Living in an extended family can offer emotional support but may also require patience and understanding.

DỄ TRÚNG TỦ & LUYỆN BỘ ĐỀ TINH HOA  
[V90.04.02B] THI ONLINE: DỰ ĐOÁN ĐẶC BIỆT  
 
CỤM ĐỘNG TỪ XUẤT HIỆN TRONG ĐỀ THI (BUỔI 3) 
Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương  
(ĐỀ VẬN DỤNG) 
 
 
Read the following texts and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct option that best fits 
each of the numbered blanks from 1 to 30.  
1. 
A new tech store was recently (1) _______ in the city, attracting many gadget lovers. To promote its grand 
opening, staff members (2) _______ flyers and discount vouchers to passersby. Thanks to its innovative 
products and excellent service, the store quickly (3) _______ among local people, becoming a popular 
destination for tech enthusiasts in just a few weeks. 
 
Question 1. A. fallen out 
B. given up 
C. set up  
D. set off  
Question 2. A. gave out  
B. called for 
C. gave in 
D. fell down  
Question 3. A. set out 
B. fell through 
C. called back 
D. caught on  
 
 
2. 
Last week, I gave my assignment (4) _______ to the teacher, hoping for a good grade. A few days later, 
she gave it (5) _______ to me with a low score, which shocked me. That’s when I realised I had (6) 
_______ my classmates in that subject. No matter how hard I tried to catch up, the gap only seemed to 
grow, making me feel more frustrated and discouraged. 
 
Question 4. A. in 
B. away 
C. off 
D. out  
Question 5. A. out 
B. off 
C. in  
D. back  
Question 6. A. given up 
B. fallen behind 
C. caught up with 
D. called off 
 
 
3. 
A charity group travelled to a remote village to cook warm meals for the locals. They (7) _______ 
volunteers and donations to support their mission. As the food simmered over open fires, it (8) _______ 
a rich, inviting aroma that brought smiles to the villagers. Once everything was ready, the team (9) 
_______ hot meals to children and the elderly, ensuring no one went hungry. 
 
Question 7. A. gave in  
B. fell down  
C. called for 
D. set up 
Question 8. A. gave away 
B. gave off  
C. gave up 
D. brought back 
Question 9. A. set out  
B. called off 
C. fell behind 
D. gave out 
 
 
Trang 18

Read the following questions and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct option that best 
fits each of the numbered blanks from 1 to 20. 
Question 1. Despite careful branding, the minimalist packaging failed to ______ with consumers seeking 
premium status. 
A. catch on 
B. call off 
C. fall behind 
D. give away 
Question 2. The negotiations ________ at the last minute, after legal counsel flagged unacceptable 
liability clauses. 
A. set aside 
B. fell through 
C. gave out 
D. called back 
Question 3. To stabilise cash flow, the founders began by _____ money for payroll during the first quarter. 
A. giving in 
B. setting out 
C. setting aside 
D. falling apart 
Question 4. During the crisis, the charity decided to ______ emergency rations to displaced families 
within forty-eight hours. 
A. call for 
B. give out 
C. catch out 
D. set off 
Question 5. To avoid prolonged conflict, he chose to ______ and accept the compromise proposed by the 
mediator. 
A. fall for 
B. give in 
C. set up 
D. catch on to 
Question 6. The professor’s ambiguous wording was designed to ______ anyone who relied on 
memorised definitions alone. 
A. fall down 
B. call in 
C. catch out 
D. set apart 
Question 7. After the corporate restructuring, several junior staff began to ______, missing deadlines and 
feedback cycles. 
A. fall behind 
B. give back 
C. call up 
D. set about 
Question 8. After weeks abroad, she spent Monday morning trying to ______ policy updates and internal 
memos. 
A. call out 
B. catch up on 
C. set off 
D. fall into 
Question 9. In the post-crisis review, the board urged executives to ______ a clear roadmap for risk 
management. 
A. give away 
B. set out 
C. fall short of 
D. call back 
Question 10. The new compliance framework may ______ stricter auditing across supply chains 
notorious for opaque labour practices. 
A. set in 
B. call for 
C. give off 
D. fall back on 
Question 11. To scale rapidly, the entrepreneur decided to ______ a lean operation with specialised roles 
and transparent KPIs. 
A. fall through 
B. set up 
C. catch on 
D. give out

Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương  
 
 
Read the following texts and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct option that best fits 
each of the numbered blanks from 1 to 30.  
Text 1 
Many working women feel guilty when they cannot finish all the household chores. After a long day at 
work, they still try to cook, clean, and take care of their families. This (1) _____ can make them feel tired 
and stressed. Over time, the stress may harm their physical and mental health. Experts say that family 
members should share the housework more (2) _____. When responsibilities are shared, women can (3) 
_____ more and feel less pressure in their daily lives. 
 
Question 1. A. pressure  
B. energy  
C. ability  
D. method 
Question 2. A. quickly  
B. fairly  
C. carefully  
D. strongly 
Question 3. A. operate 
B. control 
C. rest  
D. pause 
 
 
Text 2 
In many traditional homes, there is still a clear gender gap in daily responsibilities. Women are often (4) 
_____ to cook, clean, and take care of children, even if they also have full-time jobs. Meanwhile, men 
may spend less time doing housework. This unequal (5) _____ of work can create stress and unfair 
pressure on women. Experts say families should encourage shared responsibilities at home. When both 
men and women help with chores and childcare, family life becomes more (6) _____, and everyone can 
enjoy a healthier and more supportive home environment. 
 
Question 4. A. forced  
B. allowed  
C. expected  
D. trained  
Question 5. A. result  
B. amount  
C. policy  
D. division  
Question 6. A. peaceful  
B. balanced  
C. justified  
D. innovative 
 
 
Text 3 
In recent years, many people have begun to adopt ‘low contact’ relationships with certain family members. 
Instead of completely cutting (7) _____, they choose to limit communication and reduce emotional 
involvement. This trend often appears when family interactions cause constant stress, conflict, or 
criticism. By creating healthy (8) _____, individuals try to protect their mental well-being while still 
maintaining a basic connection. However, low contact relationships can also lead to feelings of (9) _____ 
and misunderstanding within families. Experts suggest that open communication and (10) _____ respect 
are important if families hope to rebuild trust and create more supportive relationships over time.

Teaching children that they have the ability to say no is an important part of building confidence and 
independence. When young people understand that they are allowed to refuse pressure, they develop 
stronger decision-making skills. Instead of agreeing to everything out of fear or (11) _____, they begin to 
think carefully about their values and (12) _____. This sense of personal control helps them recognise 
unhealthy situations and avoid harmful (13) _____. As a result, their ‘yes’ becomes more meaningful 
because it reflects (14) _____ choice rather than pressure. By encouraging respectful refusal, parents (15) 
_____ children to make thoughtful decisions and build healthier relationships.

Australia’s decision to restrict children’s access to social media has (16) _____ debate, but it may also 
bring unexpected benefits for families. Without constant online (17) _____, children have more 
opportunities to spend time with parents and siblings. Family conversations, shared meals, and outdoor 
activities may become more common again. Many millennials often say they miss the slower, more 
connected childhood they experienced before smartphones (18) _____ daily life. This policy could allow 
today’s children to experience similar (19) _____ of real-life interaction. By encouraging offline 
communication, families may (20) _____ emotional bonds and help young people develop healthier 
relationships within the home environment. 
 
Question 16. A. exposed  
B. sparked  
C. delivered  
D. constructed

B. movements  
C. directions  
D. reactions

B. dominated  
C. assisted  
D. prepared

D. collect

Taking eight months of paternity leave has reshaped the way I connect with my children. Previously, my 
(21) _____ workload meant I often witnessed their lives only in brief moments after work. Now, being

preparing meals, or discussing their worries before bedtime. Gradually, these shared experiences have 
deepened our trust and emotional (24) _____. I have also realised that caregiving requires patience,

it increasingly difficult for many graduates to maintain financial (27) _____. As a result, moving back 
home has become a practical, though sometimes uncomfortable, solution. While some critics view this 
trend as a sign of (28) _____  dependence, others argue that it reflects broader economic pressures rather 
than personal failure. For many families, this arrangement requires (29) _____ boundaries and 
expectations. When handled with mutual understanding, multigenerational living can strengthen family

Text 1 
In the past, women were often (1) _______ to stay at home and take care of the family. They were 
responsible for cooking, cleaning, and raising children. However, today, many women have careers and 
contribute to the family income. As a result, the (2) _______ of household chores has become more equal 
between men and women. When responsibilities are shared, family life becomes more (3) _______. This 
change helps reduce pressure on women and creates a more supportive and harmonious family 
environment for everyone.

B. allowed  
C. trained  
D. expected   
Question 2. A. division 
B. amount  
C. policy  
D. result  
Question 3. A. peaceful

C. justified

Text 2 
Family members should share household chores (4) _______ according to their age and ability. For 
example, fathers may repair things or clean the yard, mothers can cook meals or do the laundry, and 
children can wash the dishes or tidy their rooms. When chores are divided, no one feels too much (5) 
_______. Instead, everyone can support one another and feel that their contribution is meaningful. 
Moreover, sharing responsibilities allows each person to have more time to (6) _______ and relax after 
daily activities together. 
 
Question 4. A. quickly  
B. fairly  
C. carefully  
D. strongly 
Question 5. A. pressure  
B. energy  
C. ability  
D. method 
Question 6. A. operate 
B. control 
C. rest  
D. pause 
 
 
Text 3 
In modern life, family members sometimes seem to interact less with one another. However, this does not 
mean that family (7) _______ are weakening. Many people are simply busy with work, study, and 
personal responsibilities, so they need certain (8) _______ in their daily lives. This may create some (9) 
_______, but the connection within the family can still remain strong. What truly matters is the sense of 
(10) _______ respect, understanding, and support among family members. Even if they do not spend a 
lot of time together, their emotional bond can still be meaningful and lasting.

Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương  
 
 
Read the following text and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct option that best fits 
each of the numbered blanks from 1 to 6.  
In an age defined by digital connectivity, surveillance has quietly become embedded in everyday 
family life. Smartphone applications now allow parents to monitor their children’s locations in real 
time, while couples sometimes share tracking features as a form of (1) _____. Supporters argue that 
such tools enhance safety and provide peace of mind in an increasingly unpredictable world. Yet the 
normalisation of constant monitoring raises important (2) _____ questions within families.  
Tracking a child or partner may begin as an act of care, but it can gradually (3) _____ the boundary 
between protection and control. When individuals know that their movements are constantly observed, 
their sense of autonomy may be subtly (4) _____. Trust, which should form the foundation of family 
relationships, risks being replaced by quiet suspicion and digital (5) _____.   
Moreover, reliance on surveillance may discourage open communication. Instead of asking where 
someone has been or how they feel, people may simply check an app. In the long run, families must 
ask whether technological supervision truly strengthens relationships, or whether (6) _____ trust 
requires a willingness to accept uncertainty. 
 
Question 1. A. hesitation  
B. reassurance 
C. confusion 
D. opposition  
Question 2. A. ethical  
B. economic  
C. agricultural  
D. mechanical  
Question 3. A. assemble  
B. avert 
C. amplify 
D. blur 
Question 4. A. celebrated  
B. restored  
C. compounded 
D. undermined 
Question 5. A. oversight  
B. nutrition  
C. recreation  
D. embellishment  
Question 6. A. accidental  
B. fleeting 
C. genuine  
D. fictional 
 
Read the following text and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct option that best fits 
each of the numbered blanks from 7 to 12.  
The rise of the ‘boomerang generation’ has transformed many family homes into spaces where 
adulthood and childhood unexpectedly (7) ______. Increasing housing costs, precarious employment, 
and (8) ______ student debt have compelled numerous young adults to return to their parents’ homes 
after attempting independent living. In many cases, they move back into the very bedrooms they once 
occupied as teenagers, surrounded by old posters, school trophies, and fragments of an earlier identity.  
This arrangement can produce a curious emotional (9) ______. For the returning adult, the 
familiar room may (10) ______ comfort, yet it also serves as a reminder of postponed independence. 
Parents, meanwhile, must renegotiate household boundaries with children who are no longer 
adolescents but not entirely autonomous either.

TUẦN SỐ 04 
 
VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
TÀI LIỆU LIVESTREAM ĐỘC QUYỀN 
VIP 90 (2026): TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM BỘ CÂU HỎI  
DỄ TRÚNG TỦ & LUYỆN BỘ ĐỀ TINH HOA  
[V90.04.04] TINH HOA ĐỌC HIỂU (BUỔI 7) 
 
 
Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương  
LEVEL 1 
 
 
Text 1 
Many parents today try to track their teenagers’ online activities to keep them safe. The internet 
offers many useful tools for learning and communication, but it also exposes young people to risks such 
as harmful content, cyberbullying, and online scams. Because of these dangers, some parents use apps 
that allow them to check their children’s messages, websites, or screen time.  
Supporters believe this kind of monitoring helps protect teenagers from problems they may not yet 
understand. Parents can guide them and step in if something worrying happens online. However, critics 
argue that too much control may damage trust between parents and teenagers. Young people may feel that 
their privacy is not respected.  
Experts suggest that open communication is the best approach. Instead of only using technology 
to watch their children, parents should talk with them about responsible internet use. When teenagers 
understand the risks and feel trusted, they are more likely to make safe choices online. 
 
Question 1. The Internet exposes young people to all of the risks EXCEPT _____.  
A. online scams  
B. harmful content  
C. cyberbullying  
D. physical injury 
Question 2. The word “them” in paragraph 1 refers to _____. 
A. parents 
B. dangers 
C. apps 
D. teenagers 
Question 3. The phrase “step in” in paragraph 2 mostly means ______. 
A. share 
B. help 
C. cure 
D. treat 
Question 4. What do experts advise parents to do?  
A. Strictly limit teenagers’ internet access.  
B. Depend mainly on tracking apps.  
C. Discuss responsible internet use openly.  
D. Completely avoid social media discussions. 
 
 
 
Trang 31

TUẦN SỐ 04 
 
VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
LEVEL 2 
 
 
Text 2 
The “club sandwich” generation refers to adults who care for both younger and older family 
members at the same time. Many people in this group are raising children while also supporting their 
ageing parents, and sometimes even their grandparents. As a result, they often have to juggle many 
responsibilities in their daily lives.  
These adults may spend their days working, helping their children with school, and checking on 
elderly relatives who need medical care or emotional support. This situation can create stress, as they try 
to meet everyone’s needs while managing their own work and personal life. 
However, many people in the club sandwich generation also find meaning in these roles. Caring 
for different generations can strengthen family connections and teach valuable lessons about patience and 
responsibility. Even though the challenges are inevitable, many families learn to share tasks and support 
one another. With understanding and cooperation, it is possible to manage these duties while 
maintaining healthy family relationships. 
 
Question 5. The word “juggle” in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to ______. 
A. refuse 
B. balance 
C. reject 
D. maintain 
Question 6. The “club sandwich” generation have to do all of the following tasks EXCEPT ______.  
A. supporting elderly relatives  
B. helping children with schoolwork  
C. managing daily work responsibilities  
D. organising international travel 
Question 7. As stated in paragraph 3, looking after relatives ______.  
A. will reduce personal time and social freedom  
B. often requires careful planning and patience  
C. can bring personal and family value  
D. always causes pressure in daily routines 
Question 8. The word “inevitable” in paragraph 3 is OPPOSITE in meaning to _____.  
A. preventive 
B. regular 
C. normal 
D. avoidable 
Question 9. Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence in paragraph 3? 
With understanding and cooperation, it is possible to manage these duties while maintaining 
healthy family relationships. 
A. Mutual understanding and cooperation make it possible to handle responsibilities while preserving 
healthy family relationships.  
B. Mutual understanding and cooperation may maintain healthy family relationships while responsibilities 
are gradually reduced.  
C. Mutual understanding and cooperation encourage strong family relationships that eventually lessen 
caregiving responsibilities.  
D. Mutual understanding and cooperation help maintain healthy family relationships that often lead to 
fewer family duties. 
 
 
 
Trang 32

TUẦN SỐ 04 
 
VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
LEVEL 3 
 
 
Text 3 
Growing up as an only child can shape a person’s experiences in unique ways. Without siblings, 
an only child often spends more time interacting with adults, which may encourage maturity and strong 
communication skills from an early age. Many only children develop a rich inner world, enjoying 
activities such as reading, creative hobbies, or independent exploration.  
At the same time, being an only child can present certain challenges. Without brothers or sisters 
to share everyday experiences, some children may occasionally feel lonely. They may also face greater 
expectations from parents, who invest most of their attention and hopes in a single child. As a result, only 
children sometimes feel pressure to succeed academically or socially.   
However, these circumstances can also foster independence and self-reliance. Only children often 
learn to manage their time and responsibilities on their own. In many cases, they form strong friendships 
that provide companionship similar to sibling relationships.   
Ultimately, being an only child is neither an advantage nor a disadvantage in itself. Much depends 
on the family environment, parenting style, and opportunities for social interaction that help shape a 
child’s development. 
 
Question 10. As stated in paragraph 1, interaction with adults may help only children ______. 
A. rely heavily on adult guidance and daily support  
B. prefer spending time mainly with older people  
C. reduce opportunities for childhood social experiences  
D. develop maturity and communication abilities 
Question 11. Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence in paragraph 2? 
Without brothers or sisters to share everyday experiences, some children may occasionally feel 
lonely. 
A. Occasional loneliness among children may encourage the absence of siblings in daily life. 
B. Feelings of loneliness sometimes lead children to spend everyday experiences without siblings.  
C. The absence of siblings may occasionally cause some children to experience loneliness.  
D. Children’s occasional loneliness may shape their everyday experiences with siblings. 
Question 12. According to paragraph 2, only children may feel pressure because ______.  
A. they must often solve personal problems independently  
B. they spend too much time interacting with adults  
C. parents place many expectations on them  
D. they prefer focusing mainly on academic success 
Question 13. The word “companionship” in paragraph 3 is OPPOSITE in meaning to _____. 
A. connection 
B. maturity 
C. comparison 
D. isolation 
Question 14. According to paragraph 4, the effects of being an only child depend largely on _____.  
A. personality and personal ambitions  
B. education and academic ability  
C. friendships and social confidence  
D. upbringing and environment 
 
 
 
 
 
Trang 33

TUẦN SỐ 04 
 
VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
LEVEL 4 
 
 
Text 4 
The approach of a child’s eighteenth birthday is often framed as a celebration of independence, 
yet it can evoke unexpected melancholy in parents. For one mother, the moment felt strangely 
disorienting: the same person still lived in the house, yet something fundamental had shifted. [I] Legally 
and symbolically, her son had crossed the threshold into adulthood, transforming a familiar relationship 
into one governed by new boundaries.  
[II] Parenting once consisted of constant urgency—toddlers in arms, daily chaos and an endless 
rush of responsibilities. At the time, people warned that these years would pass quickly, advice that 
seemed absurd amid exhaustion. Yet, looking back, the long stretch of childhood now appears to have 
vanished with startling abruptness.   
Adulthood brings a subtle renegotiation of authority. Choices that once belonged naturally to the 
parent—diet, appearance or minor habits—are no longer theirs to regulate. Even the intricate social world 
that surrounded childhood, including friendships with other parents, begins to dissolve as the young 
person’s autonomy takes precedence.  
Ultimately, the most difficult adjustment is psychological rather than practical. [III] A parent may 
acknowledge that their child is an adult, yet believing it requires relinquishing the instinct to guide, correct 
or intervene. Accepting that independence—recognising that the person you raised now belongs to their 
own life—marks a quiet but profound transition in the emotional landscape of parenthood. [IV]  
 
Question 15. The word “disorienting” in paragraph 1 mostly means ______.  
A. unofficial 
B. unsettling 
C. encouraging 
D. severe 
Question 16. Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence in paragraph 2? 
Yet, looking back, the long stretch of childhood now appears to have vanished with startling 
abruptness.   
A. The surprising disappearance of childhood now makes earlier years appear longer in retrospect.   
B. Childhood now seems longer because its sudden disappearance is clearer when looking back.   
C. Looking back, the sudden disappearance of childhood makes earlier years feel unexpectedly extended. 
D. In retrospect, the years of childhood now seem to have disappeared with surprising suddenness.  
Question 17. Which of the following best summarises paragraph 3? 
A. Adulthood shifts authority from parents to children, while childhood social networks gradually fade.  
B. As children mature, parents increasingly guide decisions about diet, appearance and everyday habits.  
C. Adulthood encourages parents to maintain authority while preserving social connections formed during 
childhood.  
D. When children become adults, parental authority strengthens and childhood social networks expand 
further. 
Question 18. Which of the following can be inferred from the passage? 
A. Parents find their child’s eighteenth birthday emotionally easy because independence brings relief.   
B. Becoming eighteen allows young adults to maintain childhood relationships with their parents’ close 
friends.   
C. Parents may struggle emotionally to accept their child’s independence even after reaching adulthood.  
D. A large number of parents gradually lose contact with their children once they legally become adults. 
 
Trang 34

A. [III]

Shared By Fanpage: Tài Liệu Khóa Học UniMap
TUẦN SỐ 04 
 
VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
TÀI LIỆU LIVESTREAM ĐỘC QUYỀN 
VIP 90 (2026): TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM BỘ CÂU HỎI  
DỄ TRÚNG TỦ & LUYỆN BỘ ĐỀ TINH HOA  
[V90.04.04B] THI ONLINE: TINH HOA ĐỌC HIỂU  
 
(BUỔI 7) – ĐỀ VẬN DỤNG 
Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương  
 
 
LEVEL 1 
Text 1 
The generation gap in modern families is mainly caused by several social and cultural changes. One 
important cause is the difference in life experiences between parents and children. Parents often grew up 
in a different environment with different values, while young people are influenced by modern lifestyles. 
Another cause is the rapid development of technology. Teenagers spend a lot of time using the internet 
and social media, which shapes their opinions and behavior in new ways. Parents, however, may not 
always understand these digital habits. In addition, differences in expectations about education, career 
choices, and personal freedom can also create disagreements between parents and their children. These 
factors together contribute to the generation gap in many modern families. 
 
Question 1. The word “their” in the text refers to ______. 
A. teenagers  
 
B. social media 
 
C. opinions  
 
D. modern lifestyles 
Question 2. The word “disagreements” in the text is closest in meaning to _______. 
A. viewpoints 
B. disputes 
C. objectives 
D. efforts  
Question 3. Which of the following is NOT mentioned in the passage as a cause of the generation gap? 
A. Differences in life experiences 
B. Modern technology 
C. Different expectations 
 
D. Parents' digital habits 
 
Text 2 
What keeps a family close in everyday life? Surprisingly, it is often the small and simple habits. Having 
dinner together, for example, gives everyone a chance to talk about their day. Even a short conversation 
can help family members understand each other better. Another habit is helping with small tasks at home. 
When people share chores, they also share moments of laughter and cooperation. Spending a little time 
together, such as watching a film or going for a walk, can also bring people closer. These actions may 
look ordinary, but they help build trust and connection. After all, strong family relationships are often 
created not by big events, but by many small moments shared every day. 
Question 4. According to the text, even a short conversation can help family members _______. 
A. share moments of laughter 
B. understand each other better  
C. talk about their day 
 
D. build trust and connection 
Question 5. The word “they” in the text refers to _______. 
A. people  
B. chores 
C. moments  
D. small tasks  
Question 6. The word “ordinary” in the text is OPPOSITE in meaning to _______. 
A. satisfied 
B. normal 
C. adaptable 
D. exceptional 
 
Trang 36

TUẦN SỐ 04 
 
VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
LEVEL 2 
Text 3 
Parents often give advice because they care about their children and want them to make the right decisions. 
They may share their experiences and offer guidance about school, friendships, or future plans. In many 
cases, this advice is helpful and comes from a place of love. However, when parents give advice too 
frequently, teenagers may begin to feel that their opinions are not respected. Instead of feeling supported, 
they might feel controlled or misunderstood. 
As a result, teenagers may stop sharing their thoughts with their parents. They may avoid conversations 
or prefer discussing their problems with friends instead. Over time, this can create emotional distance 
within the family. Rather than giving teens constant advice, parents had better listen carefully and 
ask questions. When teenagers feel heard and respected, they are more likely to open up and accept 
guidance naturally. 
 
Question 7. According to paragraph 1, why do parents often give advice to their children? 
A. They prefer giving advice instead of listening to their children’s opinions. 
B. They believe teenagers cannot make any decisions without their control. 
C. They want to avoid talking about school, friendships, and future plans. 
D. They care about their children and want them to make the right decisions.  
Question 8. The word “they” in paragraph 1 refers to ______. 
A. parents  
B. teenagers  
C. opinions  
D. many cases 
Question 9. Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence in paragraph 2? 
Rather than giving teens constant advice, parents had better listen carefully and ask questions. 
A. Parents should listen and ask questions rather than always advising their teenage children. 
B. Parents should give teenagers more advice so that they can understand their problems better. 
C. Teenagers should stop listening to their parents if they receive too much advice. 
D. Parents should avoid discussing teenagers’ problems because advice often causes conflicts. 
Question 10. The word “accept” in paragraph 2 refers to _______. 
A. manage 
B. communicate 
C. consider 
D. embrace 
 
 
Text 4 
In recent decades, the roles of family members have undergone a significant transformation. Traditional 
stereotypes once suggested that men should act as the primary breadwinners while women were expected 
to focus mainly on domestic responsibilities. However, social and economic changes have gradually 
challenged these assumptions. Increasing access to education and career opportunities has enabled many 
women to pursue professional ambitions, while men have become more involved in childcare and 
household duties. Today, the rigid boundaries that once defined family roles are becoming less 
distinct. 
This shift reflects a broader change in how society views responsibility and partnership within families. 
Rather than following fixed expectations based on gender, many modern families now prioritize 
cooperation and shared decision-making. Couples often divide responsibilities according to their 
circumstances, abilities, and personal preferences. Consequently, traditional stereotypes about “male” and 
“female” roles are gradually fading, making space for more flexible and balanced family dynamics in 
contemporary society.

During adolescence, a noticeable shift often occurs in young people’s communication patterns: 
conversations with friends gradually replace the frequent exchanges they once had with their parents. 
From a psychological perspective, this tendency is closely linked to the process of identity formation. 
Adolescents are actively exploring their values, preferences, and social identities. In this transitional 
phase, peers become important mirrors through which teenagers evaluate themselves and experiment with 
different ways of thinking and behaving. 
Equally important is the emotional environment that peer relationships provide. Interactions among 
friends are usually characterized by a sense of equality and shared experiences. Teenagers often feel that 
their peers face similar pressures. [I] In contrast, communication with parents may unintentionally involve 
guidance, evaluation, or moral expectations, which can make adolescents more cautious about expressing 
their thoughts openly. [II] 
Nevertheless, this preference for peer communication does not necessarily imply emotional distance from 
parents. [III] Rather, it reflects a developmental process in which adolescents negotiate greater 
psychological independence. When parents remain patient, empathetic, and willing to listen without 
immediate judgment, teenagers are more likely to preserve a meaningful and supportive relationship with 
them. [IV]

TÀI LIỆU LIVESTREAM ĐỘC QUYỀN 
VIP 90 (2026): TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM BỘ CÂU HỎI  
DỄ TRÚNG TỦ & LUYỆN BỘ ĐỀ TINH HOA  
[V90.04.04A] THI ONLINE: TINH HOA ĐỌC HIỂU  
 
(BUỔI 7) – ĐỀ NÂNG CAO 
Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương  
 
 
Read the following passage and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct answer to each of 
the questions from 1 to 8. 
In many developed societies, leaving home has traditionally been regarded as a defining step 
towards adulthood. Young people move away from their parents, pursue higher education or employment, 
and establish independent households. However, this pattern has become less predictable in recent years. 
A growing number of young adults now return to live with their parents after a period of independence, a 
trend often described as the “boomerang generation.” Instead of permanently leaving home, they depart 
for a time and later come back, sometimes unexpectedly.  
For many members of this generation, returning home is not necessarily a personal failure but 
rather a response to economic pressures. Rising housing costs, unstable employment, and the burden of 
student debt have made independent living increasingly difficult for young adults. As a result, the parental 
home becomes a temporary refuge where they can reduce expenses and regain financial stability. In some 
cases, moving back allows them to save money while searching for better career opportunities or 
planning their next stage in life.  
Yet living again in childhood bedrooms can create complex emotional dynamics. Adult children 
may struggle with feelings of dependence or frustration, particularly if they once enjoyed the freedom of 
living alone. At the same time, parents who had adjusted to an “empty nest” must renegotiate household 
routines and expectations. Everyday matters such as privacy, responsibilities, and personal boundaries 
can become sources of tension as both generations attempt to adapt to a shared living arrangement.  
Despite these challenges, the phenomenon also reveals changing definitions of adulthood. Rather 
than a straightforward transition, adulthood today often unfolds gradually, shaped by economic 
uncertainty and shifting social norms. For some families, the return of adult children strengthens 
relationships and provides mutual support during difficult periods. In this sense, the boomerang generation 
illustrates how modern family life continues to evolve alongside broader social and economic 
transformations. 
 
Question 1. The word “depart” in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to _____. 
A. avoid 
B. vanish 
C. leave 
D. escape 
Question 2. All of the following factors make independent living increasingly difficult for young adults 
EXCEPT _____.  
A. unstable employment  
 
B. student debt 
C. rising housing costs  
 
D. family expectations

considering improved career prospects or future plans.  
D. At times, preparations for the next stage of life and the search for improved career prospects lead young 
adults to accumulate savings after moving back home.

A. feelings 
B. dynamics 
C. adult children 
D. parents

B. complex 
C. normal 
D. unusual

A. Returning home reflects young adults’ unwillingness to pursue higher education or employment.  
B. Parents welcome returning adult children without adjusting household routines or expectations.  
C. Living again with parents typically eliminates emotional tensions between adult children and parents. 
D. Economic pressures have encouraged many young adults to return to the parental home.  
Question 7. Which paragraph mentions conflicts arising from daily household matters? 
A. Paragraph 1 
B. Paragraph 2 
C. Paragraph 3 
D. Paragraph 4 
Question 8. Which paragraph mentions the traditional view of leaving home as a sign of adulthood? 
A. Paragraph 1 
B. Paragraph 2 
C. Paragraph 3 
D. Paragraph 4 
Read the following passage and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct answer to each of 
the questions from 9 to 18. 
For years, public discourse has been dominated by debates about Millennials and Generation Z, 
often portrayed as disruptive forces reshaping social norms and cultural expectations. Yet another cohort 
is quietly emerging behind them: Generation Alpha. Generally defined as children born from the early 
2010s onward, this group represents the first generation to be born entirely in the twenty-first century. [I] 
Many of them are the children of Millennials, meaning they are growing up in households already shaped 
by rapid technological change and shifting economic realities.  
What distinguishes Generation Alpha most clearly is the environment into which they have been 
born. Unlike previous generations that gradually adapted to digital technology, these children are 
immersed in it from the very beginning of their lives. Smartphones, streaming platforms, voice assistants, 
and algorithm-driven content are not innovations to be discovered but everyday elements of their 
childhood. As a result, their patterns of learning, entertainment, and communication are deeply 
intertwined with digital media. Researchers often describe them as the most technologically integrated 
generation yet, having never experienced a world without constant connectivity.  
However, the rise of Generation Alpha raises broader questions about how childhood itself is 
evolving. Education systems are increasingly incorporating digital tools and online platforms into 
everyday learning, reshaping traditional classroom experiences. [II] At the same time, critics express 
concern about the consequences of prolonged screen exposure, which some studies associate with reduced 
physical activity and shifting social habits among children. [III]

Beyond technology, Generation Alpha is also growing up during a period of profound global 
transformation. Climate change, economic uncertainty, and rapid scientific innovation form the backdrop 
to their early lives. Demographers suggest that this generation may become one of the most highly 
educated and technologically equipped cohorts in history, benefiting from unprecedented access to 
information and learning resources. [IV] Yet the same conditions that enable these opportunities also 
demand adaptability, resilience, and critical thinking. In many ways, Generation Alpha may ultimately 
redefine how society understands childhood, education, and the transition into adulthood in an 
increasingly complex world.

put back 
hoãn lại; đặt lại chỗ cũ 
The match was put back because of heavy rain.  
(Trận đấu bị hoãn lại vì mưa lớn.) 
put down 
ghi lại; đặt xuống 
She put down his phone number in her notebook.  
(Cô ấy ghi số điện thoại của anh ta vào sổ.) 
put down to 
cho là do 
The failure was put down to poor planning.  
(Thất bại được cho là do kế hoạch kém.) 
put across 
truyền đạt rõ ràng 
The teacher put across the idea very effectively.  
(Giáo viên truyền đạt ý tưởng rất hiệu quả.) 
put together 
lắp ráp; ghép lại 
He put together the furniture in less than an hour.  
(Anh ấy lắp ráp đồ nội thất trong chưa đến một giờ.) 
put through 
khiến ai trải qua điều khó 
khăn 
The team was put through an intense training 
programme.  
(Đội đã phải trải qua chương trình huấn luyện rất 
khắc nghiệt.) 
 
CỤM ĐỘNG TỪ VỚI TURN 
Cụm động từ 
Nghĩa 
Ví dụ 
turn on / switch on 
bật (thiết bị) 
She turned on the lights when the room became dark. 
(Cô ấy bật đèn khi căn phòng trở nên tối.) 
turn off / switch off 
tắt (thiết bị); làm ai mất 
hứng 
Please turn off the television before going to bed.  
(Hãy tắt tivi trước khi đi ngủ.) 
turn down 
từ chối; vặn nhỏ 
She turned down the job offer because the salary 
was too low.  
(Cô ấy từ chối lời mời làm việc vì lương quá thấp.) 
turn up 
xuất hiện; vặn to 
Many students turned up late for the lecture.  
(Nhiều sinh viên xuất hiện muộn trong buổi giảng.) 
turn into 
biến thành 
The quiet town turned into a busy tourist destination.  
(Thị trấn yên tĩnh đã biến thành điểm du lịch nhộn 
nhịp.) 
turn out 
hóa ra là; có mặt tại một 
sự kiện 
The story turned out to be completely true.  
(Câu chuyện hóa ra hoàn toàn là sự thật.) 
A vast crowd turned out to watch the procession.  
(Một đám đông khổng lồ đã đổ ra xem đám rước.) 
turn back 
quay lại 
Because of the storm, the hikers had to turn back.  
(Vì cơn bão, những người leo núi phải quay lại.) 
turn around 
quay lại; xoay chuyển 
tình thế 
The company managed to turn around its financial 
situation.  
(Công ty đã xoay chuyển được tình hình tài chính.) 
turn to somebody / 
something 
tìm đến ai/cái gì để được 
giúp đỡ 
In difficult times, she turned to her family for 
support.  
(Trong lúc khó khăn, cô ấy tìm đến gia đình để được 
giúp đỡ.)

turn in

CỤM ĐỘNG TỪ VỚI CUT/ RUN 
Cụm động từ 
Nghĩa 
Ví dụ 
cut down 
chặt (cây)

cut down on / cut 
back on

(Bác sĩ khuyên anh ấy nên giảm lượng đường.) 
cut off 
cắt đứt; ngắt (điện, 
nước, liên lạc)

cut out 
cắt bỏ; loại bỏ 
You should cut out unhealthy snacks from your diet.

độ ăn.) 
cut in 
xen vào (cuộc nói 
chuyện) 
She kept cutting in while I was explaining the

run into 
tình cờ gặp; gặp phải 
vấn đề 
I ran into my old teacher at the supermarket yesterday.  
(Hôm qua tôi tình cờ gặp giáo viên cũ ở siêu thị.) 
run after 
chạy đuổi theo 
The dog ran after the ball across the field.  
(Con chó chạy đuổi theo quả bóng qua cánh đồng.) 
run out of /  
run out 
cạn kiệt, hết 
We ran out of water during the trip.  
(Chúng tôi đã hết nước trong chuyến đi.) 
run away 
chạy trốn 
The thief ran away when the police arrived.  
(Tên trộm chạy trốn khi cảnh sát đến.) 
run through 
xem nhanh, thảo luận 
nhanh

note down

drop out of

drop in on

face up to

find out

Scientists are trying to find out the cause of the disease.

throw away

clean up

scare away

use up 
sử dụng hết 
We used up all the milk this morning.

switch to

get around

rinse out

pass down / hand

does not come from studying endlessly. Taking short breaks and spending time with friends can help 
students relax and maintain motivation. In fact, many teenagers like to (3) ______ with classmates after 
school, which allows them to share ideas, support one another, and return to their studies with a clearer 
mind. 
Question 1. A. carry out  
B. drop out of

D. note down

D. brush up on

habits. Local governments are urging households to (4) ______ renewable energy sources such as solar 
and wind power instead of relying on fossil fuels. At the same time, people are encouraged to (5) ______ 
unnecessary energy consumption by turning off lights and using public transport more often. Another 
important step is to recycle materials rather than simply (6) _____ them _____ after use. Small changes 
in everyday behaviour can make a significant difference to the environment over time.

TÀI LIỆU LIVESTREAM ĐỘC QUYỀN 
VIP 90 (2026): TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM BỘ CÂU HỎI  
DỄ TRÚNG TỦ & LUYỆN BỘ ĐỀ TINH HOA  
[V90.04.05B] THI ONLINE: DỰ ĐOÁN ĐẶC BIỆT  
 
CỤM ĐỘNG TỪ XUẤT HIỆN TRONG ĐỀ THI (BUỔI 4) 
Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương  
(ĐỀ VẬN DỤNG) 
 
 
Read the following texts and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct option that best fits 
each of the numbered blanks from 1 to 30.  
1. 
Yesterday, I planned to (1) _______ my grandmother for a surprise visit. However, I left home late and 
saw my bus leaving. I tried to (2) _______ it, but it was too fast. Frustrated, I waited for the next one and 
finally arrived at her house. She laughed and said I (3) _______ my dad—always running late! Despite 
the delay, we had a wonderful time together, chatting and enjoying tea. 
 
Question 1. A. take part in 
B. drop in on  
C. put up with 
D. cut down on 
Question 2. A. rinse out 
B. turn out 
C. pick up  
D. run after  
Question 3. A. hung out 
B. threw away 
C. used up 
D. took after 
 
 
2. 
Yesterday, my younger brother shocked everyone by saying he had (4) _______ university. He claimed 
that the tough exams and boring lectures had completely put him (5) _______ studying. Our parents 
panicked, trying to convince him to reconsider. However, it (6) _______ to be just an April Fool’s joke! 
He burst out laughing while we all sighed in relief. That was his best prank yet, but we won’t fall for it 
next year! 
 
Question 4. A. lived up to 
B. dropped out of  
C. cut down on 
D. dropped in on 
Question 5. A. off  
B. up 
C. on 
D. out 
Question 6. A. took off  
B. waited on 
C. turned out  
D. got around 
 
 
3. 
Wanting to live a healthier life, I decided to (7) _______ a balanced diet with more vegetables and less 
junk food. At the same time, I (8) _______ running to improve my stamina and overall fitness. To build 
strength, I also started to (9) _______ at the gym three times a week. After a few months, I felt more 
energetic and confident. 
 
Question 7. A. put off 
B. switch to 
C. use up 
D. turn into  
Question 8. A. took after  
B. brought about 
C. turned out 
D. took up 
Question 9. A. run out  
B. put up 
C. work out  
D. turn up

DỄ TRÚNG TỦ & LUYỆN BỘ ĐỀ TINH HOA  
[V90.04.05A] THI ONLINE: DỰ ĐOÁN ĐẶC BIỆT  
 
CỤM ĐỘNG TỪ XUẤT HIỆN TRONG ĐỀ THI (BUỔI 4) 
Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương  
(ĐỀ NÂNG CAO) 
 
 
Read the following questions and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct option that best 
fits each of the numbered blanks from 1 to 20. 
Question 1. Despite mounting criticism, the minister refused to ______ his controversial remarks during 
the press conference. 
A. take back 
B. turn into 
C. cut down on 
D. run into 
Question 2. The research team must carefully ______ the experimental procedure before drawing 
conclusions about the unexpected results. 
A. throw away 
B. run through 
C. hang out 
D. pass away 
Question 3. When the company expanded overseas, a regional director was appointed to _______ 
operations in Southeast Asia. 
A. cut off 
B. turn down 
C. take over 
D. pick up 
Question 4. Before travelling to Spain for a research project, the student decided to ______ his Spanish.  
A. note down  
B. run into  
C. brush up on  
D. turn down 
Question 5. To reduce household waste, many cities encourage residents to ______ unnecessary 
packaging and disposable products. 
A. cut out 
B. take after 
C. run into 
D. pass down 
Question 6. During the conference, junior researchers were encouraged to ______ their observations for 
later analysis. 
A. brush up on 
B. take over 
C. note down 
D. run after 
Question 7. After several months of intense training, the athletes finally managed to ______ a strategy 
capable of defeating rivals. 
A. turn down 
B. work out 
C. run after 
D. throw away 
Question 8. The engineer carefully ______ the malfunctioning engine to understand why the aircraft had 
suddenly failed mid-flight. 
A. ran into 
B. put aside 
C. took apart 
D. switched to 
Question 9. Scientists are trying to _______ the causes of the sudden decline in several endangered 
wildlife species.  
A. turn down  
B. find out  
C. take after  
D. hang out 
Question 10. Students often ______ valuable language skills simply by interacting with native speakers 
during international exchange programmes. 
A. run through 
B. pick up 
C. turn back 
D. cut off 
Question 11. During the debate, the professor attempted to ______ the complex theory in a way accessible 
to non-specialists. 
A. cut out 
B. pass away 
C. hang out 
D. put across

TUẦN SỐ 04 
 
VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
TÀI LIỆU LIVESTREAM ĐỘC QUYỀN 
VIP 90 (2026): TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM BỘ CÂU HỎI  
DỄ TRÚNG TỦ & LUYỆN BỘ ĐỀ TINH HOA  
[V90.04.06] TINH HOA ĐỌC HIỂU (BUỔI 8) 
 
 
Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương  
LEVEL 1 
 
 
Text 1 
Family relationships are often the most important connections in our lives. However, some families 
do not work well together. In these dysfunctional families, people may argue often, criticise each other, 
or ignore each other’s feelings. Such situations can cause stress and affect a person’s emotional well-
being. Learning how to deal with these problems is therefore very important. 
One helpful step is to recognise unhealthy behaviour. When people understand that constant 
conflict, control, or lack of support are not normal, they can start to respond differently. Experts suggest 
setting clear boundaries with family members. This means telling others what behaviour is acceptable and 
what is not. Boundaries help protect a person’s emotional health and prevent relationships from becoming 
overwhelming.  
People should also take care of themselves and seek support from friends, mentors, or 
professionals. Talking to someone can provide useful advice and emotional comfort. In addition, building 
positive relationships outside the family can create a stronger support network. By understanding family 
problems and protecting their well-being, individuals can live a more balanced and peaceful life. 
 
Question 1. In some dysfunctional families, people may do all of the following EXCEPT ______.  
A. ignore other people’s feelings  
B. criticise each other  
C. spread rumours about family members  
D. argue with one another  
Question 2. The phrase “deal with” in paragraph 1 mostly means _____. 
A. argue 
B. create 
C. tackle 
D. worsen 
Question 3. The word “they” in paragraph 2 refers to ______. 
A. experts 
B. people 
C. boundaries 
D. family members 
Question 4. As stated in paragraph 3, building positive relationships outside the family _____. 
A. may weaken a person’s connection with their own family members  
B. can sometimes create tension within an already troubled family  
C. may reduce the need to address problems within the family  
D. can help people develop a stronger network of emotional support 
 
 
Trang 56

VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
LEVEL 2 
 
 
Text 2 
The boomerang generation refers to young adults who move back to live with their parents after a 
period of independence. In the past, leaving home was often seen as a clear step into adulthood. 
However, economic and social changes have made this path less straightforward for many young people.   
One major reason for this trend is the rising cost of living. Housing prices and rent in many cities 
have increased sharply, making it difficult for young workers to afford their own homes. At the same 
time, student debt and unstable job markets can make financial independence harder to achieve. As a 
result, some young adults return to their parents’ homes in order to save money and regain financial 
stability.  
Living with parents again can bring both advantages and challenges. On the one hand, young 
people may benefit from emotional support and reduced living expenses. On the other hand, sharing a 
home can sometimes lead to tension if expectations are unclear. To avoid conflict, families often need to 
discuss responsibilities, privacy, and household rules openly.  
 
Question 5. Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence in paragraph 1? 
In the past, leaving home was often seen as a clear step into adulthood. 
A. In earlier times, moving out of the family home clearly marked the start of adulthood. 
B. In earlier times, adulthood was widely believed to lead young people to leave home.  
C. In earlier times, reaching adulthood was often considered the reason families asked children to leave 
home.  
D. In earlier times, adulthood was commonly seen as something achieved before young people left home. 
Question 6. The word “straightforward” in paragraph 1 is OPPOSITE in meaning to _____. 
A. simple 
B. affordable 
C. complex 
D. pleasant 
Question 7. Which of the following is NOT mentioned as a reason some young adults return to their 
parents’ homes?  
A. student debt 
 
B. rising housing prices 
C. unstable job markets 
 
D. parents’ expectations 
Question 8. The phrase “benefit from” in paragraph 3 is OPPOSITE in meaning to _____. 
A. stick to 
B. suffer from 
C. care for 
D. prepare for 
Question 9. Without clear expectations, sharing a home ______.  
A. increases living costs 
 
B. causes a lack of privacy  
C. can create conflicts 
 
D. weakens household rules 
 
 
Trang 57

TUẦN SỐ 04 
 
VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
LEVEL 3 
 
 
Text 3 
During the COVID-19 lockdown, many parents were suddenly required to supervise their 
children’s education at home while continuing with their own work and daily responsibilities. For many 
families, this situation created intense pressure and emotional strain. Surveys suggested that a large 
number of parents felt more stressed during later lockdowns than during the first one, as the challenges of 
homeschooling continued for months.   
One difficulty was balancing different roles at the same time. Parents had to act as employees, 
teachers and caregivers within the same household. Many worried that they were not doing enough to 
support their children’s learning. Some reported anxiety, sleepless nights and increasing frustration, which 
sometimes affected relationships within the family.  
Despite these difficulties, many families tried to adapt to the situation. Parents developed new 
routines, organised learning schedules and encouraged their children to stay motivated. Experts also 
advised parents to lower unrealistic expectations and focus on children’s emotional well-being rather than 
perfect academic results.  
The experience of homeschooling during lockdown revealed how demanding parenting can be 
during a crisis. At the same time, it highlighted the importance of patience, flexibility and emotional 
support within families. 
 
Question 10. The word “strain” in paragraph 1 is OPPOSITE in meaning to _____. 
A. anxiety 
B. tension 
C. relief 
D. creativity 
Question 11. According to surveys, parents ______.  
A. reported greater satisfaction with later lockdown learning  
B. showed greater confidence in managing homeschooling  
C. faced fewer difficulties during the later lockdown periods  
D. experienced greater stress in later lockdowns 
Question 12. The word “they” in paragraph 2 refers to _____. 
A. children 
B. parents 
C. roles 
D. relationships 
Question 13. Many parents adapted to the difficult situation during the lockdown by doing all of the 
following EXCEPT ______.  
A. organising learning schedules 
B. cancelling their children’s school lessons  
C. developing new routines  
 
D. encouraging their children to stay motivated 
Question 14. The word “demanding” in paragraph 4 is closest in meaning to _____. 
A. difficult 
B. peaceful 
C. impatient 
D. important 
Question 15. Which paragraph mentions several psychological difficulties experienced by some parents? 
A. Paragraph 1 
B. Paragraph 2 
C. Paragraph 3 
D. Paragraph 4 
 
 
Trang 58

VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
LEVEL 4 
 
 
Text 4 
Generation Z, typically defined as those born between the late 1990s and early 2010s, has become 
the subject of intense public fascination. [I] Commentators, marketers and researchers frequently analyse 
their behaviour, attitudes and cultural preferences. As a result, this cohort may be one of the most 
scrutinised generations in modern history.  
One reason for this widespread attention is that members of Generation Z have grown up fully 
immersed in digital technology. [II] Social media platforms, smartphones and algorithm-driven content 
shape how they communicate, learn and express their identities. Because their online activity produces 
large amounts of data, companies and researchers can easily observe trends in real time. [III] 
Another factor is their potential economic and cultural influence. Experts predict that Generation 
Z will become one of the most powerful consumer groups in the coming decades. Consequently, 
organisations are eager to anticipate their tastes, values and spending patterns.  
Nevertheless, some scholars argue that the intense focus on this generation is often exaggerated. 
Media narratives sometimes simplify complex social realities, creating stereotypes rather than genuine 
understanding. [IV] In reality, many behaviours attributed to Generation Z may simply reflect broader 
social changes affecting people of all ages. 
 
Question 16. The word “scrutinised” in paragraph 1 mostly means _____. 
A. randomly selected 
 
B. intensively trained 
C. carefully examined 
 
D. highly motivated 
Question 17. Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence in paragraph 2? 
Because their online activity produces large amounts of data, companies and researchers can easily 
observe trends in real time. 
A. Because companies and researchers monitor trends in real time, large amounts of data are produced by 
young people online.   
B. Because companies and researchers analyse trends immediately, young people create more online 
activity and data.   
C. Because young people’s digital behaviour generates vast data streams, companies and researchers can 
track trends instantly.  
D. Because real-time trends are easy to observe, companies and researchers produce large amounts of 
online data for young people. 
Question 18. Which of the following best summarises paragraph 3? 
A. Organisations closely analyse Generation Z because its future economic and cultural influence may 
shape markets.  
B. Organisations increasingly analyse Generation Z mainly because their digital behaviour produces 
valuable consumer data.   
C. Experts believe Generation Z already dominates global markets and strongly determines companies’ 
current strategies.   
D. Businesses are eager to follow Generation Z trends since the generation already controls spending 
worldwide. 
 
 
 
Trang 59

TUẦN SỐ 04 
 
VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
TÀI LIỆU LIVESTREAM ĐỘC QUYỀN 
VIP 90 (2026): TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM BỘ CÂU HỎI  
DỄ TRÚNG TỦ & LUYỆN BỘ ĐỀ TINH HOA  
[V90.04.06B] THI ONLINE: TINH HOA ĐỌC HIỂU  
 
(BUỔI 8) – ĐỀ VẬN DỤNG 
Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương  
 
 
LEVEL 1 
Text 1 
Many parents notice that their teenage children talk to them less than before. Why does this happen? One 
reason is that teenagers are trying to become more independent. They may want more privacy and feel 
uncomfortable sharing every detail of their lives. Another reason is that teenagers sometimes think their 
parents will not understand them, especially when it comes to friends, trends, or personal feelings. In 
addition, school pressure and social activities may also take up much of their time and energy. As a result, 
they may prefer spending time alone or with friends. Understanding these reasons can help parents 
communicate more patiently with their teenage children.  
 
Question 1. The word “them” in the text refers to ________.  
A. lives 
B. friends 
C. parents  
D. teenagers  
Question 2. The phrase “take up” in the text is closest in meaning to _______. 
A. occupy 
B. employ 
C. communicate 
D. share  
Question 3. Which of the following is NOT mentioned in the passage as a reason why teenagers talk to 
their parents less? 
A. The influence of video games and online entertainment 
B. Teenagers’ desire for more independence and privacy 
C. Teenagers’ belief that their parents may not understand them 
D. School pressure and social activities taking up their time 
 
Text 2 
Technology often widens the generation gap in modern families in amusing ways. Young people usually 
adapt to new devices very quickly, while their parents sometimes struggle with them. Teenagers may 
spend hours chatting online, watching videos, or scrolling through social media, which feels completely 
normal to them. Parents, however, may find these habits confusing or even unnecessary. For example, a 
teenager can easily set up an app in minutes, while a parent might still be trying to remember the password. 
This difference in digital skills can create misunderstandings in daily life. As a result, technology 
sometimes makes parents feel left behind, and teenagers feel that their parents simply do not understand 
their world. 
 
Question 4. The word “them” in the text refers to _______. 
A. parents  
B. new devices  
C. young people  
D. modern families  
Question 5. The word “unnecessary” in the text is OPPOSITE in meaning to _______. 
A. considerate 
B. changeable 
C. essential 
D. fixed

VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
Question 6. According to the passage, how does technology affect parents and teenagers? 
A. Parents feel left behind, while teens feel their parents do not understand their world. 
B. It helps parents and teenagers communicate more easily through digital habits. 
C. Technology encourages parents to spend time using social media with their children. 
D. It allows both parents and teenagers to develop the same level of digital skills. 
 
LEVEL 2 
Text 3 
In today’s digital world, many parents worry about what their children do online. Because of this concern, 
some parents choose to check their children’s phones to make sure they are safe. They may want to know 
who their children talk to, what websites they visit, or whether they face risks such as cyberbullying or 
harmful content. From this point of view, checking a phone can be seen as a way for parents to protect 
and guide their children in a complex online environment. 
However, others believe that this action may harm trust between parents and teenagers. Young people 
often value their privacy and may feel uncomfortable if their parents read their messages without 
permission. Instead of secretly checking phones, parents might communicate openly and discuss online 
safety with their children. Building trust and understanding may be more effective than strict control in 
the long run. 
 
Question 7. The word “complex” in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to ______.  
A. repetitive 
B. plain 
C. complicated 
D. simple 
Question 8. The word “secretly” in paragraph 2 is OPPOSITE in meaning to _______. 
A. privately 
B. confidently 
C. strongly 
D. publicly 
Question 9. The word “their” in paragraph 2 refers to _______. 
A. young people 
B. parents  
C. messages  
D. phones 
Question 10. According to paragraph 1, why do many parents check their children’s phones? 
A. Because they think teenagers should not use phones at all. 
B. Because they want to control every conversation their children have. 
C. Because they want their children to spend more time online. 
D. Because they want to make sure their children are safe online.  
 
Text 4 
Research on divorced families suggests that parental separation can influence children in several 
psychological and social ways. Studies often report that children may initially experience feelings of 
uncertainty, anxiety, or emotional instability, particularly during the period immediately following the 
divorce. Changes in living arrangements and parental availability can disrupt a child’s sense of 
security. In some cases, prolonged conflict between parents before or after the separation may intensify 
these effects and affect children’s academic performance or social behavior. 
However, research also shows that divorce does not inevitably lead to negative outcomes. Many children 
adapt successfully, especially when parents maintain supportive and cooperative relationships after 
separation. A stable environment, consistent communication, and emotional reassurance can significantly 
reduce potential stress. When both parents remain actively involved in their children’s lives, children are 
more likely to develop resilience and maintain healthy emotional and social development over time. 
 
 
Trang 62
