C. Whether to restore an estranged relationship depends on specific conditions and circumstances.  
D. Online communities help repair family bonds by validating personal emotional experiences.

Despite its painful consequences, estrangement is not always permanent. 
A. [I] 
B. [II] 
C. [III] 
D. [IV]

B. Family breakdown, widely discussed today, arises from digital conflicts, where public disputes 
intensify tensions, though reconciliation remains achievable through communication. 
C. Addressing family breakdown requires empathy and communication, as conflicting perspectives can

D. Family estrangement increasingly affects ordinary households, often caused by public disputes, while

VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
TÀI LIỆU LIVESTREAM ĐỘC QUYỀN 
VIP 90 (2026): TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM BỘ CÂU HỎI  
DỄ TRÚNG TỦ & LUYỆN BỘ ĐỀ TINH HOA  
[V90.ĐH.07] ĐỌC HIỂU C1 – C2 BỘ ĐỀ DỄ TRÚNG TỦ 
 
(BUỔI 7) 
Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương  
 
 
Read the following passage and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct answer to each of 
the questions from 1 to 8. 
Disability is invariably overlooked in the workplace, and that must change. Budgets and task 
forces opt for tackling gender disparity one year and racial inequality the next. This ‘a la carte’ approach 
to inclusion is stifling progress. The inclusion of marginalised groups is not a trend, and it is 
incredibly reductive to treat it as such. 
Leadership is a fundamental pillar of progress. Leaders should be leading by example as they set 
the precedent for what is deemed to be acceptable. Discrimination is exacerbated by behaviour that gives 
the impression that it is ok to distinguish between employees based on a protected characteristic. 
Moreover, the resounding silence within the C-Suite when it comes to disability is unconscionable. 
Business leaders have not been encouraged to speak out about disability and do not have the confidence 
to self-identify.  
The disabled community needs non-disabled allies to push for inclusion; it also requires leaders 
who lead by example by disclosing their disabilities. Leaders who self-identify can provide positive role 
models for all by demonstrating that meaningful work and contributing to businesses and society are not 
solely the preserve of the non-disabled.  
A great recent example is Jun Sawada, Chairman of the board of Nippon Telegraph and Telephone, 
talking publicly about his eye condition called keratoconus, which causes irregularities in his corneas that 
result in vision loss. He cannot see with his naked eye, instead using hard contact lenses, and is thus able 
to avoid a significant impact on his daily life. Contact lenses serve as a brilliant example of technology 
that makes the impossible possible in our daily lives and are proof that innovative technology has the 
power to change our world for the better. Leaders have a responsibility to ensure a fully accessible world 
that is inclusive of all and must speak out to erode the negative connotations attached to disability and 
employment. 
(Adapted from https://www.forbes.com/) 
 
Question 1. The word “overlooked” in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to _______. 
A. highlighted 
B. examined 
C. ignored  
D. appreciated 
Question 2. Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence in paragraph 1? 
The inclusion of marginalised groups is not a trend, and it is incredibly reductive to treat it as such. 
A. Treating the integration of disadvantaged groups as a trend reflects a manageable approach. 
B. Viewing the integration of disadvantaged groups as a passing trend oversimplifies the issue.  
C. Considering group integration as a temporary trend helps firms address it more efficiently. 
D. Recognising the integration of disadvantaged groups as a trend facilitates implementation. 
 
 
Trang 72

B. Leaders who openly disclose their disabilities can serve as positive role models for others.  
C. Most business leaders are confident and willing to speak publicly about their disabilities. 
D. Technological solutions have no meaningful impact on improving daily life for disabled individuals.

A. Paragraph 1 
B. Paragraph 2 
C. Paragraph 3 
D. Paragraph 4 
Question 8. In which paragraph is an example of a real business leader used to support the argument?

B. Paragraph 3 
C. Paragraph 4 
D. Paragraph 2 
 
Read the following passage and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct answer to each of 
the questions from 9 to 18. 
All around the world, inequalities persist in the complex web of social, economic, and ecological 
factors that mediate food security outcomes at different human and institutional scales. There have been 
rapid and continuous improvements in agricultural productivity and better food security in many regions 
of the world during the past 50 years due to an expansion in crop area, irrigation, and supportive policy 
and institutional initiatives. However, in Sub-Saharan Africa, the situation is inverted. Statistics show that 
food insecurity has risen since 2015 in Sub-Saharan African countries, and the situation has worsened 
owing to the conflict and the ongoing implications of the COVID-19 threat.  
The effort to eradicate hunger, food insecurity, and other forms of malnutrition has been the topic 
of many studies. Nevertheless, despite recent advances in these areas, many nations, particularly in 
developing countries like Sub-Saharan Africa, still face significant food insecurity challenges. In the mid-
1970s, food insecurity prompted world leaders to acknowledge for the first time their shared duty 
to end hunger and malnutrition. Nonetheless, in the 48 least developed countries, per capita food 
consumption fell between 1980 and 1998, while it rose in most of the developing world. [I] 
Stable distribution networks are critical to food security. Since the beginning of 2020, the COVID-
19 epidemic has been putting this stability through one of the most severe tests it has ever experienced. 
[II] As evidenced by lockdowns, economic downturns, trade restrictions, and soaring food prices, the 
pandemic has become more than just a health issue. It is becoming a significant economic threat to world 
food security. Food costs have hit all-time highs due to wars, food supply issues, and the ongoing 
economic impacts of the COVID-19 epidemic. People in poor and middle-income countries spend a large 
portion of their income on food, leaving them particularly vulnerable to price increases. [III]

The information on Sub-Saharan African Food Security (SSAFB) estimates that one in three 
persons in the region is malnourished. It will be difficult for Africa to have sustainable economic 
development if its people are malnourished and unwell. [IV] 
(Adapted from https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/) 
 
Question 9. According to paragraph 1, the persistence of inequalities in the complex web of social, 
economic, and ecological factors ______. 
A. affects ecological systems and has an impact on institutional scales 
B. has eliminated improvements in agricultural productivity worldwide 
C. continues to influence food security outcomes at various levels  
D. is no longer relevant as food security has improved in recent decades 
Question 10. The word “implications” in paragraph 1 can be best replaced by _______. 
A. consequences 
B. intentions 
C. solutions 
D. situations 
Question 11. What paradox is mentioned regarding the last two decades of the 20th century? 
A. Many studies have focused on addressing hunger and malnutrition despite ongoing challenges in 
developing countries. 
B. Global leaders began to recognise their responsibility to eliminate hunger and malnutrition during that 
period. 
C. Food consumption per person dropped in the least developed countries but rose in most developing 
regions.  
D. Developing countries, especially in Sub-Saharan Africa, continue to face serious food insecurity issues. 
Question 12. Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence in paragraph 2? 
In the mid-1970s, food insecurity prompted world leaders to acknowledge for the first time their 
shared duty to end hunger and malnutrition.  
A. Food insecurity in the mid-1970s had a maximal impact on global leaders’ awareness of their role in 
addressing hunger and malnutrition. 
B. In the mid-1970s, global leaders had already long accepted individual responsibility for solving hunger 
and malnutrition issues worldwide. 
C. In the mid-1970s, worse food insecurity pushed global leaders to first recognise a shared responsibility 
to combat hunger and malnutrition.  
D. During the middle of the 1970s, global leaders successfully eliminated hunger and malnutrition through 
coordinated international actions. 
Question 13. Which of the following best summarises paragraph 3? 
A. Distribution networks have improved significantly since 2020, helping reduce food prices and 
strengthen global food security systems. 
B. The COVID-19 pandemic has mainly remained a health crisis with limited economic effects on global 
food systems and distribution networks. 
C. Food prices have stabilised despite global conflicts, and most countries have been able to maintain 
secure and affordable food supplies. 
D. Global crises have disrupted food distribution systems, increasing prices and threatening food security, 
especially in poorer countries.  
Question 14. The word “its” in paragraph 4 refers to ________. 
A. information 
B. Africa 
C. development 
D. people

TUẦN SỐ 08 
 
VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
TÀI LIỆU LIVESTREAM ĐỘC QUYỀN 
VIP 90 (2026): TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM BỘ CÂU HỎI  
DỄ TRÚNG TỦ & LUYỆN BỘ ĐỀ TINH HOA  
[V90.08.01] DỰ ĐOÁN ĐẶC BIỆT COLLOCATION    
 
XUẤT HIỆN TRONG ĐỀ THI (BUỔI 2) 
Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương  
 
 
Read the following texts and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct option that best fits 
each of the numbered blanks from 1 to 30.  
Text 1 
Healthy living requires consistent choices that support both body and mind. Rather than choosing to (1) 
_____ on a diet, individuals benefit from gradual adjustments that help them (2) _____ fit over time. 
Regular exercise, balanced meals, and sufficient sleep contribute to long-term wellbeing, while visiting a 
doctor to (3) _____ a check-up ensures that problems are detected early and managed effectively. This 
approach promotes resilience and reduces the risk of illness overall. 
 
Question 1. A. take 
B. go 
C. give 
D. bring

D. break

D. have 
 
Text 2 
Our school organised a charity event to (4) _____ funds for cancer victims, bringing together students, 
teachers, and local residents. Many volunteers believed that even small contributions could (5) _____ a 
change in patients’ lives. Through performances, sales, and donations, the event encouraged community 
support. Although the effort seemed modest, it was enough to (6) _____ an impact and inspire others to 
continue helping those in need.

Family life is shaped by small, meaningful routines that strengthen relationships over time. Children are 
often encouraged to (7) _____ a helping hand with daily tasks, which teaches responsibility and care. 
Simple duties like (8) _____ the table before meals help create a sense of order, while remembering to (9) 
_____ the bed each morning builds discipline. These habits, repeated consistently, (10) _____ the 
foundation for mutual respect and cooperation within the household. 
 
Question 7. A. take 
B. put 
C. do 
D. give 
Question 8. A. making 
B. keeping 
C. setting 
D. raising

B. bring

everyone sits down. Through these repeated actions, families gradually (10) ____ the foundation for

XUẤT HIỆN TRONG ĐỀ THI (BUỔI 2) – ĐỀ NÂNG CAO 
Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương  
 
 
Read the following texts and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct option that best fits 
each of the numbered blanks from 1 to 18.  
Text 1 
Performing well in a job interview requires more than preparation; it involves a deliberate strategy and 
careful self-presentation. To begin with, candidates should (1) _____ a positive impression the moment 
they enter the room, as first perceptions are often decisive. It is advisable to demonstrate enthusiasm while 
maintaining composure, which helps convey (2) _____ interest. During the discussion, applicants ought 
to provide clear examples of their achievements and (3) _____ the requirements outlined in the job 
description. In addition, being able to express ideas clearly under pressure is (4) _____ valued. Candidates 
who (5) ______ initiative tend to be viewed more favourably, especially when they engage actively with 
the conversation. Ultimately, those who (6) ______ relevant experience beforehand are far better equipped 
to handle demanding interview situations with confidence. 
 
Question 1. A. set 
B. come 
C. create 
D. engage 
Question 2. A. shallow 
B. insincere 
C. emerging 
D. genuine 
Question 3. A. make 
B. give 
C. set 
D. meet 
Question 4. A. largely 
B. highly 
C. widely 
D. quietly  
Question 5. A. show 
B. make 
C. put 
D. get  
Question 6. A. lend 
B. view 
C. meet 
D. gain 
 
 
Text 2 
Extreme weather events are becoming increasingly frequent, (7) _____ serious concerns worldwide. 
Scientists warn that climate change may pose a (8) _____ to vulnerable communities, particularly in 
coastal regions. Severe storms can cause widespread damage, destroying homes and infrastructure within 
hours. In many cases, governments must take (9) _____ action to protect residents and minimise losses. 
However, long-term strategies are (10) ______ essential, as policymakers attempt to address the issue of 
rising global temperatures. Failure to (11) _____ effectively may have serious consequences for future 
generations. Experts continue to play a vital role in analysing climate patterns and proposing solutions. 
Without coordinated efforts, it will be difficult to reduce the (12) _____ of further disasters and ensure 
environmental stability. 
 
Question 7. A. putting 
B. making 
C. giving 
D. raising 
Question 8. A. need 
B. threat 
C. fact 
D. loss  
Question 9. A. current 
B. existing 
C. immediate 
D. pressing

TÀI LIỆU LIVESTREAM ĐỘC QUYỀN 
VIP 90 (2026): TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM BỘ CÂU HỎI  
DỄ TRÚNG TỦ & LUYỆN BỘ ĐỀ TINH HOA  
[V90.08.02] LÀM CHỦ KIẾN THỨC NGỮ PHÁP    
 
LIÊN TỪ VÀ TRẠNG TỪ LIÊN KẾT (BUỔI 1) 
Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương  
 
 
Liên từ kết hợp (FANBOYS) 
for (vì) 
I couldn’t go home, for it rained heavily.  
and (và) 
My mother works as a nurse and my father works as a lawyer.  
nor (cũng không) 
We can neither change nor improve it. 
but (nhưng) 
We ran after him, but he escaped. 
or (hoặc) 
Are you comfortable or shall I turn the heat down? 
yet (thế nhưng) 
He is old, yet he’s attractive.  
so (vì vậy) 
I was lost so I bought a street map. 
 
Liên từ tương quan 
either …. or (hoặc …. hoặc) 
Either you leave now or I call the police! 
neither ……. nor  
(không …. cũng không) 
I neither know nor care what happened to him. 
not only ……. but also  
(không những ….. mà còn) 
He’s not only handsome but also rich.  
both …… and (cả ….. và) 
Both Nam and Hung knew the truth.  
 
Liên từ phụ thuộc 
because/ as/ since (vì) 
He hated being in the army because/as/since he had to obey 
commands. 
though/ even though/ although 
(mặc dù)/ even if (cho dù) 
Although I don’t like him, I have to ask him for help.  
while/ whereas (trong khi) 
He must be about 60, whereas his wife looks about 30. 
 
Một số liên từ khác 
so that (để mà) 
I spoke slowly so that everyone could understand me. 
as if/ as though (như thể là) 
He looked at me as if he had seen a ghost. 
Supposing that you lost your job tomorrow, how would you 
support yourself? 
If (nếu)/ unless (nếu … không)/ 
provided that/ supposing that/ as 
long as/ so long as (với điều kiện/ 
giả sử) 
 
 
 
Trang 9

In many modern classrooms, blended learning has become a practical approach as technology continues 
to reshape education. (7) _____ traditional lessons remain important, teachers now combine online 
platforms and face-to-face instruction because this method allows greater flexibility. Students can review 
materials at home and then apply knowledge in class, (8) _____ they develop both independence and 
collaboration skills. Moreover, digital tools provide instant feedback, while teachers can monitor progress 
more effectively. (9) _____, this model requires strong self-discipline, and some learners may struggle

In today’s dynamic labour market, job hopping—changing jobs frequently within a short period—
has become increasingly common among young professionals. (11) _____, this expectation has weakened 
as workers seek faster career progression and more diverse experiences. By moving across organisations, 
individuals are exposed to different systems and working cultures, which can broaden their perspective 
and strengthen adaptability. Some are also motivated by higher salaries or better opportunities, (12) ____. 
(13) _____, particularly when a candidate’s career path appears inconsistent or lacks depth. 
Employers may question reliability, as investing in training becomes risky (14) ____. While varied 
experience may seem valuable, it does not always lead to expertise, especially when individuals fail to 
stay long enough to master their roles. (15) _____, making it harder to secure stable and senior positions. 
 
Question 11.

B. If long-term employment sometimes provides financial security  
C. Although long-term employment once symbolised stability  
D. Since long-term employment can support gradual career growth

A. while staying in one company may reduce both career flexibility and promotion chances  
B. although working in one place may offer both stability and long-term security  
C. whereas choosing one position may provide both comfort and predictable routines  
D. since remaining in one role may limit both income growth and skill development

Read the following texts and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct option that best fits 
each of the numbered blanks from 1 to 30.  
1. 
When it comes to school performance, talent may indeed provide an initial advantage, (1) _______ it 
cannot substitute for dedication and a growth mindset. (2) _______, students should focus on building 
effective study habits and embracing challenges. (3) _______, schools should encourage and acknowledge 
hard-working students rather than focusing only on talented ones. 
 
Question 1. A. and 
B. yet  
C. so 
D. or 
Question 2. A. For instance 
B. To sum up 
C. Therefore  
D. Additionally 
Question 3. A. Firstly 
B. Otherwise 
C. Nonetheless 
D. In addition 
 
 
2. 
(4) _______ one's character often develops throughout his life, the family plays a pivotal role in shaping 
it in the early days. (5) _______ a supportive family environment fosters confidence and empathy, a 
neglectful or toxic environment can lead to emotional struggles. (6) _______ children spend most of their 
formative years at home, their values and behaviours are deeply influenced by their parents and siblings. 
 
Question 4. A. Because  
B. As 
C. Although  
D. Once 
Question 5. A. After 
B. Because  
C. Like  
D. Whereas 
Question 6. A. So that 
B. Since 
C. As soon as 
D. Although 
 
 
3. 
The development of technology has made sharing and accessing information easier than ever. Young 
people's daily use of social media also means they face peer pressure every day. (7) _______, the fear of 
judgment and rejection is increasingly becoming a serious psychological problem not only in the young 
(8) _______ in people of all ages. (9) _______ this issue is thoroughly addressed, it can cause larger 
problems on a societal scale. 
 
Question 7. A. First 
B. In the same way 
C. Thus 
D. In contrast 
Question 8. A. but also  
B. even if  
C. if only  
D. so that  
Question 9. A. If 
B. As long as  
C. Unless  
D. Provided that  
 
 
Trang 14

7. 
Youth unemployment has become a pressing issue in many countries. It arises (21) _______ the skills 
taught in schools do not align with the demands of the modern job market. Many young people struggle 
to find stable employment (22) _______ they are well-equipped and eager to work. (23) _______, 
industries face labour shortages in specific sectors, highlighting a mismatch between job seekers and 
available roles. Addressing this issue requires both systemic reforms (24) _______ individual efforts to 
adapt to changing economic conditions. If governments and educational institutions collaborate, young 
workers may gain better job opportunities, which helps stabilise the labour market. (25) _______, 
reducing youth unemployment will contribute to societal stability and economic growth in the long term. 
 
Question 21. A. so  
B. before  
C. although  
D. when 
Question 22. A. or  
B. though  
C. and  
D. so 
Question 23. A. Meanwhile  
B. To sum up 
C. For instance 
D. Similarly 
Question 24. A. for 
B. or 
C. and  
D. but 
Question 25. A. Besides  
B. On the contrary 
C. However 
D. Next 
 
 
8. 
Rural migration is often seen as a practical response to limited opportunities in the countryside, 
especially when young people seek better education and more stable employment. (26) _____, many 
individuals are able to increase their income and improve their living standards. In addition, exposure to 
modern working environments can help them develop new skills and expand their professional networks. 
As a result, migration not only benefits individuals but also contributes to economic growth, (27) ____.  
(28) _____. While cities may provide opportunities, they often struggle to accommodate a rapidly 
growing population, leading to overcrowding and pressure on public services. (29) ______, particularly 
when living costs are high and job security is uncertain. Rural areas can also experience labour shortages, 
which may weaken agricultural production. (30) _____. 
 
Question 26. 
A. Although cities provide a greater variety of public services  
B. Because cities offer a wider range of jobs  
C. While cities create a higher demand for basic housing  
D. Since cities attract a large number of young workers 
Question 27. 
A. so that many workers still prefer stable jobs in rural areas  
B. since a larger workforce supports urban industries  
C. though some industries require highly specialised training  
D. if urban areas often experience rapid population growth 
Question 28.  
A. Therefore, this trend continues to attract more people to urban centres  
B. In addition, this shift can improve access to modern facilities and services  
C. However, this movement also creates several challenges that cannot be ignored  
D. Meanwhile, this process reflects changes in economic and social conditions

Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương  
 
 
Read the following texts and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct option that best fits 
each of the numbered blanks from 1 to 18.  
Text 1 
In an era defined by accelerating urban expansion and technological convergence, the notion of 
smart cities has emerged as a multifaceted response to increasingly complex urban challenges. (1) ____, 
digitally embedded systems enable authorities to coordinate services with far greater precision and 
flexibility. By integrating real-time data into transport networks, energy distribution, and waste 
management, cities can anticipate disruptions before they escalate, (2) _____. (3) ____, which enhances 
long-term strategic development rather than short-term fixes. As a consequence, smart cities not only 
optimise daily operations but also create environments that are more adaptive, resource-efficient, and 
economically competitive.  
(4) _____. Although these technologies promise greater convenience and responsiveness, they also 
raise significant issues related to data privacy, particularly when surveillance mechanisms become 
embedded in routine activities. (5) ____, which may disrupt essential services if not properly managed. 
Moreover, unequal access to digital resources can widen existing social disparities, as not all residents 
benefit equally from technological advancements. (6) ____, ensuring that smart city development remains 
inclusive, secure, and sustainable over time. 
 
Question 1. 
A. Supposing that traditional urban systems frequently fail to support growing populations  
B. Whereas modern technologies gradually reshape the structure of urban environments  
C. While conventional infrastructure often struggles to accommodate rising populations  
D. Since urban authorities increasingly depend on digital tools for management 
Question 2. 
A. despite improving both performance and productivity  
B. consequently increasing both pressure and complexity  
C. meanwhile reducing both control and supervision  
D. thereby improving both efficiency and sustainability 
Question 3. 
A. For instance, these technologies enable authorities to monitor urban systems effectively  
B. In contrast, digital networks support governments in managing resources more efficiently  
C. Furthermore, such systems allow urban planners to make evidence-based decisions  
D. Meanwhile, smart solutions encourage cities to adopt more advanced infrastructure 
 
 
 
Trang 18

Question 4. 
A. As a consequence, the expansion of smart urban systems introduces a range of serious challenges that 
affect social development  
B. In contrast, the expansion of smart urban systems introduces a range of structural changes that influence 
daily activities  
C. Nevertheless, the expansion of smart urban systems introduces a range of critical concerns that require 
careful consideration  
D. As earlier mentioned, the expansion of smart urban systems introduces a range of useful features that 
improve city efficiency 
Question 5.

B. To illustrate this, the use of digital platforms requires careful monitoring of user data  
C. Also, reliance on complex digital infrastructures increases vulnerability to cyber threats

support. Schools should incorporate activities that develop emotional awareness, helping students 
recognise and manage stress rather than ignore it. (11) _____ where expectations are realistic and 
communication remains open. Clear limits on academic workload and digital use can also reduce 
unnecessary pressure, allowing teenagers to maintain balance. (12) _____, young people are more likely 
to build resilience and protect their psychological well-being. 
 
Question 7. 
A. Even if earlier generations often pursued more traditional career paths  
B. Unless earlier generations often faced more unpredictable social pressures  
C. As earlier generations often relied on more structured career paths  
D. While earlier generations often followed more predictable paths

respond not through words but through feeling, (22) _____. A rhythm, repeated and recognised, invites 
movement; a melody, carried across cultures, (23) _____. This form of connection—subtle yet 
powerful—emerges even when meaning is not clearly understood, (24) ____. 
In gatherings where people from different backgrounds come together, music frequently becomes 
the centre of interaction, around which communication forms naturally. When individuals participate, 
whether by listening, moving, or performing, they engage in an experience (25) _____. Cultural 
differences remain present, yet they do not prevent connection, because the focus shifts from 
understanding language to sharing experience. The process involves perception, reaction, and 
interpretation—stages (26) _____. (27) _____, the shared moment creates a sense of unity that is difficult 
to replace. 
Across societies, music continues to move, adapt, and transform as it passes between groups. It 
carries identity, expresses emotion, and (28) _____—functions that remain significant regardless of 
context. As individuals encounter new styles, their perspectives expand, while their responses—shaped 
by both familiarity and curiosity—(29) _____. Through repeated exposure, through participation, through 
reflection, connections strengthen, which allows music to act not simply as entertainment but as a bridge 
linking cultures in ways (30) _____. 
 
Question 21.  
A. their attempt to communicate 
B. who attempts to communicate 
C. attempt to communicate  
D. whose attempt to communicate 
Question 22. 
A. allows interaction to develop in a different way 
B. and which allows interaction to develop in a different way 
C. which allows interaction to develop in a different way 
D. allowed interaction to develop in a different way 
Question 23. 
A. encourages shared attention 
B. shared attention is encouraged 
C. which encourages shared attention 
D. when shared attention is encouraged 
Question 24. 
A. whose emotional response does not depend on translation 
B. emotional response does not depend on translation 
C. due to emotional response does not depend on translation 
D. since emotional response does not depend on translation 
Question 25. 
A. does not require explanation 
B. it does not require explanation 
C. and does not require explanation 
D. that does not require explanation 
Question 26. 
A. which people connect without direct conversation 
B. through which people connect without direct conversation 
C. which people connect without direct conversation through it 
D. people who connect without direct conversation through it 
 
Trang 29

Shared By Fanpage: Tài Liệu Khóa Học UniMap
TUẦN SỐ 08 
 
VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
TÀI LIỆU LIVESTREAM ĐỘC QUYỀN 
VIP 90 (2026): TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM BỘ CÂU HỎI  
DỄ TRÚNG TỦ & LUYỆN BỘ ĐỀ TINH HOA  
[V90.08.03A] THI ONLINE: LÀM CHỦ KIẾN THỨC NGỮ PHÁP  
 
CÁC LOẠI MỆNH ĐỀ (BUỔI 3) – ĐỀ NÂNG CAO  
Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương  
 
 
Read the following texts and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct option that best fits 
each of the numbered blanks from 1 to 18.  
Text 1 
As urban populations expand and infrastructure becomes increasingly strained, cities are 
compelled to adopt systems that can manage complexity more efficiently. Smart cities—urban 
environments (1) _____—emerge not merely as a trend but as a response to growing demands. What 
distinguishes such cities is that information, collected and analysed in real time, informs decisions that 
affect transport, energy, and public services. When systems operate in coordination, resources are 
allocated more effectively, (2) _____. 
Within this framework, technologies that monitor movement, consumption, and behaviour—
though often invisible to residents—(3) _____. While sensors regulate traffic flow and optimise energy 
use, integrated platforms allow authorities to respond rapidly to changing conditions. The process, 
involving collection, analysis, and adaptation, requires continuous interaction between systems and users. 
As data becomes central to governance, questions arise regarding privacy, control, and transparency, 
especially when individuals are unaware of (4) _____. 
Rather than functioning as isolated innovations, smart city systems operate as interconnected 
networks that redefine how cities are organised. It is not only efficiency but also adaptability, not only 
control but also responsiveness, (5) _____. As technologies evolve and expectations shift, urban 
environments continue to transform, which demands careful consideration of both benefits and risks. The 
challenge lies in ensuring that (6) ____, so that technological progress supports not only functionality but 
also the wellbeing of all residents. 
 
Question 1.  
A. which data-driven technologies are embedded into daily operations 
B. where data-driven technologies embedded into daily operations 
C. in which data-driven technologies are embedded into daily operations 
D. whose data-driven technologies embedded into daily operations 
Question 2. 
A. which reduces waste and improves overall functionality 
B. and which reduces waste and improves overall functionality 
C. it reduces waste and improves overall functionality 
D. and reduces waste and improves overall functionality 
Question 3. 
A. shapes how urban life is experienced 
B. shaping how urban life is experienced 
C. that shapes how urban life is experienced 
D. shape how urban life is experienced 
 
Trang 31

TUẦN SỐ 08 
 
VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
Question 4. 
A. how information is used  
B. when information which is used 
C. information is used 
 
D. that information which is used 
Question 5. 
A. that determines their effectiveness 
B. determines their effectiveness 
C. this determines their effectiveness 
D. yet determines their effectiveness 
Question 6.  
A. whose development remains inclusive 
B. when development remains inclusive 
C. development remains inclusive 
D. its development remains inclusive 
 
 
Text 2 
Artificial light, once limited to specific purposes, now surrounds daily life to such an extent that 
many people no longer experience true darkness. Because devices remain active late into the night and 
(7) ____, exposure becomes continuous, which gradually alters how the body responds to rest and activity. 
The situation—often ignored until its effects become noticeable—develops quietly, as individuals adjust 
their routines (8) _____. 
When the body attempts to follow a natural cycle, signals that depend on light and darkness 
become unclear, which leads to disrupted sleep patterns. Although people may believe that staying awake 
longer increases productivity, the opposite often occurs, (9) _____. A person who uses screens late at 
night, who keeps lights on for long periods, or who rarely experiences darkness may begin to notice 
changes in mood and energy. These responses, shaped by both environment and habit, do not appear 
suddenly but (10) _____. 
The difficulty lies not only in reducing light but (11) _____. Instead of viewing brightness as 
harmless, individuals begin to understand that balance requires control—when to reduce exposure, how 
to adjust surroundings, and why rest depends on darkness. As awareness grows and habits shift, 
environments become more adaptable, allowing people to regain a sense of rhythm (12) _____. 
 
Question 7. 
A. where environments are designed to stay bright 
B. environments that are designed to stay bright 
C. because environments are designed to stay bright 
D. whose environments are designed to stay bright 
Question 8. 
A. that questioned the impact 
B. questioning the impact 
C. questioned the impact 
 
D. without questioning the impact 
Question 9. 
A. since concentration weakens and fatigue builds up over time 
B. when concentration weakened and fatigue builds up over time 
C. with concentration weakened and fatigue builds up over time 
D. whose concentration weakens and fatigue builds up over time 
Question 10. 
A. the emergence through repeated exposure 
B. which emerge through repeated exposure 
C. its emergence through repeated exposure 
D. emerge through repeated exposure 
 
Trang 32 
 
Cô Phương chúc các em lớp VIP 90: ÔN LÀ TRÚNG TỦ - THI LÀ TRÚNG TUYỂN

TUẦN SỐ 08 
 
VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
Question 11. 
A. which recognise how deeply it has become part of daily behaviour 
B. recognise how deeply it has become part of daily behaviour 
C. in recognising how deeply it has become part of daily behaviour 
D. having recognised how deeply it has become part of daily behaviour 
Question 12.  
A. had gradually been lost  
B. it had gradually been lost 
C. which having gradually been lost 
D. that had gradually been lost 
 
 
Text 3 
The first night alone in a new place often feels unfamiliar, not because the space is different, but 
because there is no one to rely on when something unexpected happens. When a problem appears—
whether it is a simple task or a difficult decision—the responsibility does not shift to others, (13) _____. 
Although this may create uncertainty at first, it also reveals how much control a person (14) _____. 
As time passes, patterns begin to change in ways that are not always obvious. Decisions that once 
seemed unimportant, such as how to organise time or how to manage money, (15) _____. A person who 
delays tasks, who avoids planning, or who ignores small responsibilities may face larger problems later, 
whereas someone who adjusts habits, who reflects on outcomes, and who maintains consistency (16) 
_____. This process—shaped by repetition, awareness, and correction—does not follow a fixed path but 
evolves through experience. 
Independence, rather than being a single achievement, becomes a continuous process (17) _____ 
while maintaining balance. It is not the absence of support but the ability to act without it, not the removal 
of difficulty but the capacity to handle it, that defines this stage. Over time, as choices accumulate and 
consequences become clearer, confidence grows in a way that cannot be taught but (18) _____. 
 
Question 13. 
A. that forces individuals to respond on their own 
B. forces individuals to respond on their own 
C. and which forces individuals to respond on their own 
D. which forces individuals to respond on their own 
Question 14. 
A. who actually has over their daily life 
B. actually has over their daily life 
C. and actually has over their daily life 
D. actually having over their daily life 
Question 15. 
A. the gradual influence on stability 
B. whose influence on stability is gradual 
C. gradually influence stability 
D. which gradually influence stability 
Question 16. 
A. a stronger sense of control is developed 
B. develops a stronger sense of control 
C. as a stronger sense of control is developed 
D. which develops a stronger sense of control 
 
 
 
Trang 33

Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương  
 
 
Read the following texts and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct option that best fits 
each of the numbered blanks from 1 to 25.  
Text 1 
Fake news spreads quickly, so readers must stay alert. First, check the source carefully, and make sure it 
is reliable. (1) _____, even trusted websites can sometimes share misleading information. Look for 
evidence such as data or expert opinions, (2) _____ strong claims require support. (3) _____, compare the 
news with other sources to see if details match. If something feels suspicious, pause and think before 
sharing it. By doing this, you protect yourself and help others avoid false information. 
 
Question 1. A. Therefore  
B. However  
C. For example  
D. As a result 
Question 2. A. although  
B. because  
C. unless  
D. while 
Question 3. A. In contrast  
B. In addition  
C. Consequently  
D. Otherwise 
 
 
Text 2 
Handling money wisely requires discipline, yet it also depends on clear priorities. Many people earn 
enough, (4) _____ they still struggle because they spend without thinking. Therefore, setting limits 
becomes necessary, and each decision should serve a purpose. (5) _____ saving may seem difficult at 
first, it gradually builds stability over time. Moreover, people who review their habits regularly can adjust 
their plans, (6) _____ they avoid waste and maintain better control of their finances. 
 
Question 4. A. but 
B. so 
C. or 
D. otherwise 
Question 5. A. Since 
B. Supposing 
C. Although 
D. If  
Question 6. A. yet 
B. unless 
C. though 
D. so 
 
 
Text 3 
Ocean pollution is increasing rapidly, and it affects ecosystems worldwide. Many people recognise the 
danger, (7) _____ they continue to use plastic excessively. (8) _____, waste accumulates in oceans, and 
marine animals often mistake it for food. Since governments have introduced some policies, progress has 
been made, but it remains limited. (9) _____, industries still release harmful substances into the sea. If 
individuals reduce consumption and support environmental actions, the situation can improve; (10) 
_____, the damage will become irreversible. 
 
 
 
Trang 35

Question 7. A. so 
B. for 
C. and 
D. yet 
Question 8. A. As a result 
B. For instance 
C. Still 
D. Likewise 
Question 9. A. In fact 
B. As earlier noted 
C. Nevertheless 
D. On the contrary 
Question 10. A. still 
B. hence 
C. otherwise 
D. all the same 
 
 
Text 4 
Online shopping has become increasingly popular, and it offers great convenience to consumers. People 
can browse many products at home (11) ____ they save time and effort. However, some buyers remain 
cautious, (12) _____ they cannot check the quality directly. Therefore, reading reviews becomes 
important, and it helps them make better decisions. (13) _____ prices seem attractive, customers should 
compare options carefully. Besides, secure payment methods are necessary, so personal information stays 
protected. (14) _____, yet it may require more time. (15) ______, they can enjoy the benefits of online 
shopping without facing unnecessary risks. 
 
Question 11. A. since 
B. though 
C. while 
D. as if 
Question 12. A. but 
B. whereas 
C. as long as 
D. because 
Question 13. A. Even if 
B. Unless 
C. Whereas 
D. Supposing 
Question 14.  
A. For instance, shoppers choose stores to check products closely  
B. In fact, some customers visit shops to examine items closely  
C. Hence, physical shopping offers a more direct experience  
D. In contrast, traditional shopping allows direct experience 
Question 15.  
A. Unless people make quick decisions without checking all important details  
B. Even if buyers consider reviews and compare all available options  
C. Whereas shoppers remain careful when selecting reliable online platforms  
D. If people make careful choices and check reviews before buying online 
 
Text 5 
Rooftop gardens are becoming more common in modern cities, (16) _____. Urban residents often 
lack regular contact with nature, and rooftop gardens provide them with direct access to greenery. As 
urban areas continue to expand, more buildings are designed to include these spaces (17) _____. Thus, 
rooftops that were once unused are now turned into productive areas, and they help improve air quality as 
well as reduce urban heat.  
(18) _____, they still require careful planning and maintenance. (19) _____. Additionally, plant 
choices should match local conditions, (20) _____. In this way, rooftop gardens can function effectively, 
and they support sustainable living while creating more liveable urban environments. 
 
Question 16. 
A. even if they reduce urban heat in crowded city areas  
B. although they increase costs for building construction  
C. for they create green spaces where land is limited  
D. while they improve air quality in dense city zones

D. if people look for greener places in crowded cities 
Question 18.

B. If rooftop gardens appear in dense urban spaces  
C. Since rooftop gardens attract urban dwellers  
D. Even if rooftop gardens offer clear advantages 
Question 19.  
A. In contrast, proper setups must be arranged so that extra liquid is carried away

D. Still, plant systems must be managed so that growth conditions are fully supported

B. if they cannot remain healthy  
C. while they become weaker  
 
D. or they may fail to survive 
 
Text 6 
In many rural regions today, farming is no longer shaped by individual decisions alone, but it is 
organised through project-based models that guide production from start to finish. A group of farmers 
may agree to follow a shared plan, (21) _____. Thus, crops are selected with clear targets, and resources 
are used more efficiently. (22) _____, the overall output remains stable. This model helps farmers 
anticipate demand, (23) ______. 
Yet project farming does not guarantee success in every situation, (24) _____. For example, a 
change in weather patterns may affect yields, whereas market expectations may also shift unexpectedly. 
In this context, flexibility becomes essential, and farmers must adjust their strategies when necessary, (25) 
_____. Still, when cooperation is maintained and information flows effectively, project farming can 
support long-term development and create more reliable agricultural systems. 
 
Question 21. 
A. because a company supports planning and production across markets  
B. although a company manages pricing and promotion across markets 
C. while a company coordinates supply and distribution across markets  
D. even if a company controls pricing and distribution across markets 
Question 22.  
A. Whereas each stage follows a different process  
B. As long as each stage is carefully monitored  
C. Though each stage depends on changing weather  
D. Supposing each stage fails to meet planned targets

Along many coastlines, the growing use of sunscreen has raised concerns about its impact on 
marine ecosystems, and this issue is becoming more visible as tourism expands. People apply protective 
products before swimming, (1) ______. When swimmers enter the sea, residues wash off their skin, so 
various substances are released directly into fragile habitats. Consequently, coastal areas with heavy 
visitor activity tend to experience higher levels of contamination. (2) ______, the environmental cost of 
certain ingredients cannot be ignored. 
Scientific studies have identified specific compounds that pose risks to marine life, because they 
can interfere with biological processes in sensitive species. For example, coral reefs may suffer from 
bleaching or reduced growth, (3) ______. In addition, these chemicals may accumulate over time, and 
their long-term effects are still being examined. (4) ______, as marine systems depend on delicate balance. 
Efforts to address this issue are gradually increasing, and both individuals and industries play a 
role in reducing harm. Some regions have introduced restrictions on harmful ingredients, so safer 
alternatives are becoming more available. (5) ______, and many consumers continue to choose products 
without considering their environmental impact. (6) ______, the pressure on marine ecosystems can be 
reduced, while sustainable tourism continues to develop. 
 
Question 1. 
A. and the interaction between these chemicals and ocean water is rarely considered  
B. so the way these chemicals affect ocean water is overlooked by people 
C. yet few consider how these chemicals interact with ocean water  
D. if the impact of these substances on ocean water tends to be ignored 
Question 2. 
A. Unless shielding skin from sunlight remains important  
B. Since guarding skin against sunlight remains important  
C. If protecting skin from sunlight remains important  
D. While protection from sunlight remains important 
Question 3. 
A. even if fish together with other species may face problems in development  
B. so fish along with other marine life may experience growth issues  
C. whereas fish and other organisms can experience developmental problems  
D. since fish as well as other species may show signs of poor development

employees to operate beyond traditional office spaces. Many organisations have embraced this shift, and

LEVEL 1 
 
 
 
Text 1 
Singapore is widely regarded as a green city, not only because of its many parks but also due to 
the way nature is carefully integrated into urban life. Despite its limited land and dense population, the 
country has developed a clear strategy to balance economic growth with environmental protection, which 
can be seen in its clean streets and well-planned green areas. For instance, places like Gardens by the Bay, 
which attract both locals and tourists, show how technology and nature can work together. 
Moreover, the government encourages people to use public transport, while strict rules help reduce 
pollution and keep the city tidy. Many modern buildings are designed with rooftop gardens or vertical 
plants, making them both attractive and environmentally friendly. As a result, the temperature in the city 
is better controlled, and the air quality remains high. 
Although maintaining such a system requires strong policies and public awareness, Singapore 
proves that sustainable development is possible. Its success suggests that even highly urbanised cities can 
protect the environment if long-term planning and responsible behaviour are taken seriously.  
 
Question 1. The word “integrated” in paragraph 1 is OPPOSITE in meaning to ____. 
A. contributed 
B. separated 
C. selected 
 D. enforced  
Question 2. What is indicated about Gardens by the Bay in paragraph 1? 
A. It was built mainly for local residents’ daily exercise. 
B. It replaces traditional parks in the city centre. 
C. It shows a blend of technology and natural elements. 
D. It is located far from the main urban areas. 
Question 3. The word “them” in paragraph 2 refers to  ______. 
A. rooftop gardens 
 
B. strict rules 
 
C. vertical plants 
 
D. modern buildings 
Question 4. What is required to maintain a green system in Singapore?  
A. Careful planning and modern buildings across urban areas  
B. Strong policies and widespread public awareness  
C. High levels of tourism and international attention  
D. Large natural spaces outside the city centre

Text 2 
Bristol - a vibrant city - has gained recognition as one of the most cycle-friendly urban areas in the 
United Kingdom, where both residents and local authorities have gradually reshaped daily transport 
habits. Although it once relied heavily on cars, Bristol has invested significantly in cycling infrastructure, 
including dedicated bike lanes, traffic-calmed streets, and secure parking facilities, which together 
encourage more people to choose bicycles over private vehicles. 
What distinguishes Bristol is not only its physical design but also its strong cycling culture, which 
is supported by community initiatives and educational campaigns that promote safe riding practices. Many 
employers provide incentives for staff who cycle to work, while schools organise programmes to acquaint 
students with road safety and environmental responsibility. As a result, cycling has become both a 
practical and socially valued activity. 
Moreover, the environmental benefits are clearly visible, as reduced traffic congestion contributes 
to improved air quality and a quieter urban atmosphere. While challenges such as hilly terrain and 
unpredictable weather still exist, continuous efforts to enhance accessibility demonstrate the city’s long-
term commitment. Ultimately, Bristol illustrates how thoughtful urban planning, combined with public 
engagement, can transform transport systems in a sustainable and inclusive way. 
 
Question 5. The word “it” in paragraph 1 refers to _____. 
A. transport 
B. the UK 
C. Bristol 
D. recognition 
Question 6. Bristol has invested significantly in all of the following cycling infrastructure EXCEPT 
______.  
A. traffic-calmed streets  
 
B. secure parking facilities  
C. underground train systems  
D. dedicated bike lanes 
Question 7. What is stated about community support in Bristol?  
A. It increases the number of tourists visiting the city. 
B. It plays a role in promoting cycling culture. 
C. It improves the quality of roads across the city. 
D. It reduces the cost of transport for residents. 
Question 8. The word “acquaint” in paragraph 2 can be best replaced by _____. 
A. familiarise 
B. promote 
C. discourage 
D. require  
Question 9. Which of the following is NOT true according to paragraph 3?  
A. Lower traffic levels contribute to better air conditions. 
B. Natural factors like terrain still create certain challenges. 
C. Public involvement supports sustainable transport development. 
D. Environmental benefits are unrelated to traffic reduction.

VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
LEVEL 3 
 
 
Text 3 
Across the globe, rapidly expanding urban populations have compelled governments to rethink 
how cities are organised and managed, leading to the emergence of smart cities, where advanced 
technologies are embedded into everyday systems. Rather than depending solely on traditional 
infrastructure, these cities rely on data-driven strategies to enhance efficiency, sustainability, and overall 
quality of life, particularly in sectors such as transport, energy, healthcare, and public administration. This 
transformation reflects a broader shift towards more adaptive and responsive urban environments. 
A key characteristic of smart cities is the widespread use of sensors, artificial intelligence, and 
interconnected devices, which continuously gather and analyse vast amounts of data in real time. For 
instance, intelligent transport systems can dynamically adjust traffic signals to minimise congestion, while 
smart grids regulate electricity distribution according to actual demand, thereby reducing waste. In 
addition, digital platforms enable residents to access public services with greater convenience, whether 
they are reporting infrastructure issues, completing administrative procedures, or receiving timely updates 
from local authorities. These innovations not only streamline daily life but also encourage civic 
engagement. 
Nevertheless, the development of smart cities presents complex challenges, including concerns 
over data privacy, cybersecurity risks, and unequal access to digital resources. If such issues are not 
carefully addressed, technological progress may inadvertently deepen social inequalities. Even so, when 
guided by transparent policies and inclusive planning, smart cities hold significant potential to create more 
resilient, sustainable, and citizen-centred urban spaces. 
 
Question 10. The word “embedded” in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to _____. 
A. adjusted 
B. reflected 
C. collaborated 
D. incorporated  
Question 11. Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence in paragraph 1? 
This transformation reflects a broader shift towards more adaptive and responsive urban 
environments. 
A. A wider move towards flexible and responsive city systems is reflected in this change. 
B. This transformation results from cities becoming more efficient and well-managed. 
C. More advanced and modern urban systems are discouraged by this broader shift. 
D. This broader change is driven by increasingly dynamic and well-organised cities.  
Question 12. Smart grids are mentioned in paragraph 2 to ______.  
A. describe how traffic signals are adjusted in cities  
B. illustrate how energy use is efficiently managed  
C. explain how residents access digital services daily  
D. show how transport systems reduce road congestion 
Question 13. The word “they” in paragraph 2 refers to _____. 
A. digital platforms  
 
B. public services  
 
C. residents  
 
D. local authorities 
Question 14. The word “inadvertently” in paragraph 2 is OPPOSITE in meaning to _____.  
A. extremely 
 
B. excessively 
  
C. intentionally 
 
D. dishonestly  
 
 
Trang 49

VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
Question 15. The efficacy of smart cities depends on ______.  
A. ongoing investment in digital systems  
 
LEVEL 4 
B. strong links between firms and governments  
C. broad public support for new technology  
D. transparent policies and inclusive planning 
 
 
 
 
Text 4 
In many developing and industrialised nations alike, rural migration has become an increasingly 
prominent phenomenon, reshaping both demographic patterns and socio-economic structures. As 
individuals depart from agricultural regions in search of improved prospects, urban centres experience 
rapid population growth, which, although economically stimulating, often places considerable strain on 
infrastructure and public services. This movement is rarely arbitrary; rather, it is driven by a complex 
interplay of push and pull factors that influence personal and collective decisions. 
On the one hand, limited employment opportunities, declining agricultural productivity, and 
inadequate access to education and healthcare compel rural inhabitants to seek alternatives elsewhere. [I] 
On the other hand, cities present the promise of higher wages, diversified career paths, and enhanced 
living standards, which can appear particularly appealing to younger generations. Nevertheless, the 
transition is seldom seamless, as migrants frequently encounter precarious working conditions, elevated 
living costs, and social marginalisation. [II] 
Despite these challenges, rural migration continues to exert a transformative impact on both origin 
and destination areas. [III] While urban economies may benefit from an expanded labour force, rural 
communities often experience depopulation and a loss of human capital. Consequently, policymakers 
are increasingly compelled to devise balanced strategies that address the root causes of migration, 
ensuring that rural regions are not neglected while urban growth remains sustainable and inclusive. [IV] 
 
Question 16. Which of the following best summarises paragraph 1? 
A. Rural migration leads to urban population growth, which often places pressure on infrastructure and 
services. 
B. People leave agricultural areas mainly to seek better opportunities in rapidly expanding urban centres. 
C. Rural migration is increasing globally, reshaping societies as people move due to push and pull factors. 
D. Urban growth resulting from migration can be economically beneficial despite infrastructure and 
service strain. 
Question 17. The word “interplay” in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to _____. 
A. combination 
B. differentiation 
C. recognition 
D. satisfaction 
Question 18. Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence in paragraph 3? 
While urban economies may benefit from an expanded labour force, rural communities often 
experience depopulation and a loss of human capital.   
A. An expanded urban workforce brings economic gains, whereas rural regions suffer population decline 
and talent loss. 
B. Urban labour expansion largely occurs because rural communities continue to lose both population and 
skilled workers.

TUẦN SỐ 08 
 
VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
TÀI LIỆU LIVESTREAM ĐỘC QUYỀN 
VIP 90 (2026): TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM BỘ CÂU HỎI  
DỄ TRÚNG TỦ & LUYỆN BỘ ĐỀ TINH HOA  
[V90.08.05B] THI ONLINE: TINH HOA ĐỌC HIỂU  
 
(BUỔI 12) – ĐỀ VẬN DỤNG 
Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương  
 
 
LEVEL 1 
Text 1 
 
A livable city is not just a place with tall buildings and busy streets; it is somewhere people actually 
enjoy living. First of all, it should be clean and safe, so residents do not have to worry about pollution or 
crime around them. A good public transport system is also important, because nobody likes being stuck 
in traffic for hours. In addition, a livable city needs green spaces like parks, where people can relax, 
exercise, or simply escape from the noise. Affordable housing and friendly communities make life easier 
and more pleasant. In short, a livable city is one that balances convenience, comfort, and a bit of charm—
because life is too short to live somewhere that feels like a daily struggle. 
 
Question 1. Which of the following is NOT mentioned as a feature of a livable city? 
A. Clean and safe environment 
B. Advanced healthcare systems  
C. Good public transport system 
D. Green spaces like parks 
Question 2. The word “them” in the text refers to ______. 
A. tall buildings  
B. residents  
C. busy streets 
D. people  
Question 3. The word “Affordable” in the text is opposite in meaning to _______. 
A. Expensive 
B. Helpful 
C. Reasonable 
D. Flexible  
 
 
Text 2 
 
Traffic congestion is a serious problem in many big cities around the world. For example, in 
Tokyo, millions of people travel every day, making roads and trains extremely crowded, especially during 
rush hour. Similarly, London often faces heavy traffic, and drivers sometimes spend hours just moving a 
short distance. In developing cities like Hanoi, the rapid increase in motorbikes and cars has made the 
situation even worse. These examples show that congestion is not only a local issue but a global challenge. 
Without better planning and public transport, city life can quickly turn into a daily struggle rather than a 
pleasant experience. 
 
Question 4. Which of the following is NOT mentioned as a city facing traffic congestion? 
A. New York 
 
B. Tokyo 
 
C. London 
 
D. Hanoi 
Question 5. The word “increase” in the text is OPPOSITE in meaning to _______.  
A. rise 
 
B. transport 
 
C. narrative 
 
D. decline  
 
 
Trang 52

VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
Question 6. According to the text, what may result from poor planning and public transport? 
A. Urban conditions may improve and reduce traffic congestion significantly. 
B. City life may become a daily struggle instead of a pleasant experience.  
C. The environment may become more comfortable and convenient for residents. 
D. Transportation systems may operate more efficiently in crowded areas. 
 
 
LEVEL 2 
Text 3 
 
Rural-to-urban migration has become an increasingly common trend in many parts of the world, 
as people leave the countryside in search of better living conditions. In countries like Vietnam and India, 
large numbers of young workers move to major cities such as Ho Chi Minh City or Mumbai, hoping to 
find higher-paying jobs, better education, and access to modern healthcare services. Urban areas also offer 
a more vibrant lifestyle, with greater exposure to technology, entertainment, and cultural diversity, which 
can be highly appealing to younger generations. 
 
However, this rapid migration brings several challenges. Cities often struggle with overcrowding, 
traffic congestion, and rising living costs, making life stressful for many newcomers. Rural areas may 
face labour shortages, especially in agriculture, which can slow down local development. Therefore, 
governments should invest more in rural infrastructure and job creation, so that people can have real 
choices instead of being pushed to leave their hometowns. 
 
Question 7. According to the passage, why has rural-to-urban migration become an increasing trend? 
A. Because cities provide more stable weather conditions. 
B. Because people seek better living conditions in cities.  
C. Because urban governments offer financial incentives. 
D. Because transportation systems make it easier to travel. 
Question 8. Which of the following is NOT mentioned in paragraph 1 as something urban areas provide? 
A. Better environmental quality 
B. Higher-paying job opportunities 
C. Better education and healthcare 
D. Greater access to technology 
Question 9. Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence in paragraph 2? 
Rural areas may face labour shortages, especially in agriculture, which can slow down local 
development. 
A. Farming in rural areas always provides sufficient labour to support rapid development. 
B. Rural areas usually have enough workers in agriculture and develop at a stable pace. 
C. The countryside may lack workers, particularly in farming, hindering regional growth.  
D. Local development improves when more workers leave agriculture in rural regions. 
Question 10. Which of the following is TRUE according to the passage? 
A. Governments discourage investment in rural infrastructure to promote urban growth. 
B. Urban areas usually reduce stress levels and improve living conditions for all newcomers. 
C. Rural regions always develop faster when large numbers of workers move to cities. 
D. Rapid migration can cause overcrowding, traffic congestion, and rising living costs in cities.  
 
 
 
 
Trang 53

VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
Text 4 
 
Waste management in urban areas is not simply a technical issue but a reflection of deeper 
structural problems in modern cities. Rapid urbanisation has led to excessive consumption, yet waste 
systems often remain outdated and reactive rather than preventive. As a result, cities struggle with 
inefficient collection, overfilled landfills, and a heavy reliance on dumping instead of sustainable 
treatment. This reveals a clear mismatch between economic growth and environmental responsibility. 
 
More critically, the problem is closely linked to governance and public behaviour. Authorities 
may introduce policies, but weak enforcement and limited public awareness reduce their effectiveness. 
At the same time, consumers continue to generate large amounts of waste without considering long-term 
consequences. Therefore, real progress requires a systemic shift—from linear consumption patterns to 
circular models, stronger regulations, and a change in mindset. Otherwise, waste will remain an 
unavoidable burden rather than a manageable resource. 
 
Question 11. According to the passage, although rapid urbanisation leads to excessive consumption, 
______. 
A. waste collection systems become less sustainable and effective over time 
B. cities cannot easily manage waste through modern and efficient treatment systems 
C. environmental responsibility is always reduced alongside economic growth 
D. waste management systems are often outdated and reactive instead of preventive  
Question 12. The word “reveals” in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to _______. 
A. shows 
B. hides 
C. ignores 
D. changes 
Question 13. The word “their” in paragraph 2 refers to _______. 
A. authorities  
B. policies 
C. public awareness  
D. consumers  
Question 14. Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence in paragraph 2? 
More critically, the problem is closely linked to governance and public behaviour. 
A. Public behaviour has an influence on government decisions in waste management. 
B. The problem is mainly caused by technical limitations in waste treatment systems. 
C. More importantly, the issue is strongly connected to how people govern and behave.  
D. Governance and public actions are separate factors that rarely affect each other. 
Question 15. Which of the following is TRUE according to the passage? 
A. Cities often rely on dumping instead of using sustainable waste treatment methods. 
B. Waste management systems in urban areas are preventive and well-developed. 
C. Public awareness is always high, so waste generation is carefully controlled. 
D. Strong regulations are fully enforced, making waste problems easy to manage. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Trang 54

VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
LEVEL 3 
Text 5 
 
Balancing development and sustainability has become one of the most pressing dilemmas of the 
modern world. Economic growth is often prioritised, especially in rapidly developing countries such as 
Vietnam, where industrialisation and urban expansion promise higher living standards, increased 
employment opportunities, and deeper global integration. [I] However, this progress frequently comes at 
the expense of natural resources, environmental quality, and long-term ecological stability. 
 
The tension lies in the fact that short-term economic gains can undermine future prosperity. 
Overexploitation of resources, pollution, and habitat destruction may boost productivity today but create 
irreversible damage tomorrow. [II] This contradiction exposes the limits of traditional development 
models that prioritise output over sustainability. Without strategic planning and strict control, growth can 
become self-defeating, gradually eroding the very foundations on which it depends. 
 
[III] Governments must integrate sustainability into economic strategies through stricter 
regulations, investment in green technologies, and long-term planning frameworks. Equally important is 
the role of individuals and businesses in adopting responsible consumption and production practices. [IV] 
Only by aligning development with environmental protection can societies ensure progress that is not only 
rapid but also resilient, inclusive, and truly sustainable. 
 
Question 16. Maintaining a balance between development and sustainability _______. 
A. is one of the most pressing dilemmas in the modern world 
B. is no longer a concern in rapidly developing countries today 
C. depends on short-term economic growth in all situations 
D. affects environmental issues without economic consequences 
Question 17. The word “undermine” in paragraph 2 is opposite in meaning to _______. 
A. reinforce 
B. weaken 
C. damage 
D. reduce 
Question 18. Which of the following best summarises paragraph 2? 
A. Long-term prosperity can be achieved without controlling pollution or managing natural resources 
carefully. 
B. Economic growth always strengthens environmental protection and ensures long-term stability for 
future development. 
C. Resource use and industrial expansion rarely affect ecosystems or create risks for future economic 
sustainability. 
D. Short-term economic gains can damage resources and environments, making growth unsustainable 
over time.  
Question 19. Where in the passage does the following sentence best fit? 
Therefore, achieving a balance requires a fundamental shift in both policy and mindset. 
A. [I] 
B. [II] 
C. [III] 
D. [IV]  
Question 20. What can be inferred from the passage? 
A. Uncontrolled growth may harm long-term sustainability and development. 
B. Sustainable development requires no major changes in policies or behavior. 
C. Economic growth always benefits the environment in developing countries. 
D. Individuals play no role in achieving sustainability and development balance. 
 
 
Trang 55

TÀI LIỆU LIVESTREAM ĐỘC QUYỀN 
VIP 90 (2026): TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM BỘ CÂU HỎI  
DỄ TRÚNG TỦ & LUYỆN BỘ ĐỀ TINH HOA  
[V90.08.05A] THI ONLINE: TINH HOA ĐỌC HIỂU  
 
(BUỔI 12) – ĐỀ NÂNG CAO 
Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương  
 
 
Read the following passage and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct answer to each of 
the questions from 1 to 8. 
In recent years, the concept of smart cities has gained considerable traction as urban populations 
continue to expand and governments seek more efficient ways to manage increasingly complex systems. 
Rather than relying solely on conventional infrastructure, these cities integrate digital technologies, 
including sensors, mobile networks, and data platforms, into everyday operations. This shift reflects a 
broader ambition to create urban environments that are not only technologically advanced but also more 
responsive to citizens’ needs and behaviours. 
At the core of this transformation lies the extensive use of big data, which is collected through a 
wide array of sources such as mobile phones, interconnected devices, and urban monitoring systems. 
These continuous streams of information are processed and analysed in real time, enabling city authorities 
to make informed decisions regarding traffic flow, energy distribution, and emergency response. In this 
context, data serves as a crucial tool that allows cities to anticipate potential problems, rather than simply 
reacting to them after they occur. 
However, the growing dependence on data-driven systems has also generated significant 
concerns, particularly in relation to privacy and surveillance. As vast quantities of personal 
information are gathered, often without individuals being fully aware of it, questions arise about 
ownership, consent, and accountability. If such issues are not addressed through transparent governance 
and strict regulations, public trust may gradually erode, undermining the effectiveness of smart city 
initiatives. 
Despite these challenges, smart cities remain a compelling vision for future urban development, 
especially as technological capabilities continue to advance. When supported by ethical frameworks and 
inclusive policies, they can enhance governance, improve resource allocation, and foster more liveable 
environments. In the long term, their success will depend not only on technological innovation but also 
on the ability of policymakers to strike a careful balance between efficiency, security, and individual 
rights.  
 
Question 1. All of the following are integrated in everyday operations in smart cities EXCEPT _____.  
A. mobile networks  
 
B. sensors  
 
C. public parks  
 
D. data platforms 
Question 2. The word “anticipate” in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to _____.  
A. suspect 
 
B. expect 
 
C. compare 
 
D. assess  
 
 
 
Trang 56

Question 3. Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence in paragraph 3? 
However, the growing dependence on data-driven systems has also generated significant concerns, 
particularly in relation to privacy and surveillance. 
A. Significant worries about personal privacy and monitoring have arisen from increasing reliance on 
digital data systems. 
B. Growing concerns about personal privacy and monitoring have encouraged greater reliance on digital 
information systems. 
C. The expansion of digital information systems is driven by rising public concern over privacy and 
monitoring practices. 
D. Concerns over personal privacy and monitoring have declined as reliance on digital data systems 
increases. 
Question 4. The word “erode” in paragraph is OPPOSITE in meaning to _____. 
A. decline 
B. fluctuate 
C. collapse 
D. increase  
Question 5. The word “they” in paragraph 4 refers to ____. 
A. frameworks 
B. policies 
C. smart cities 
D. capabilities 
Question 6. Which of the following is TRUE about smart cities according to the passage?  
A. Modern urban areas depend on older systems instead of adopting advanced technologies. 
B. Analytical processing enables officials to foresee issues instead of responding afterwards. 
C. Concerns about personal information are addressed through laws in many urban areas. 
D. Scientific progress ensures future success without balancing safety and individual freedoms. 
Question 7. Which paragraph mentions the role of real-time data in supporting urban decision-making? 
A. Paragraph 1 
B. Paragraph 2 
C. Paragraph 3 
D. Paragraph 4 
Question 8. Which paragraph mentions a key condition for the success of smart cities?  
A. Paragraph 1 
B. Paragraph 2 
C. Paragraph 3 
D. Paragraph 4 
 
Read the following passage and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct answer to each of 
the questions from 9 to 18. 
In an era defined by relentless urban expansion, where metropolitan centres have long been 
regarded as the epicentres of opportunity and innovation, a subtle yet significant demographic reversal 
has begun to emerge. Counter-urbanisation, a process whereby individuals relocate from densely 
populated cities to rural or semi-rural areas, reflects a growing disillusionment with the pressures 
associated with urban living. Although cities continue to attract investment and talent, an increasing 
number of people are reconsidering the long-term sustainability of such environments. 
This shift is underpinned by a confluence of economic, social, and technological factors. Escalating 
living costs, particularly in housing, have rendered urban life financially untenable for many, while 
advancements in digital connectivity have enabled remote work to become a viable alternative. [I] 
Consequently, individuals are no longer tethered to specific geographic locations, allowing them to 
prioritise quality of life over proximity to traditional employment hubs. In addition, rural areas, once 
perceived as lacking in amenities, have undergone considerable development, thereby narrowing the 
disparity between urban and non-urban regions. 
[II] Nevertheless, the transition is not devoid of complexities, as counter-urbanisation presents 
both opportunities and challenges for receiving communities. While an influx of new residents may 
stimulate local economies and revitalise declining areas, it can also exert pressure on limited 
infrastructure and disrupt established social dynamics. [III]  Long-standing inhabitants may experience

The rapid expansion of digital platforms has created an environment in which connection, identity, 
and social validation are increasingly mediated through constant online presence, a condition that, while 
often framed as empowering, (18) _____. Among girls and young women in particular, the persistence of 
social media use cannot be understood simply as a matter of preference, since it is deeply shaped by what 
is commonly termed the ‘fear of missing out’ (FOMO), which compels individuals to remain engaged 
even when their experiences are negative or distressing. Research conducted by Girlguiding indicates that 
more than half of girls aged 11 to 21 feel unable to take a break from social media despite exposure to 
harmful content, suggesting that (19) _____.

encountering cyberstalking or unsolicited images, while a large majority experience sexism in daily digital 
interactions. Although such experiences weaken confidence and increase anxiety, users often remain 
active (20) _____.  
At the same time, the data reveals a deeper contradiction in the perceived value of digital 
connectivity, (21) _____. This suggests that the promise of connection, which sustains prolonged 
engagement, is often not realised in practice, thereby reinforcing a form of participation that is habitual 
rather than fulfilling. Nevertheless, a notable proportion of respondents continue to express hope and 
curiosity about the future, indicating that, despite current challenges, there remains potential for reform 
through education, policy intervention, and (22) _____.

Sleep plays a crucial role in shaping how memories are stored and recalled, yet recent research 
suggests that its benefits extend beyond simple retention. A study led by Kimberly Fenn at Michigan State 
University highlights how sleep may actively reduce errors in memory, offering valuable insights for both 
academic and everyday situations. While memory has long been understood as a process strengthened by 
rest, this research shifts attention towards the accuracy of recall, especially in distinguishing correct 
information from misleading or false details. 
In the study, college students were exposed to lists of words through reading or listening tasks, 
after which their ability to recognise these words was tested twelve hours later. One group completed the 
task after a full day without sleep, whereas another group was tested after a period of rest. The findings 
were consistent across multiple experiments: participants who had slept were significantly less likely to 
select incorrect words, indicating a lower susceptibility to false memories. This suggests that sleep does
