not merely reinforce memory but also refines it, allowing individuals to filter out inaccuracies more 
effectively. 
Although the exact mechanism remains unclear, researchers propose that sleep may strengthen the 
‘source’ of a memory, meaning the context in which information is first acquired. When this contextual 
detail is preserved, individuals are better able to judge whether a memory is reliable. Alternatively, 
those who remain awake for extended periods may experience cognitive overload, as new information 
interferes with previously learned material, thereby increasing the likelihood of confusion and error. 
The implications of these findings are far-reaching, particularly for populations vulnerable to 
memory decline. Older adults, who are generally more prone to false memories, may benefit from 
improved sleep patterns that enhance memory accuracy. While further investigation is necessary, this 
research offers a promising perspective on how a simple habit - adequate sleep - could contribute to better 
cognitive performance and overall quality of life. 
(Adapted from Complete IELTS)

The prospect of driverless robot taxis has emerged as a compelling intersection between 
technological innovation and environmental sustainability, particularly in the context of escalating 
concerns about climate change. As urban populations expand and transport demand intensifies, 
conventional car ownership models—characterised by inefficiency and excessive emissions—are 
increasingly scrutinised. [I] In this evolving landscape, autonomous vehicles, especially when deployed 
as shared taxi services, are being reimagined as a transformative solution capable of redefining mobility 
patterns while simultaneously mitigating environmental degradation. 
Central to this vision is the remarkable potential of autonomous electric taxis to significantly 
reduce greenhouse gas emissions. Studies suggest that such vehicles could emit up to 94 per cent fewer 
emissions per mile than traditional petrol-powered cars, primarily due to their lightweight design and 
energy efficiency. This reduction is further amplified by the concept of ‘right-sizing,’ whereby vehicles 
are tailored to the number of passengers on each journey, thereby minimising unnecessary energy 
consumption. Moreover, the anticipated transition towards renewable energy sources in electricity 
generation is expected to further diminish the carbon footprint of these systems, reinforcing their 
environmental advantages.  
Beyond emissions reduction, autonomous taxis offer a range of operational efficiencies that could 
enhance the sustainability of urban transport networks. [II] By optimising routes, smoothing acceleration 
and braking, and reducing congestion through coordinated movement, they can achieve incremental yet 
cumulative energy savings. Additionally, their integration into shared mobility systems may decrease the 
total number of vehicles required. [III] Such systemic changes could fundamentally reshape urban 
environments, promoting more efficient land use and lowering overall resource consumption. 
Nevertheless, the environmental benefits of driverless taxis are not guaranteed and depend 
heavily on patterns of adoption and policy regulation. While shared usage models are associated with 
substantial emission reductions, increased convenience and lower travel costs may paradoxically 
encourage greater travel demand, potentially offsetting these gains. [IV] Consequently, the extent to 
which autonomous taxis contribute to climate mitigation will ultimately hinge on the implementation of 
supportive policies that prioritise shared mobility, renewable energy integration, and sustainable urban 
planning. 
(Adapted from https://www.nationalgeographic.com)

B. are increasingly viewed as outdated transport solutions  
C. are more associated with inefficiency and high emissions  
D. are more capable of reducing environmental damage effectively

VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
TÀI LIỆU LIVESTREAM ĐỘC QUYỀN 
VIP 90 (2026): TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM BỘ CÂU HỎI  
DỄ TRÚNG TỦ & LUYỆN BỘ ĐỀ TINH HOA  
[V90.ĐH.08] ĐỌC HIỂU C1 – C2 BỘ ĐỀ DỄ TRÚNG TỦ 
 
(BUỔI 8) 
Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương  
 
 
Read the following passage and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct answer to each of 
the questions from 1 to 8. 
The 1960 street map of Lagos, Nigeria, shows a small western-style coastal city surrounded by a 
few semi-rural African villages. Paved roads quickly turn to dirt, and fields to forest. There are few 
buildings over six floors high and not many cars. 
No one foresaw what happened next. In just two generations, Lagos grew 100-fold, from under 
200,000 people to nearly 20 million. Today, one of the world’s 10 largest cities, it sprawls across nearly 
1,000 sq km. Vastly wealthy in parts, it is largely chaotic and impoverished. Most residents live in 
informal settlements, or slums. The great majority are not connected to piped water or a sanitation system. 
The city’s streets are choked with traffic, its air is full of fumes, and its main dump covers 40 hectares 
and receives 10,000 metric tons of waste a day. 
But new research suggests that the changes Lagos has seen in the last 60 years may be nothing to 
what might take place in the next 60. If Nigeria’s population continues to grow and people move to cities 
at the same rate as now, Lagos could become the world’s largest metropolis, home to 85 or 100 million 
people. By 2100, it is projected to be home to more people than California or Britain today, and to stretch 
hundreds of miles – with enormous environmental effects. 
Hundreds of far smaller cities across Asia and Africa could also grow exponentially, say the 
Canadian demographers Daniel Hoornweg and Kevin Pope at the University of Ontario Institute of 
Technology. They suggest that Niamey, the barely known capital of Niger, a West African country with 
the highest birth rate in the world, could explode from a city of fewer than one million people today to be 
the world’s eighth-largest city, with 46 million people, in 2100. Sleepy Blantyre in southern Malawi could 
mushroom to the size of New York City today. 
(Adapted from https://www.theguardian.com/) 
 
Question 1. Which of the following is NOT mentioned in paragraph 1 about Lagos in 1960? 
A. Limited number of cars on the roads 
B. A small coastal city with a Western-style 
C. Few buildings higher than six floors 
D. A large number of high-rise buildings 
Question 2. Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence in paragraph 2? 
Vastly wealthy in parts, it is largely chaotic and impoverished. 
A. Although some areas are very rich, most remain disordered and poor. 
B. The entire city is wealthy, well-organised, and economically developed. 
C. Most parts of the city are rich, with only a few poor and chaotic areas. 
D. The city is mainly prosperous, with limited signs of poverty or disorder. 
Question 3. The word “sprawls” in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to _______. 
A. gathers 
B. concentrates 
C. stretches  
D. shrinks 
 
Trang 68

Question 4. The word “it” in paragraph 3 refers to ________. 
A. the world 
B. the same rate  
C. Lagos  
D. California 
Question 5. The word “Sleepy” in paragraph 4 is OPPOSITE in meaning to _______.  
A. lively 
B. tired 
C. quiet 
D. calm 
Question 6. Which of the following is TRUE according to the passage? 
A. Most cities in Africa and Asia are expected to grow more and more rapidly by 2100. 
B. Lagos is currently the largest modern city in the world with over 100 million people. 
C. Lagos may reach a population of up to 100 million if current growth trends continue.  
D. Niamey has already owned a population comparable to New York City today. 
Question 7. In which paragraph is the future growth of smaller cities like Niamey mentioned? 
A. Paragraph 1 
B. Paragraph 2 
C. Paragraph 3 
D. Paragraph 4 
Question 8. In which paragraph is the prediction about Lagos becoming the world’s largest city 
mentioned? 
A. Paragraph 1 
B. Paragraph 3 
C. Paragraph 4 
D. Paragraph 2 
 
Read the following passage and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct answer to each of 
the questions from 9 to 18. 
In 1950, 40% of the world’s human population lived in rural areas. Today, that figure has been cut 
in half according to the UN, as 80% of people on the planet now live in towns and cities. Our urban areas 
continue to expand to accommodate our growing population. The same UN report shows that since 1975, 
the amount of land classified as “built-up” and occupied by humans grew almost twice as fast as the global 
population. As a result, the built-up area per person rose from 43 m² to 63 m². In a new study published 
in Nature Cities, researchers have found that this rapid urbanization, combined with competition from 
agriculture for flat land, is driving city growth into hillsides, mountains, and other upland areas. The 
consequences for the planet's biodiversity are dire. 
Using 30m-resolution spatial landform maps that cover the globe, the team showed that between 
2000 and 2020, hillside urban areas grew by 11.65 million hectares (Mha) globally—an area larger than 
the country of Iceland (10.1 Mha). [I] In terms of geographical extent, Asia saw the greatest increase in 
hillside urbanization, at 5.09 Mha over the twenty-year study period, with China representing the majority 
(3.00 Mha) of that total. Hillside areas are where most residual terrestrial biodiversity resides. [II] Habitats 
such as forests and wetlands are refuges for countless species of plants and animals. As cities begin to 
encroach on hillside areas, natural habitats are lost or fragmented, isolating species and 
exacerbating soil erosion. 
By utilizing land-use maps, the study demonstrates the direct consequences of hillside urbanization 
on habitat loss and fragmentation at global, continental, national, and local scales. Globally, it accounted 
for an estimated 6.73 Mha of habitat loss between 2000 and 2020. Hillside urbanization alone was 
responsible for 31.11% of forest loss, 3.50% of grassland loss, 26.79% of shrubland loss, 3.26% of 
wetland loss, and 10.80% of unused land loss. [III] 
The researchers also examined the world’s biodiversity hotspots—regions particularly rich in 
unique biological resources and under severe threat. These regions occupy less than 2.4% of Earth’s land 
surface but are home to more than half of the world’s plant species and at least 42% of all terrestrial 
vertebrate species. [IV] The most affected region is the Mediterranean basin, which experienced a habitat 
loss rate of approximately 65%. 
(Adapted from https://www.forbes.com/) 
 
Trang 69

Question 9. According to the passage, the percentage of people living in rural areas today is _______. 
A. 20% 
B. 40% 
C. 60% 
D. 80% 
Question 10. The word “dire” in paragraph 1 can be best replaced by _______. 
A. minor 
B. severe  
C. unclear 
D. positive 
Question 11. According to the passage, what causes cities to expand into hillsides and upland areas? 
A. Government policies encouraging development only in mountainous regions 
B. Increased biodiversity and the need to protect natural habitats in cities 
C. Rapid urbanization combined with competition for flat land from agriculture  
D. The availability of better infrastructure and transport in upland areas 
Question 12. Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence in paragraph 2? 
As cities begin to encroach on hillside areas, natural habitats are lost or fragmented, isolating 
species and exacerbating soil erosion.  
A. Urban growth in hillside areas damages habitats, though it allows species to stay together and does not 
increase soil erosion. 
B. As cities expand into hillside regions, habitats may be disturbed, but species remain connected, and 
soil erosion is limited. 
C. As cities expand into hillside regions, habitats are destroyed or broken up, separating species and 
worsening soil erosion.  
D. The spread of cities into hillsides can affect habitats, but species are not isolated, and soil erosion 
becomes more severe. 
Question 13. Which of the following best summarises paragraph 2? 
A. Hillside urbanization has expanded rapidly, especially in Asia, leading to habitat loss, species isolation, 
and increased soil erosion. 
B. Urban growth in hillside areas has been limited, causing only minor environmental changes and little 
impact on natural habitats. 
C. Most hillside regions remain protected despite urban expansion, allowing ecosystems to stay stable 
and species to survive easily. 
D. The spread of cities into upland areas mainly improves land use without significantly affecting 
biodiversity or environmental conditions. 
Question 14. The phrase “These regions” in paragraph 4 refers to ________. 
A. unique biological resources  
B. the world’s biodiversity hotspots 
C. Earth’s land surface  
 
D. The researchers 
Question 15. Where in the passage does the following sentence best fit? 
“However, more than one-third (~33.3%) of all hillside urban expansion is occurring in these areas.” 
A. [I] 
B. [II] 
C. [III] 
D. [IV] 
Question 16. Which of the following figures is NOT correct according to the passage? 
A. Hillside urban areas increased by 15.65 million hectares. 
B. Built-up area per person rose from 43 m² to 63 m². 
C. Hillside urban areas grew by 11.65 million hectares globally. 
D. Biodiversity hotspots occupy less than 2.4% of Earth’s land surface.

A. Population growth has little impact on land use, as cities expand slowly and do not significantly affect

Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương  
 
 
Read the following texts and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct option that best fits 
each of the numbered blanks from 1 to 30.  
Text 1 
Wise budgeting (1) _____ an important role in managing personal finances effectively. To avoid 
unnecessary spending, people should (2) _____ a habit of tracking their daily expenses and setting clear 
limits. It is also important to (3) _____ in mind that saving a small amount regularly can lead to long-term 
financial stability. By following these simple steps, individuals can build better financial habits and feel 
more confident about their future.

B. plays 
C. brings 
D. lands  
Question 2. A. get 
B. fix 
C. meet 
D. make 
 
Question 3. A. give

D. go  
 
 
Text 2 
Joining the Songkran festival can be exciting if you prepare well in advance. Before travelling, it is 
important to (4) _____ research about local customs and popular places to visit. You should also (5) _____ 
reservations early, as hotels and transport can be fully booked during this busy time. In addition, (6) _____ 
into consideration the weather and safety rules, so you can enjoy the water activities comfortably and 
responsibly. 
 
Question 4. A. do 
B. have 
C. make 
D. fill  
Question 5. A. act 
B. make 
C. lay 
D. go  
Question 6. A. take 
B. put 
C. give 
D. ask  
 
 
Text 3 
Preserving heritage sites is essential because they reflect a (7) _____ of cultural pride in a community. 
However, many people (8) _____ these places for granted and fail to protect them properly. To deal with 
this issue, governments should (9) ______ a proactive approach by raising awareness and supporting 
conservation projects. In addition, (10) ______ rules are needed to control tourist activities and prevent 
damage to historical landmarks. By protecting these sites, societies can maintain their identity and pass 
valuable traditions on to future generations.

TÀI LIỆU LIVESTREAM ĐỘC QUYỀN 
VIP 90 (2026): TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM BỘ CÂU HỎI  
DỄ TRÚNG TỦ & LUYỆN BỘ ĐỀ TINH HOA  
[V90.09.02] LÀM CHỦ KIẾN THỨC NGỮ PHÁP    
 
CÂU ĐIỀU KIỆN  
Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương  
 
 
A. Câu điều kiện loại 1 (có thể xảy ra ở hiện tại hoặc tương lai) 
Cấu trúc: If S + V(htđ), S will V 
Ex: If the weather is nice, we will go out tomorrow. 
 
B. Câu điều kiện loại 2 (trái với hiện tại) 
Cấu trúc: If S + V(qkđ), S would/could V 
Ex: If I were you, I would apply for that job. 
 
C. Câu điều kiện loại 3 (trái với quá khứ) 
Cấu trúc: If S + V(qkht), S would have PII 
Ex: If I had known her address, I would have sent her this present. 
 
D. Câu điều kiện trộn (loại 3 – loại 2) 
Cấu trúc: If S + V(qkht), S would V 
Ex: If I hadn’t stayed up too late last night, I wouldn’t feel tired now. 
 
E. Một số loại câu điều kiện khác 
- Unless + mệnh đề HTĐ, S will V: nếu … không 
Ex: Unless it rains, we will go out. 
 
- Supposing (that): giả sử 
Ex: Supposing (that) he had access to the Internet, he could attend the meeting.  
 
- As long as = So long as = Provided that: miễn là, với điều kiện là  
Ex: I will lend you the money so long as you pay me back. 
 
- otherwise: kẻo không thì 
Ex: He helped me, otherwise I would have lost my money.  
 
- or/or else: kẻo không 
Ex: Hurry up or you’ll be late for school. 
 
- in case: phòng khi 
Ex: Take this map in case you get lost. 
 
 
Trang 9

TUẦN SỐ 09 
 
VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
- but for = without + Noun: nếu không có … 
Ex: But for his help, I would have died. 
 
- If it weren’t for + Noun, S would V/ If it hadn’t been for + Noun, S would have PII: nếu không có ….  
Ex: If it hadn’t been for his determination, he wouldn’t have won the gold medal. 
 
* Lưu ý: 
Khi viết lại với câu điều kiện loại 2 và loại 3, cần viết lại sao cho trái với thực tế ở câu gốc. 
Ví dụ: I don’t have enough money, so I can’t go travelling this summer.  
 
 Cách viết đúng: If I had enough money, I could go travelling this summer. 
 Cách viết sai: If I didn’t have enough money, I couldn’t go travelling this summer. 
 
 
PRACTICE 
Exercise 1: Read the following texts and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct option that 
best fits each of the numbered blanks from 1 to 25.  
Text 1 
Adolescence is a stage when young people begin to shape their identity and question expectations around 
them. If they (1) _____ guidance from supportive adults, they can build confidence and make wiser 
decisions. Teenagers need to manage school pressure and relationships carefully; (2) _____, they may 
feel overwhelmed and lose direction. In addition, teenagers may face peer pressure and end up engaging 
in risky behaviour. Unless they (3) ____ how to say no, they will be easily influenced and follow choices 
that lead them in the wrong direction. 
 
Question 1. A. receive 
B. received 
C. will receive 
D. had received  
Question 2. A. however 
B. thus 
C. otherwise 
D. unless 
Question 3. A. learned 
B. had learned 
C. learn 
D. were learning 
 
Text 2 
Choosing a career path is an important step for teenagers as they begin to understand their strengths and 
interests. They can explore different options through internships and part-time jobs, as long as they (4) 
_____ open to new experiences. Many students feel uncertain about their future because they lack clear 
direction. (5) _____ proper guidance, they may struggle to make suitable choices. In addition, (6) _____ 
teenagers understand their abilities and long-term goals, they will find it difficult to choose a suitable 
career path. 
 
Question 4. A. stayed 
B. will stay 
C. were staying 
D. stay 
Question 5. A. Due to 
B. Without 
C. In spite of 
D. Regardless of  
Question 6. A. when 
B. if 
C. because 
D. unless

Text 3 
Self-driving cars are becoming more common as technology improves and cities look for safer transport 
solutions. (7) _____, they can reduce accidents caused by human error. Many people still hesitate to trust 
them because of safety concerns and possible system failures. To address these worries, engineers 
continue to improve sensors and control systems. As long as the technology proves reliable in real 
conditions, (8) _____. (9) _____, trust would remain low. With greater public trust, self-driving cars could 
become a normal part of daily life. If they were widely used, (10) _____. 
 
Question 7. 
A. Because they are used widely 
 
B. While they are used widely 
C. Though they are used widely 
 
D. If they are used widely 
Question 8. 
A. public confidence will gradually decline 
B. public confidence would gradually rise 
C. public confidence would gradually drop 
D. public confidence will gradually increase 
Question 9. 
A. But for these continuous improvements 
B. Despite these continuous improvements 
C. Due to these continuous improvements 
D. In place of these continuous improvements 
Question 10.  
A. they would increase the risk of accidents on a large scale  
B. they would make transport systems less efficient and harder to manage  
C. they would reduce people’s trust in modern technology over time  
D. they would transform travel and make transport more efficient 
 
Text 4 
Urban sprawl is a growing issue as cities expand outward to meet the demand for housing and space. (11) 
_____, it will place heavy pressure on transport systems and public services. Many people move to 
suburban areas for comfort, which gradually weakens the structure of city centres and increases reliance 
on private vehicles. Governments need to manage this expansion carefully. (12) ______, development 
can be directed in a more sustainable way. These policies also help protect green spaces and maintain a 
balanced urban structure. (13) _____, land use may become inefficient and difficult to control over time. 
Cities must invest in infrastructure and public transport. (14) _____. If it weren’t for these planning efforts, 
(15) _____. 
 
Question 11. 
A. Unless this trend continues 
 
B. If this trend continues 
C. Since this trend continues 
 
D. Though this trend continues 
Question 12.  
A. But for the enforcement of clear planning policies  
B. Unless clear planning policies are enforced 
C. Despite the enforcement of clear planning policies 
D. Provided that clear planning policies are enforced 
Question 13.  
A. Thanks to effective regulation  
B. If it hadn’t been for effective regulation 
C. Irrespective of effective regulation 
D. Without effective regulation

Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
Question 14.  
A. Otherwise, traffic congestion and environmental problems will become more serious 
B. Therefore, traffic congestion and environmental problems will become more serious 
C. However, traffic congestion and environmental problems will become more serious 
D. In fact, traffic congestion and environmental problems will become more serious 
Question 15.  
A. controlled development would improve steadily and support sustainability  
B. uncontrolled growth would spread rapidly and cause long-term damage 
C. urban areas would remain stable and maintain balanced environmental conditions  
D. city planning would become more effective and ensure sustainable future growth 
 
Text 5 
Independence is an essential stage in a teenager’s development as they begin to make decisions 
and understand responsibility. Parents often give them more freedom so that they can gain real-life 
experience. Teenagers should learn basic skills early, (16) _____. Many young people feel more confident 
when they are trusted, which helps them build a stronger sense of identity. (17) _____, they may make 
poor decisions without thinking carefully.   
At the same time, support from adults still plays an important role in guiding them. But for the 
help provided by parents and teachers, (18) _____. They are encouraged to plan ahead and think through 
their choices (19) _____. This preparation allows them to respond more calmly when facing difficulties. 
(20) _____, they will not become truly independent. 
 
Question 16. 
A. if they may struggle when they have to solve problems on their own 
B. though they may struggle when they have to solve problems on their own 
C. or they may struggle when they have to solve problems on their own 
D. since they may struggle when they have to solve problems on their own 
Question 17. 
A. If teenagers are given too much freedom 
 
B. Though teenagers are given too much freedom 
C. Because teenagers are given too much freedom 
D. Whereas teenagers are given too much freedom 
Question 18. 
A. many teenagers would easily develop independence without support  
B. many teenagers would gain independence quickly through their own efforts  
C. many teenagers would become fully independent at an early stage in life 
D. many teenagers would find it difficult to develop independence 
Question 19. 
A. while unexpected situations arise 
 
B. unless unexpected situations arise 
C. though unexpected situations arise 
 
D. in case unexpected situations arise

Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
Question 20.  
A. Having taken responsibility for their actions 
B. Unless teenagers take responsibility for their actions 
C. When teenagers take responsibility for their actions 
D. If teenagers take responsibility for their actions  
 
Choose the option that best paraphrases each of the following sentences. 
Question 21. An influx of rural migrants puts pressure on the city's infrastructure. 
A. If more people migrated to the city, it would put considerable pressure on its infrastructure.  
B. Unless people migrate to the city, its infrastructure will not be significantly strained.  
C. Supposing that fewer rural people migrated to the city, its infrastructure would not come under strain. 
D. But for an influx of rural migrants, the city’s infrastructure would not have come under strain. 
Question 22. By exploring new concepts on their own, students will develop the skills needed for 
independent learning. 
A. Provided that students explore new concepts on their own, they will develop the skills needed for 
independent learning. 
B. Supposing that students had explored new concepts on their own, they would have developed the skills 
needed for independent learning. 
C. If students didn’t explore new concepts on their own, they would develop the skills needed for 
independent learning. 
D. If students explored new concepts on their own, they would develop the skills needed for independent 
learning. 
Question 23. Frank discussions help resolve family conflicts and bridge the generation gap. 
A. Unless family members discuss frankly, their conflicts will be resolved, helping to narrow the 
generation gap.  
B. Supposing that family conflicts were resolved, frank discussions could help narrow the generation gap.  
C. If it hadn’t been for frank discussions, family conflicts would have escalated, widening the generation 
gap.  
D. Without frank discussions, family conflicts would be harder to resolve and the generation gap would 
widen. 
Question 24. With collective efforts, cultural heritage sites can be preserved for future generations. 
A. If it weren’t for collective efforts, cultural heritage sites could be preserved for future generations.  
B. Without joint efforts, cultural heritage sites could not be preserved for future generations.  
C. Future generations could help preserve cultural heritage sites if they were willing to join hands.  
D. Cultural heritage sites can be preserved provided that future generations are determined to join hands.  
Question 25. Citizens in smart cities feel a lack of privacy due to the presence of CCTV cameras. 
A. If CCTV cameras were installed in smart cities, citizens would suffer from a lack of privacy.  
B. Citizens in smart cities would have felt more secure supposing that CCTV cameras hadn’t been 
installed.  
C. But for the presence of CCTV cameras, citizens in smart cities will experience reduced privacy.   
D. If it weren’t for the presence of CCTV cameras in smart cities, citizens wouldn’t feel their privacy is 
compromised.   
 
 
 
Trang 13

Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương  
 
 
Exercise 1: Read the following texts and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct option that 
best fits each of the numbered blanks from 1 to 30.  
1. 
(1) _______ students explore different career options early, they will make better decisions about their 
future. (2) _______, they might struggle to find a career that truly suits them. Moreover, (3) _______ 
proper advice from teachers and parents, many students will even end up in jobs they may dislike. 
Therefore, career orientation is crucial for their long-term success and happiness. 
 
Question 1. A. Since 
B. Because 
C. If  
D. While 
Question 2. A. If 
B. As long as 
C. Supposing 
D. Otherwise 
Question 3. A. but for 
B. in addition to 
C. with regard to 
D. apart from 
 
2. 
Teaching children to help at home is not just about cleaning—it’s about preparing them for adulthood. 
Kids will struggle with independence in the future (4) _______ they learn basic chores. (5) _______ they 
are given even small responsibilities early, they will develop discipline and self-reliance. On the other 
hand, (6) _______ children don't have to do household chores, they will grow up feeling overwhelmed 
when facing real-life tasks on their own.  
 
Question 4. A. as long as 
B. while 
C. given that 
D. unless 
Question 5. A. Unless 
B. As long as  
C. Even though 
D. In spite of 
Question 6. A. so 
B. as 
C. if  
D. since 
 
3. 
In today’s world, technical knowledge alone is not enough for success. Students should also focus on soft 
skills (7) _______. Indeed, (8) _______, many professionals will find it difficult to adapt to workplace 
challenges. Moreover, the fact is that (9) _______. Therefore, balancing soft and hard skills is essential 
for career growth. 
 
Question 7. 
A. if they will struggle to communicate and collaborate in an effective way 
B. unless they struggle to communicate and collaborate effectively 
C. or they will struggle to communicate and collaborate effectively 
D. and some will struggle to communicate and collaborate effectively 
 
 
Trang 14

Question 8.  
A. with strong soft skills 
 
B. but for strong soft skills 
C. thanks to strong soft skills 
 
D. and strong soft skills 
Question 9. 
A. if employers prioritise both types of skills, their workforce will be more efficient 
B. unless employers’ workforce is inefficient, they will prioritise both types of skills  
C. employers prioritise both types of skills when their workforce will be more efficient 
D. employers who prioritise both types of skills have an inefficient workforce 
 
 
4.  
Yesterday, Andie invited me to a picnic in the park, and I agreed immediately. (10) _______. So, I tried 
to complete my assignments before noon (11) _______. Later, I bought some snacks and soft drinks (12) 
_______. The afternoon was so much fun. (13) _______. 
 
Question 10. 
A. I couldn’t join them even when having finished my homework 
B. Before I finished my homework, I had joined the trip 
C. As long as I finished my homework, I could join the trip 
D. Unless I finished my homework, I could join the trip 
Question 11. 
A. or I wouldn’t have enough time to prepare anything 
B. if I wouldn’t have enough time to prepare anything 
C. and I wouldn’t have enough time to prepare anything 
D. so that I wouldn’t have enough time to prepare anything 
Question 12.  
A. and there wasn’t enough food for everyone 
B. but there wasn’t enough food for everyone 
C. in case there wasn’t enough food for everyone 
D. so there wasn’t enough food for everyone 
Question 13. 
A. If it hadn’t been for Andie’s invitation, I might have missed a wonderful experience 
B. If Andie had invited me to the picnic, I might have missed a wonderful experience 
C. If Andie doesn’t invite me to the trip, I will miss a wonderful experience 
D. Unless Andie invites me to the trip, I might have missed a wonderful experience  
 
 
5. 
Self-learning is a crucial skill in today’s fast-changing world. (14) _______, they will be able to acquire 
new knowledge on their own. This skill is also essential for those who have entered the world of work, 
(15) _______. However, not everyone can develop self-learning easily. (16) _______. Moreover, this skill 
is best to be instilled early. (17) _______, students could become more adaptable and confident in their 
future careers.

Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
Question 27. Individuals will gain confidence in presenting their ideas by practising public speaking 
regularly. 
A. If individuals practised public speaking regularly, they would gain confidence in presenting their ideas. 
B. Supposing that individuals had practised public speaking regularly, they would have gained confidence 
in presenting their ideas. 
C. If individuals didn’t practise public speaking regularly, they would gain confidence in presenting their 
ideas. 
D. Provided that individuals practise public speaking regularly, they will gain confidence in presenting 
their ideas. 
Question 28. Strong teamwork enhances workplace efficiency and fosters better relationships among 
employees. 
A. Unless employees work as a team, workplace efficiency will improve, leading to stronger relationships. 
B. Supposing that workplace efficiency were already high, teamwork could still help employees build 
better relationships. 
C. But for strong teamwork, workplace efficiency could decline, and relationships among employees 
might suffer. 
D. If it hadn’t been for teamwork, employees wouldn’t have been able to build strong relationships at 
work. 
Question 29. With sustainable practices, natural resources can be protected for future generations. 
A. If it weren’t for sustainable practices, natural resources could be protected for future generations. 
B. Natural resources can be protected provided that future generations are determined to act responsibly.  
C. Future generations could help protect natural resources if they were willing to take action. 
D. Without sustainable practices, natural resources could not be protected for future generations. 
Question 30. People in big cities suffer from air pollution due to excessive vehicle emissions. 
A. If vehicle emissions were higher, air pollution in big cities would become severe. 
B. People in big cities would enjoy cleaner air if it weren’t for excessive vehicle emissions. 
C. But for vehicle emissions, air pollution in big cities will remain a serious issue. 
D. Supposing that fewer vehicles were on the road, air pollution in big cities could decrease significantly.

CÂU ĐIỀU KIỆN – ĐỀ NÂNG CAO  
Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương  
 
 
Read the following texts and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct option that best fits 
each of the numbered blanks from 1 to 12.  
Text 1 
In recent years, an increasing number of celebrities have embraced environmentally conscious 
lifestyles, using their influence to promote sustainable consumption and climate awareness. (1) _____, 
they can encourage their audiences to adopt more responsible habits. Their actions often include 
supporting eco-friendly brands, reducing waste, and investing in renewable initiatives, which collectively 
shape public perception of sustainability. 
At the same time, public reactions to such efforts are not always positive. Supposing celebrities 
exaggerate their environmental contributions, (2) _____. Some individuals attempt to minimise their 
impact through private measures, but these efforts often remain unnoticed by the wider public. (3) _____, 
many environmental campaigns would struggle to attract attention and gain broader support. 
Sustained commitment, however, is essential for long-term impact. (4) _____, their advocacy will 
appear more authentic and trustworthy. They need to maintain consistency between their public statements 
and personal behaviour, (5) ____. Unless their actions genuinely reflect their environmental message, (6) 
_____. Many of them also prepare for potential criticism in case their actions are misunderstood or 
misinterpreted by the public. 
 
Question 1.  
A. Unless they communicate these values consistently 
B. Given that they communicate these values consistently 
C. Whereas they communicate these values consistently 
D. If they communicate these values consistently 
Question 2. 
A. audiences may respond with greater enthusiasm towards their public image  
B. their influence could expand rapidly across different social media platforms  
C. environmental awareness might increase among followers and young audiences  
D. their credibility may quickly be questioned by critics and the media 
Question 3. 
A. Because of the attention generated by figures  
B. Although campaigns receive support from global groups 
C. But for the visibility created by well-known figures  
D. Since influencers promote sustainability across platforms 
 
 
 
Trang 19

B. As celebrities promote sustainable practices to audiences  
C. If celebrities are more transparent about their practices  
D. Because audiences expect honesty from public figures 
Question 5.  
A. which helps their public image remain stable  
B. as their reputation continues to improve  
C. and they can build stronger connections  
D. otherwise their influence may weaken over time 
Question 6.  
A. their efforts will receive stronger support  
 
B. they will gain more trust from followers  
C. audiences will become increasingly sceptical 
D. their influence will continue to expand 
 
Text 2 
As urban congestion intensifies and concerns about road safety continue to grow, self-driving 
vehicles are increasingly regarded as a promising solution for modern transport systems. (7) _____, these 
vehicles are designed to navigate complex environments with minimal human input. If they are introduced 
gradually, they can improve traffic flow while reducing accidents caused by human error. Many cities are 
therefore experimenting with pilot programmes to assess their performance under controlled conditions. 
(8) _____. Autonomous systems must be able to respond effectively to unpredictable situations, 
especially those involving ethical decisions. Unless manufacturers ensure consistent performance across 
a wide range of scenarios, (9) _____. Engineers are working to refine both software and hardware in order 
to enhance system stability and decision-making accuracy. 
Public perception ultimately determines (10) _____. Transparent communication plays a crucial 
role in shaping user confidence and expectations. Drivers also need to understand how these systems 
operate in practice. (11) _____. If users begin to recognise the practical benefits of autonomous vehicles, 
(12) _____, allowing this technology to become an integral part of everyday life. 
 
Question 7. 
A. Having powered by advanced algorithms and real-time data processing 
B. Though powered by advanced algorithms and real-time data processing 
C. Powered by advanced algorithms and real-time data processing 
D. If powered by advanced algorithms and real-time data processing 
Question 8. 
A. Therefore, concerns about safety and system reliability remain widely discussed today  
B. As earlier mentioned, issues of control and long-term responsibility continue to be debated  
C. As a result, questions about system limits and public safety still remain unresolved  
D. Despite their potential, questions about reliability and accountability remain unresolved 
Question 9.  
A. few experts remain cautious  
B. safety is no longer a concern 
C. public trust will remain limited  
D. governments may delay taking action

Question 13. Due to pervasive surveillance in urban environments, individual privacy is significantly 
compromised.

B. Pervasive surveillance in urban environments causes a significant loss of individual privacy. 
C. Individual privacy remains protected despite the widespread use of surveillance in cities. 
D. Urban surveillance systems enhance public safety without affecting individual privacy. 
Question 14. As a result of prolonged economic mismanagement, public confidence in financial 
institutions has deteriorated.

C. Public confidence in financial institutions has remained largely stable despite prolonged economic 
mismanagement over time. 
D. Effective and well-coordinated financial policies have strengthened public confidence in financial 
institutions over time. 
Question 15. Pervasive exposure to algorithm-driven content leads to heightened cognitive bias among

complex information in digital environments.  
C. Users are increasingly able to engage critically with online content despite the widespread presence of 
algorithm-driven systems.  
D. The growing influence of algorithm-driven content has resulted from declining awareness among users 
in the digital context.

Text 1 
Early this morning, the city was covered in (1) _____ fog, making it difficult to see even nearby buildings. 
Later in the day, (2) _____ rain began to fall, causing traffic delays and flooding in several areas. Given 
the recent changes in weather patterns, such conditions (3) _____ as no surprise to local residents, who 
have grown used to sudden shifts between fog and intense rainfall.

B. wide 
C. large 
D. thick

B. strong 
C. deep 
D. high

Text 2 
During a group project at school, I (4) ____ a chance to work closely with new classmates. At first, we 
were quite different, but we soon realised that we (5) ____ a lot in common, especially our interest in 
creative ideas. Because of that, we were able to support each other and share responsibilities. When one 
member struggled with a task, the others were always willing to (6) _____ him a hand, which made our 
teamwork more effective and enjoyable. 
 
Question 4. A. made 
B. got 
C. took 
D. faced 
Question 5. A. had 
B. made 
C. put 
D. met  
Question 6. A. fix 
B. leave 
C. give 
D. ask  
 
 
Text 3 
Stereotypes against men still exist in many societies, often shaping how people judge their behaviour. 
Some people (7) ____ a belief that men must always be strong and unemotional, which can create pressure 
in daily life. As a result, men are often expected to (8) ____ high standards for themselves and avoid 
showing weakness. However, this can make it difficult for them to (9) _____ an ear to others or share 
their own feelings. When these pressures build up, some may even (10) _____ their temper more easily. 
Challenging such stereotypes can help create a more balanced and understanding society. 
 
Question 7. A. kick 
B. set 
C. make 
D. hold 
Question 8. A. face 
B. lend 
C. set 
D. fill

TÀI LIỆU LIVESTREAM ĐỘC QUYỀN 
VIP 90 (2026): TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM BỘ CÂU HỎI  
DỄ TRÚNG TỦ & LUYỆN BỘ ĐỀ TINH HOA  
[V90.09.04] TINH HOA ĐỌC HIỂU (BUỔI 13) 
 
 
Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương  
LEVEL 1 
 
 
 
Text 1 
Dining customs are different across countries, and they reflect each culture’s values and traditions. 
In some places, people follow strict rules at the table, while in others, meals are more relaxed and 
informal. For example, in Japan, people often say a short phrase before eating to show thanks for the food. 
In contrast, in some Western countries, it is common to start eating as soon as everyone is served.  
In many Asian countries, sharing food is an important part of the meal. Dishes are placed in the 
centre, and people take small portions to their own plates. However, in countries like the United States or 
the United Kingdom, each person usually has their own dish. Table manners also vary; for instance, 
making noise while eating may be polite in some cultures but rude in others. 
Understanding these customs is important, especially when travelling. By respecting local habits, 
people can avoid misunderstandings and show good manners. As a result, learning about dining 
customs can help us connect with others and enjoy meals more comfortably. 
 
Question 1. The word “strict” in paragraph 1 is OPPOSITE in meaning to ____. 
A. normal 
B. flexible 
C. fixed 
D. unclear  
Question 2. What is indicated about dining customs in many Asian countries?   
A. People eat quickly and finish meals at the same time.  
B. Food is shared, and people take small portions for themselves.  
C. Each person orders a separate dish for their own meal. 
D. Meals usually include only one main dish and no side dishes. 
Question 3. The word “vary” in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to  ______. 
A. differ 
B. appear 
C. accept 
D. refuse 
Question 4. Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence in paragraph 3? 
By respecting local habits, people can avoid misunderstandings and show good manners. 
A. Observing local customs enables people to prevent confusion and act politely in social situations.  
B. Preventing confusion allows people to observe local customs and behave in a polite way.  
C. Polite behaviour results in people following local customs and avoiding possible confusion.  
D. Following local customs encourages people to show politeness and reduce possible misunderstandings.

Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
LEVEL 2 
 
 
Text 2 
Beliefs play a crucial role in shaping human behaviour, as they influence how people interpret the 
world and make decisions. These beliefs may come from religion, culture, family traditions, or personal 
experiences. For example, individuals who value honesty are more likely to act truthfully, even in difficult 
situations. In contrast, those who believe success is the most important goal may focus strongly on 
competition and achievement. As a result, beliefs often guide not only actions but also attitudes 
towards others. 
In many societies, shared beliefs help create a sense of identity and social order. People tend to 
conform to common rules because they believe these rules are right or necessary. For instance, respect 
for elders in some cultures leads younger people to behave politely and listen carefully to advice. 
Similarly, beliefs about education can affect how seriously students approach their studies. When 
education is seen as a path to a better future, individuals are more inclined to work hard and remain 
motivated. 
However, beliefs are not always fixed, as they can change over time through new experiences and 
information. Exposure to different cultures or ideas may challenge existing views and lead to new ways 
of thinking. Therefore, understanding the role of beliefs can help explain human behaviour and promote 
greater tolerance in society. 
 
Question 5. Beliefs that people hold may come from all of the following EXCEPT _____.   
A. culture  
 
B. government policies  
C. family traditions  
 
D. personal experiences 
Question 6. Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence in paragraph 1? 
As a result, beliefs often guide not only actions but also attitudes towards others. 
A. Behaviour and attitudes towards others often shape the beliefs people develop.  
B. The way people view others and behave can determine their beliefs over time.  
C. People’s behaviour and attitudes towards others may influence the beliefs they form.   
D. Beliefs often shape both behaviour and the way people view others in society. 
Question 7. The phrase “conform to” in paragraph 2 is OPPOSITE in meaning to ______. 
A. accept 
B. follow 
C. reject 
D. discourage  
Question 8. In some cultures, respect for elders _______.   
A. is shown through giving expensive gifts to older family members  
B. requires young people to follow strict rules in all situations  
C. encourages younger people to act politely and listen to advice 
D. allows elders to make all important decisions for the family 
Question 9. The word “inclined” in paragraph 2 can be best replaced by ______. 
A. unwilling 
B. likely 
C. cautious 
D. aggressive 
Question 10. What is implied about beliefs in paragraph 3?  
A. Beliefs are mainly shaped by family traditions and early life experiences. 
B. Beliefs remain stable because people rarely question their personal views. 
C. Beliefs depend mostly on education rather than personal or social influences. 
D. Beliefs can change when people are exposed to new ideas or cultures.

VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
LEVEL 3 
 
 
Text 3 
Culture shock is often described as a gradual process rather than a single emotional reaction, 
emerging when individuals are immersed in an unfamiliar cultural context for an extended period. 
Initially, many people experience a sense of fascination, as differences in lifestyle, communication 
patterns, and social conventions appear intriguing. However, this initial curiosity tends to diminish over 
time, giving way to a more challenging phase characterised by frustration, confusion, and even emotional 
fatigue. At this stage, individuals may struggle to interpret social cues or respond appropriately in 
everyday interactions. 
 
A key factor contributing to culture shock is the mismatch between deeply held assumptions and 
the norms of the host culture. Behaviours that seem acceptable in one context may be misunderstood or 
even considered inappropriate in another. Consequently, individuals may begin to question their own 
identity, particularly when they feel excluded or unable to integrate effectively. Moreover, limited 
language proficiency can intensify this sense of isolation, making it difficult to establish meaningful 
relationships or seek support. 
Nevertheless, culture shock should not be viewed solely as a negative experience, since it can 
foster significant personal development. By gradually adjusting their perspectives, individuals often 
develop greater resilience, cultural sensitivity, and adaptability. Over time, what once seemed unfamiliar 
may become manageable, or even enriching, thereby allowing individuals to function more confidently 
in diverse cultural settings.  
 
Question 11. The word “intriguing” in paragraph 1 is OPPOSITE in meaning to ______. 
A. uninteresting 
B. convenient 
C. interesting 
D. harmonious  
Question 12. After the initial curiosity fades, individuals experience all of the following EXCEPT _____.   
A. frustration  
 
B. physical exhaustion  
C. confusion  
 
D. emotional fatigue 
Question 13. According to paragraph 2, what gives rise to culture shock?   
A. differences in food preferences and daily routines across various cultural settings  
B. disinterest in learning a new language when living in a foreign country  
C. a gap between personal beliefs and the social norms of the host culture  
D. a lack of interest in adapting to new environments and social expectations 
Question 14. What is mentioned about language barriers?   
A. It leads to misunderstandings in formal situations such as school settings.  
B. It affects how people understand written texts in an unfamiliar language.  
C. It limits access to information about services in the local community.  
D. It increases isolation and makes forming relationships more difficult. 
Question 15. Which of the following is NOT true according to paragraph 3?   
A. Culture shock can support personal growth as people adapt over time. 
B. People may become more flexible by adjusting their perspectives gradually. 
C. Culture shock is beneficial but fails to contribute to personal development. 
D. Unfamiliar situations can become easier and even more meaningful with time. 
 
 
 
Trang 33

VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
LEVEL 4 
 
 
Text 4 
Globalisation has profoundly reshaped cultural landscapes, facilitating unprecedented levels of 
interaction among societies. Through the rapid exchange of information, goods, and people, cultural 
boundaries have become increasingly permeable. On the one hand, this interconnectedness has enabled 
individuals to access diverse traditions, languages, and artistic expressions that were once geographically 
distant. As a result, cultural awareness has expanded, fostering greater tolerance and cross-cultural 
understanding in many parts of the world. 
However, the influence of globalisation on cultural diversity is far from unequivocal. [I] While 
exposure to different cultures can enrich societies, it may also lead to cultural homogenisation, where 
dominant global trends overshadow local identities. For instance, the widespread popularity of 
international media and consumer brands often diminishes the visibility of indigenous customs and 
languages. [II] 
At the same time, globalisation can act as a catalyst for cultural revitalisation. In response to 
external influences, communities may actively seek to protect and promote their traditions, thereby 
reinforcing their cultural identity. [III] Digital platforms, in particular, provide opportunities for 
marginalised voices to share their narratives on a global scale. [IV] Therefore, although globalisation 
presents both challenges and opportunities, its impact on cultural diversity ultimately depends on how 
societies negotiate the balance between integration and preservation. 
 
Question 16. Which of the following best summarises paragraph 1? 
A. Globalisation increases interaction, expanding cultural access and awareness while improving 
tolerance.  
B. Globalisation enables people to access a wide range of cultural traditions and artistic expressions 
worldwide.  
C. Rapid exchanges of information, goods, and people have made cultural boundaries increasingly open 
across societies.  
D. Greater interaction among societies has contributed to higher levels of cultural awareness in certain 
parts of the world. 
Question 17. The word “unequivocal” in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to _____. 
A. unusual 
B. predictable 
C. explicit 
D. insignificant  
Question 18. Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence in paragraph 3? 
In response to external influences, communities may actively seek to protect and promote their 
traditions, thereby reinforcing their cultural identity. 
A. A stronger cultural identity often drives communities to safeguard and promote traditions when facing 
external pressures.  
B. Under outside pressures, communities safeguard and promote traditions, which strengthens their 
cultural identity. 
C. Traditions are safeguarded and promoted as communities build a stronger cultural identity under 
outside influences.  
D. External pressures are strengthened as communities safeguard and promote traditions and reinforce 
their cultural identity.

Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
TÀI LIỆU LIVESTREAM ĐỘC QUYỀN 
VIP 90 (2026): TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM BỘ CÂU HỎI  
DỄ TRÚNG TỦ & LUYỆN BỘ ĐỀ TINH HOA  
[V90.09.04B] THI ONLINE: TINH HOA ĐỌC HIỂU  
 
(BUỔI 13) – ĐỀ VẬN DỤNG 
Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương  
 
 
LEVEL 1 
Text 1 
 
Eating habits are quite different around the world, and it’s honestly a bit fascinating! In the United 
Kingdom, people enjoy a hearty breakfast with eggs, toast, and a nice cup of tea, though many now choose 
something quick on busy mornings. Meanwhile, in Japan, meals are usually lighter and healthier, with 
rice, fish, and vegetables being common. For example, a typical Japanese breakfast may include miso 
soup and grilled fish, which might seem unusual but is quite interesting to visitors. In Italy, people take 
their time to enjoy meals, especially pasta, often shared with family. It’s rather charming, I think, how 
each country’s eating habits reflect its culture, making food not just necessary but also a delightful cultural 
experience! 
 
Question 1. Which of the following is NOT mentioned as part of a British breakfast? 
A. Rice 
B. Eggs  
C. Tea 
D. Toast 
Question 2. The word “unusual” in the text is opposite in meaning to _______. 
A. unique 
B. strange 
C. normal 
D. flexible  
Question 3. The word “their” in the text refers to ______. 
A. eating habits  
B. visitors 
C. people  
D. meals 
 
Text 2 
 
Body language can be quite different across cultures, and it’s rather amusing to notice! In the 
United Kingdom, people tend to keep a polite distance when talking, and too much physical contact might 
feel uncomfortable. However, in Italy, people often use expressive hand gestures and stand closer, which 
shows friendliness and enthusiasm. In Japan, bowing is a common way to greet others, and direct eye 
contact is sometimes avoided as a sign of respect. For example, a simple nod in Japan can carry a lot of 
meaning. It’s quite fascinating, really, how these small movements can say so much, and understanding 
them can help us communicate better when meeting people from different cultures! 
 
Question 4. Which of the following is NOT mentioned as part of body language in Japan? 
A. Nodding 
 
B. Bowing for greeting 
C. Avoiding direct eye contact 
D. Using expressive hand gestures  
Question 5. The word “amusing” in the text is OPPOSITE in meaning to _______. 
A. entertaining 
 
B. boring  
 
C. enjoyable 
 
D. amazing

VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
Question 6. According to the passage, understanding these small movements ______. 
A. can improve communication across cultures 
B. reduces the need for verbal interaction entirely 
C. makes cultural differences less important 
D. prevents misunderstandings in every situation 
 
LEVEL 2 
Text 3 
 
Globalization has raised concerns about whether it destroys local culture, and the answer is not 
entirely simple. On the one hand, it can weaken traditional values by spreading global trends, especially 
through media, technology, and international business. Young people may prefer foreign music, fashion, 
or lifestyles, gradually ignoring their own cultural practices. As a result, unique traditions, languages, and 
customs may fade over time. This process can lead to cultural uniformity, where different societies begin 
to look and behave in similar ways. 
 
On the other hand, globalization does not always destroy culture but can also support and reshape 
it. It allows countries to share and promote their traditions on a global stage, increasing cultural awareness 
and appreciation. Local cultures can adapt by combining traditional elements with modern 
influences. This will create new and dynamic forms of expression. In this sense, globalization acts more 
as a force of change rather than destruction, depending on how societies respond and preserve their 
cultural identity. 
 
Question 7. The word “foreign” in paragraph 1 is opposite in meaning to _______.  
A. domestic 
B. international 
C. traditional 
D. overseas 
Question 8. According to paragraph 1, cultural uniformity is ______. 
A. a condition in which only traditional values are preserved without any change 
B. a process where cultures completely disappear due to external influences 
C. a situation where different societies become similar in behavior and lifestyle  
D. a system where global culture replaces all local customs entirely 
Question 9. Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence in paragraph 2? 
Local cultures can adapt by combining traditional elements with modern influences. 
A. Local cultures must reject modern influences to preserve their original traditions. 
B. Modern influences prevent local cultures from maintaining any traditional practices.  
C. Traditional elements are often replaced entirely by modern ideas in local cultures. 
D. Local cultures can change by blending traditional features with contemporary influences.  
Question 10. Which of the following is TRUE according to the passage? 
A. Globalization always destroys traditional cultures, leaving no chance for adaptation. 
B. Globalization can both weaken and reshape local cultures, depending on responses.  
C. Local cultures cannot survive once exposed to global media and business influences. 
D. Cultural uniformity happens only when countries actively reject foreign ideas. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Trang 37

VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
Text 4 
 
Tourism often creates a tension with cultural authenticity, as the desire to attract visitors can 
influence how traditions are presented. On one hand, local communities may adjust their customs, 
festivals, or daily practices to suit tourists’ expectations. Cultural performances can become more 
commercialized, simplified, or even exaggerated, which may reduce their original meaning. Over time, 
this can lead to a loss of authenticity, as traditions are no longer practiced naturally but are instead shaped 
by economic needs and outside demand. 
 
On the other hand, tourism can also play a positive role in preserving culture. It provides financial 
support that encourages communities to maintain traditional crafts, food, and festivals. Without tourism, 
some cultural practices might disappear due to a lack of interest or resources. Moreover, when 
managed responsibly, tourism can promote respect and a deeper understanding between cultures. 
Therefore, the key issue is balance—ensuring that cultural heritage is protected while still allowing 
tourism to contribute to local development and cultural pride. 
 
Question 11. The word “their” in paragraph 1 refers to _______.  
A. traditions  
 
B. local communities  
 
C. visitors  
 
D. expectations 
Question 12. The word “original” in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to _______. 
A. authentic 
B. modern 
C. artificial 
D. altered 
Question 13. Why can tourism affect cultural authenticity? 
A. Local people refuse to share their traditions with outsiders for economic reasons. 
B. Tourism completely prevents communities from practicing any traditional customs. 
C. Visitors usually ignore local culture and avoid participating in cultural activities. 
D. Traditions may be modified or commercialized to meet tourists’ expectations.  
Question 14. Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence in paragraph 2? 
Without tourism, some cultural practices might disappear due to a lack of interest or resources. 
A. Tourism guarantees the survival of all cultural practices regardless of changing interests or available 
resources. 
B. In the absence of tourism, certain traditions could fade because of limited support or declining interest.  
C. Cultural traditions remain strong even without tourism because communities always preserve them 
independently. 
D. A lack of tourism encourages greater participation in cultural practices due to increased local 
awareness. 
Question 15. Which of the following is TRUE according to the passage? 
A. Tourism can encourage preservation by providing financial support for cultural practices. 
B. Tourism has no impact on how traditions are performed or presented in local communities. 
C. Cultural authenticity is always strengthened when traditions are adapted for tourists. 
D. Local customs remain completely unchanged regardless of economic or visitor influences. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Trang 38

VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
LEVEL 3 
Text 5 
 
Culture is often perceived as something stable, almost like a fixed identity shared by a group of 
people. Traditions, beliefs, and social norms are passed down through generations, creating a sense of 
continuity and belonging. [I] This stability helps individuals understand their roots and maintain social 
cohesion. In this sense, culture appears relatively fixed, as communities tend to preserve what they value 
and resist sudden or radical changes that might threaten their identity. 
 
However, culture is far from static. [II] It is constantly shaped by historical events, technological 
developments, and interactions between societies. As people migrate, communicate, and exchange ideas, 
cultural practices evolve, sometimes subtly and sometimes dramatically. [III] New values emerge, old 
customs are reinterpreted, and entirely new forms of expression are created. This dynamic nature shows 
that culture is not a rigid system but a living process that adapts to changing circumstances. 
 
[IV] While certain core elements may remain stable over time, the way they are expressed often 
shifts in response to new contexts. Rather than asking whether culture is fixed or changing, it is more 
accurate to see it as both: rooted in the past yet continuously evolving in the present and future. 
 
Question 16. What is a common assumption about culture? 
A. It is stable and shared as a fixed identity within a group. 
B. It changes rapidly without any connection to traditions or past influences. 
C. It is shaped only by modern technology and global communication. 
D. It exists independently of social norms and shared beliefs. 
Question 17. The word “resist” in paragraph 1 is opposite in meaning to _______. 
A. reject 
B. oppose 
C. embrace  
D. refuse 
Question 18. Which of the following best summarises paragraph 2? 
A. New cultural practices completely replace old traditions, leaving no connection to previous beliefs or 
shared values. 
B. Culture remains unchanged because traditions are preserved, and communities avoid any external 
influences or technological developments. 
C. Cultural change happens only during major historical events, with little impact from daily interactions 
or communication between societies. 
D. Culture is dynamic, shaped by interactions, migration, and innovation, leading to evolving values, 
reinterpretations, and new expressions.  
Question 19. Where in the passage does the following sentence best fit? 
Ultimately, culture exists in a balance between preservation and transformation. 
A. [I] 
B. [II] 
C. [III] 
D. [IV]  
Question 20. What can be inferred from the passage? 
A. Culture is, in fact, not as rigid as commonly believed. 
B. Culture develops without influence from other societies. 
C. Cultural change always destroys social cohesion. 
D. External influences rarely shape cultural development. 
 
 
 
 
Trang 39

TÀI LIỆU LIVESTREAM ĐỘC QUYỀN 
VIP 90 (2026): TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM BỘ CÂU HỎI  
DỄ TRÚNG TỦ & LUYỆN BỘ ĐỀ TINH HOA  
[V90.09.04A] THI ONLINE: TINH HOA ĐỌC HIỂU  
 
(BUỔI 13) – ĐỀ NÂNG CAO 
Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương  
 
 
Read the following passage and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct answer to each of 
the questions from 1 to 8. 
In an increasingly globalised world, language is no longer a fixed system but a dynamic entity 
shaped by its speakers. The growing interaction between cultures has intensified linguistic exchange, 
allowing languages such as Spanish and English to evolve beyond their traditional boundaries. As people 
migrate, communicate, and collaborate across borders, languages adapt to new contexts, reflecting the 
diversity of their users rather than adhering to rigid norms. 
Spanish, in particular, illustrates how a global language can embrace variation as a source of 
strength. With hundreds of millions of speakers across different continents, it has developed numerous 
regional forms, each influenced by local histories and cultural identities. Rather than resisting these 
differences, many Spanish speakers view them as enriching the language, enabling it to remain relevant 
in diverse settings. This openness contrasts with more prescriptive attitudes that attempt to standardise 
language use, often limiting its natural development.   
Nevertheless, linguistic diversity is not without tension. Differences in accent, vocabulary, and 
usage can still lead to prejudice, especially in societies where certain forms of speech are considered more 
prestigious. In Spain, for example, some regional and Latin American varieties have historically been 
stigmatised. However, younger generations are increasingly challenging such biases, recognising that 
linguistic diversity reflects social reality rather than deficiency.   
Ultimately, the evolution of language in a globalised context underscores a broader cultural 
principle: diversity fosters creativity rather than division. As languages continue to change, their vitality 
depends on the willingness of communities to accept variation and resist narrow definitions of 
correctness. In this sense, linguistic diversity is not a problem to be solved but a resource that enhances 
communication, identity, and cultural expression in a connected world. 
 
Question 1. Languages adapt to new contexts when people do all of the following EXCEPT _____.   
A. communicate across borders  
B. adjust traditional grammar rules  
C. migrate to different regions  
D. collaborate with others internationally 
Question 2. The word “embrace” in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to _____.  
A. reject 
B. indicate 
C. overlook 
D. adopt  
Question 3. The word “them” in paragraph 2 refers to ______. 
A. differences 
B. speakers 
C. histories 
D. forms  
Question 4. The word “prestigious” in paragraph 3 is OPPOSITE in meaning to _____. 
A. respected 
B. comparative 
C. undervalued 
D. accepted  
 
 
 
Trang 40

VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
Question 5. Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence in paragraph 4? 
As languages continue to change, their vitality depends on the willingness of communities to accept 
variation and resist narrow definitions of correctness. 
A. The survival of changing languages relies on communities accepting diversity and rejecting strict rules 
about correctness. 
B. Communities tend to accept diversity when languages survive despite continuous changes in different 
cultural contexts.  
C. The readiness of communities grows as languages accept diversity and avoid strict rules about 
linguistic correctness.  
D. Strict rules about correctness shape communities as languages survive and encourage acceptance of 
linguistic diversity. 
Question 6. Which of the following is TRUE according to the passage?   
A. Linguistic differences are widely accepted, so prejudice rarely appears in modern societies. 
B. Standardised language use helps languages develop naturally across different cultural contexts. 
C. Linguistic diversity can strengthen creativity and support communication in a globalised world. 
D. Regional varieties are no longer criticised as younger generations fully accept them.  
Question 7. Which paragraph mentions past prejudice against certain language forms in Spain? 
A. Paragraph 1 
B. Paragraph 2 
C. Paragraph 3 
D. Paragraph 4 
Question 8. Which paragraph mentions how Spanish speakers are receptive to linguistic variation? 
A. Paragraph 1 
B. Paragraph 2 
C. Paragraph 3 
D. Paragraph 4 
 
Read the following passage and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct answer to each of 
the questions from 9 to 18. 
In contemporary debates surrounding cultural exchange, the notion of borrowing from other 
cultures is frequently framed as problematic, often associated with accusations of appropriation or 
exploitation. However, such a perspective risks overlooking the inherently dynamic nature of culture 
itself. Rather than existing as fixed, isolated systems, cultures have historically evolved through 
continuous interaction, adaptation, and reinterpretation. In a globalised context, this process has 
intensified, enabling individuals to engage with diverse traditions in ways that transcend geographical and 
social boundaries. 
Proponents of cultural exchange argue that borrowing is not merely inevitable but fundamentally 
constructive. When individuals adopt elements from other cultures - whether in language, fashion, or 
artistic expression - they participate in a broader process of creative transformation. [I] Indeed, the sharing 
of cultural practices can promote mutual understanding, encouraging individuals to appreciate 
perspectives beyond their immediate social frameworks. As some commentators suggest, societies should 
aim to ensure that all groups are valued equally and are free to draw inspiration from one another. [II]   
Nevertheless, concerns about power imbalance cannot be entirely dismissed. Cultural borrowing 
becomes contentious when it occurs within unequal social structures, where dominant groups may benefit 
disproportionately while marginalised communities are overlooked or misrepresented. [III] In such 
cases, the issue lies not in the act of borrowing itself, but in the conditions under which it takes place. 
Addressing these disparities requires a more nuanced understanding of cultural interaction, one that 
distinguishes between respectful engagement and exploitative appropriation. 
Ultimately, the exchange of cultural ideas should be seen as a defining feature of a pluralistic 
society rather than a threat to its integrity. When approached with sensitivity and mutual respect, cultural

VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
borrowing can serve as a powerful means of fostering creativity, inclusivity, and social cohesion. [IV] 
Rather than attempting to rigidly police cultural boundaries, societies may benefit more from cultivating 
environments in which diverse influences are acknowledged, valued, and meaningfully integrated. 
 
Question 9. Which of the following best summarises paragraph 1? 
A. Cultural borrowing is debated, but cultures evolve dynamically through ongoing interaction and 
adaptation. 
B. Cultural borrowing is often criticised and linked to accusations of appropriation and exploitation.  
C. Cultures have historically developed through interaction, adaptation, and reinterpretation over time.  
D. Globalisation allows individuals to engage with diverse traditions beyond geographical and social 
boundaries. 
Question 10. What is indicated about cultures in paragraph 1?   
A. They are clearly defined systems that remain stable across different societies.  
B. They are constantly shaped and transformed through ongoing interaction and change.  
C. They are influenced mainly by geographical boundaries rather than social factors.  
D. They are preserved by avoiding contact with other cultures and outside influences. 
Question 11. The word “they” in paragraph 2 refers to _____. 
A. cultures 
B. elements 
C. proponents 
D. individuals 
Question 12. What is implied about the sharing of cultural practices?   
A. It can reduce cultural conflicts between societies. 
B. It can increase economic cooperation among countries. 
C. It can deepen their understanding of others. 
D. It can improve communication in professional environments. 
Question 13. The word “contentious” in paragraph 3 mostly means _____. 
A. offensive 
B. disturbed 
C. controversial  
D. widespread  
Question 14.  Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence in paragraph 3? 
In such cases, the issue lies not in the act of borrowing itself, but in the conditions under which it 
takes place.  
A. In these situations, the problem stems from the circumstances surrounding cultural borrowing rather 
than the act itself. 
B. In these situations, the act of borrowing shapes the conditions in which cultural exchange becomes 
problematic over time.  
C. In these situations, the conditions are influenced by the act of borrowing when issues arise in cultural 
interactions.  
D. In these situations, borrowing becomes the central issue as the surrounding conditions are affected by 
its presence. 
Question 15. What is needed to resolve inequalities in cultural borrowing?   
A. stronger legal frameworks to regulate how cultural elements are shared globally  
B. wider access to cultural products across different international markets and communities  
C. greater awareness of economic benefits in cultural exchange and global cooperation efforts 
D. a deeper awareness of cultural interaction that separates respectful use from exploitation 
 
 
 
 
Trang 42

VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
TÀI LIỆU LIVESTREAM ĐỘC QUYỀN 
VIP 90 (2026): TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM BỘ CÂU HỎI  
DỄ TRÚNG TỦ & LUYỆN BỘ ĐỀ TINH HOA  
[V90.09.05] LÀM CHỦ KIẾN THỨC NGỮ PHÁP    
 
ĐẢO NGỮ VÀ CÂU CHẺ 
Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương  
 
 
1. Đảo ngữ 
Đảo ngữ với các cụm từ có NO 
At no time + đảo ngữ: không lúc nào mà 
At no time do I suspect him.  
On no account + đảo ngữ: không khi nào mà 
On no account must you touch the wire. 
Under no circumstances + đảo ngữ: không có lúc 
nào mà 
Under no circumstances are students allowed to 
talk in class. 
In no way + đảo ngữ: không đời nào mà 
In no way did I intend to offend you. 
By no means + đảo ngữ: không bằng cách nào mà, 
không thể nào mà 
By no means are students allowed to use 
smartphones in class. 
On no occasion + đảo ngữ: không lúc nào mà 
On no occasion are women allowed to wear short 
skirts. 
 
Đảo ngữ với các cụm có ONLY 
Only after + V-ing/mệnh đề + đảo ngữ: chỉ sau khi 
Only after working with him did I realise the 
importance of patience. 
Only when + mệnh đề + đảo ngữ: chỉ khi 
Only when I arrived at school did I realise I had 
forgotten my homework. 
Only by + V-ing + đảo ngữ: chỉ bằng cách (nhấn 
mạnh tính duy nhất) 
Only by practising English frequently can you 
master it. 
Only if + mệnh đề + đảo ngữ: chỉ nếu như, chỉ khi 
Only if everybody agreed would I accept this 
position. 
 
Một số dạng đảo ngữ thường gặp khác 
No sooner had I gone out than it started to rain. 
= Hardly had I gone out when it started to rain. 
No sooner + had S PII than + mệnh đề QKD 
= Hardly/Barely/Scarcely + had S PII when + mệnh 
đề QKD: vừa mới … thì 
Not until + mốc thời gian/mệnh đề + đảo ngữ 
= It was not until + mốc thời gian/mệnh đề + that + 
mệnh đề: mãi cho đến khi 
Not until he turned 9 was he able to speak. 
= It was not until he turned 9 that he was able to 
speak. 
So + adj/adv + đảo ngữ + that + mệnh đề 
Such + be + Noun + that + mệnh đề 
quá … đến nỗi mà  
So quickly did he run that the others couldn't 
catch up with him. 
Such was the wind that we couldn't open the 
window. 
 
Trang 44
