VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
Not only + đảo ngữ + but also + …: không chỉ … 
mà còn 
Not only was it raining all day at the wedding, 
but the band was also late. 
- Điều kiện loại 1: Should S + V, S will V 
- Điều kiện loại 2:  
+ Were + S + Noun/adj, S would V 
+ Were + S + to V, S would V 
- Điều kiện loại 3: Had + S + PII, S would have PII 
Should you work hard, you’ll pass the exam. 
Were I taller, I would join the basketball team. 
Were he to work harder, he would be promoted. 
Had I not driven carelessly, I wouldn’t have 
caused that accident. 
No matter how hard I tried, I couldn’t pass the 
exam. 
Busy as he was, he was willing to help me. 
No matter how + adj/adv + S + V, mệnh đề. 
= However + adj/adv + S + V, mệnh đề 
= Adj/adv + as/though + S + V, mệnh đề 
dù cho, …. 
 
2. Câu chẻ 
It is/was + thành phần cần nhấn mạnh + that + …: Chính … 
Ví dụ: It was his hard work that brought him success. 
 
PRACTICE 
Choose the option that best paraphrases each of the following sentences. 
Question 1. Doing household chores teaches children responsibility and helps them develop essential life 
skills at the same time. 
A. Were children willing to do household chores, they would become responsible and learn important life 
skills. 
B. Not only does doing household chores teach children responsibility, but it also helps them develop 
crucial life skills. 
C. It is doing household chores responsibly that helps children gain important skills needed to succeed in 
life. 
D. Such is the responsibility of doing household chores that children can nurture skills necessary for life 
success. 
Question 2. Governments must take stricter measures to solve the issue of plastic pollution. 
A. It is stricter measures that governments must take to solve the issue of plastic pollution. 
B. In no way will governments be able to address plastic pollution with stricter measures.  
C. Not only do governments take stricter measures, but they may also tackle the issue of plastic pollution.  
D. Only by taking stricter measures will governments prevent plastic pollution from worsening. 
Question 3. Teenagers hardly recognise how much peer pressure influences their decisions. 
A. Little do teenagers realise how much peer pressure impacts their decisions.  
B. Never will teenagers be aware of how much peer pressure determines their decisions. 
C. It is peer pressure that has a profound impact on how teenagers make decisions.  
D. If peer pressure were insignificant, teenagers would be able to make wise decisions. 
Question 4. He attributed his good health to a balanced diet and regular exercise.  
A. Not until he had followed a balanced diet and done exercise regularly did his health improve.  
B. It was his good health that caused him to follow a balanced diet and do regular exercise.  
C. Had it not been for a balanced diet and regular exercise, he wouldn’t have been in good health.  
D. Not only did his health improve thanks to a balanced diet, but he also started to exercise regularly. 
 
Trang 45

Question 5. Unless young people manage their finances wisely, they may struggle to live independently. 
A. Only by managing their finances wisely will young people definitely succeed in living independently.  
B. Were it not for young people’s wise money management, they could not start living independently.  
C. No matter how wisely young people manage money, they will find it challenging to live independently.  
D. Only if young people manage their finances wisely are they likely to avoid struggling to live 
independently. 
Question 6. People can only fully enjoy the benefits of a smart city when its technology is used efficiently. 
A. People could fully enjoy the benefits of a smart city were its technology efficient.  
B. Only when its technology is used efficiently can people fully enjoy the benefits of a smart city. 
C. It is the efficiency of its technology that makes life in a smart city more beneficial to people. 
D. Efficiently as its technology is employed, life in a smart city seems less beneficial to people. 
Question 7. The government didn’t invest in public transport, so traffic congestion remained a nightmare 
for commuters. 
A. Not only did the government fail to invest more in public transport, but commuters also experienced 
serious traffic jams.  
B. Hardly had the government allocated funds to public transport when traffic congestion wouldn’t 
become a burden to commuters.  
C. It was the government’s inability to invest in public transport that exposed commuters to severe traffic 
congestion.  
D. Had the government allocated funds to public transport, commuters would not have continued to suffer 
from severe traffic congestion. 
Question 8. Plagiarism is illegal, but many university students still copy others' work without citing 
references. 
A. It is the illegality of plagiarism that encourages many university students to copy others' work without 
citing references. 
B. Illegal as plagiarism is, many university students still copy others' work without citing references.  
C. Not until plagiarism is made illegal do university students stop copying others' work without citing 
references. 
D. Not only is plagiarism illegal, but many university students also copy others' work without citing 
references. 
Question 9. They didn’t have access to education. This hindered their ability to pursue high-paying jobs. 
A. Had they not had access to education, they would have struggled to seek high-paying jobs.  
B. Not only did they fail to have access to education, but they also couldn’t find high-paying jobs.  
C. It was the lack of access to education that made it difficult for them to pursue high-paying jobs.  
D. No sooner had they had access to education than they succeeded in pursuing high-paying jobs. 
Question 10. The government must take immediate action to address climate change, or the consequences 
will be severe.  
A. If the government is sluggish in taking action, the consequences of climate change will be serious.  
B. Not only must the government take immediate action, but the consequences of climate change will 
become less serious.  
C. It is immediate action by the government that will determine the severity of climate change 
consequences.  
D. Such is the urgency of addressing climate change that delaying action will lead to irreversible 
consequences.

Question 11. The media keeps the public up to date and shapes opinions on critical issues. 
A. It is the influence of the media that shapes the public’s opinions on serious issues by keeping them 
informed.  
B. So powerful is the media that it keeps the public informed by shaping their opinions on current issues.  
C. Only by shaping opinions on serious issues does the media try to inform the public.  
D. Not only does the media inform the public, but it also shapes opinions on important issues. 
Question 12. The film was deemed too violent and was removed from all social media platforms. 
A. Such was the violence of the film that it was banned from all social media platforms. 
B. Not only was the film too violent, but all social media platforms also imposed a ban on it. 
C. So violent was the film that all social media platforms delayed broadcasting it.  
D. Had the film been violent, it would have been deleted from all social media platforms. 
Question 13. He can cause an accident if he continues driving at such breakneck speed, but he isn’t aware 
of that much. 
A. Little does he realise that he can cause an accident if he keeps driving at such breakneck speed. 
B. Not only does he fail to realise that he can cause an accident, but he also keeps driving at such breakneck 
speed. 
C. Not until he causes an accident can he realise that he is driving at such breakneck speed. 
D. Were he to cause an accident, he would realise that he is driving at such breakneck speed. 
Question 14. Traumatised by the bullying, he fell into depression and stopped going to school. 
A. Not until he became hopeless and couldn’t go to school did he become traumatised by the bullying. 
B. It was the trauma of the bullying that caused him to become depressed and stop going to school.  
C. Had it not been for the traumatic bullying, he would have become hopeless and stopped going to school. 
D. Scarcely had he become traumatised by the bullying when he became depressed and couldn’t go to 
school. 
Question 15. Though exhausted from the day’s work, Simpson was willing to do household chores. 
A. No sooner had Simpson taken care of household chores than he felt weary of the day’s work.  
B. Not until Simpson became tired after a long day at work did he start doing housework.  
C. Fatigued as he was from the day’s work, Simpson didn’t hesitate to do housework.  
D. It was a tiring day at work that encouraged Simpson to take on household chores. 
Question 16. Due to their complexity, family conflicts can only be resolved through frank discussions. 
A. So complex are family conflicts that they can only be resolved through frank discussions. 
B. It is the complexity of family conflicts that requires frank discussions to avoid them.  
C. Only by discussing frankly can family conflicts be settled despite their complexity.  
D. Were family conflicts complex, only frank discussions could help settle them.  
Question 17. If heritage sites are protected, future generations can appreciate their historical and cultural 
significance.  
A. Were heritage sites to be protected, future generations could appreciate their historical and cultural 
significance.  
B. Only by protecting heritage sites can future generations realise how significant it is to appreciate their 
history and culture.  
C. Not only are future generations aware of the importance of their history and culture, but they also 
contribute to preserving heritage sites. 
D. It is the preservation of heritage sites that helps future generations appreciate their historical and 
cultural significance.

Question 18. By travelling abroad, a person can gain a deeper understanding of different cultures and 
traditions. 
A. It is travelling abroad that gives a person a chance to deepen their knowledge about different cultures 
and traditions.  
B. Not only does a person travel abroad, but they also get insights into different cultures and traditions.  
C. Only when travelling abroad does a person become more knowledgeable about different cultures and 
traditions.  
D. Only by travelling abroad can a person better understand different cultures and traditions.  
Question 19. Using smartphones during lectures is a source of distraction, yet few students acknowledge 
this. 
A. Not only does using smartphones during lectures cause distractions, but not many students are aware 
of this.  
B. No sooner does using smartphones during lectures lead to distractions than more students realise this.  
C. Little do students realise that they will become distracted when using smartphones during lectures. 
D. Only by using smartphones during lectures do students realise how distracted they become. 
Question 20. Due to the harmful effects of plastic bags on the environment, numerous countries have 
enforced strict rules on their usage.  
A. However adverse the environmental impact of plastic bags is, a lot of countries have implemented 
strict policies to regulate their use. 
B. Such is the negative impact of plastic bags on the environment that many countries have imposed strict 
regulations on their use. 
C. It is the detrimental impact of plastic bags on the environment that has forced many countries to enact 
strict measures to control their use. 
D. Only after many countries had adopted strict regulations to limit their usage did the environmental 
impact of plastic bags become less severe.  
Question 21. Teenagers become depressed as a result of excessive social media use. 
A. Were teenagers to use social media excessively, they would become depressed.  
B. Only when teenagers use social media regularly do they suffer from depression. 
C. Only by using social media frequently do teenagers experience depression. 
D. It is the excessive use of social media that causes teenagers to fall into depression.  
Question 22. The use of AI tools for assignments is not allowed at any educational institution whatsoever. 
A. In no way is the use of AI tools for assignments prohibited at any educational institution. 
B. Under no circumstances are AI tools used for assignments at any educational institution. 
C. By no means are AI tools for assignments banned from any educational institution. 
D. On no account was the use of AI tools for assignments permitted at any educational institution. 
Question 23. Success in getting a high-paying job depends on good preparation. 
A. It is good preparation that lands a person a high-paying job.  
B. Were a job well-paid, it would require good preparation.  
C. Only when a candidate is successful in securing a well-paid job are they well-prepared.  
D. Not only does a person prepare well beforehand, but they also get a high-paying job.

Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương  
 
 
Choose the option that best paraphrases each of the following sentences. 
Question 1. Modern supercomputers can process massive datasets efficiently and predict weather patterns 
with high accuracy. 
A. Processing massive datasets efficiently, modern supercomputers allow accurate weather predictions. 
B. Only by handling massive datasets efficiently can modern supercomputers predict weather patterns 
accurately. 
C. It is their ability to process large datasets efficiently that enables modern supercomputers to predict the 
weather accurately. 
D. Not only can modern supercomputers process massive datasets efficiently, but they can also predict 
weather patterns accurately. 
Question 2. Reading books broadens students' knowledge and improves their critical thinking skills. 
A. Were students to read books regularly, they would expand their knowledge and enhance their critical 
thinking. 
B. Such is the importance of reading books that students can enhance their critical thinking and 
knowledge. 
C. It is through reading books that students gain knowledge and develop critical thinking skills. 
D. Not only does reading books broaden students' knowledge, but it also sharpens their critical thinking 
skills. 
Question 3. As Emily faced financial struggles, she understood the importance of saving money. 
A. No sooner had Emily encountered financial difficulties than she realised the importance of saving 
money. 
B. Only by understanding the importance of saving money could Emily face financial struggles. 
C. Only after Emily had faced financial struggles did she understand the necessity of saving money. 
D. It is Emily’s realisation of the importance of saving money that makes her financially struggle. 
Question 4. Achieving fluency in a foreign language requires consistent practice. 
A. Only when a learner becomes fluent in a foreign language do they realise the importance of consistent 
practice. 
B. Were a language easy to learn, it would not require consistent practice. 
C. It is consistent practice that makes a person fluent in a foreign language.  
D. Not only does a person need to practice consistently, but they also have to immerse themselves in the 
language. 
 
 
 
 
 
Trang 50

Question 5. Students are not allowed to bring mobile phones into the examination hall under any 
condition. 
A. At no time are students prohibited from bringing mobile phones into the examination hall. 
B. Under no circumstances can students bring mobile phones into the examination hall. 
C. By no means are students encouraged to bring mobile phones into the examination hall. 
D. On no account have students been allowed to bring mobile phones into the examination hall. 
Question 6. Children develop anxiety due to excessive academic pressure. 
A. It is excessive academic pressure that causes children to develop anxiety.  
B. Only when children are pressured academically do they experience anxiety. 
C. Only by putting excessive pressure on children do they become anxious. 
D. Were children to be under excessive academic pressure, they would develop anxiety. 
Question 7. Companies cannot reduce carbon emissions unless they adopt greener production methods. 
A. Not only do companies adopt greener production methods, but they also reduce carbon emissions. 
B. Under no circumstances can companies lower carbon emissions by adopting greener production 
methods. 
C. It’s essential for companies to adopt greener production methods if they want to reduce carbon 
emissions. 
D. Only after adopting greener production methods did companies successfully cut carbon emissions. 
Question 8. Because of the rapid rise in global temperatures, scientists have urged governments to take 
immediate action. 
A. However rapid the increase in global temperatures is, scientists have not called for immediate 
government action. 
B. Such is the rapid rise in global temperatures that scientists have urged governments to act immediately. 
C. It is the alarming increase in global temperatures that compels governments to take immediate action. 
D. Only after governments implement strict measures will the rise in global temperatures become less 
concerning. 
Question 9. Spending too much time on social media negatively affects mental health, but many people 
fail to recognise this. 
A. Little do people realise that excessive use of social media can have harmful effects on their mental 
health.  
B. No sooner do people spend excessive time on social media than they understand its negative effects on 
mental health. 
C. Not only does spending too much time on social media harm mental health, but many people also fail 
to realise it. 
D. Only by spending too much time on social media do people realise how harmful it is to their mental 
health. 
Question 10. Students do not fully understand the importance of time management until they start 
working. 
A. It is time management that plays a vital role in students’ future success. 
B. Never do students realise how crucial time management is before working. 
C. Little do students understand the significance of time management until they start working.  
D. Not only do students learn time management, but they also improve their productivity.

Question 11. By reading extensively, students can broaden their knowledge and improve their critical 
thinking skills. 
A. It is reading extensively that allows students to expand their knowledge and sharpen their critical 
thinking skills. 
B. Not only do students read extensively, but they also develop better critical thinking skills. 
C. Only when reading extensively do students enhance their knowledge and critical thinking abilities. 
D. Only by reading extensively can students broaden their knowledge and improve their critical thinking 
skills. 
Question 12. If renewable energy sources are widely adopted, environmental pollution can be 
significantly reduced. 
A. Were renewable energy sources to be widely adopted, environmental pollution could be significantly 
reduced. 
B. It is the widespread adoption of renewable energy sources that can lead to a significant reduction in 
environmental pollution.  
C. Not only does the use of renewable energy reduce pollution, but it also ensures sustainable 
development. 
D. Not until adopting renewable energy sources can environmental pollution be significantly reduced. 
Question 13. Because of its high cost, quality education is only accessible to wealthy families. 
A. So expensive is quality education that only wealthy families can afford it. 
B. It is the high cost of quality education that allows any family to access it. 
C. Only by lowering the cost can quality education become accessible to more families. 
D. Were quality education expensive, only wealthy families would be able to access it. 
Question 14. She succeeded in her career thanks to her perseverance and hard work. 
A. Had it not been for her perseverance and hard work, she wouldn’t have succeeded in her career.  
B. It is her perseverance and hard work that contributed to her career success. 
C. Not until she had worked hard with perseverance did she achieve success in her career. 
D. Not only did she succeed in her career, but she also became an inspiration to others. 
Question 15. Although suffering from a severe headache, Linda continued working on her project. 
A. No sooner had Linda finished working on her project than she suffered from a severe headache. 
B. Not until Linda suffered from a severe headache did she stop working on her project. 
C. It was a severe headache that forced Linda to continue working on her project. 
D. Severe as her headache was, Linda didn’t stop working on her project. 
Question 16. If employees do not adapt to technological changes, they will struggle to stay competitive 
in the job market. 
A. Only by adapting to technological changes will employees certainly remain competitive in the job 
market. 
B. Were employees not to adapt to technological changes, they could not stay competitive in the job 
market. 
C. No matter how well employees adapt to technology, they will struggle to stay competitive. 
D. Not until employees adapt to technological changes do they maintain their competitiveness in the job 
market. 
 
 
 
Trang 52

Question 17. Shocked by the sudden loss of his job, he struggled with anxiety and lost confidence in 
himself. 
A. Not until he lost all confidence in himself did he realise the impact of losing his job. 
B. It was the shock of losing the job that caused him to struggle with anxiety and lose confidence. 
C. Had it not been for the sudden job loss, he wouldn’t have struggled with anxiety and lost confidence. 
D. Scarcely had he lost his job when he started struggling with anxiety and self-doubt. 
Question 18. She may miss a great career opportunity if she keeps refusing job offers, but she doesn’t 
realise that. 
A. Little does she realise that she may miss a great career opportunity if she keeps refusing job offers. 
B. Not only does she refuse job offers, but she also fails to realise she may miss a great career opportunity. 
C. Not until she misses a great career opportunity does she realise that she has refused too many job offers. 
D. Were she to miss a great career opportunity, she would realise that she should not have refused job 
offers. 
Question 19. Students can only achieve high academic performance if they develop effective study habits. 
A. Only when effective study habits are developed can students achieve high academic performance. 
B. What helped the students achieve high academic performance was their effective study habits. 
C. It is effective study habits that encourage students to perform well academically. 
D. Effective as students' study habits are, their academic performance remains average. 
Question 20. The storm was extremely powerful, so it caused severe damage to the entire coastal area. 
A. Had the storm been powerful, it would have destroyed the entire coastal area. 
B. Not only was the storm powerful, but it also damaged the entire coastal area severely. 
C. So powerful was the storm that it delayed recovery efforts along the coastal region. 
D. Such was the power of the storm that it caused severe damage to the entire coastal area. 
Question 21. The company didn’t adopt digital marketing strategies, so its sales declined significantly. 
A. Had the company embraced digital marketing strategies, its sales would not have plummeted.  
B. Hardly had the company implemented digital marketing when its sales continued to decline. 
C. It is the company's lack of digital marketing strategies that led to a sharp drop in sales. 
D. Not only did the company fail to adopt digital marketing strategies, but its sales also dropped 
dramatically. 
Question 22. Technology enhances communication and improves efficiency in various industries. 
A. Not only does technology enhance communication, but it also improves efficiency in various 
industries.  
B. So essential is technology that it improves efficiency while enhancing communication in multiple 
industries. 
C. Only by improving efficiency can technology be seen as a tool for enhancing communication. 
D. It was the advancement of technology that enhanced communication and boosted efficiency in various 
industries. 
Question 23. Companies must innovate constantly to stay competitive, or they will fall behind in the 
market. 
A. It is continuous innovation that helps companies maintain their competitiveness in the market. 
B. Not only must companies innovate constantly, but they also need to attract more customers. 
C. Were companies slow to innovate, they would struggle to stay competitive in the market.  
D. Such is the importance of innovation that companies without it will fail to survive in the market.

TÀI LIỆU LIVESTREAM ĐỘC QUYỀN

DỄ TRÚNG TỦ & LUYỆN BỘ ĐỀ TINH HOA  
[V90.09.05A] THI ONLINE: LÀM CHỦ KIẾN THỨC NGỮ PHÁP  
 
ĐẢO NGỮ VÀ CÂU CHẺ – ĐỀ NÂNG CAO  
Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương  
 
 
Read the following texts and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct option that best fits 
each of the numbered blanks from 1 to 18.  
Text 1 
Travelling without parents, once perceived as an intimidating undertaking, has gradually come to 
be regarded as a formative experience through which young individuals, exposed to unfamiliar cultural, 
linguistic, and social contexts, (1) _____. Removed from the structured environment of family 
supervision, they must navigate uncertainty while simultaneously managing practical responsibilities that, 
under normal circumstances, would have been shared or delegated. (2) _____.   
At the same time, the absence of parental guidance introduces a layer of vulnerability that cannot 
be underestimated, particularly when unexpected complications arise in environments where support 
systems are limited or entirely absent. (3) ____. This necessity, though potentially overwhelming, often 
accelerates the development of critical thinking, (4) _____.   
Ultimately, the significance of travelling independently lies not merely in exposure to new places 
but in the internal transformation it facilitates, a process through which individuals develop resilience, 
autonomy, and a heightened awareness of their own decision-making patterns. (5) _____. By confronting 
situations that resist immediate resolution, they cultivate not only adaptability but also (6) _____. 
 
Question 1.  
A. having compelled to reassess both their capabilities and their limitations 
B. compelled to reassess both their capabilities and their limitations 
C. who compelled to reassess both their capabilities and their limitations 
D. are compelled to reassess both their capabilities and their limitations 
Question 2. 
A. Although this experience may seem challenging, many still rely on others  
B. While such situations demand adaptability, individuals prefer familiar routines  
C. So liberating is this experience that it reshapes their sense of independence  
D. As a result of these challenges, some may struggle to adapt effectively 
Question 3. 
A. If young travellers face unexpected difficulties, they may depend entirely on external support systems  
B. As young travellers experience serious difficulties, they tend to seek assistance from others nearby  
C. Should young travellers encounter serious difficulties, they will have to rely on their own judgement  
D. When young travellers deal with major problems, they are often supported by familiar social networks 
 
 
 
 
Trang 55

VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
Question 4. 
A. because individuals tend to rely on familiar patterns rather than adapting to new situations 
B. while individuals may struggle to respond effectively when faced with unfamiliar challenges 
C. as individuals learn to weigh consequences, reassess decisions, and adapt strategies in real time  
D. since individuals are inclined to avoid complex situations that normally require careful judgement 
Question 5.  
A. Though young travellers face real challenges, they gradually become more confident  
B. It is through facing challenges alone that young travellers become more mature  
C. When individuals travel alone, they often depend more on others around them  
D. While independent travel offers clear benefits, many still remain dependent 
Question 6.  
A. capable of operating effectively in complex and unpredictable environments 
B. who are capable of operating effectively in complex and unpredictable environments 
C. which is the capacity to operate effectively in complex and unpredictable environments 
D. the capacity to operate effectively in complex and unpredictable environments 
 
Text 2 
In recent decades, rapid urban expansion has reshaped economic structures and social patterns, 
drawing millions into densely populated cities in pursuit of opportunity. (7) _____, particularly as 
congestion, environmental degradation, and psychological strain intensify. Counter-urbanisation, 
therefore, has emerged not merely as a demographic trend but as a deliberate lifestyle shift, whereby 
people relocate from metropolitan centres to smaller towns or rural areas in search of equilibrium. (8) 
____, but the aspiration for autonomy, sustainability, and a more meaningful connection with one’s 
surroundings.   
(9) _____. In many cases, it is the erosion of personal well-being that compels individuals to 
abandon urban environments, as they increasingly prioritise mental clarity and social cohesion over 
material advancement. (10) _____. This transition has been further facilitated by technological 
advancements, which enable remote collaboration without the constraints of physical proximity.   
Despite its appeal, counter-urbanisation is not without its complexities, (11) _____. Nevertheless, 
it is precisely this redistribution of population that policymakers must address if balanced regional 
development is to be achieved. By investing in digital connectivity, education, and healthcare in non-
urban areas, governments can mitigate disparities and ensure that (12) _____. 
 
Question 7. 
A. Moreover, an increasing proportion of residents continue to embrace the perceived benefits of urban 
expansion  
B. Consequently, a considerable number of policymakers have attempted to reinforce the structural 
stability of cities  
C. However, a growing number of individuals have begun to reassess the long-term viability of this model  
D. In contrast, a substantial segment of the population remains committed to maintaining established 
patterns of urban life 
 
 
 
 
Trang 56

Helping Kids Do Chores

and builds (1) _____ at home.

● Give clear instructions: Use simple words (4) _____ children understand each task. 
● Break tasks into steps: This helps children learn step by step. 
● Let them choose: Giving options helps them feel involved and willing. 
● Be patient: Don’t expect quick results; it’s important to learn the (5) _____ first. 
With patience and support, chores can become a useful habit (6) _____ prepares children for the future.

The term “brain rot,” once dismissed as mere internet slang, has increasingly been invoked to 
describe a deeper cognitive concern associated with excessive engagement with low-quality digital 
content. Although not a clinical diagnosis, it broadly refers to the perceived decline in attention, memory, 
and intellectual focus resulting from prolonged exposure to fast-paced, fragmented media 
environments.  While the phrase may sound exaggerated, emerging research suggests that (18) _____ in 
an age dominated by algorithm-driven platforms.

consumed; as studies indicate, constant interaction with short-form videos - designed to maximise 
engagement through rapid stimulation - can fragment attention and weaken impulse control.  (19) _____, 
it becomes increasingly difficult for individuals to sustain focus on tasks that require deeper cognitive 
effort, such as reading or problem-solving. This cognitive pattern, which is further intensified by frequent 
interruptions and notifications, (20) _____, and reduced academic performance. 
Nevertheless, experts caution against drawing overly deterministic conclusions, (21) 
_____.  Indeed, when digital use is more intentional and cognitively engaging, its negative impact appears 
to diminish significantly. Consequently, rather than rejecting technology altogether, a more balanced 
approach - one that prioritises mindful consumption and sustained attention - may be essential in 
mitigating the subtle yet (22) _____. 
(Adapted from CNN)

A curious idea has captured public attention: could eating chocolate make people more intelligent? 
This question gained popularity after a study suggested a strong link between a country’s chocolate 
consumption and its number of Nobel Prize winners. Although the claim appears surprising, it raises an 
interesting debate about how diet might influence brain function and whether such a connection can be 
taken seriously. 
The study, conducted by Franz Messerli, compared national chocolate consumption with the 
number of Nobel laureates per capita. The results revealed a remarkably close relationship, with countries 
that consumed more chocolate often producing more prize winners. Switzerland, for instance, ranked 
highly in both categories, while Sweden stood out as an odd case, achieving high academic success despite 
relatively low chocolate intake. These findings, while striking, prompted both curiosity and 
scepticism among researchers. 
Some scientists have attempted to explain this connection by referring to earlier studies. Research 
on animals has shown that cocoa may improve memory and cognitive performance, and similar effects 
have been observed in elderly individuals with mild cognitive impairment. In addition, a few Nobel 
laureates themselves have humorously suggested that chocolate played a small role in their success, as it 
can improve mood and overall well-being, which may indirectly support creative thinking and 
productivity.

VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
introduced digital nomad visas, allowing individuals to reside legally while working for foreign 
employers. These initiatives are often motivated by economic considerations, as governments seek to 
revitalise local economies by drawing in relatively affluent, mobile professionals. As a result, mainstream 
workers - rather than just early adopters - are increasingly embracing this lifestyle, blurring the boundary 
between work and travel.   
However, the allure of working from idyllic destinations conceals a range of practical and 
psychological challenges. While social media often portrays digital nomadism as an unproblematic 
blend of productivity and leisure, the reality can be far more complex. [II] Issues such as unstable 
infrastructure, time-zone differences, and a lack of community can undermine both professional efficiency 
and personal well-being. Moreover, the constant need to adapt to new environments may generate a sense 
of dislocation, complicating efforts to establish routine or long-term stability.   
Consequently, digital nomadism should not be regarded as a universal solution to the constraints 
of traditional employment. [III] While it offers unprecedented autonomy and flexibility, it also requires 
resilience, self-discipline, and careful planning. As more countries compete to attract remote workers and 
more individuals experiment with mobile careers, the future of work is likely to become increasingly fluid 
- yet also more complex, shaped by both opportunity and constraint. [IV] 
(Adapted from The Guardian)

C. Digital nomadism gains popularity as individuals pursue travel opportunities and flexible working 
conditions abroad.  
D. Visa schemes reshape remote work trends, creating uncertainty about their wider economic and social 
impact globally. 
Question 34. Countries such as Portugal, Colombia, Cyprus are mentioned in paragraph 2 as ______.   
A. examples of countries increasing opportunities for people who seek temporary work  
B. places encouraging tourism through cultural and economic development programmes  
C. examples of countries granting legal residence rights to foreign remote workers  
D. regions experiencing economic growth due to visa schemes for digital workers 
Question 35. The phrase “this lifestyle” in paragraph 2 refers to _____.  
A. specialised visa 
B. digital nomadism 
C. the boundary 
D. expansion

Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
TÀI LIỆU LIVESTREAM ĐỘC QUYỀN 
VIP 90 (2026): TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM BỘ CÂU HỎI  
DỄ TRÚNG TỦ & LUYỆN BỘ ĐỀ TINH HOA  
[V90.ĐH.09] ĐỌC HIỂU C1 – C2 BỘ ĐỀ DỄ TRÚNG TỦ 
 
(BUỔI 9) 
Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương  
 
 
Read the following passage and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct answer to each of 
the questions from 1 to 8. 
Many researchers have written about culture shock, and it is widely recognised that there are four 
different stages to the process – honeymoon, negotiation, adjustment, and adaptation. The Honeymoon 
Stage is the first stage of culture shock, and it can often last for several weeks or even months. This is the 
euphoric phase when you’re fascinated by all the exciting and different aspects of your new life – from 
the sights and smells to the pace of life and cultural habits. 
Next is the negotiation stage, which is characterised by frustration and anxiety. This usually hits 
around the three-month mark, although it can be earlier for some individuals. As the excitement gradually 
disappears, you are continually faced with difficulties or uncomfortable situations that may offend or 
make you feel disconnected. At this point, you also start to miss your friends and family back home and 
idealise the life you had there. This is often when physical symptoms can appear, and you may experience 
minor health ailments as a result of the transition. 
Thankfully, this phase will come to an end as you begin to move into the adjustment phase, usually 
at around six to twelve months. This is the stage where life gradually starts to get better, and routine sets 
in. You begin to get your bearings and become more familiar with the local way of life, food, and customs. 
By this point, you may have made a few friends or learnt some of the languages, helping you to adjust 
and better understand the local culture. You may still experience some difficulties at this stage, but 
you’re now able to handle them in a more rational and measured way. 
Finally, you reach the adaptation stage, sometimes known as the bicultural stage. You now feel 
comfortable in your new country and better integrated – you have successfully adapted to your new way 
of life. You no longer feel isolated and lonely and are used to your new daily activities and friends. While 
you may never get back to the heightened euphoria you felt during the honeymoon stage, you’ve now 
gained a strong sense of belonging and finally feel at home in your new environment. 
(Adapted from https://www.now-health.com/) 
 
Question 1. Which of the following is NOT mentioned as a stage of culture shock in paragraph 1? 
A. Honeymoon stage 
B. Integration stage  
C. Negotiation stage 
D. Adaptation stage  
Question 2. The word “euphoric” in paragraph 1 is opposite in meaning to _______.  
A. miserable 
B. joyful 
C. excited 
D. delighted 
Question 3. The word “disappears” in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to _______. 
A. emerges 
B. vanishes 
C. grows 
D. remains 
Question 4. The phrase “this phase” in paragraph 3 refers to ________. 
A. an end  
 
B. the adjustment phase 
C. a result  
 
D. the negotiation stage

Question 5. Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence in paragraph 3? 
You may still experience some difficulties at this stage, but you’re now able to handle them in a 
more rational and measured way. 
A. When difficulties continue, you are still unable to respond in a calm or rational manner. 
B. Although challenges remain, you can now deal with them more calmly and logically.  
C. Despite problems, people tend to react emotionally rather than think carefully at this stage. 
D. As issues persist, individuals struggle to manage them in a balanced and controlled way. 
Question 6. Which of the following is TRUE according to the passage? 
A. The adaptation stage brings back the same excitement as the honeymoon. 
B. The honeymoon stage is marked by anxiety and physical discomfort. 
C. The adjustment stage removes all cultural difficulties and challenges. 
D. In the negotiation stage, people often feel frustrated and miss home.  
Question 7. In which paragraph is the gradual improvement and development of routine described? 
A. Paragraph 1 
B. Paragraph 2 
C. Paragraph 3 
D. Paragraph 4 
Question 8. In which paragraph are physical symptoms and homesickness discussed? 
A. Paragraph 1 
B. Paragraph 3 
C. Paragraph 4 
D. Paragraph 2 
 
Read the following passage and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct answer to each of 
the questions from 9 to 18. 
It is no exaggeration to say that the United States always was, is now, and always will be a 
nation of immigrants. From the first migrants who crossed the Bering Strait to Alaska and migrated 
down the West coast, possibly as early as around 20,000 years ago, to today’s immigrants coming from 
all four corners of the earth, America was built by immigrants. In a sense, America is like a huge puzzle, 
completely finished on one end, but continually growing as new pieces are added to the other, representing 
newly arriving immigrants. 
These people are continually changing America’s identity. [I] It is often said that America is a 
melting pot in which newly arrived immigrants merge with those already here to produce a new breed of 
Americans. To draw an analogy, the idea is that integrating new immigrants is like baking a cake. The 
ingredients of flour, shortening, eggs, and sugar are mixed together to bake the American cake. Contrast 
that view with that of Canada, which sees itself as a cultural mosaic of brightly colored bits of ethnicity, 
culture, racial identity, and language embedded side by side. [II] These visual metaphors attempt to 
portray each country’s policies and how they incorporate new immigrants into their societies. Critics of 
these older formulations advance the notions of diversity and inclusion as better views on how 
immigration and cultural policies should deal with immigrants in their societies. 
Just as immigrants are changing the identity of America, however, the country is also changing the 
identity of immigrants. [III] Consider that on the first day of arrival on American soil, immigrants bring 
with them their identities forged back home. These identities may include a different language, culture, 
religion, dress, and values - differences that are not ‘normal’ in North America. In time, many immigrants 
adapt and take on the ways of the majority in America. An example is that male Sikhs sometimes abandon 
their turbans and clothes and cut their hair. [IV] By and large, such immigrants love America and are glad 
they were allowed to come here. Yet many also love their former homeland as well. There is nothing 
strange or wrong here: just as one can love her mother and father at the same time, she can also love 
America as well as Italy, for example, if that is where she is from. 
(Adapted from https://www.forbes.com/)

SẮP XẾP TRONG ĐỀ THI (BUỔI 1) – ĐỀ NÂNG CAO

Mark the letter A, B, C or D to indicate the best arrangement of utterances to make a meaningful 
exchange in each of the following questions from 1 to 20. 
Question 1.

noticing.

e. Nina: Reusing helps with that, because everyday items can last longer than we expect.

C. b – a – e – d – c  
D. b – d – e – a – c

a. Daniel: That’s a good idea, since it cuts emissions and lets you experience places more slowly.

D. c – b – a

b. Anna: I think the washing machine is one of the most useful inventions for housewives.  
c. Anna: The washing machine has really changed daily life at home. 
d. Linda: Because of that, housewives feel less tired after doing housework.  
e. Linda: Yes, it saves a lot of time compared to washing clothes by hand.  
A. b – d – c – e – a  
B. c – d – b – e – a  
C. c – e – a – d – b  
D. b – e – a – d – c

A. c – a – b  
B. a – c – b  
C. a – b – c  
D. c – b – a  
Question 20. 
a. Alex: That’s why I believe stronger laws are needed to protect species from disappearing. 
b. Alex: I’ve been thinking about hunting animals, and it worries me how it affects wildlife. 
c. Jordan: Some people say it is tradition, but uncontrolled hunting clearly harms animal populations.  
A. b – c – a  
B. a – b – c  
C. a – c – b  
D. b – a – c

VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
TÀI LIỆU LIVESTREAM ĐỘC QUYỀN 
VIP 90 (2026): TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM BỘ CÂU HỎI  
DỄ TRÚNG TỦ & LUYỆN BỘ ĐỀ TINH HOA  
[V90.10.02] TINH HOA ĐỌC HIỂU (BUỔI 14) 
 
 
Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương  
 
Quét mã QR để Ôn luyện chuyên sâu Từ vựng với EZ Vocab và Review Từ vựng;  
cá nhân hóa việc học Từ vựng với Flashcard Từ vựng của tôi trên App: Tiếng Anh cô Mai Phương 
 
 
 
LEVEL 1 
 
 
 
 
Text 1 
Steve Jobs was an American businessman and inventor who changed the way people use 
technology. He was born in 1955 and grew up in California. From a young age, he showed a strong interest 
in electronics and design. In 1976, he helped start Apple with his friend Steve Wozniak. Their first 
computers were simple, but they quickly became popular among users who wanted something easy to 
use.   
Jobs believed that technology should be both useful and beautiful. Because of this idea, Apple 
created products that were not only powerful but also attractive. After leaving the company for a period, 
he returned in the late 1990s and led Apple to great success. Under his leadership, the company introduced 
devices like the iPod, the iPhone, and the iPad, which transformed how people listen to music, 
communicate, and work. Although he faced many challenges, Jobs remained creative and determined. He 
passed away in 2011, but his ideas continue to influence the modern world. 
 
Question 1. Which of the following is NOT true about Steve Jobs in paragraph 1?   
A. He was an American businessman and inventor.  
B. He showed early interest in electronics and design.  
C. He started Apple alone in 1976.  
D. He grew up in California. 
 
Trang 16

TUẦN SỐ 10 
 
VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
Question 2. The word “they” in paragraph 1 refers to _____. 
A. first computers 
 
B. electronics 
 
C. users 
 
D. Jobs and Wozniak 
Question 3. The word “transformed” in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to  ______.  
A. moved 
B. touched 
C. changed 
D. fixed 
LEVEL 2 
Question 4. Which of the following can be implied about Steve Jobs?   
A. His influence continued even after his death.  
B. His work stopped affecting people before he died.  
C. His ideas were only important during his lifetime.  
D. His success depended mainly on other people. 
 
 
 
 
 
Text 2 
Throughout history, many female scientists have made important contributions to science, yet they 
did not receive the recognition they deserved. In many cases, social barriers and gender bias limited their 
opportunities, even when their work was essential to major discoveries. As a result, their achievements 
were often ignored or credited to male colleagues, which has affected how scientific history is 
remembered today.  
One well-known example is Rosalind Franklin, whose research on DNA structure played a crucial 
role in understanding the double helix. Although her work provided key evidence, she did not receive the 
same level of recognition as others involved in the discovery. Similarly, Lise Meitner made significant 
contributions to the study of nuclear fission, but the Nobel Prize was awarded only to her male 
collaborator. These cases highlight a pattern in which women’s work was undervalued despite its 
importance.   
In recent years, there has been greater effort to acknowledge these overlooked scientists and 
correct historical records. By recognising their contributions, society not only honours their achievements 
but also encourages future generations of women to pursue careers in science. 
 
Question 5. The word “they” in paragraph 1 refers to ______.  
A. contributions 
 
B. cases 
 
C. barriers 
 
D. female scientists 
Question 6. The word “credited” in paragraph 1 mostly means ______. 
A. expected 
B. assigned 
C. prevented 
D. overlooked  
Question 7. What is indicated about Rosalind Franklin?   
A. She worked independently on several scientific projects.  
B. She did not receive equal recognition for her contribution.  
C. She was the only scientist studying DNA structure. 
D. She refused to share her research with others. 
 
 
 
 
Trang 17

Question 8. What is indicated about Lise Meitner?   
A. She led the research on nuclear fission independently.  
B. She received international awards for her scientific work.  
C. She stopped her research before the discovery was completed.  
D. She did not receive the Nobel Prize despite her contribution. 
Question 9. Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence in paragraph 2? 
These cases highlight a pattern in which women’s work was undervalued despite its importance. 
A. These examples show that women’s contributions were often undervalued even though they were 
important.  
B. These examples show that women’s contributions were important because they were often 
undervalued.   
C. These examples suggest that the importance of women’s work resulted from it being undervalued.   
D. These examples indicate that women’s work became less important as it was increasingly valued. 
Question 10. The word “acknowledge” in paragraph 3 can be best replaced by _____. 
A. integrate 
B. recognise 
C. contribute 
D. accommodate 
LEVEL 3 
 
 
 
 
 
Text 3 
Stephen Hawking was one of the most influential physicists of the modern era, widely respected 
for his work on black holes and the origins of the universe. Born in 1942 in England, he showed an early 
interest in science, although he was not always considered an outstanding student. He later studied at the 
University of Oxford and then at Cambridge, where he began to develop his ideas about cosmology.   
At the age of 21, Hawking was diagnosed with a serious disease called ALS, which gradually 
limited his physical movement. Despite this challenge, he continued his research with remarkable 
determination, refusing to let his condition define his life. Over time, he lost the ability to speak and relied 
on a speech-generating device to communicate, yet he remained active in both academic and public life.   
Hawking made groundbreaking contributions to science, particularly his theory that black holes 
can emit radiation, now known as Hawking radiation. In addition, his book A Brief History of Time made 
complex scientific ideas accessible to a wide audience, which greatly increased public interest in 
cosmology. Although he faced severe physical limitations, his intellectual strength and curiosity never 
faded, and his legacy continues to inspire scientists and learners around the world. 
 
Question 11. The word “outstanding” in paragraph 1 is OPPOSITE in meaning to ______. 
A. excellent 
B. curious 
C. indifferent 
D. average 
Question 12. What is indicated about Hawking in paragraph 2? 
A. He stopped working after his illness became more serious.  
B. He remained committed to his work despite his condition.  
C. He depended on others to complete most of his research.  
D. He recovered quickly and returned to normal life. 
 
 
 
Trang 18

Question 14. Which of the following is NOT true about Hawking according to paragraph 3?

B. His book helped make complex science easier for readers.  
C. His work had little impact on public interest in cosmology.

Question 15. The word “faded” in paragraph 3 is closest in meaning to _____.  
A. declined 
B. exposed 
C. maintained

LEVEL 4 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Text 4 
Greta Thunberg has emerged as one of the most influential climate activists of the 21st century, 
transforming public discourse through her unwavering commitment and moral clarity. Born in Sweden in 
2003, she first gained attention in 2018 when she began a solitary school strike outside the Swedish 
parliament, demanding stronger governmental action on climate change. What initially appeared to be an 
individual protest quickly evolved into a global phenomenon, as students around the world joined her 
movement, now widely known as Fridays for Future. 
Thunberg’s impact lies not only in her ability to mobilise large numbers of young people but also 
in her direct and uncompromising rhetoric. By openly criticising political leaders and exposing what she 
perceives as their failure to act decisively, she has challenged established norms of political 
communication. [I] Her speeches, often characterised by emotional intensity and factual precision, have 
resonated with audiences who feel frustrated by slow progress on environmental issues. [II] At the same 
time, her approach has attracted criticism from those who argue that her tone is overly confrontational. 
[III] Her activism has encouraged greater awareness of environmental urgency and has pressured 
institutions to reconsider their policies. While the long-term impact of her efforts remains uncertain, 
her role in redefining youth engagement in political activism is undeniable. [IV] 
 
Question 16. Which of the following best summarises paragraph 1? 
A. Greta Thunberg, a Swedish activist, led a youth climate movement that gained global attention.  
B. Greta Thunberg began a protest outside parliament, urging governments to act on climate change.   
C. Greta Thunberg became notorious through her strong views and clear messages on environmental 
issues.   
D. Greta Thunberg inspired students worldwide, who joined a growing movement for environmental 
protection. 
Question 17. The word “uncompromising” in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to ______. 
A. odd 
B. firm 
C. flexible 
D. risky

[V90.10.02B] THI ONLINE: TINH HOA ĐỌC HIỂU  
 
(BUỔI 14) – ĐỀ VẬN DỤNG 
Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương  
 
 
Quét mã QR để Ôn luyện chuyên sâu Từ vựng với EZ Vocab và Review Từ vựng;

LEVEL 1 
Text 1 
 
Oprah Winfrey was born into a poor family in rural Mississippi. Her early life was difficult, as she 
faced poverty and many personal challenges. However, she worked hard at school and showed great talent 
in speaking. Later, she began working in radio and television, slowly building her career. Her big success 
came with The Oprah Winfrey Show, which became one of the most popular talk shows in the world. 
Through her program, she inspired millions of people by sharing real stories and important life lessons. 
Oprah also became a successful businesswoman and a global influencer. Today, she is known not only 
for her wealth but also for her charity work, helping others improve their lives. 
 
Question 1. Which of the following is NOT mentioned in the text as one of Oprah Winfrey’s 
achievements? 
A. Charity work 
 
B. The Oprah Winfrey Show 
C. Winning an Oscar  
 
D. Being a successful businesswoman 
Question 2. The word “wealth” in the text is closest in meaning to _______. 
A. richness 
B. strength 
C. poverty 
D. norm  
Question 3. The word “their” in the text refers to ______. 
A. life lessons 
B. others  
C. lives 
D. charity work

VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
Text 2 
 
In life, many successful people do not achieve their goals easily but must overcome repeated 
failure. A clear example is Jack Ma, who was born into a modest family in China and faced many 
difficulties in his early years. He failed important school exams several times and was rejected from many 
jobs, including one at KFC. However, he never gave up and continued to improve himself, especially his 
English skills. Later, he discovered the internet and saw a valuable opportunity. In 1999, he founded 
Alibaba, which started as a small company but soon grew rapidly. Today, Jack Ma is a billionaire and a 
global business icon, proving that failure can lead to great success. 
 
Question 4. Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence in the text? 
In life, many successful people do not achieve their goals easily but must overcome repeated failure. 
A. Repeated failure prevents most individuals from ever achieving success in life. 
B. Many successful individuals achieve their goals easily without experiencing any difficulties. 
C. Successful people usually avoid failure completely and succeed on their first attempt. 
D. Lots of successful individuals reach their goals only after getting continual many failures.  
Question 5. The word “rejected” in the text is OPPOSITE in meaning to _______. 
A. disagreed 
B. refused 
C. accepted 
D. dismissed 
Question 6. Jack Ma _______. 
A. founded Alibaba in 1999 after discovering the internet as a valuable opportunity 
B. was accepted into a job at KFC after completing his early education in China 
C. passed all important school exams successfully without facing any major difficulties 
D. gave up his goals after being rejected from several jobs in his early years 
 
 
 
LEVEL 2 
Text 3 
 
Fame is often seen as a dream, bringing wealth, admiration, and influence. However, behind the 
glamorous image, it can come with intense pressure and personal struggles. Many celebrities are expected 
to be perfect at all times, which can lead to stress, loneliness, and emotional challenges. The higher they 
rise, the more they are judged, making it difficult to live a normal life or find true peace. 
 
The life of Michael Jackson clearly shows this reality. Known as the “King of Pop,” he achieved 
global success at a very young age. Despite his talent and fame, he faced constant media attention and 
criticism. His desire for perfection pushed him to work endlessly, often at the cost of his health and 
happiness. In the end, his story reminds us that fame has a price, and true well-being is more important 
than public success. 
 
Question 7. The word “glamorous” in paragraph 1 is opposite in meaning to _______.  
A. impressive 
B. attractive 
C. ordinary  
D. luxurious 
Question 8. Which of the following is NOT mentioned in paragraph 1 as a feeling experienced by 
celebrities under the pressure of being perfect? 
A. jealousy 
 
B. stress 
 
C. loneliness 
 
D. emotional challenges 
 
 
Trang 22

VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
Question 9. Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence in paragraph 2? 
His desire for perfection pushed him to work endlessly, often at the cost of his health and happiness.  
A. His desire for perfection allowed him to sacrifice his work for better health and happiness. 
B. His strong need to be perfect made him work nonstop, sacrificing his health and happiness.  
C. He worked less overtime because he wanted to protect his health and personal happiness. 
D. His success came mainly from working comfortably without facing any pressure or personal cost.  
Question 10. Which of the following is TRUE according to the passage? 
A. Michael Jackson avoided media attention and lived a peaceful life. 
B. Fame provides admiration without any negative effects on celebrities. 
C. Celebrities are rarely judged by the public and can easily live normal lives. 
D. Fame can bring success, but also cause pressure and personal struggles.  
 
 
Text 4 
 
Talent is often seen as the key to success, but in reality, it is only the starting point. Without hard 
work, even the most gifted individuals may fail to achieve their full potential. Talent can give 
someone an advantage, but it cannot replace effort, discipline, and perseverance. Many people with 
average abilities have succeeded because they worked harder and stayed focused on their goals. In 
contrast, those who rely only on talent may lose motivation when they face challenges or competition. 
 
This idea can be seen in the lives of successful figures like Cristiano Ronaldo and Thomas Edison. 
Ronaldo was talented, but he became one of the greatest footballers through strict training and constant 
practice. Edison, on the other hand, failed thousands of times before inventing the light bulb. Their stories 
show that success comes from the combination of natural ability and relentless hard work. Therefore, 
while talent is important, it is hard work that truly leads people to greatness. 
 
Question 11. The word “it” in paragraph 1 refers to _______.  
A. success 
B. talent  
C. the key  
D. reality 
Question 12. The word “motivation” in paragraph 1 is OPPOSITE in meaning to _______. 
A. determination 
 
B. inspiration 
 
C. enthusiasm 
 
D. discouragement  
Question 13. Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence in paragraph 1? 
Without hard work, even the most gifted individuals may fail to achieve their full potential. 
A. Highly talented people can always succeed without putting in much effort or discipline. 
B. Even highly talented people may not reach their full potential if they do not work hard.  
C. Hard work is necessary for gifted individuals as their natural ability guarantees success. 
D. People with little talent will achieve more success than gifted individuals without any effort. 
Question 14. Which of the following is TRUE according to the passage? 
A. People with average abilities rarely achieve success even if they work very hard. 
B. Success usually comes from both natural talent and continuous hard work.  
C. Cristiano Ronaldo became successful mainly because of his natural talent alone. 
D. Thomas Edison succeeded quickly without experiencing repeated failures.

VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026

Question 15. Despite the importance of talent, _______. 
A. a person will reach their full potential with only hard work 
B. individuals cannot achieve their goals easily without it 
C. some talented people may avoid challenges and competition 
D. hard work is the main factor that leads people to true success  
 
 
LEVEL 3 
Text 5 
 
Education has never been a natural state of equality; rather, it is the result of long, often difficult 
struggles against deeply rooted systems of exclusion. Even today, equal access to education remains an 
unfinished goal, shaped by inequalities in gender, class, and geography. [I] What we now take for 
granted—universal schooling, the right to learn, and the idea that knowledge should be accessible to all—
exists largely because earlier generations refused to accept injustice and fought to expand these rights. 
 
Figures such as Malala Yousafzai, Nelson Mandela, and Horace Mann embody this enduring 
struggle. [II] Malala risked her life advocating for girls’ education in the face of violent opposition. 
Mandela viewed education as a powerful tool to dismantle apartheid and promote social justice. Long 
before them, Horace Mann laid the foundation for public education systems. [III] 
 
Their efforts remind us that educational equality is neither complete nor guaranteed. Progress must 
be defended and extended through continued commitment, policy reform, and social awareness. [IV] 
Without such vigilance, the gaps they fought to close may easily widen again, leaving future generations 
to repeat the same battles. 
 
Question 16. Nowadays, education _______. 
A. has become completely equal and no longer faces any challenges worldwide 
B. refused to accept injustice and discrimination and fought to expand these rights  
C. is no longer influenced by factors such as gender, class, or geography 
D. is still not equally accessible to everyone due to ongoing social inequalities  
Question 17. The word “injustice” in paragraph 1 is opposite in meaning to _______. 
A. rejection 
B. inequality

Read the following passage and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct answer to each of 
the questions from 1 to 8. 
Taylor Swift is one of the most successful and influential artists of her generation, widely 
recognised for her ability to adapt and evolve across different musical styles. Born in 1989 in the United 
States, she began her career as a country singer, writing songs that reflected personal experiences and 
everyday emotions. Her early success was built on relatable storytelling, which helped her connect deeply 
with listeners and quickly establish a loyal fan base. 
As her career developed, Swift made a strategic transition from country to pop music, which 
significantly broadened her international appeal. Albums such as 1989 marked a turning point, featuring 
polished production and memorable melodies that attracted a wider audience. Despite this shift, her 
songwriting remained central, as she continued to explore themes of identity, relationships, and personal 
growth in a way that felt both honest and engaging. 
Beyond her musical achievements, Swift has played a notable role in shaping discussions 
about artists’ rights and creative ownership. She has publicly addressed issues related to control over 
her work and has taken concrete steps to reclaim her recordings. Through these actions, she has influenced 
industry practices while also strengthening her connection with fans, who admire her determination and 
transparency. 
Although she has faced criticism and public scrutiny at various stages of her career, Swift has 
demonstrated resilience and a strong sense of artistic direction. Rather than allowing setbacks to define 
her, she has consistently used them as opportunities for growth and reinvention. As a result, her influence 
now extends beyond music, shaping cultural trends and inspiring audiences around the world.

Marie Curie stands as one of the most remarkable figures in the history of science, renowned for 
her groundbreaking contributions to the study of radioactivity. Born in Poland in 1867, she later moved 
to Paris, where she pursued higher education despite significant financial and social obstacles. [I] At a 
time when women were largely excluded from scientific circles, Curie demonstrated exceptional 
perseverance, immersing herself in rigorous research that would ultimately reshape modern physics and 
chemistry. 
Working alongside her husband Pierre Curie, she conducted meticulous experiments that led to 
the discovery of two new elements, polonium and radium. Her work not only advanced scientific 
understanding but also introduced the concept of radioactivity as a fundamental property of certain 
elements. What distinguished Curie’s research was not merely the novelty of her findings but also the

precision and determination with which she pursued them, often under physically demanding conditions 
in poorly equipped laboratories. 
Curie’s achievements were recognised with two Nobel Prizes, making her the first person to 
receive this honour in two different scientific fields. [II] During the First World War, she applied her 
knowledge to develop mobile X-ray units, which significantly improved medical treatment for wounded 
soldiers. This practical application of science underscored her belief that knowledge should serve 
humanity. [III] 
Despite her extraordinary accomplishments, Curie faced persistent gender discrimination 
throughout her career. Nevertheless, her intellectual resilience and unwavering dedication allowed her 
to overcome these barriers and leave an enduring impact on the scientific community. Today, she is 
remembered not only as a brilliant scientist but also as a symbol of determination and innovation, whose 
work continues to influence research and inspire generations worldwide. [IV]

SẮP XẾP TRONG ĐỀ THI (BUỔI 2) – ĐỀ VẬN DỤNG

cá nhân hóa việc học Từ vựng với Flashcard Từ vựng của tôi trên App: Tiếng Anh cô Mai Phương 
 
 
 
 
Mark the letter A, B, C or D to indicate the best arrangement of sentences to make a meaningful text 
in each of the following questions from 1 to 20. 
Question 1.  
Hi Emma,  
a. I’ve already started using it, and it’s perfect for jotting down my thoughts and ideas.  
b. Let’s meet up soon so I can show you how I’m using it!  
c. The cover design is exactly my style, and the paper quality is great for writing.  
d. Thanks so much for the beautiful notebook you gave me last week!  
e. You always pick such thoughtful gifts, and I truly appreciate it.  
Write back soon, 
Max

week.  
d. If that’s not possible, could you recommend some resources I can use for extra practice?  
e. I’d really appreciate any guidance you can provide.

Read the following passage and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct option that best fits 
each of the numbered blanks from 1 to 5. 
Plastic pollution is becoming a serious global problem, especially as large companies continue to 
produce huge amounts of plastic each year. Recent reports show that by 2030, products from Coca-Cola 
alone could create about 602 million kilograms of plastic waste entering oceans and waterways annually. 
This amount is extremely large and shows how single-use plastic packaging (1) ______. Because Coca-
Cola is one of the biggest beverage companies in the world, its impact on the environment is much greater 
than that of smaller companies. 
This growing problem is not only harmful to marine life but also (2) ______. Scientists have found 
that tiny plastic particles, called microplastics, can enter the human body and may be linked to serious 
diseases. At the same time, experts argue that (3) ______. Instead, they suggest using more reusable 
packaging, which can be used many times and reduce waste significantly. 
In the future, companies will need to take stronger action (4) ______. This includes cutting down 
on single-use plastics and increasing the use of reusable materials. Governments and consumers also play 
an important role by supporting sustainable products and reducing their own plastic use. (5) _______. 
 
Question 1.  
A. is still widely used 
 
B. which is still widely used 
C. and still widely used 
 
D. still widely used

Read the following passage and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct option that best fits

Artificial intelligence is often presented as a force that will improve productivity and create new 
opportunities; however, recent analysis suggests that its impact may be far more disruptive, especially 
when combined with rising energy costs. As companies face increasing pressure to reduce expenses, many 
are turning to automation not only to improve efficiency but also to replace human labour, (6) _____. 
While earlier technological revolutions eventually created new employment, there is growing concern that 
this time the scale and speed of change could be much greater, (7) _____.   
At the same time, the energy crisis is intensifying these effects, since high production costs and 
limited resources encourage businesses to rely even more heavily on AI-driven systems. As a result, a 
dangerous cycle may emerge, in which job losses reduce consumer spending, (8) _____, thereby causing 
even more unemployment. Some experts warn that this process could trigger a wider economic slowdown 
or even financial instability if it continues unchecked.   
Despite these risks, not everyone agrees that such a “doomsday scenario” will become reality, as 
history has shown that (9) ______. Nevertheless, because the current situation involves both rapid 
automation and external economic pressures, it is widely argued that governments must act quickly by 
investing in education, retraining workers, and creating policies that support a more balanced transition. 
Without such measures, (10) _____. 
 
Question 6. 
A. the acceleration of job losses across different sectors 
B. job losses may be accelerated across different sectors 
C. which may accelerate job losses across different sectors 
D. when jobs losses may be accelerated across different sectors

The promise of driverless cars, once presented as an inevitable step towards a more efficient and 
safer transport system, is increasingly being questioned as real-world developments fail to match earlier 
expectations. (1) _____, the idea that autonomous vehicles will soon dominate the roads remains 
uncertain, particularly as several high-profile projects have struggled to achieve commercial viability. 
Rather than transforming mobility in the way many predicted, (2) _____, raising doubts about whether 
they can function effectively at scale. 
One of the central issues lies in the assumption that technological innovation alone can solve 
deeply rooted transport problems, when in reality, congestion, inefficiency, and environmental concerns 
(3) _____. Even if vehicles become driverless, they are still cars, occupying space, contributing to traffic, 
and reinforcing patterns of individual rather than collective travel. As a result, the widespread adoption 
of autonomous cars may do little to reduce the number of vehicles on the road, and could, in some cases, 
(4) _____. 
From this perspective, a more effective approach would be to prioritise investment in public 
transport systems, which are capable of moving larger numbers of people more efficiently while reducing 
environmental impact. While driverless technology may still have a role to play, particularly in supporting 
specific services, (5) _____. The debate, therefore, is not simply about whether autonomous vehicles will 
succeed, but about how societies choose to organise mobility in a way that genuinely addresses the 
challenges of modern urban life.

when sleep requirements are significantly higher.

creating a cycle in which disrupted nights (2) _____. Studies indicate that when children sleep badly, 
parents’ sleep is also affected, which in turn increases stress and reduces daily productivity. Moreover, 
insufficient rest has been linked to negative mood, weaker concentration, and poorer daytime performance 
in children, suggesting that (3) _____. Because sleep occurs within a shared family environment, it cannot 
be treated as an isolated habit but must be understood as a collective responsibility. 
To address this issue, experts emphasise the importance of consistent routines and supportive 
environments, (4) _____. Establishing regular bedtimes, reducing screen exposure, and creating calming 
pre-sleep activities can significantly improve sleep quality. In addition, open conversations about the 
benefits of sleep, when framed in simple and positive ways, (5) _____. Ultimately, when families

In an era shaped by global travel, digital media, and fast-moving trends, cultural boundaries are 
more visible yet more porous than ever. As people encounter symbols, clothing, music, and rituals from 
beyond their own communities, (1) _____. Cultural appropriation, a term that has gained prominence in 
public debate, refers to situations in which aspects of a culture are adopted by outsiders, often without 
understanding their original meaning or historical context. While exchange is a natural feature of human 
societies, tension emerges when power, history, and inequality are ignored. 
At the centre of the discussion is the idea that (2) ______. When a dominant group adopts practices 
from a marginalised community, especially if those practices were once ridiculed or suppressed, the act 
can feel exploitative rather than appreciative. Critics argue that meaning is stripped away when cultural 
symbols are reduced to aesthetic choices, turning lived traditions into commodities. Supporters of open 
cultural exchange, by contrast, suggest that creativity thrives when influences overlap, (3) ______. 
Understanding cultural appropriation therefore requires attention to context, including who is 
borrowing, why it is happening, and (4) ______. Practices borrowed thoughtfully, while recognising their 
origins and significance, can foster dialogue rather than resentment. (5) ______, leaving those whose

Read the following passage and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct option that best fits 
each of the numbered blanks from 1 to 5. 
Adolescence is a difficult time for both parents and teenagers (1) ______. Physically, as a 
dependent pre-teen child, you are being transformed into an independent young adult. In general, 
emotionally and socially, the adolescents like you are experiencing profound changes in terms of freedom, 
privacy, interests, decisions and opinions.         
First, you want more freedom to choose who to see and when. (2) ______. Now you want to spend 
some more time on your own. Second, your parents made most decisions before adolescence. But now 
you want to decide things for yourselves. You hate being told what to do all the time. (3) ______. You 
see the world differently, develop your own views and your own sense of right or wrong. You feel you 
have all the answers while adults don't.       
All these perfectly normal changes (4) _______. So what can you, as adolescents, do to stay on 
good terms with your parents? Firstly, let your parents know that you still love and value them. Secondly, 
try to agree on rules and boundaries and prove to be dependable. Keep your words and stick to the rules. 
Thirdly, try to understand why they might be irritated by your behaviour. Show your parents that you are 
capable of an independent life by taking care of your own school work and maintaining a healthy lifestyle. 
Finally, it's all about communication! Keep talking to your parents. (5) _______. 
 
 
 
 
 
Trang 73
