Read the following passage and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct answer to each of 
the questions from 23 to 30. 
For many adults today, caring responsibilities no longer follow a simple, one-directional path; 
instead, they extend across generations, creating what is widely known as the “sandwich generation.” 
These individuals, often in midlife, find themselves supporting both their children and ageing parents at 
the same time, a situation that is becoming increasingly common as people live longer and start families 
later. While this dual role may appear manageable in theory, it often places individuals under sustained 
pressure, particularly when financial, emotional, and time-related demands begin to overlap.   
What makes this experience particularly complex is that caregiving is not something that can easily 
be scheduled or limited; rather, it tends to expand, gradually shaping daily life. Many people report that, 
instead of fitting care around their routines, they reorganise their entire lives around the needs of others, 
leaving little room for rest or personal development. This shift, which can happen almost unnoticed, may 
lead to long-term exhaustion, especially when support systems are limited or inconsistent.   
In addition, the emotional impact of this role is often underestimated, even though it plays a central 
part in the experience. Individuals may feel a strong sense of responsibility towards both generations, 
yet struggle with guilt when they are unable to meet every expectation. At the same time, financial 
strain can increase, particularly when the costs of healthcare and childcare rise simultaneously, making it 
difficult to maintain stability while continuing to provide care. 
Despite these challenges, the sandwich generation also demonstrates remarkable resilience, as 
many individuals develop coping strategies that allow them to manage competing demands. However, 
unless broader social support is strengthened - through policies, workplace flexibility, or community 
resources - the burden is likely to grow, raising important questions about how societies value and sustain 
care in the long term. 
(Adapted from The Guardian)

A. Because individuals rarely feel responsible for both generations, they tend to feel guilty even after 
meeting most demands.   
B. Only when every expectation is fulfilled do people begin to feel uneasy about caring for both age 
groups.   
C. Although many feel deep obligation to both age groups, they often feel guilty when failing to satisfy 
every demand.   
D. Despite experiencing a strong duty towards both generations, individuals hardly feel guilty when 
unable to meet all expectations.

Read the following passage and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct answer to each of 
the questions from 31 to 40. 
The rapid expansion of data centres, driven by the exponential growth of artificial intelligence and 
cloud computing, has begun to exert a significant yet underreported impact on the environment. While 
these facilities underpin the digital economy, their energy consumption is immense, with some estimates 
suggesting that global demand from data centres could rival that of entire countries in the near future. [I] 
This surge in electricity use, intensified by the need for uninterrupted operation, places mounting pressure 
on already strained power grids, particularly in regions where energy infrastructure is fragile or heavily 
reliant on fossil fuels. [II] 
Beyond their appetite for electricity, data centres generate substantial amounts of heat, a by-
product of the continuous processing of vast data streams. To prevent overheating, operators rely on 
advanced cooling systems, many of which consume additional energy and contribute indirectly to 
environmental degradation. In some cases, clusters of data centres have been linked to the formation of 
localised “heat islands,” whereby surrounding areas experience elevated temperatures. Such thermal 
effects, although not always immediately visible, can disrupt local ecosystems and intensify broader 
climatic stressors over time. 
Equally concerning is the scale of water usage associated with cooling operations. [III]  In certain 
regions, especially those already facing water scarcity, data centres require enormous volumes of water 
to maintain optimal operating conditions. This creates a troubling paradox: technologies that promise 
efficiency and innovation simultaneously place pressure on finite natural resources, raising questions 
about sustainability and equitable resource distribution. 
Although major technology firms have begun to invest in renewable energy and explore alternative 
cooling methods, progress remains uneven and, at times, largely symbolic. [IV] Without a more 
coordinated and transparent approach - one that addresses energy demand, thermal emissions, and water 
consumption in tandem - the environmental footprint of data centres is likely to expand further, prompting 
urgent debate over how digital advancement can be balanced with long-term ecological responsibility. 
(Adapted from CNN)

A. justify 
B. regulate 
C. power 
D. implement

Read the following passage and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct answer to each of 
the questions from 1 to 8. 
Andrew Tate, a well-known influencer on TikTok, has gained significant traction with audiences 
of young men whose ages range from primary to post-secondary. He often chooses videos of himself 
discussing controversial topics to generate views on social media, and his viewers are presented with 
fantasies of what an “ideal” and “successful” life can be.  
Tate’s version of an ideal, successful life is one that promotes male supremacy and female 
inferiority, including the subjugation of women. The premise of much of his content revolves around the 
idea that men and their behaviours should be aggressive, misogynistic, derogatory, and violent. For 
instance, Tate views women as the property of men, and he says that women bear responsibility for their 
attacks. 
Tate has a huge audience. Based on views and subscriptions to his TikTok channel, Tate was a 
leading name in the summer of 2022. Tate’s apparent abundant wealth and willingness to share 
instructions on how to accumulate such wealth give young men and boys an economic incentive to listen 
to him. Hustler University, an online learning platform developed by Tate, invites people to pay for a 
subscription so they can learn how to mirror Tate’s wealthy lifestyle. His global mentorship program 
further entices individuals to absorb and repeat his behaviours in public spaces such as schools. 
As a teacher who has taught across Canada, I have seen how toxic masculine behaviour allows 
young boys to distance themselves from others. It is common to see young men and boys practice the 
expressions and behaviours of what men are supposedly meant to express in a public setting. These 
behaviours and expressions are encouraged and enhanced by individuals such as Tate. These expressions

The trajectory of life is often determined by a mere handful of important decisions. These pivot

back and look introspectively at your life, it’s likely that you’ll see a few events that changed everything.

Often, you don’t have any inkling that any solitary decision might end up as consequential as those 
mentioned above. [II] That’s the rub; all of your choices matter, but some will matter much more than 
others. All choices aren’t created equal. The ultimate chain of positive or negative events set into motion 
by a single decision can’t be fully known in advance. It’s difficult, if not impossible, to separate emotion 
from rationale when you make routine choices. [III] If you don’t have long-term aspirations to guide you, 
it’s easy to go ‘around and around’ without making real progress. 
One useful tip is to look at decisions beyond the “it’s a good deal’ aspect alone. Your life can 
easily get off track by following the path of ‘good deals’ that have little to do with your longer-term goals. 
[IV] The pivotal points in your life often come packaged as dilemmas. Difficult choices between 
alternatives present both opportunities and dangers.

Read the following passage and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct option that best fits 
each of the numbered blanks from 1 to 5. 
In recent years, growing concern has emerged over the extent to which modern education systems 
prioritise examination performance at the expense of broader development, a trend strongly criticised by 
Alan Milburn. Drawing on survey data from teachers, he argues that an excessive focus on testing not 
only narrows the curriculum but also (1) ______. A significant proportion of educators believe that, while 
academic attainment remains important, the current system fails to cultivate essential competencies such 
as communication, collaboration, and adaptability, (2) ______.   
This imbalance, which is rooted in accountability frameworks that prioritise measurable outcomes, 
has led to a form of education in which success is frequently equated with exam results rather than 
meaningful learning. As a consequence, skills that are less easily quantified - such as creativity, resilience, 
and emotional intelligence - are often marginalised, (3) ______. Furthermore, the neglect of vocational 
pathways and real-world experiences has widened the gap between schooling and employment, leaving 
many young people uncertain about how to apply their knowledge beyond the classroom.   
(4) ______, particularly the integration of academic and vocational learning alongside improved 
career guidance and stronger links between schools and employers. Advocates argue that, (5) ______, the 
number of young people disengaged from both education and employment may continue to rise.

current episode is not only its scale but also its persistence, as elevated ocean temperatures have remained 
unusually high across consecutive seasons. Because corals rely on a delicate symbiotic relationship with

vulnerable to mortality.

heatwaves, which, rather than allowing reefs to regenerate, (8) ______. In earlier decades, bleaching was 
often confined to specific regions and followed by intervals of cooler conditions; however, the present 
pattern suggests a shift towards near-constant thermal pressure. As marine heat spreads across different 
ocean basins simultaneously, (9) ______, indicating that geographic refuge is becoming increasingly

Read the following passage and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct option that best fits 
each of the numbered blanks from 11 to 15. 
In recent years, the way people consume news has undergone a significant transformation, largely 
driven by rapid technological advances. With the widespread use of smartphones and digital platforms, 
(11) ______. As a result, traditional printed newspapers, which once played a central role in daily life, 
(12) ______. 
For many publishers, this shift has created serious financial challenges. Advertising revenue, 
which used to support print journalism, has increasingly moved online, where competition is far more 
intense. (13) ______, not all have succeeded in attracting enough paying readers. At the same time, the 
speed at which news is now produced and shared means that stories, which would once have been 
carefully edited before publication, are sometimes released with less scrutiny, raising concerns about 
accuracy and reliability. 
Despite these challenges, the disappearance of printed newspapers is not entirely certain. While 
younger generations tend to prefer digital formats, there are still readers who value the physical experience 
of holding a newspaper, as well as (14) ______. It is possible that (15) ______, serving a smaller but loyal 
audience that appreciates their depth and credibility.

Read the following passage and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct answer to each of 
the questions from 23 to 30. 
The world can be difficult to navigate alone, and many of us seek guidance from people we admire. 
We learn from and often copy these people, so it is wise to choose role models who put our ideals into 
practice. They can teach us, and reassure us when our self-esteem is low.   
We often choose public figures as role models. YouGov conducts polls of thousands of people to 
discover the world’s most widely admired women and men. These women and men come from many 
walks of life. According to the 2020 poll, Barack and Michelle Obama were the most admired man and 
woman in the world. Angelina Jolie, who has used her fame as an actress to air the poor, was the second-
most popular woman. People also admire religious figures such as the pope and the Dalai Lama. It’s 
amazing how much we idolise these public figures even though most of us will never get to meet 
these famous idols in person!   
Celebrity role models may be common, but the most important role models are those who are 
closest to us and who impact our personal development. We often look up to friends, family, and 
coworkers that we aspire to emulate. A competent leader in the workplace may inspire us to greater 
success. A supportive family member may show us the value of loving ourselves. A good friend may 
display courage, emboldening us to take risks and step bravely into the unknown.   
It’s important to remember that we are also role models. People judge our actions and are 
influenced by our decisions. Children emulate the adults in their lives. Our friends look to us—their 
peers—for approval and disapproval. Being a role model gives everyone a sense of responsibility because 
we know that others are likely to copy our behaviour. In the end, we should work toward becoming our 
own best role model, and as such, our actions should follow this popular quote: “Be the change you want 
to see in the world!” 
(Adapted from Master Blueprint)

C. examine public attitudes towards environmental protection campaigns   
D. measure international opinions about modern educational systems

The rapid expansion of artificial intelligence has begun to expose a largely overlooked 
consequence of the digital revolution: an extraordinary surge in electricity consumption, driven primarily 
by the enormous computational demands of AI data centres. While artificial intelligence is frequently 
celebrated for its transformative potential across sectors ranging from healthcare to finance, far less 
attention has been devoted to the immense pressure such systems are placing on ageing electricity grids, 
many of which were never designed to accommodate facilities consuming as much power as small cities. 
According to energy analysts, the unprecedented growth of AI infrastructure is already forcing utility 
companies to reconsider long-term energy planning and grid expansion.   
Particularly alarming is the concentration of these data centres within a limited number of regions, 
where demand for electricity has risen so sharply that concerns about grid reliability, rising consumer 
costs, and delayed climate goals have become increasingly difficult to ignore. In some states, utility 
providers have warned that ordinary households may ultimately shoulder part of the financial burden 
associated with upgrading transmission systems and maintaining stable electricity supplies for technology 
companies whose appetite for computational power continues to intensify. [I] Major technology 
companies believe artificial intelligence will eventually improve energy efficiency. [II] However, critics 
consider these claims uncertain because the industry still depends heavily on fossil fuels and energy-
intensive cooling systems. 
 
Trang 53

Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
The situation has become even more contentious as governments attempt to balance 
technological innovation with environmental sustainability. Some energy experts have cautioned that, 
unless regulatory frameworks evolve rapidly enough to ensure that technology companies contribute 
fairly to infrastructure costs, the economic and environmental consequences could become increasingly 
severe. [III] At the same time, the unpredictable pace of AI development has complicated traditional 
forecasting models, making it extraordinarily difficult for regulators and utility providers to estimate 
future electricity demand with any degree of certainty.   
Nevertheless, despite mounting concerns surrounding AI’s environmental footprint, many 
researchers remain cautiously optimistic that advances in renewable energy, battery storage, and more 
efficient computing systems may eventually mitigate some of the industry’s most destabilising effects. 
[IV] Yet the broader debate continues to raise a troubling question: whether a technology designed to 
shape humanity’s future might simultaneously undermine the very infrastructure upon which modern 
society depends.  
(Adapted from CNN)

innovation with environmental sustainability.  
A. Public authorities are facing growing disputes while attempting to reconcile digital progress with 
ecological protection.   
B. Ecological priorities have become less controversial because lawmakers increasingly encourage rapid 
scientific advancement.

TUẦN SỐ 11 
 
VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
TÀI LIỆU LIVESTREAM ĐỘC QUYỀN 
VIP 90 (2026): TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM BỘ CÂU HỎI  
DỄ TRÚNG TỦ & LUYỆN BỘ ĐỀ TINH HOA  
[V90.11.05] TINH HOA ĐỌC HIỂU (BUỔI 15) 
 
 
Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương  
 
Quét mã QR để Ôn luyện chuyên sâu Từ vựng với EZ Vocab và Review Từ vựng;  
cá nhân hóa việc học Từ vựng với Flashcard Từ vựng của tôi trên App: Tiếng Anh cô Mai Phương 
 
 
 
QR dành riêng cho khóa học VIP 90 PRO 3M 
LEVEL 1 
 
 
 
Text 1 
Moving to university is an exciting step, but it also requires students to become more independent. 
For many young people, this is the first time they live away from their families and make their own 
decisions. They have to manage their daily routines, study schedules, and social lives without constant 
support from their parents. As a result, independence becomes an important skill that students need to 
develop. 
Being independent brings many benefits. First, students learn how to take responsibility for their 
actions, such as attending classes on time and completing assignments. Second, they develop useful life 
skills like cooking, budgeting, and solving problems on their own. Moreover, independence helps them 
become more confident, as they learn to handle challenges and adapt to new environments. 
However, becoming independent can also be difficult at first. Some students may feel lonely or 
stressed because they are not used to living alone. They might struggle with time management or making 
the right decisions. Therefore, it is important for students to stay organised and ask for help when 
needed. 
 
Question 1. University students have to manage all of the following EXCEPT _______.   
A. their social lives  
 
B. their study schedules  
C. their parents’ personal decisions  
D. their daily routines 
 
Trang 56

TUẦN SỐ 11 
 
VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
Question 2. The word “them” in paragraph 2 refers to ________.  
A. skills 
B. classes 
C. assignments 
D. students 
Question 3. The word “handle” in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to  ________. 
A. come up 
B. deal with 
C. stick to 
D. attend to  
Question 4. Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence in paragraph 3? 
Therefore, it is important for students to stay organised and ask for help when needed. 
LEVEL 2 
A. Maintaining order and seeking assistance at the right time are essential for learners.   
B. Learners tend to remain well-structured only when they rarely request guidance.   
C. Being orderly results mainly from avoiding support rather than turning to others.   
D. Students are inclined to look for guidance only after losing control of organisation. 
 
 
 
 
 
Text 2 
Some people believe that young people should become independent as early as possible. They 
argue that independence helps teenagers grow faster and learn important life skills. When young people 
make their own decisions, they begin to understand responsibility and become more confident in 
themselves. They also learn how to solve problems in real life instead of depending too much on their 
parents. Early independence can encourage them to explore their interests and build a clearer sense 
of identity. 
Other people have a different view. They think that becoming independent too early can be risky 
because young people are not fully mature yet. At this stage, they may not always think carefully before 
making decisions or consider long-term consequences. Living without enough support can make them 
feel stressed, confused, or even lonely. Some may struggle to manage their time, money, or studies 
effectively. Without guidance, they can easily develop bad habits or make choices that negatively affect 
their future. 
Overall, independence is essential, but it should be developed step by step rather than introduced 
too early. Young people still require guidance and support as they continue to grow and mature. They can 
start with smaller responsibilities, such as organising their time or assisting with everyday tasks, before 
gradually taking on more onerous roles. This balanced approach enables them to build experience while 
still receiving support when necessary, ultimately helping them become more responsible and better 
prepared for adulthood. 
 
Question 5. Which of the following is NOT stated as a benefit of young people making their own 
decisions?   
A. gaining a stronger sense of responsibility and personal confidence  
B. building closer relationships with friends through shared experiences  
C. developing the ability to handle real-life problems independently  
D. reducing reliance on parents when facing everyday situations 
 
 
 
 
Trang 57

VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
Question 6. Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence in paragraph 1? 
Early independence can encourage them to explore their interests and build a clearer sense of 
identity. 
A. A well-defined sense of identity is more likely to emerge when opportunities to pursue personal 
interests are limited during early stages.   
B. The ability to explore one’s own preferences tends to develop after a stable sense of self has already 
been fully formed.   
C. A stronger awareness of personal identity arises as a consequence of limiting exposure to varied 
interests rather than encouraging exploration early on. 
D. Greater autonomy at a young age can help individuals discover their passions and form a stronger sense 
of who they are.   
Question 7. The word “mature” in paragraph 2 is OPPOSITE in meaning to ______.  
A. reluctant 
B. careless 
C. inexperienced  
D. sociable  
Question 8. Young people who do not receive enough support are likely to _______.   
A. avoid taking part in community activities  
B. experience emotional difficulties  
C. lose interest in building close relationships  
D. reject advice from others in most situations 
Question 9. The word “onerous” in paragraph 3 is closest in meaning to ______. 
A. demanding 
B. repetitive 
C. potential 
D. rewarding 
LEVEL 3 
Question 10. What is implied in paragraph 3?   
A. Young people should avoid taking on responsibilities until they fully mature  
B. Independence can be developed effectively without any external support  
C. Taking on major responsibilities early leads to faster personal growth  
D. A gradual balance of independence and support leads to better development 
 
 
 
 
Text 3 
Cultural variation plays a crucial role in shaping how independence is fostered in children. In many 
Western societies, autonomy is actively promoted from an early stage. Children are encouraged to 
articulate their views, make personal decisions, and assume responsibility for their actions. Parents may 
permit them to handle basic tasks independently, such as preparing simple meals or organising their 
schedules. This approach is widely believed to cultivate confidence, self-reliance, and the capacity to 
navigate challenges later in life. 
By contrast, in numerous Asian contexts, child-rearing practices tend to prioritise familial bonds 
and sustained parental guidance. Adults often remain closely involved in their children’s choices and daily 
lives for an extended period. As a result, young people may depend more heavily on their families for 
support, while conformity is frequently valued above individual preference. However, this does not imply 
that autonomy is neglected; rather, it is developed more gradually and typically within a structured and 
supportive family environment. Consequently, individuals may develop a strong sense of responsibility 
not only towards themselves but also towards their family unit. 
 
Trang 58

TUẦN SỐ 11 
 
VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
These contrasting approaches highlight that independence is not a universal or fixed construct but 
one that is shaped by underlying cultural norms and values. While some societies place greater emphasis 
on individual freedom, others prioritise interpersonal relationships and collective responsibility. Adopting 
a balanced perspective, in which autonomy is nurtured alongside appropriate support, can enable 
individuals to become capable and self-sufficient while still maintaining meaningful social connections. 
 
Question 11. Children in Western families are encouraged to do all of the following EXCEPT ______.   
A. express their opinions  
 
B. rely on their families  
C. take responsibility for their actions  
D. make their own personal choices 
Question 12. The word “cultivate” in paragraph 1 can be best replaced by ______. 
A. develop 
B. impress 
C. regulate 
D. adjust  
Question 13. As stated in paragraph 2, adults in Asian societies _______.   
A. emphasise strict discipline over emotional connection in family life  
B. allow children to make independent decisions at an early stage  
C. stay closely involved in their children’s lives for longer  
D. expect children to manage their own time without supervision 
Question 14. The word “conformity” in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to _______.  
A. disregard 
B. obedience 
C. adaptation 
D. personalisation 
LEVEL 4 
Question 15. Which of the following is TRUE according to paragraph 3?   
A. Independence develops similarly across cultures regardless of social values. 
B. A balance between autonomy and support helps individuals develop effectively. 
C. Societies that value relationships tend to ignore independence completely. 
D. Individual freedom is always more important than collective responsibility. 
 
 
 
 
Text 4 
The notion of independence is often romanticised, yet it may be inherently paradoxical. It is 
commonly associated with freedom, self-determination, and the ability to shape one’s own path. People 
who are independent are often seen as strong and capable, able to make decisions without relying on 
others. [I] This sense of autonomy can be empowering, as it allows individuals to pursue their goals and 
develop a clear sense of identity. 
At the same time, independence can create a feeling of distance from others. When individuals 
become too focused on self-reliance, they may hesitate to seek help or share their struggles. [II] This 
can gradually lead to isolation, even if they appear confident on the outside. In some cases, the desire to 
appear independent may prevent meaningful connections, as people avoid showing vulnerability or 
depending on others. [III] As a result, independence can quietly limit emotional support and a sense of 
belonging. 
[IV] It offers freedom, but it may also come at the cost of connection. A more balanced perspective 
may be to view independence not as complete separation, but as the ability to stand on one’s own while 
still maintaining relationships. In this sense, true independence may involve knowing when to rely on 
oneself and when to reach out to others. 
 
 
Trang 59

D. It is by sharing personal struggles that individuals are more likely to develop a stronger sense of

Question 18. Which of the following can be inferred from the passage?  
A. Independence ensures individuals maintain strong relationships while fully avoiding any emotional 
distance. 
B. True independence involves balancing self-reliance with maintaining meaningful connections to

C. Emotional distance strengthens independence by encouraging individuals to rely less on relationships. 
D. Independence enables individuals to form superficial connections while discouraging openness in 
relationships.  
Question 19. Where in the passage does the following best fit? 
This tension highlights the complex nature of independence. 
A. [I] 
B. [II] 
C. [III] 
D. [IV] 
Question 20. Which of the following best summarises the passage? 
A. Independence is praised for encouraging individuals to shape their own paths, yet this emphasis may 
weaken emotional closeness and reduce meaningful human connection.  
B. While often idealised, independence can isolate individuals, so true development requires balancing 
personal autonomy with maintaining meaningful relationships and social support.  
C. An excessive focus on handling everything independently may cause individuals to appear self-assured 
outwardly, while experiencing reduced support from those around them.  
D. The perception of self-sufficiency as a sign of strength overlooks how prioritising personal control can 
limit opportunities for emotional exchange and a genuine sense of belonging.

[V90.11.05B] THI ONLINE: TINH HOA ĐỌC HIỂU  
 
(BUỔI 15) – ĐỀ VẬN DỤNG 
Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương  
 
 
Quét mã QR để Ôn luyện chuyên sâu Từ vựng với EZ Vocab và Review Từ vựng;

QR dành riêng cho khóa học VIP 90 PRO 3M 
 
LEVEL 1 
Text 1 
 
In modern life, people are expected to face many challenges and make decisions on their own. One 
important quality that helps them succeed is independence. When individuals learn to rely on themselves, 
they become more confident and responsible. They can develop important life skills such as decision-
making, problem-solving, and time management. For example, students who study on their own often 
understand lessons better and feel more confident in exams. Independence also teaches people to take 
responsibility for their actions, which is necessary for success in both school and work. Although support 
from others is still important, being independent allows people to grow stronger and more prepared for 
real-life challenges. 
 
Question 1. Which of the following is NOT mentioned in the text as a skill developed through 
independence? 
A. decision-making 
 
B. facing challenges  
 
C. problem-solving 
 
D. time management 
Question 2. The word “their” in the text refers to ______. 
A. students  
B. lessons  
C. exams 
D. life skills  
Question 3. The word “support” in the text is closest in meaning to _______. 
A. opposition 
B. assistance  
C. employment 
D. retirement

TUẦN SỐ 11 
 
VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
Text 2 
 
Teaching children to be independent from a young age is very important for their development. 
When children learn to do simple tasks by themselves, such as dressing, cleaning up, or organizing their 
school things, they become more confident and responsible. Independence also helps them solve problems 
and make small decisions in daily life. For example, a child who is encouraged to choose their clothes or 
complete homework alone will feel proud of their achievements. However, parents should guide and 
support them instead of doing everything for them. This balance helps children feel safe while still 
learning new skills. In the long run, these children are more likely to grow into responsible adults who 
can take care of themselves and contribute to society. 
 
Question 4. The word “they” in the text refers to ______.  
A. problems  
B. children  
C. school things 
D. simple tasks  
Question 5. The word “complete” in the text is closest in meaning to _______. 
A. finish 
B. start 
C. delay 
D. ignore 
Question 6. In the long term, independent children _______. 
A. will no longer find it hard to adapt to new environments 
B. make better decisions in difficult situations later in life 
C. may become responsible adults and contribute to society  
D. lose the ability to manage their own responsibilities 
 
 
LEVEL 2 
Text 3 
 
Independence is often seen as a positive quality because it helps people become confident and 
responsible. When individuals rely on themselves, they can make their own decisions and develop 
important life skills such as problem-solving and time management. For example, independent students 
usually study more effectively and feel more prepared for exams. This ability also allows people to adapt 
better to new situations and face challenges without always depending on others. 
 
However, independence is not always a good thing if it is taken too far. People who are too 
independent may refuse help even when they need it, which can lead to stress or poor decisions. In 
some situations, teamwork and support from others are more effective than working alone. For instance, 
in group projects or at work, cooperation often leads to better results. Therefore, while independence is 
important, it should be balanced with the ability to ask for help and work with others. 
 
Question 7. Why is independence often considered a positive quality? 
A. It helps people achieve greater financial success and gain recognition in society later in life. 
B. It allows people to build stronger social relationships through communication with others. 
C. It encourages individuals to develop creativity and express their personal ideas more freely. 
D. It helps people become more confident, responsible, and able to make their own decisions.  
Question 8. The word “prepared” in paragraph 1 is opposite in meaning to _______.  
A. unready 
B. confident 
C. capable 
D. organized

VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
Question 9. Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence in paragraph 2? 
People who are too independent may refuse help even when they need it, which can lead to stress or 
poor decisions. 
A. Highly independent people always make better decisions because they do not rely on others for help. 
B. Being independent helps people accept support easily, which reduces stress and improves their 
decisions. 
C. Excessive independence can make people reject necessary help, resulting in stress or bad decisions.  
D. Refusing help has no effect on stress levels or decision-making for independent individuals.  
Question 10. Which of the following is TRUE according to the passage? 
A. Independence mainly eliminates the need for support in most real-life situations. 
B. Independence is valuable, but it works best when combined with cooperation.  
C. Independence generally reduces the effectiveness of teamwork in group settings. 
D. Independence primarily focuses on avoiding reliance in all forms of collaboration. 
 
Text 4 
 
Independence is understood differently across cultures, depending on social values and traditions. 
In many Western countries, such as the United States, independence is highly valued. Young people are 
often encouraged to make their own decisions, live separately from their families, and take responsibility 
for their lives at an early age. This helps them build confidence and personal identity. Schools and parents 
usually support this by giving children freedom to express opinions and solve problems on their own. 
 
In contrast, in many Asian cultures like Japan or Vietnam, independence is often balanced with 
strong family connections. Children are taught to respect their parents and consider the needs of the group 
before their own. Living with family for a longer time is common and not seen as a lack of 
independence. Instead, cooperation and responsibility within the family are valued. These differences 
show that independence is shaped by culture, and there is no single correct way to define it. 
 
Question 11. The word “their” in paragraph 1 refers to _______. 
A. opinions  
B. children  
C. problems  
D. parents  
Question 12. Which of the following is NOT mentioned in paragraph 1 as something encouraged for 
young people in Western countries? 
A. maintaining a strong financial state 
B. making their own decisions 
C. living separately from their families 
D. taking responsibility for their lives 
Question 13. Across different cultures, independence _______. 
A. replaces the importance of family connections 
B. is always defined in the same way 
C. is valued in Western countries and ignored by others 
D. is understood and expressed in different ways  
Question 14. Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence in paragraph 2? 
Living with family for a longer time is common and not seen as a lack of independence. 
A. Living with family for too long is often viewed as a clear sign of dependence and limited independence. 
B. Staying with family for a longer period is normal and does not mean someone lacks independence.  
C. Remaining with family is usually discouraged because it prevents individuals from becoming 
independent. 
D. Living separately from family is the only way for children to prove true independence in Asian cultures.

VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
Question 15. Which of the following is TRUE according to the passage? 
A. In Western countries, young people avoid making their own decisions early. 
B. In many Asian cultures, living with family longer is common.  
C. In Asian cultures, independence means living alone as soon as possible. 
D. Independence is defined the same way in all cultures 
 
LEVEL 3 
Text 5 
 
Independence is often praised as a sign of maturity, yet it is frequently confused with isolation—a 
far more problematic state. True independence refers to the ability to think, decide, and act autonomously 
while remaining socially connected. [I] It empowers individuals to take responsibility for their lives 
without becoming dependent on constant guidance. Isolation, by contrast, implies emotional or social 
disconnection, where a person withdraws from meaningful relationships and support systems. 
 
The distinction becomes clear in practice. [II] An independent individual can work alone, form 
personal opinions, and still collaborate effectively when needed. They understand that seeking advice is 
not a weakness but a strategic choice. In contrast, someone drifting into isolation may reject help, avoid 
interaction, and gradually lose the ability to engage with others. [III] While independence strengthens 
resilience and adaptability, isolation often leads to loneliness, reduced well-being, and limited personal 
growth. 
 
[IV] Independence should not mean cutting oneself off from others, but rather developing a stable 
sense of self within a network of relationships. In an increasingly individualistic world, it is essential to 
recognize that human beings are both autonomous and inherently social. Confusing independence with 
isolation risks undermining not only personal development but also the very connections that sustain it. 
 
Question 16. True independence _______. 
A. means acting autonomously while rejecting all forms of social interaction. 
B. means acting independently while avoiding all meaningful social connections 
C. means depending on others while still maintaining meaningful social connections 
D. means acting autonomously while maintaining meaningful social connections  
Question 17. The word “drifting into” in paragraph 2 is opposite in meaning to _______.  
A. gradually entering  
 
B. moving away from 
 
C. slowly falling into 
 
D. becoming involved in 
Question 18. Which of the following best summarises paragraph 2? 
A. Isolation allows individuals to develop better decision-making skills than those who remain 
independent. 
B. Independence requires avoiding interaction, whereas isolation encourages people to cooperate more 
effectively. 
C. Independence and isolation both strengthen personal growth by limiting the need for social 
relationships. 
D. Independence involves self-reliance with openness to help, while isolation leads to withdrawal and 
loneliness.   
Question 19. Where in the passage does the following sentence best fit? 
Therefore, the key lies in balance. 
A. [I] 
B. [II] 
C. [III] 
D. [IV]

[V90.11.05A] THI ONLINE: TINH HOA ĐỌC HIỂU  
 
(BUỔI 15) – ĐỀ NÂNG CAO 
Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương  
 
 
Quét mã QR để Ôn luyện chuyên sâu Từ vựng với EZ Vocab và Review Từ vựng;

Read the following passage and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct answer to each of 
the questions from 1 to 8. 
The relationship between independence and success is often seen as straightforward. Many people 
believe that the more independent you are, the more successful you will become. There is some truth in 
this idea. Independence gives individuals the freedom to make their own choices, take risks, and follow 
their own direction instead of waiting for others to decide for them. 
In real life, independent people often stand out because they take initiative. A student who 
studies without being reminded, or someone who actively looks for opportunities, is more likely to achieve 
their goals. They do not wait for the “perfect moment.” They act, make mistakes, and learn from them. 
This ability to rely on oneself can build resilience, which is essential for long-term success. Many 
entrepreneurs, for example, started with nothing but an idea and the courage to try. 
Still, independence alone is not enough. Success rarely happens in isolation. People need support, 
advice, and sometimes even a bit of luck. Someone who refuses to listen to others or work in a team may 
miss valuable perspectives. In school or at work, collaboration often leads to better results. Even the most 
successful individuals have mentors, partners, or teams behind them. 
There is also a quieter side to success that is often overlooked. It is not only about achieving goals 
but also about maintaining balance and well-being. A person who is too independent may push themselves 
too hard and feel exhausted or disconnected. Success becomes more meaningful when it is shared with 
others. Independence helps you move forward, but connection gives that journey purpose and direction. 
 
Trang 66

In real life, independent people often stand out because they take initiative.

Question 5. The word “overlooked” in paragraph 4 is closest in meaning to ______.

B. valued 
C. ignored 
D. violated  
Question 6. Which of the following is TRUE according to the passage?   
A. Support from others may reduce people’s drive to reach their goals. 
B. Success grows from self-trust, while teamwork has less value. 
C. Self-trust helps success, but teamwork and advice still matter. 
D. Working by oneself brings better results than group effort does. 
Question 7. Which paragraph mentions emotional distance caused by excessive independence? 
A. Paragraph 1 
B. Paragraph 2 
C. Paragraph 3 
D. Paragraph 4 
Question 8. Which paragraph mentions a possible disadvantage of rejecting others’ input? 
A. Paragraph 1 
B. Paragraph 2 
C. Paragraph 3 
D. Paragraph 4 
 
Read the following passage and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct answer to each of 
the questions from 9 to 18. 
Parenting approaches are often reflected in how comfortably children navigate life on their own. 
In some families, children are gradually trusted with small decisions - choosing activities, organising their 
time, or dealing with minor problems. [I] Children become more willing to take initiative and less afraid 
of making mistakes, as they begin to see challenges as something manageable rather than threatening. 
This kind of environment does not remove support, but it changes how support is given. 
In other households, a different pattern can be observed. Care is expressed through constant 
supervision and frequent intervention. Parents tend to step in quickly to prevent discomfort or failure, 
sometimes solving problems before the child has a chance to respond. While this involvement is often 
driven by concern, it can unintentionally reduce opportunities for children to practice independence. When 
faced with unfamiliar situations, they may hesitate, seek reassurance, or doubt their own judgment, even 
in relatively simple matters. 
These differences tend to become more visible over time. [II] As children move into more 
demanding environments, such as university or the workplace, those who have been given space to make 
decisions often adjust more smoothly. They are more likely to manage their time, cope with pressure, and 
recover from setbacks. Others, despite having strong abilities, may experience uncertainty or anxiety, not

because they lack potential, but because they are less accustomed to acting independently. [III] What 
appears to be a difference in personality may, in fact, be rooted in earlier patterns of upbringing. 
Research on parenting styles frequently points to the value of balance. A gradual shift from 
guidance to autonomy appears to support both competence and emotional stability. Children benefit from 
knowing that support is available, but not constantly imposed. [IV]  Independence, in this sense, is not 
something that suddenly emerges, but something quietly shaped through repeated, everyday interactions 
within the family, where both trust and guidance coexist. 
 
Question 9. According to paragraph 1, when children are trusted with small decisions, _______.

their confidence in handling unfamiliar situations.   
B. Constant adult protection, motivated by care, often focuses on preventing discomfort and avoiding 
failure in children’s daily experiences.   
C. When facing new circumstances, young people may feel unsure and repeatedly look for guidance 
instead of relying on their own thinking. 
D. Ongoing guidance from adults, even when driven by concern, can enhance young people’s ability to 
respond effectively to unfamiliar situations without hesitation. 
Question 11. As stated in paragraph 2, when parents step in early, ______.

Question 12. The word “reassurance” in paragraph 2 is OPPOSITE in meaning to _____.

B. struggle 
C. controversy 
D. insecurity  
Question 13. What is implied in paragraph 3?

B. Managing pressure depends mainly on external guidance rather than personal skills.  
C. Experiencing setbacks makes individuals less confident in difficult situations.  
D. Early decision-making experience helps individuals adapt better to new environments. 
Question 14. The word “they” in paragraph 3 refers to _____. 
A. differences 
B. abilities 
C. children 
D. environments

What appears to be a difference in personality may, in fact, be rooted in earlier patterns of 
upbringing. 
A. Earlier childhood experiences are often shaped by aspects of character that people initially notice in

B. Differences in behaviour can gradually develop from personal character rather than from earlier

C. Apparent variations in character can often be traced back to earlier childhood influences.

Roles of Nonverbal Communication 
Body language (1) _____ many roles in social interactions. It can help facilitate the following:  
● Earning trust: Engaging in eye contact, nodding your head (2) _____ listening, and even 
unconsciously mirroring another person's body language are all signals that you and someone 
else are bonding.

how they feel.  
● Tuning in to your own needs: Our own body language can reveal a lot about how we're feeling.

may be a sign that you do not feel completely at (5) _____ in your current environment. Your 
body might be telling you that you're feeling unsafe, (6) _____, or any number of emotions.

Recent research has raised growing concerns about the possible connection between ultra-
processed foods and dementia risk, particularly as these products have become increasingly prevalent in 
modern diets. Ultra-processed foods include items such as packaged snacks, sugary drinks, instant 
noodles, and ready-made meals, many of which contain artificial additives, refined ingredients, and high 
levels of sugar or unhealthy fats. According to researchers from Monash University in Australia, even a 
relatively small increase in the consumption of these foods may negatively affect attention and other 
cognitive functions linked to brain health.   
The study analysed thousands of adults between the ages of 40 and 70 who showed no signs of 
dementia at the beginning of the research. Researchers discovered that every 10% increase in ultra-
processed food intake was associated with lower attention scores and a higher risk of cognitive 
deterioration. Surprisingly, the negative effects persisted even among participants who generally 
followed healthy diets.  
Although scientists emphasised that the findings do not prove a direct causal relationship, many 
experts believe the study highlights the importance of paying closer attention to food processing itself 
rather than focusing only on calories or nutrients. Some specialists suggest that ultra-processed foods may 
contribute to inflammation, poor blood circulation, and disruptions in the gut-brain connection, all of 
which could influence cognitive performance over time.   
As ultra-processed foods now make up a large proportion of diets in many countries, researchers 
are encouraging people to replace some packaged products with fresher alternatives such as fruits, 
vegetables, nuts, and natural protein sources. While occasional consumption is unlikely to cause 
immediate harm, experts warn that long-term dietary habits may play a far more significant role in 
protecting brain health than many people realise.   
(Adapted from CNN)

The rapid emergence of artificial intelligence has profoundly altered the creative industries, forcing 
art schools and young creators to reconsider not only how artistic work is produced but also what 
originality itself may mean in an era increasingly shaped by algorithms. While many artists initially 
regarded generative AI as a direct threat to human creativity, some educational institutions have begun 
encouraging students to engage critically with these technologies rather than reject them outright. At 
several leading art and design schools, AI tools are now being incorporated into workshops and 
experimental projects, where students are taught to evaluate both the possibilities and limitations of 
machine-generated content.   
Supporters of this approach argue that artificial intelligence can accelerate processes such as 
prototyping, conceptual development, and visual experimentation, enabling students to explore a wider 
range of creative possibilities within a much shorter period of time. In some classrooms, the relationship 
between teachers and students has become noticeably more collaborative. [I] Nevertheless, concerns 
surrounding artistic integrity remain deeply contentious, particularly among creators who fear that 
excessive dependence on automated tools may gradually erode the value of craftsmanship, technical skill, 
and human imagination.

Read the following passage and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct answer to each of 
the questions from 1 to 8. 
Balancing sociability and intentional solitude 
The COVID-19 pandemic drastically changed the landscape of human interaction, igniting a 
strong desire for sotheircial connection at a time when gatherings were limited. Now that restrictions have 
largely been lifted, many may feel pressured to "make up for lost time," which can lead to prioritizing 
social events even when not feeling up to it. This may cause guilt over canceling plans, leading to social 
burnout and neglecting personal needs for rest. Here, we'll explore the benefits of social connection and 
intentional solitude and offer strategies to empower you to meet your social needs in a way that feels right 
for you. 
 Humans are inherently social, and maintaining interpersonal relationships is crucial for their 
physical and mental health. Despite the end of strict isolation measures, the lingering effects of pandemic-
induced isolation, such as increased depression, anxiety, and substance use disorders, are still prevalent. 
Reengaging with the world thoughtfully by gradually increasing social activity and engaging in 
meaningful interactions that strengthen bonds and emotional support is essential.  
Despite the numerous advantages of companionship, intentional solitude is equally vital for 
mental health. Whether you consider yourself an introvert, an extrovert, or somewhere in between, 
everyone needs time to recharge and reflect. A study published by Frontiers involving 2,035 individuals, 
including adolescents, adults, and older adults, highlighted the varied benefits of spending time alone, 
such as increased feelings of competency, self-growth, and autonomy.  
 
Trang 78

Independence is a skill that caregivers are supposed to teach and foster in their children in order to 
support their self-sufficiency into adulthood. In healthy caregiver-child relationships, there is a balance 
where the parent or caregiver gives (teaches, models) and the child takes (learns, imitates). This form of 
modeling and imitation helps support and guide a child’s autonomy. However, independence can become 
excessive for some who grew up experiencing a role reversal between their caregiver and themselves, 
which is commonly seen in narcissistic or enmeshed families. In these situations, independence can veer 
into unhealthy hyper-independence, often as a response to past trauma. 
Parentification is a form of childhood trauma where there is a role reversal that happens 
between the primary caregiver and the child. In parentified children, the parent imposes their unmet 
emotional, physical, or psychological needs onto the child. [I] A common outcome of parentification is 
that the child becomes an adult who either feels a compulsion to care-take others in their lives with a

constant need to feel needed, or they can become hyper-independent as a result of traumatic or challenging 
events experienced in childhood. Traumatic events may include parental divorce, substance abuse in the 
family, violence, poverty, or serious mental health issues or irresponsibility in the parent. [II] 
For example, narcissistic parents who bully or make unrealistic demands on their kids can be 
conditioning their child to become an overachiever, perfectionistic, and equally harsh on themselves in 
their adult lives. [III] The child may become an adult who believes they cannot make a mistake, must be 
“perfect,” and cannot rely on anyone but themselves. These early traumatic experiences with narcissistic 
parenting can lead to a child becoming an adult who feels compelled to only rely on themselves, even 
when faced with a need for support. 
Similarly, a caregiver who suffers from mental illness such as bipolar disorder or borderline 
personality disorder may be impulsive, reckless, or irresponsible with their finances or their relationships. 
[IV] This can condition a child to be hyper-vigilant and take on the role of protecting or caring for their 
parent, or getting a job to put food on the table. 
(Adapted from https://www.psychologytoday.com/) 
 
Question 9. Healthy caregiver-child relationships are where _______. 
A. children take responsibility for managing their caregivers’ problems 
B. caregivers rely on children to meet their emotional and physical needs 
C. caregivers guide, and children learn through a balanced exchange  
D. caregivers expect children to act independently without any guidance 
Question 10. The phrase “veer into” in paragraph 1 mostly means _______. 
A. shift toward 
B. move away from 
C. return to 
D. remain in 
Question 11. Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence in paragraph 2? 
Parentification is a form of childhood trauma where there is a role reversal that happens between 
the primary caregiver and the child. 
A. Parentification is a form of childhood trauma in which the child takes on the caregiver’s role due to a 
reversal in responsibilities. 
B. Parentification describes a situation where caregivers provide more support, allowing children to 
remain free from responsibilities. 
C. Parentification happens when both the caregiver and the child share equal roles without affecting the 
child’s emotional development. 
D. Parentification refers to a normal stage where children become independent without experiencing any 
psychological pressure.  
Question 12. According to paragraph 2, parents of parentified children _______. 
A. encourage children to rely more on others instead of becoming independent 
B. provide balanced support while allowing children to develop naturally 
C. impose their unmet emotional, physical, or psychological needs onto the child  
D. avoid placing any responsibility or pressure on their innocent children

Read the following passage and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct option that best fits 
each of the numbered blanks from 1 to 5. 
India is experiencing one of its most severe heatwaves in modern history, with temperatures 
climbing to dangerous levels across dozens of cities. In late April, all 50 of the world’s hottest cities were 
reportedly located in India, an event (1) ______.  
(2) ______. Hospitals have reported growing numbers of heat-related illnesses, particularly among 
outdoor workers, elderly residents, and people living in densely populated urban areas, where concrete 
structures absorb and trap heat. Scientists warn that the combination of rapid urbanisation, declining green 
spaces, and human-induced climate change has intensified the severity of these heatwaves, (3) ______. 
What has especially alarmed researchers is the persistence of unusually high nighttime temperatures, 
which, rather than allowing the human body to recover, continue exposing vulnerable populations to 
prolonged thermal stress.   
Although India has experienced heatwaves before, experts believe the current conditions reflect a 
broader global climate crisis whose effects are becoming increasingly difficult to ignore. Environmental 
researchers have cautioned that unless governments invest more heavily in climate adaptation strategies, 
renewable energy, and heat-resistant urban planning, (4) ______. As temperatures continue to rise, the 
heatwave has become not merely a temporary weather event, but (5) ______.

Question 1.  
A. whose climate experts described as highly unusual and without modern precedent 
B. is described as highly unusual and without modern precedent by climate experts 
C. which described as highly unusual and without modern precedent by climate experts 
D. that climate experts described as highly unusual and without modern precedent 
Question 2.  
A. Across several regions, temperatures have remained unusually high throughout the entire summer   
B. The consequences of this extreme weather have extended far beyond physical discomfort   
C. Many urban residents have attempted to reduce electricity consumption during recent heatwaves   
D. In several major cities, air pollution levels were monitored more carefully than before 
Question 3.  
A. while pollution levels continue rising across many industrial cities   
B. due to governments introducing stricter environmental measures nationwide   
C. with farming activities suffering during the usually dry weather conditions   
D. whose frequency and duration appear to be increasing year after year 
Question 4.  
A. millions of people could face worsening living conditions in the coming decades   
B. public concern may decline significantly despite rising climate-related health risks   
C. temperatures in some crowded cities might fall sharply during future summer months   
D. energy companies might consider reducing investment in cleaner technologies 
Question 5.  
A. due to a stark warning about the accelerating consequences of climate change 
B. a stark warning about the accelerating consequences of climate change 
C. which is a stark warning about the accelerating consequences of climate change 
D. it is a stark warning about the accelerating consequences of climate change 
 
Read the following passage and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct option that best fits 
each of the numbered blanks from 6 to 10. 
Modern education systems are increasingly being criticised for placing too much emphasis on 
examinations while failing to prepare students for the realities of adult life and employment. In the United 
Kingdom, former minister Alan Milburn recently argued that schools have become excessively focused 
on academic performance, (6) ______.  
Many educators argue that although examinations may measure academic knowledge, they cannot 
fully assess qualities such as creativity, resilience, or adaptability, (7) ______. As artificial intelligence 
continues transforming industries at an unprecedented speed, some experts warn that education systems 
designed primarily around memorisation and standardised testing (8) ______. This concern has become 
even more significant because many entry-level jobs, which once provided practical experience for young 
people, are gradually disappearing or changing rapidly due to technological development.   
Although academic achievement remains important, critics believe (9) ______. They suggest that 
stronger connections between schools and employers, alongside greater access to vocational training and 
practical work experience, could help young people develop confidence and independence more 
effectively. Unless education systems become more balanced and responsive to social change, there are 
growing fears that future generations may continue achieving high exam results (10) ______.

Walking through a modern supermarket, customers are surrounded by products carrying labels 
such as “natural”, “green”, and “environmentally friendly”. To many shoppers, these expressions suggest 
that (16) ______. Yet recent research from Australia indicates that such claims are often vague marketing 
tools rather than trustworthy sources of scientific information. After analysing thousands of supermarket 
products, researchers discovered that environmental language appeared far more frequently than 
independently verified sustainability data. 
What makes the issue particularly concerning is that consumers may believe (17) ______ while 
actually receiving very little reliable information. Some products promoted as environmentally 
responsible were found to have carbon footprints similar to, or even greater than, ordinary alternatives 
without green labels. Because terms like “natural” and “sustainable” are rarely defined clearly by law, 
companies can use them in ways (18) ______. As a result, shoppers who wish to support environmentally 
responsible businesses may struggle to distinguish genuine sustainability efforts from carefully designed 
advertising strategies. 
The problem becomes even more complicated when supermarkets compete aggressively for 
environmentally conscious customers, (19) ______. Rather than depending entirely on marketing claims, 
researchers suggest introducing clearer labelling systems that allow consumers to compare products more 
accurately. (20) ______.

B. customers increasingly prefer products advertised through social media and digital marketing

D. if they are making ethical choices

D. to create positive impressions without technically providing false information

A. while companies continue expanding food exports to international supermarket chains   
B. since attractive packaging and persuasive wording can strongly influence buying habits   
C. although supermarkets have reduced prices on environmentally friendly household products  
D. with consumers preferring imported products over locally produced supermarket goods

meat, shells, and eggs, but also by the economic realities faced by coastal communities. In many regions,

spans continents. Despite protective laws, the persistence of this trade reflects how deeply cultural 
practices and financial necessity (1) _____.

past decades, hundreds of thousands of turtles were taken annually, yet improved regulation, awareness 
campaigns, and shifting social attitudes (3) _____. This trend, however, does not eliminate concern, as 
exploitation still occurs in multiple regions, particularly where enforcement remains limited.

restriction; (4) _____. In some communities, former poachers have been successfully integrated into 
conservation efforts, transforming them into protectors of the very species they once exploited. Such 
initiatives highlight that long-term solutions depend not only on legal frameworks but also on creating 
sustainable alternatives, ensuring that (5) _____.

[V90.12.02] TINH HOA ĐỌC HIỂU (BUỔI 16) 
 
 
Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương  
 
Quét mã QR để Ôn luyện chuyên sâu Từ vựng với EZ Vocab và Review Từ vựng;  
cá nhân hóa việc học Từ vựng với Flashcard Từ vựng của tôi trên App: Tiếng Anh cô Mai Phương 
 
 
QR dành riêng cho khóa học VIP 90 PRO 3M 
LEVEL 1 
 
 
 
Text 1 
Air travel in Europe has grown quickly again after the Covid-19 pandemic, but this recovery has 
created serious environmental concerns. Recent reports show that airline emissions in Europe are now 
higher than they were before the pandemic, despite promises from the aviation industry to reduce 
pollution. Low-cost airlines, especially Ryanair, have carried millions more passengers in recent years, 
leading to a sharp rise in carbon dioxide emissions. Experts warn that this trend makes it more difficult 
for Europe to achieve its climate goals.   
Many environmental groups believe stronger action is necessary. At present, Europe’s emissions 
trading system only covers flights within Europe, while many long-haul flights are not included. As a 
result, a large amount of pollution remains uncontrolled. Some experts suggest expanding environmental 
taxes on airlines and investing more money in sustainable aviation fuel. However, airline companies argue 
that they are already improving fuel efficiency by using newer aircraft. They also claim that rising fuel 
prices, rather than environmental policies, are increasing ticket costs for passengers.   
 
Question 1. According to paragraph 1, airline emissions in Europe before the Covid-19 pandemic ______.   
A. were controlled by international environmental organisations  
B. were lower than the current level across Europe  
C. became harmless because fewer people travelled abroad  
D. were affected by changes in tourism policies

Text 2 
Modern technology has made life more convenient than ever before. With a single tap on a 
smartphone, people can order food, watch entertainment, or communicate instantly with others. However, 
many experts now argue that this constant access to stimulation may be affecting mental well-being. 
According to recent discussions about “dopamine friction”, people are becoming too dependent on quick 
rewards provided by digital devices. Frequent notifications, short videos, and endless scrolling encourage 
the brain to seek immediate satisfaction rather than long-term fulfilment. As a result, many individuals 
report feeling mentally exhausted despite spending more time on activities designed to entertain them.  
 
Researchers suggest that the human brain functions more effectively when tasks involve some 
degree of effort or patience. Activities such as reading a challenging book, learning a musical instrument, 
or exercising regularly often require persistence before producing satisfaction. Nevertheless, these slower 
experiences may create a stronger sense of achievement and emotional stability. Some psychologists 
believe that modern society has reduced too much “friction” from daily life, replacing meaningful effort 
with constant convenience. This shift may explain why many people feel overstimulated yet emotionally 
unfulfilled at the same time.   
To address this problem, some individuals have begun limiting screen time or practising 
“dopamine detox” habits. These strategies encourage people to spend more time offline, focus on face-to-
face interaction, and participate in less stimulating activities. Experts emphasise that the goal is not to 
eliminate technology completely but to develop healthier habits and regain control over attention and 
emotional balance.

B. increasingly rely on instant rewards from digital technology  
C. prefer long-term fulfilment to short moments of satisfaction  
D. realise that online content is designed to make them tired

Text 3 
Many parents today rely on digital devices to occupy or calm their children, particularly during 
stressful or demanding moments. Smartphones and tablets can provide immediate distraction, allowing 
adults to complete household tasks or enjoy temporary relief from constant caregiving responsibilities. 
Nevertheless, health experts are becoming increasingly concerned about the long-term 
consequences of excessive screen exposure among young children. According to recent parenting 
guidelines, prolonged use of digital media during early childhood may interfere with language acquisition, 
emotional regulation, and the development of social interaction skills. Specialists argue that while screens 
may appear convenient in the short term, they cannot replace the cognitive benefits of direct human 
communication and physical play.   
Researchers also emphasise that the issue extends beyond the amount of screen time itself. The 
type of content children consume and the circumstances in which devices are used may significantly 
influence developmental outcomes. Passive viewing habits, particularly when children are left alone with 
screens for extended periods, are considered especially problematic. By contrast, educational programmes 
watched together with parents may have fewer harmful effects because they encourage discussion and 
interaction. Some psychologists further warn that frequent reliance on digital devices as emotional 
distractions may prevent children from learning how to tolerate boredom, frustration, or delayed 
gratification. Over time, this dependence on constant stimulation could weaken attention span and reduce 
resilience in challenging situations. 
In response to these concerns, experts encourage parents to establish healthier digital boundaries 
rather than banning technology entirely. They recommend creating screen-free routines during meals, 
before bedtime, and in social settings. Many specialists also advise parents to model balanced technology 
habits themselves, since children often imitate adult behaviour more closely than verbal instructions.

LEVEL 1 
Text 1 
 
Nowadays, more and more people are becoming self-made entrepreneurs thanks to technology and 
social media. Instead of working for large companies, many young adults choose to start their own 
businesses and follow their passions. Some sell handmade products online, while others create content or 
provide services through digital platforms. These entrepreneurs are often creative, independent, and 
willing to take risks in order to achieve success. In addition, being self-employed allows them to have 
flexible schedules and develop valuable skills such as communication, problem-solving, and leadership. 
Although building a business requires hard work and patience, many people believe it is a rewarding 
journey. The rise of self-made entrepreneurs shows that with determination and innovative ideas, ordinary 
people can create successful careers for themselves. 
 
Question 1. Which of the following is NOT described as a characteristic of young entrepreneurs? 
A. independent 
 
B. creative 
 
C. successful  
 
D. willing to take risks

A. convenient 
B. adjustable 
C. rigid 
D. comfortable

A. ordinary people 
B. successful careers  
C. valuable skills  
D. innovative ideas

Text 2 
 
As technology develops rapidly, the job market is also changing in many ways. One major concern 
today is that some jobs may disappear because of artificial intelligence. AI can already perform repetitive 
tasks faster and more accurately than humans, so jobs such as data entry clerks, cashiers, translators, and 
factory workers may be at risk. In addition, customer service staff may be replaced by chatbots that can 
answer common questions at any time. Even some creative jobs, such as basic graphic design or content 
writing, may be affected by AI tools. However, this does not mean humans will become useless. People 
should learn new skills, especially problem-solving, communication, and technology skills, to adapt to 
the future workplace. 
 
Question 4. Which of the following jobs is NOT mentioned as being at risk because of AI? 
A. factory workers  
B. doctors    
C. cashiers   
D. translators  
Question 5. The word “disappear” in the text is opposite in meaning to _______. 
A. vanish   
B. fade   
C. emerge   
D. remove 
Question 6. The disappearance of jobs because of AI does not mean that _______. 
A. people will stop working  
B. AI will control everything 
C. humans will become useless 
D. technology will stop developing 
 
 
LEVEL 2 
Text 3 
 
The internet is now flooded with low-quality content created by artificial intelligence, often called 
“AI slop.” This term refers to articles, videos, images, or social media posts that are produced quickly 
with little creativity or accuracy. Because AI tools can generate massive amounts of content in seconds, 
many websites focus more on quantity than quality in order to gain views and advertising revenue. As a 
result, users sometimes struggle to find reliable or meaningful information online. 
 
In the future, AI-generated content will probably become even more common, which could change 
the way people use the internet. While AI can save time and support creativity, too much low-quality 
content may reduce trust in online platforms. Therefore, human creativity and critical thinking will 
become increasingly important. People may need to pay more attention to checking sources and 
identifying authentic content rather than simply believing everything they see online. 
 
Question 7. According to paragraph 1, what is “AI slop”? 
A. Content shared online by professional human creators 
B. Information produced carefully by experts and journalists 
C. Low-quality content created by artificial intelligence 
D. Online material designed mainly for educational purposes 
Question 8. The word “reliable” in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to _______. 
A. harmful   
B. expensive   
C. trustworthy   
D. confusing

Text 4 
 
With the rapid development of technology, online courses have become increasingly popular 
around the world. Many people now study through digital platforms because online learning is flexible, 
convenient, and often more affordable than traditional education. Students can access lessons anytime and 
learn from experts regardless of geographical distance. In addition, online courses allow learners to study 
at their own pace, which is especially beneficial for working adults or busy students. 
 
However, it is unlikely that online courses can completely replace traditional education. 
Schools and universities provide direct interaction between teachers and students, which helps learners 
develop communication, teamwork, and social skills. Face-to-face learning environments also encourage 
discipline and stronger academic engagement. Furthermore, some subjects, such as medicine or 
engineering, require practical training that cannot be fully delivered online. Therefore, although online 
education offers many advantages, traditional education still plays an essential role in providing a more 
comprehensive learning experience. 
 
Question 11. Why do many people nowadays choose to study through digital platforms? 
A. Because online courses always provide free university degrees. 
B. Because digital learning completely removes the need for teachers. 
C. Because online learning is flexible, convenient, and often cheaper. 
D. Because students can learn important subjects through the internet. 
Question 12. The word “their” in paragraph 1 refers to _______. 
A. learners  
B. online courses  
C. experts  
D. adults  
Question 13. The word “comprehensive” in paragraph 2 is OPPOSITE in meaning to _______. 
A. detailed 
B. complete 
C. limited 
D. thorough

Text 5 
 
The zero-waste movement has emerged as a highly influential environmental trend, encouraging 
individuals to minimize consumption and eliminate unnecessary waste. Advocates often promote reusable 
products, package-free shopping, and sustainable living as solutions to the global pollution crisis. [I] 
Although the movement is well-intentioned and environmentally beneficial in many respects, the notion 
of achieving absolute zero waste is largely impractical in contemporary society. 
 
A primary obstacle lies in the fact that modern life is fundamentally dependent on systems that 
inevitably generate waste. [II] Essential sectors such as healthcare, transportation, manufacturing, and 
food distribution rely heavily on disposable materials and plastic packaging to ensure efficiency, hygiene, 
and affordability. Even the most environmentally conscious individuals cannot entirely detach themselves 
from these infrastructures. [III] Furthermore, sustainable alternatives are frequently costly, inaccessible, 
or inconvenient, particularly for people living in developing regions or maintaining demanding lifestyles. 
 
Instead of pursuing an unrealistic ideal, society should prioritize attainable forms of environmental 
responsibility. Reducing excessive consumption, supporting recycling initiatives, and encouraging 
corporations to adopt sustainable practices are far more feasible objectives. [IV] But expecting individuals 
to completely eradicate waste from their lives overlooks the economic and structural realities of the 
modern world. 
 
Question 16. The zero-waste movement _______. 
A. completely solves the global pollution crisis in modern society 
B. mainly encourages people to ignore environmental problems today 
C. promotes reducing waste and living in a more sustainable way 
D. requires all individuals to stop using plastic products immediately 
Question 17. The word “overlooks” in paragraph 3 is closest in meaning to _______. 
A. increases   
B. supports   
C. improves   
D. ignores
