Read the following passage and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct answer to each of 
the questions from 1 to 8. 
Seagrass meadows are among the ocean’s most important ecosystems, supporting marine 
biodiversity while storing large quantities of carbon dioxide. However, these underwater habitats have 
been declining rapidly because of pollution, climate change, coastal construction, and destructive fishing 
practices. Scientists warn that the disappearance of seagrass could severely affect fish populations, water 
quality, and coastal protection. Traditional restoration projects often depend on divers manually planting 
seagrass, but this method is expensive, slow, and physically arduous. 
To address these limitations, technology companies and marine researchers are increasingly 
exploring robotic restoration systems. One organisation, ReefGen, has developed underwater robots 
capable of planting seagrass much faster and more accurately than human divers. They can operate in 
difficult underwater environments, injecting seeds directly into the seabed with minimal disturbance. 
Researchers believe robotic systems could eventually restore damaged marine ecosystems on a much 
larger scale than previously possible. In addition, the technology may reduce labour costs and improve 
safety for conservation workers operating in hazardous conditions. 
Technology also plays an important role in monitoring restoration progress. Advanced sensors and 
underwater cameras allow scientists to track seagrass growth, sediment conditions, and carbon absorption 
over time. This information helps researchers identify which restoration methods are most effective 
in different marine environments. Supporters argue that combining engineering with marine science 
could transform ocean conservation by making large-scale ecological recovery more practical and 
efficient.

the environmental pressures menacing marine ecosystems. Rising ocean temperatures, industrial 
pollution, and unsustainable coastal development continue to damage seagrass habitats worldwide. 
Scientists therefore emphasise that long-term ocean restoration will require stronger environmental 
policies, international cooperation, and significant reductions in greenhouse gas emissions.

for coastal restoration programmes.   
D. Environmental engineers will use these measurements to understand technological operations within

B. threatening

D. exploring

Read the following passage and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct answer to each of 
the questions from 9 to 18. 
Social media was initially celebrated for allowing people to maintain friendships across long 
distances and busy schedules. Platforms such as Instagram, TikTok, and Snapchat appeared to strengthen 
communication by enabling constant interaction through messages, photos, and shared experiences. [I] 
Nevertheless, an increasing number of psychologists now question whether these digital connections 
genuinely deepen relationships or merely create the illusion of closeness. Recent studies suggest that while 
online platforms may increase the frequency of contact, they do not necessarily foster emotional intimacy 
or long-term social fulfilment. In some cases, excessive engagement with digital communication may 
even intensify feelings of isolation despite continuous virtual interaction.   
Researchers argue that online communication often lacks the subtle emotional signals present in 
face-to-face conversations, including body language, tone of voice, and spontaneous reactions. [II] As a 
result, interactions conducted primarily through screens may become more performative and less 
emotionally authentic. Furthermore, social media platforms frequently encourage users to present highly 
curated versions of their lives, leading individuals to compare their friendships with unrealistic online 
portrayals. [III] According to behavioural specialists, the constant visibility of social gatherings and 
friendships online may distort perceptions of belonging and social success.   
Another growing concern involves the transformation of friendship itself into a form of measurable 
online performance. Metrics such as follower counts, likes, and public interactions increasingly shape 
how social value is perceived among younger generations. Some experts believe this environment 
encourages superficial validation rather than meaningful companionship. In addition, algorithm-driven 
content exposure may reinforce social comparison by repeatedly prioritising emotionally charged or 
idealised content capable of attracting engagement. Over time, individuals may begin evaluating their 
personal relationships through digital visibility rather than emotional trust or mutual support.  
Despite these concerns, specialists emphasise that social media itself is not inherently destructive 
to friendships. The impact largely depends on how platforms are used and the balance individuals maintain 
between online and offline interaction. Many researchers argue that digital communication can still 
strengthen relationships when combined with meaningful in-person experiences and genuine emotional 
exchange. [IV] Consequently, the challenge facing modern society may not involve abandoning social 
media altogether, but learning how to use it without allowing virtual interaction to replace authentic 
human connection.   
 
Question 9. What is indicated about social media platforms?   
A. They reduced face-to-face interaction.  
B. They seemed to improve communication.  
C. They encouraged private conversations.  
D. They replaced traditional friendships. 
Question 10. The word “they” in paragraph 1 refers to ______.  
A. studies 
B. connections 
C. people 
D. online platforms 
Question 11. The word “authentic” in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to ______. 
A. genuine 
B. abstract 
C. short-lived 
D. sustainable  
Question 12. According to paragraph 2, regularly viewing other people’s online social activities ______.   
A. strengthens confidence in relationships  
B. promotes healthier social behaviour  
C. increases participation in communities  
D. can lead to unrealistic self-evaluation

because many fish farms operate in semi-enclosed coastal areas, pollutants can accumulate over time 
rather than dispersing naturally. The situation becomes even more concerning (2) ______, despite growing

affecting wild fish populations. While the industry argues that modern farming methods are becoming

employment, researchers warn that short-term economic benefits could eventually create larger ecological

Flooding is becoming a growing threat for many major cities around the world as climate change 
continues to affect weather patterns and sea levels. Scientists warn that extreme rainfall, stronger storms, 
and rising oceans (18) ______. Cities such as Jakarta, New York, Bangkok, and Miami are considered 
especially vulnerable (19) ______. According to climate researchers, rapid urban development has also 
reduced the ability of natural land surfaces to absorb water effectively.   
What makes the situation more alarming is that many cities are expanding faster than their flood 
protection systems can adapt. In some urban areas, roads, drainage systems, and underground transport 
networks were designed decades ago, long before climate-related flooding became more intense and 
unpredictable. Scientists have also found that land subsidence, which causes the ground to sink gradually, 
is worsening flood risks in several coastal cities. (20) _______.

planning and climate adaptation strategies. Some governments are investing in flood barriers, improved

Read the following passage and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct answer to each of 
the questions from 23 to 30. 
Satellite observations have revealed that the rate of global sea level rise increased unexpectedly in 
the early 2010s, raising concern among climate scientists worldwide. Around 2012, the average annual 
rise accelerated noticeably and has remained at a higher level ever since. Although some researchers 
believe natural climate variation may partly explain the abrupt shift, others argue that the trend is strongly 
connected to the intensifying effects of global warming, which continue to alter the behaviour of oceans 
and polar ice sheets. 
 
Scientists explain that sea levels are rising for several interconnected reasons. As global 
temperatures increase, glaciers and the enormous ice sheets of Greenland and Antarctica melt more 
rapidly, and this adds additional water to the oceans. At the same time, warmer ocean water expands, 
which causes sea levels to rise even further. Researchers have also suggested that falling aerosol pollution 
in highly industrialised countries may have accelerated warming because aerosols previously reflected 
sunlight and partially reduced the effects of greenhouse gases. Without them, the planet has been warming 
more quickly since around 2010, and this has intensified the pressure on oceans and coastal regions 
worldwide. 
Another important discovery involves the warming of deep ocean waters more than two kilometres 
below the surface. Because most monitoring systems cannot measure temperatures at such extreme 
depths continuously, scientists suspect that important data may still be missing. Recent ocean models 
indicate that deep ocean warming could explain part of the unexplained increase in sea levels observed 
after 2016, especially in sections of the North Atlantic Ocean near the eastern coast of the United States. 
Although the yearly increase may appear relatively small, researchers warn that the long-term 
consequences could become extremely grave for coastal cities vulnerable to flooding. Scientists therefore 
emphasise the need for further investigation, particularly into deep ocean processes, in order to improve 
predictions about how rapidly sea levels may continue rising during the coming decades. 
(Adapted from https://www.newscientist.com)

B. Owing to limited observations in extremely deep seas, specialists believe certain information remains 
unavailable.   
C. Deep-sea equipment functions efficiently at great depths, allowing analysts to avoid major uncertainty.   
D. As underwater investigations have become increasingly reliable, scientists assume hidden patterns are 
disappearing.

Read the following passage and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct answer to each of 
the questions from 31 to 40. 
I don’t know if it happens the other way round, but almost everybody who lives in the city 
sometimes dreams of leaving it. We do it while sitting in traffic that barely moves, while carrying 
groceries past overflowing rubbish bins, while lying awake at night listening to arguments drifting through 
thin apartment walls. My husband and I have spent years having those uncertain conversations about 
escape, weighing urban excitement against the fantasy of quieter lives somewhere beyond the ring roads 
and railway stations. We tell ourselves that we would miss the theatres, cafés, galleries, and anonymous 
energy of the city, yet we also imagine enormous skies, empty footpaths, and mornings filled with 
birdsong rather than sirens. 
[I] One wet November evening, after another exhausting week of overcrowded trains and polluted 
air, we abruptly decided to leave London and move to a small coastal town several hours away. Since 
then, our house has become a landscape of cardboard boxes, discarded clothes, dusty shelves, and objects 
whose emotional significance I had long forgotten. [II] Old birthday cards slide from drawers; toys 
abandoned years ago reappear beneath beds; photographs remind us of friendships that quietly 
disappeared without explanation. Although most of these possessions are trivial, throwing them away 
feels oddly painful, as though each item represents a fragment of the life we are preparing to abandon. 
Yet the countryside we dream about may not truly exist anymore. Modern rural life is far less 
idyllic than many city dwellers prefer to imagine. Industrial farming has transformed huge areas of land 
into efficient production zones dominated by chemicals, machinery, and endless roads crowded with 
heavy vehicles. Villages that once supported close communities now contain empty shops, neglected 
pubs, and commuters who spend most of their lives elsewhere. [III] Even nature itself appears 
increasingly fragile, threatened by pollution, coastal erosion, and unpredictable weather patterns linked to 
climate change. 
Still, despite all these doubts, I remain excited by the possibility of change. Perhaps what attracts 
us most is not the countryside itself, but the idea that life can still be rearranged before it becomes 
entirely fixed. I imagine quieter evenings, longer conversations, and children growing up with fields 
instead of concrete outside their windows. Maybe we will eventually regret the decision and return to the 
city we once defended so passionately. [IV] But for now, the thought of beginning again feels strangely 
liberating, as though reinvention itself were a form of hope. 
(Adapted from https://www.theguardian.com)

[V90.12.05] TINH HOA ĐỌC HIỂU (BUỔI 17) 
 
 
Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương

LEVEL 1 
 
 
 
Text 1 
Scientists are becoming increasingly concerned about the possible return of a “super El Niño” in 
2026. El Niño is a natural climate pattern caused by unusually warm water in the Pacific Ocean, but 
stronger events can seriously affect weather conditions around the world. Experts warn that the developing 
phenomenon could combine with global warming to create more extreme climate disasters.    
One major concern is the rise in global temperatures. Climate researchers believe that a super El 
Niño could increase the frequency of heatwaves, droughts, floods, and wildfires in many regions. Some 
countries in Asia and Australia may experience severe dry conditions, while other areas could face heavy 
rainfall and storms. Scientists have already reported unusual fire outbreaks and hazardous weather 
phenomena in several parts of the world.   
Although forecasting systems are now more advanced than in the past, experts still say preparation 
is essential. Governments are being encouraged to strengthen disaster response plans and improve water 
and food security before conditions become more serious.   
 
Question 1. What is indicated about El Niño in paragraph 1?   
A. It develops near Europe.  
 
B. It may trigger global disasters.  
C. It mainly affects winter tourism.  
D. It permanently cools oceans.

VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
Question 2. Climate researchers believe that a super El Niño could increase the frequency of all of the 
following EXCEPT ______.   
A. severe droughts  
 
B. destructive wildfires  
C. underwater earthquakes   
D. extreme heatwaves 
Question 3. The word “hazardous” in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to ______.  
A. regular 
B. ordinary 
C. dangerous 
D. conventional  
Question 4. What is indicated in paragraph 3?   
A. Forecasting systems are unreliable.  
 
LEVEL 2 
B. Food supplies are improving globally.  
C. Governments have reduced disaster funding.  
D. Early preparation remains necessary. 
 
 
 
 
 
Text 2 
Artificial intelligence is rapidly changing the way students approach writing assignments, 
especially in universities. A fiction-writing professor at MIT recently described how many students 
secretly relied on AI tools to produce essays and creative stories. Although the generated work often 
appeared polished and grammatically accurate, the professor noticed that much of it lacked originality, 
emotional depth, and authentic personal expression.   
During class discussions, several students eventually admitted that they had used AI because they 
feared failure or felt insecure about their writing abilities. Their confessions led to a broader conversation 
about the true purpose of writing. According to the professor, writing is not simply about producing 
perfect sentences. Instead, it is a demanding intellectual process through which people organise thoughts, 
confront uncertainty, and gradually develop their own voice.  
At the same time, some experts acknowledge that AI can still serve as a useful educational tool 
when used responsibly. Rather than banning the technology completely, they argue that schools should 
teach students how to use it ethically while still preserving independent thinking and creativity. The debate 
reflects growing concerns about whether convenience and efficiency may eventually replace the human 
struggle that often produces meaningful writing.   
 
Question 5. The word “polished” in paragraph 1 can be best replaced by ______.  
A. complex 
B. refined 
C. careless 
D. repetitive 
Question 6. The professor in the text found that much of the work submitted by his students lacked all of 
the following EXCEPT ______.   
A. genuine self-expression   
B. creative originality  
C. emotional complexity  
 
D. grammatical precision 
Question 7. According to paragraph 2, why did some students use AI to do their work?   
A. They wanted faster graduation.  
B. They lacked confidence in writing.  
C. They enjoyed technological experiments.  
D. They were required by teachers. 
Question 8. The word “it” in paragraph 2 refers to ______. 
A. purpose 
B. process 
C. writing 
D. dependence 
 
Trang 55

VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
Question 9. The word “confront” in paragraph 2 is OPPOSITE in meaning to ______. 
A. avoid 
B. handle 
C. manage 
D. adapt  
LEVEL 3 
Question 10. What can be implied in paragraph 3?   
A. AI should replace writing teachers completely.  
B. Creative writing no longer requires effort.  
C. Excessive reliance on AI may weaken creativity.  
D. Schools are planning to prohibit technology. 
 
 
 
 
 
Text 3 
Recent research has raised serious concerns about the long-term consequences of air pollution 
exposure during pregnancy. A study conducted by researchers at King’s College London discovered that 
babies exposed to elevated levels of pollution in the womb, particularly during the first trimester, tended 
to develop language skills more slowly than other children. Scientists examined nearly 500 infants born 
in London and measured their exposure to pollutants such as nitrogen dioxide and fine particulate matter.   
The findings revealed that toddlers exposed to higher pollution levels often performed worse in 
language assessments at 18 months old. Premature babies appeared especially vulnerable, as they also 
demonstrated weaker motor development. Researchers explained that the earliest stages of pregnancy are 
critical for brain formation, meaning environmental conditions during this period may significantly 
influence cognitive growth later in life. Although the precise long-term effects remain uncertain, experts 
fear these developmental delays could eventually affect educational performance and information 
processing abilities.   
The study has also intensified debates about environmental inequality. Campaigners argue that 
poorer and marginalised communities are disproportionately exposed to polluted air because they are 
more likely to live near congested roads and industrial zones. Many scientists therefore insist that stricter 
environmental regulations are urgently needed. They emphasise that air pollution should no longer be 
viewed solely as an environmental issue, but also as a major public health and social justice concern.   
 
Question 11. What did the study conducted by researchers at King’s College London find, according to 
paragraph 1?   
A. Air pollution reduced infants’ behavioural difficulties.  
B. London children developed communication skills unusually early.  
C. Prenatal pollution exposure delayed children’s language development.  
D. Traffic pollution increased infants’ sleeping difficulties significantly. 
Question 12. What is indicated about premature babies in paragraph 2?   
A. They showed poorer movement-related development.  
B. They adapted to polluted environments more effectively.  
C. They developed communication abilities more rapidly.  
D. They experienced fewer neurological complications overall. 
 
 
 
Trang 56

VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
Question 13. What is indicated in paragraph 2?   
A. Scientists can accurately predict every future consequence.  
B. Developmental challenges may influence children’s social relationships.  
C. Early developmental problems may influence later learning abilities.  
D. Information-processing skills may strengthen physical coordination. 
Question 14. The word “marginalised” in paragraph 3 mostly means _______. 
A. emerging 
B. disadvantaged 
C. aggressive 
D. resilient  
LEVEL 4 
Question 15. What do scientists say about air pollution in paragraph 3?   
A. It threatens international economic development.  
B. It damages wildlife in coastal environments.  
C. It increases pressure on farming communities.  
D. It creates both health and social inequalities. 
 
 
 
 
 
Text 4 
Parenting group chats on platforms such as WhatsApp have become an increasingly significant 
part of modern family life. Although these online spaces are often criticised for encouraging comparison, 
judgement, and unnecessary anxiety, many parents describe them as indispensable sources of emotional 
reassurance and practical guidance. For new mothers especially, the isolation of early parenthood can feel 
overwhelming once family visits end and daily routines become exhausting. [I]  
Many participants explain that these digital communities offer a form of solidarity that traditional 
support networks sometimes fail to provide. Parents raising twins, for instance, often feel that only others 
with similar experiences can genuinely understand the physical and emotional demands involved. [II] 
Several mothers admitted that they relied heavily on group chats during sleepless nights, medical 
difficulties, or periods of self-doubt. Unlike face-to-face parenting groups, which some perceived as 
performative or intimidating, online discussions occasionally allowed for greater honesty and 
vulnerability. [III] 
Nevertheless, the culture surrounding parenting chats remains deeply divisive. Critics argue that 
some groups become toxic environments dominated by judgement, competitive parenting, or relentless 
streams of trivial messages. Public debates surrounding these conversations have intensified in recent 
years, particularly as digital communication increasingly replaces in-person interaction. [IV] Yet 
despite these criticisms, many parents continue to view such chats as a contemporary version of the 
“village” traditionally associated with raising children collectively. In an era marked by geographical 
distance, demanding work schedules, and social fragmentation, smartphones may now provide the sense 
of community that many families previously found offline.   
 
Question 16. The word “intimidating” in paragraph 2 is OPPOSITE in meaning to ______. 
A. negligible 
B. encouraging 
C. inaccessible 
D. threatening   
 
 
 
 
Trang 57

Question 17. Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence in paragraph 3? 
Public debates surrounding these conversations have intensified in recent years, particularly as 
digital communication increasingly replaces in-person interaction. 
A. As virtual exchanges increasingly substitute direct social contact, disputes over these discussions have 
grown more prominent.   
B. Since public disagreement surrounding these chats has expanded, digital platforms now encourage

D. Because physical gatherings are becoming less common, controversies about digital parenting forums 
have diminished considerably. 
Question 18. Which of the following can be inferred from the passage?   
A. Parenting applications have diminished emotional dependence between relatives during challenging 
stages of childcare.  
B. Digital parenting communities increasingly compensate for weakening face-to-face support structures 
within contemporary society.   
C. Online parenting discussions encourage participants to conceal vulnerability behind carefully

D. Geographical mobility has strengthened neighbourhood relationships among parents confronting

Question 19. Where in the passage does the following best fit? 
In such moments, online chats frequently provide immediate companionship and advice. 
A. [I] 
B. [II] 
C. [III] 
D. [IV] 
Question 20. Which of the following best summarises the passage? 
A. Parenting chats increasingly replace traditional childcare networks by offering emotional reassurance 
despite criticism surrounding toxic online interactions.   
B. Many mothers rely heavily on online parenting discussions because digital communication provides 
companionship during emotionally exhausting childcare experiences worldwide.   
C. Critics increasingly condemn parenting group chats for intensifying competitive behaviour and 
encouraging emotionally harmful interactions between participants constantly.   
D. Smartphones now provide poor families with supportive parenting communities, although debates 
persist regarding their psychological and social consequences.

(BUỔI 17) – ĐỀ VẬN DỤNG 
Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương  
 
 
Quét mã QR để Ôn luyện chuyên sâu Từ vựng với EZ Vocab và Review Từ vựng;

LEVEL 1 
Text 1 
 
K-pop has become a global phenomenon and strongly influences teenagers around the world. 
Many young people enjoy K-pop because of its catchy music, impressive choreography, and talented 
idols. In addition, K-pop encourages teenagers to learn about Korean culture, fashion, and even the Korean 
language. Social media also plays an important role in spreading K-pop trends quickly across different 
countries. Many fans join online communities where they can share music, videos, and opinions with 
others who have the same interests. Furthermore, K-pop often inspires teenagers to become more 
confident and creative through dancing, singing, or creating fan content. Overall, K-pop is not only a form 
of entertainment but also a cultural influence that connects young people globally. 
 
Question 1. Which of the following is NOT mentioned as a reason why many young people enjoy K-
pop? 
A. catchy music 
 
B. impressive choreography 
C. its concert tickets 
 
D. its talented idols 
Question 2. The word “they” in the text refers to _______. 
A. opinions  
B. videos 
C. many fans  
D. communities  
Question 3. The word “globally” in the text is closest in meaning to _______. 
A. locally   
B. privately   
C. separately   
D. worldwide  
 
 
 
Trang 59

VIP 90PLUS: TỔNG ÔN TRỌNG ĐIỂM & LUYỆN ĐỀ TINH HOA NĂM 2026 
Biên soạn: Cô Vũ Thị Mai Phương || Độc quyền và duy nhất tại: tienganhcomaiphuong.vn  
 
Text 2 
 
In many countries, cities are becoming more crowded while rural areas are losing population, 
especially young people. Nowadays, many teenagers and young adults choose to leave the countryside to 
seek better opportunities in urban areas. One major reason is that cities offer more jobs with higher salaries 
and better working conditions. In addition, young people often move to continue their education because 
universities and training centers are usually located in large cities. Life in urban areas is also considered 
more exciting, with modern entertainment, technology, and social activities. Meanwhile, the countryside 
may seem quiet and lacking in opportunities for personal growth. As a result, more and more young people 
are deciding to build their future in cities rather than staying in rural communities. 
 
Question 4. Which of the following is NOT mentioned as a reason why young people move to cities? 
A. cleaner air and quieter streets  
B. modern entertainment and technology 
C. better job opportunities  
D. access to universities and training centers 
Question 5. The word “crowded” in the text is opposite in meaning to _______. 
A. modern  
.    
C. empty   
D. exciting 
Question 6. Compared to cities, rural areas _______. 
A. provide more universities and modern entertainment 
B. attract more young adults because of higher salaries 
C. are becoming crowded due to population growth  
D. offer fewer opportunities for personal development 
 
 
LEVEL 2 
Text 3 
 
Many young people struggle with the difficult decision of choosing between passion and money 
when planning their future careers. On the one hand, following a passion can bring happiness, motivation, 
and a strong sense of purpose. People who truly enjoy their work are often more creative and willing 
to work hard despite challenges. However, jobs connected to personal interests may not always provide 
financial stability or high salaries, which can create stress in the long term. 
 
On the other hand, choosing a well-paid career can offer security and a comfortable lifestyle. 
Financial stability allows people to support their families, travel, and enjoy better opportunities in life. 
Nevertheless, working only for money may eventually lead to boredom, frustration, or emotional 
exhaustion if the job feels meaningless. Therefore, instead of viewing passion and money as complete 
opposites, people should try to find a balance between personal satisfaction and practical needs. A 
successful career is often one that provides both financial security and genuine fulfillment. 
 
Question 7. When choosing a career, many young people often struggle between _______. 
A. studying abroad and staying close to their families 
B. working independently and becoming part of companies 
C. following their passion and earning a high income 
D. developing creative skills and improving physical health 
Question 8. The word “satisfaction” in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to _______.  
A. pressure   
B. failure   
C. contentment   
D. confusion 
 
 
Trang 60

Question 9. Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence in paragraph 1? 
People who truly enjoy their work are often more creative and willing to work hard despite 
challenges.  
A. Individuals who are passionate about their work tend to be creative and highly motivated.  
B. Workers who love their jobs rarely continue working hard during challenging situations. 
C. People usually become less creative when they face difficulties in their favorite jobs. 
D. Employees prefer easy jobs as difficult work reduces creativity and personal motivation. 
Question 10. Which of the following is TRUE according to the passage? 
A. Jobs related to personal interests always provide high salaries and financial security. 
B. Working only for money usually guarantees happiness and emotional satisfaction in life. 
C. People should completely ignore passion when choosing their future careers. 
D. A successful career often combines financial stability with personal fulfillment.  
 
Text 4 
 
The illegal wildlife trade has become a serious environmental problem in many Asian countries. 
Rare animals such as elephants, tigers, rhinos, and pangolins are often hunted or captured to be sold for 
traditional medicine, luxury products, or exotic pets. This activity not only threatens endangered species 
with extinction but also damages ecosystems and biodiversity. In addition, illegal wildlife trading is highly 
profitable, which makes it difficult for governments to completely stop despite stricter laws and 
international cooperation. 
 
Another major concern is that the wildlife trade can increase the risk of dangerous diseases 
spreading between animals and humans. Many wild animals are kept in poor and crowded conditions 
before being transported illegally across borders. To address this issue, governments should strengthen 
law enforcement and raise public awareness about wildlife protection. Individuals also play an important 
role by refusing to buy products made from endangered animals and supporting conservation efforts. 
 
Question 11. Which of the following is NOT mentioned as a purpose of hunting endangered animals? 
A. traditional medicine 
 
B. luxury products 
C. scientific research 
 
D. exotic pets 
Question 12. Why is it difficult for governments to completely stop illegal wildlife trading? 
A. Because illegal wildlife trading is highly profitable.  
B. Because international organizations refuse to cooperate with them. 
C. Because endangered animals can reproduce quickly. 
D. Because governments are not aware of its environmental damage. 
Question 13. The word “efforts” in paragraph 2 can be best replaced by _______. 
A. problems   
B. arguments   
C. attempts   
D. dangers  
Question 14. Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence in paragraph 2? 
Many wild animals are kept in poor and crowded conditions before being transported illegally 
across borders. 
A. Wild animals are usually protected carefully before being moved legally to different countries. 
B. Endangered species are commonly transported safely across borders under government supervision. 
C. Illegal wildlife traders often improve living conditions for animals during international transportation. 
D. Many wild animals live in unhealthy, overcrowded places before being smuggled into other countries 
illegally.

Question 15. What can be inferred from the passage? 
A. Wildlife protection depends entirely on governments. 
B. Public behavior can help reduce illegal wildlife trade.  
C. Illegal wildlife trading only affects endangered animals. 
D. Most endangered animals are now safely protected. 
 
 
LEVEL 3 
Text 5 
 
In the digital era, achieving viral fame has become easier than ever before. A single video, 
photograph, or controversial statement can transform an ordinary individual into an internet sensation 
overnight. [I] Although viral popularity is often associated with wealth, influence, and public admiration, 
it also carries numerous hidden consequences that are frequently overlooked by society. 
 
One significant drawback of viral fame is the intense public scrutiny that accompanies sudden 
popularity. [II] Individuals who unexpectedly become famous are often exposed to relentless criticism, 
online harassment, and invasive attention from strangers. Since social media users constantly demand 
new content, many viral celebrities experience enormous pressure to maintain relevance and satisfy 
audience expectations. Furthermore, internet fame is typically short-lived, meaning that public attention 
can disappear as rapidly as it emerged, leaving individuals emotionally exhausted or insecure. [III] In 
extreme cases, the psychological burden of fame may contribute to anxiety, isolation, or a loss of personal 
identity. 
 
For these reasons, viral fame should not be romanticized as an effortless path to success. The 
internet can undoubtedly create remarkable opportunities. [IV] Rather than blindly pursuing online 
attention, people should prioritize personal well-being, authenticity, and meaningful achievements beyond 
temporary digital recognition. 
 
Question 16. According to paragraph 1, viral fame _______. 
A. always guarantees long-term success and emotional happiness for ordinary individuals 
B. only happens to celebrities who already possess wealth, influence, and public recognition 
C. can suddenly turn ordinary people into internet sensations through online attention 
D. mainly affects professional entertainers rather than normal social media users online 
Question 17. Which of the following best summarises paragraph 2? 
A. Viral celebrities enjoy constant public support and easily maintain their online popularity forever. 
B. Viral fame can bring criticism, pressure, short-lived attention, and serious emotional difficulties.  
C. Internet fame helps people avoid criticism, pressure, and emotional problems from social media users. 
D. Sudden popularity mainly gives people confidence, personal security, and a stronger sense of identity. 
Question 18. The word “effortless” in paragraph 3 is opposite in meaning to _______. 
A. simple   
B. natural   
C. tough 
D. convenient 
Question 19. Where in the passage does the following sentence best fit? 
However, it can also amplify negativity and emotional vulnerability. 
A. [I] 
B. [II] 
C. [III] 
D. [IV]  
 
 
 
 
Trang 62

Read the following passage and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct answer to each of 
the questions from 1 to 8. 
Britain’s declining birthrate has become a growing concern for economists and policymakers 
because it may create serious long-term challenges for the country. Recent statistics show that the fertility 
rate in England and Wales has fallen well below the level needed to maintain a stable population. Experts 
believe that a combination of economic pressures, social changes, and personal choice is discouraging 
many young adults from starting families. Rising housing costs, insecure employment, and expensive 
childcare have made parenthood increasingly difficult for many couples.    
For many young people, financial insecurity remains the main reason for delaying or avoiding 
parenthood. In large cities especially, high rent prices and the cost of everyday living consume a 
significant part of household income. Some adults also worry about wider issues such as climate change, 
declining public services, and mental health problems among younger generations. Although surveys 
suggest that many people still desire two children, uncertainty about the future has made family planning 
far more complicated than before.   
The falling birthrate could also create economic difficulties in the coming decades. As the 
population ages, there may be fewer working-age people available to support healthcare systems, 
pensions, and other public services through taxation. Schools in some regions are already experiencing 
declining student numbers, leading to concerns about funding cuts and staff reductions. Experts warn 
that governments may need to rely more heavily on immigration or encourage older people to 
remain economically active for longer.

Read the following passage and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct answer to each of 
the questions from 9 to 18. 
The ethical legitimacy of zoos has become an increasingly contentious issue in recent years. 
Supporters argue that modern zoos play a crucial role in wildlife conservation, scientific research, and 
environmental education, particularly at a time when habitat destruction and climate change are 
accelerating species extinction worldwide. [I] Many institutions now present themselves not as 
entertainment venues, but as conservation centres dedicated to breeding endangered animals and raising 
public awareness about biodiversity loss.   
Nevertheless, critics contend that keeping wild animals in captivity remains fundamentally 
inhumane, regardless of educational intentions. They argue that even sophisticated enclosures cannot 
replicate the complexity of natural habitats or satisfy animals’ psychological and behavioural needs. Large 
mammals such as elephants, polar bears, and big cats are frequently cited as examples of species that 
experience chronic stress, abnormal behaviour, and physical deterioration when confined. Opponents also 
question whether observing captive animals genuinely promotes conservation, or merely normalises 
human domination over wildlife.   
The debate has intensified further because many zoos increasingly rely on commercial attractions 
to survive financially. Interactive experiences, animal performances, and social-media-driven tourism 
have led some campaigners to accuse zoos of prioritising visitor revenue over animal welfare. [II] 
Furthermore, conservation organisations have pointed out that relatively few captive species are ever 
successfully reintroduced into the wild. [III] 
Despite these criticisms, defenders insist that abolishing zoos entirely could undermine important 
conservation programmes and educational initiatives. Several endangered species, including rare 
primates and amphibians, have survived partly because of coordinated captive breeding efforts. 
[IV] Advocates therefore argue that the future of zoos should involve stricter welfare regulations, larger 
naturalistic environments, and a stronger emphasis on ecological restoration rather than public 
entertainment. The controversy ultimately reflects a wider philosophical question concerning whether 
humans can ethically justify confining animals for purposes they claim benefit the natural world itself.   
 
Question 9. The word “contentious” in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to ______. 
A. persistent 
B. controversial 
C. symbolic 
D. superficial  
Question 10. According to paragraph 1, instead of presenting themselves as entertainment venues, many 
zoos now ______.   
A. focus on expanding international tourism activities  
B. encourage visitors to purchase exotic animals legally  
C. promote wildlife protection and environmental education  
D. specialise in training animals for public performances 
Question 11. Which of the following best summarises paragraph 2? 
A. Critics believe captivity harms animals psychologically while reinforcing problematic attitudes toward 
wildlife exploitation.   
B. Opponents argue that sophisticated zoo enclosures frequently fail to reproduce authentic environmental 
conditions adequately today.   
C. Animal rights campaigners increasingly condemn educational programmes that encourage excessive 
tourism within contemporary zoological institutions worldwide.

Artificial intelligence is transforming the modern workplace so rapidly that many employees have 
started questioning whether their careers will remain secure in the coming years. (18) ______, businesses 
are increasingly using advanced systems to perform repetitive office tasks that were previously handled 
by human workers. As companies attempt to reduce costs while maintaining productivity, many 
employees have become anxious about the possibility of gradual job displacement.   
(19) ______. Certain companies have already reduced their workforce after introducing AI systems 
able to analyse data, write reports, and respond to customers within seconds. While some specialists argue 
that automation may eventually create new categories of employment, others warn that workers lacking 
digital skills could struggle to adapt to increasingly competitive labour markets. (20) ______, 
governments and educational institutions are under growing pressure to provide large-scale retraining 
programmes.   
At the same time, many economists believe the future of employment will depend less on whether 
AI replaces humans entirely and more (21) ______. Jobs requiring creativity, emotional intelligence, and 
complex decision-making are generally considered more resistant to automation, whereas routine 
administrative positions remain particularly vulnerable. Nevertheless, despite widespread concern 
surrounding the expansion of artificial intelligence, some experts maintain that employees who learn to 
collaborate with AI rather than compete against it (22) ______.   
(Adapted from https://edition.cnn.com)

Many people assume that blogging and vlogging are effortless routes to fame and financial success. 
Since social media platforms allow anyone to share opinions, hobbies, or personal experiences with a 
global audience, it may appear that becoming an online celebrity requires little more than creativity and a 
camera. Nevertheless, despite the glamorous image often associated with influencers, establishing a 
successful online presence is considerably more demanding than many people realise. 
Marketing consultant Kate Ross, who advises companies on digital promotion strategies, argues 
that content creators who focus exclusively on making money rarely achieve long-term success. 
According to her, genuine enthusiasm for a subject is essential because audiences quickly recognise 
whether creators are authentic or simply attempting to attract attention. She also explains that bloggers 
and vloggers must consistently produce engaging material capable of maintaining viewers’ interest over 
extended periods. Without originality and commitment, online creators are unlikely to develop a 
loyal audience. 
Furthermore, financial rewards generally emerge far more slowly than people expect. Seventeen-
year-old Rosie Bea, who has a YouTube fashion channel, says her blog only started attracting the attention 
of advertisers after she had put in months and months of unpaid work and built up a big fan base. Rosie 
was also combining her vlog work with her law studies, and she advises vloggers in the same position to 
make sure that they plan carefully so that they have time to do both things properly. 
Although newspapers frequently highlight stories describing the rapid rise of young influencers, 
genuine success in the online world is rarely immediate. Many prominent bloggers and vloggers have 
achieved recognition because they invested enormous amounts of time refining their content and building 
meaningful relationships with audiences. In reality, the media usually notices these individuals only after 
they have already established substantial popularity through persistence, discipline, and sustained 
dedication to their work. 
(Adapted from Solutions)

The Great Barrier Reef, one of the world’s most extraordinary marine ecosystems, is facing 
intensifying environmental pressure as climate change continues to raise ocean temperatures around 
Australia. Scientists have recently warned that large sections of the reef are experiencing severe coral 
bleaching, a phenomenon that occurs when corals expel the algae essential for their survival after 
prolonged exposure to unusually warm water. Although coral bleaching has occurred before, researchers 
emphasise that the frequency and magnitude of recent events suggest that the reef is becoming 
increasingly vulnerable to irreversible ecological deterioration.   
Marine biologists have described the current crisis as part of a broader climatic transformation 
affecting ecosystems across the planet. In some areas, underwater heat stress has spread so extensively 
that scientists have compared the damage to “underwater wildfires”, since enormous sections of coral 
communities appear devastated within remarkably short periods of time. [I] Coral reefs serve as crucial 
habitats where countless marine species find both shelter and sources of food. As these ecosystems 
deteriorate, increasing numbers of sea creatures may struggle to survive in affected areas. Scientists also 
warn that the damage could severely affect Australia’s tourism sector. [II] 
Experts argue that the situation has been exacerbated by a convergence of environmental factors, 
including marine heatwaves, the El Niño climate pattern, coastal pollution, and agricultural runoff. While 
certain coral species have demonstrated limited resilience under extreme conditions, scientists warn that 
repeated bleaching events occurring before ecosystems fully recover could permanently destabilise

Florence Nightingale is widely remembered as one of the most influential figures in the history of 
modern healthcare. Born into a wealthy British family, Nightingale was expected to lead a 
comfortable and conventional life. However, she chose a very different path, driven by a strong sense 
of duty and a deep concern for human suffering. At a time when nursing was often viewed as low-status 
work, Nightingale challenged social expectations and helped transform it into a respected profession. 
Her reputation was firmly established during the Crimean War, where she and a group of nurses 
cared for wounded soldiers in military hospitals. The conditions she encountered were appalling: the 
wards were overcrowded, poorly ventilated, and dangerously unhygienic. Rather than simply accepting 
these problems, Nightingale reorganized the hospitals, improved sanitation, and paid close attention to 
patients’ basic needs, including clean bedding, fresh air, and proper food. Her nightly rounds with a lamp 
later earned her the famous title “the Lady with the Lamp.” 
What made Nightingale exceptional was not only her compassion, but also her ability to combine 
care with evidence. She collected data, analyzed death rates, and used statistics to prove that many soldiers 
were dying from preventable diseases rather than from their wounds. Her work showed that good 
healthcare depended not just on doctors and medicine, but also on cleanliness, organization, and intelligent 
management. This scientific approach made her a pioneer in both nursing and public health. 
Florence Nightingale’s legacy remains powerful because she changed both attitudes and systems. 
She founded a professional training school for nurses and set standards that influenced hospitals across 
the world. More importantly, she showed that one determined person could challenge outdated beliefs and

Read the following passage and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct answer to each of 
the questions from 9 to 18. 
There are days when the sky is low and gray, when the world is heavy, and the most well-
intentioned plans to “get outside” dissolve into a stalemate with the couch. On such days, hope matters. 
Every little bit of joy matters. Don’t beat yourself up for reaching for your phone or laptop. But instead 
of doomscrolling, bloomscroll – deliberately search for true stories of human kindness, images of natural 
beauty, and videos of baby animals.  
Researchers studying awe have found that brief experiences of awe, which can be encouraged with 
nature videos, can lower stress, reduce rumination, and increase feelings of connection and meaning in 
life. These moments of “wow” shift the nervous system toward calm and reduced stress-related 
activation. Even images of nature, like photos and short videos, can lift mood and reduce mental stress. 
[I] Lab studies show that viewing images of natural environments (think forests, oceans, and flowers) 
increases comfort and relaxation while decreasing anxiety and other negative affect, likely by calming 
brain regions activated in depression and anxiety. [II] Even imagining natural scenes can reduce stress 
markers like skin conductance and heart rate.  
Psychologically, witnessing kindness is not just “feel‑good content.” Studies show that simply 
seeing acts of kindness can boost feelings of happiness, calm, gratitude, and inspiration, and make people 
more generous. You might watch a video of neighbors forming a human chain in floodwater to pull a 
driver from a stranded car, then going home soaked and anonymous. [III] Your nervous system reacts 
with, “There are still good people. You’re allowed to exhale.” Acts of kindness can reduce symptoms of 
depression and anxiety and increase life satisfaction more than some standard therapies. For the giver, 
kindness gifts back a better mood, lower stress hormones like cortisol, improved blood pressure, and even 
lower mortality risk over time. For the receiver, it can ease loneliness, lift mood, and restore a sense of 
being valued. 
Of course, helping your real neighbors, planting a community garden, and smelling real blooms 
remain the gold standard. [IV] Human laughter, feeding a puppy, and smelling jasmine at dusk are 
embodied experiences no screen can replace. But when the weather is stormy, or the body is tired, or the 
world feels too sharp at the edges, bloomscrolling can be a bridge to better. Let your world expand, and 
let your mind and body remember what it feels like to open, to hope, and to be human. 
(Adapted from https://www.psychologytoday.com/)

B. Avoiding all forms of technology and focusing only on outdoor physical activities instead

D. Using social media to follow celebrities, entertainment trends, and controversial discussions 
Question 10. The phrase “the gold standard” in paragraph 4 mostly means _______.  
A. the very expensive and luxurious activity 
B. the traditional habit people rarely practice today 
C. the method used to reduce stress and anxiety 
D. the best or most effective example of something
